topics.title: Operating System Overview - Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operating System Overview - Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

An **Operating System (OS)** is a fundamental, system-level software program that acts as an **intermediary layer** (an abstraction interface) between the **computer hardware** (raw physical resources like the CPU, RAM, disks, and I/O devices) and the **end user/application programs**. It manages all hardware and software resources, provides essential services to application programs, and offers a stable, consistent **Application Programming Interface (API)** through which user applications can interact with the underlying hardware.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, the OS is best understood as a **Resource Manager** and a **Control Program**. It does not perform "useful work" itself; rather, it creates the environment in which useful work is done efficiently, securely, and portably.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (High-Yield):**
> *"An Operating System is a collection of system software that manages computer hardware resources and provides common services for the execution of application programs. It is the most fundamental of all system software."*

The two complementary views of an Operating System that are frequently asked in KTU Board Examinations are:

| **Viewpoint** | **Description** | **Target Audience** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resource Manager View** | OS allocates, schedules, deallocates, and protects hardware resources (CPU, memory, disk, I/O) among competing processes. | System Designers, OS Engineers |
| **Extended Machine / Virtual Machine View** | OS hides the messy, complex hardware details and presents the user with a clean, friendly, high-level abstraction (e.g., graphical icons instead of disk sectors). | Application Developers, End Users |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large, busy **hotel**. The hotel has a fixed number of rooms (CPU cores), a fixed supply of food (RAM), a kitchen (disk/storage), and many hungry guests (processes). Without a manager, the guests would fight over rooms, the kitchen would run out of ingredients randomly, and the entire hotel would collapse into chaos within hours.

The **Operating System is the Hotel Manager**:
- When a guest (process) arrives, the manager checks them in and assigns a room (Memory Allocation).
- The manager decides which room service order (I/O request) is prepared first (CPU Scheduling).
- The manager ensures no guest enters another guest's room (Memory Protection / Security).
- The manager handles disputes and keeps a ledger of who-owes-what (File System / Accounting).
- If a guest wants a luxury suite but the hotel is full, the manager politely provides a "virtual" view of a suite (Virtualization / Abstraction).

In this analogy:
- The **Hotel Building** = Computer Hardware
- The **Hotel Manager** = Operating System
- The **Guests** = User Processes / Applications
- The **Rooms, Food, Kitchen** = Memory, CPU, Disk

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The OS serves three primary purposes: **(1) Convenience** (makes the computer easier to use), **(2) Efficiency** (allows hardware to be used efficiently), and **(3) Evolution/Abstraction** (allows new features and hardware to be supported without rewriting applications).

## 1.3 Operating System Functions — The Big Picture

The core functions of an Operating System can be broken down into **eight primary functional categories**, each of which is a guaranteed question in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Mastering these is non-negotiable for full marks.

1. **Process Management**
2. **Memory Management**
3. **File Management**
4. **I/O Device Management**
5. **CPU Scheduling**
6. **Protection and Security**
7. **Error Detection and Handling**
8. **Resource Allocation and Accounting**

> [!NOTE]
> **University Examiner's Tip:** Always state the *purpose* of each function before the *mechanism*. Examiners award marks for demonstrating that you understand *why* the OS does something, not just *what* it does.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Functions — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Process Management
A **process** is a program in execution. A single compiled program (e.g., `chrome.exe`) can spawn multiple processes. The OS must handle the complete life cycle of a process.

**Operational Steps Managed by the OS:**
- Creation and termination of user and system processes.
- **Suspension and resumption** of processes (preemptive multitasking).
- Provision of mechanisms for **process synchronization**, **inter-process communication (IPC)**, and **deadlock handling**.
- Maintenance of the **Process Control Block (PCB)** — a data structure containing the PID, program counter, register state, memory pointers, and scheduling information for every process.

### 2.1.2 Memory Management
Memory (RAM) is a finite, volatile, and the most contested resource in any computer system. The OS must:

- **Track** which parts of memory are currently in use and by whom.
- **Allocate** and **deallocate** memory blocks to processes dynamically.
- Decide which processes (or parts of processes) to keep in memory and which to swap to disk (**Swapping / Paging**).
- Implement **Virtual Memory** — a technique that gives each process the illusion of having its own contiguous, dedicated memory space, regardless of physical RAM size.

**Key Concept — The Address Binding Hierarchy:**

$$ \text{Source Code Addresses} \xrightarrow{\text{Compile}} \text{Relocatable Addresses} \xrightarrow{\text{Link}} \text{Executable Addresses} \xrightarrow{\text{Load}} \text{Runtime Physical Addresses} $$

### 2.1.3 File Management
A **file** is a logical collection of information stored on a secondary storage device (HDD, SSD). The OS abstracts raw disk sectors into named, hierarchical **files and directories**.

**Functions Performed:**
- **File creation, deletion, and modification.**
- Maintenance of **directories and folder structures** (e.g., NTFS, ext4).
- Mapping of logical file names to physical disk locations (via the **File Allocation Table / Inode Table**).
- Implementation of **file permissions, access control lists (ACLs)**, and **backup mechanisms**.

### 2.1.4 I/O Device Management
The OS hides the **enormous heterogeneity** of I/O devices (keyboards, mice, printers, USB drives, GPUs, network cards) behind a uniform interface.

**Architectural Layers:**
- **Device Drivers:** Hardware-specific software modules that translate generic OS commands into device-specific instructions.
- **I/O Scheduling:** Determines the order in which I/O requests are serviced to maximize throughput (e.g., SCAN, C-SCAN, FCFS disk scheduling).
- **Buffering, Caching, and Spooling:** Used to reconcile the massive speed mismatch between the CPU and I/O devices.

### 2.1.5 CPU Scheduling
When multiple processes are in the ready state, the OS must decide **which one runs on the CPU** at any given instant.

**Goal:** Maximize **CPU Utilization**, **Throughput**, and **Fairness** while minimizing **Turnaround Time**, **Waiting Time**, and **Response Time**.

**Common KTU Algorithms (covered in Module 2):** FCFS, SJF, Priority Scheduling, Round Robin, Multilevel Queue.

### 2.1.6 Protection and Security
- **Protection** is a *mechanism* — it controls access of processes or users to resources defined by the system.
- **Security** is a *policy* — it defends the system against internal and external threats (malware, unauthorized access, denial of service).
- Implemented via **authentication** (passwords, biometrics) and **authorization** (permission bits, capabilities, access control lists).

### 2.1.7 Error Detection and Handling
The OS must constantly monitor the system for both **hardware errors** (bad memory sector, power failure, overheating, disk failure) and **software errors** (divide by zero, invalid memory access, stack overflow). It must log these errors and either recover gracefully or terminate the offending process to protect the kernel.

### 2.1.8 Resource Allocation and Accounting
The OS must keep detailed **usage statistics** for billing (in cloud systems), performance tuning, and future planning. For example, tracking which user consumed the most CPU time, or which disk has the highest I/O wait.

## 2.2 The Dual-Mode Operation (Kernel vs User Mode)

A cornerstone concept in OS design is the **dual-mode operation**, which is fundamental to system security and stability.

| **Mode** | **Privilege Level** | **Code That Runs Here** | **Can Access Hardware Directly?** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Kernel Mode** (Supervisor / Privileged / System Mode) | **Ring 0** (Highest) | OS Kernel, Device Drivers, Core System Services | **Yes — Full, unrestricted access** |
| **User Mode** (Unprivileged / User) | **Ring 3** (Lowest) | Application Software (Chrome, MS Word, Games) | **No — Must request via System Calls** |

**Mode Bit:** A special CPU hardware register (the **mode bit**) is `0` in kernel mode and `1` in user mode. When a user application needs a privileged operation (e.g., reading a file), it triggers a **system call** (e.g., `read()`, `write()`), which causes a **trap** to the kernel, switches the mode bit to `0`, executes the service, and returns to user mode with the bit set back to `1`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** The transition from User Mode → Kernel Mode → User Mode incurs a performance overhead known as **System Call Overhead** and **Context Switch Cost**. This is a frequent 3-mark question in Part A.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept / Metric** | **Formula / Definition** | **Purpose / Used In** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CPU Utilization** | $\text{CPU Utilization} = 1 - p^{n}$ | Probability that all $n$ processes are in I/O wait ($p$) |
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT = \text{Completion Time} - \text{Arrival Time}$ | Total time a process spends in the system |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT = TAT - \text{Burst Time}$ | Time a process spends in the Ready Queue |
| **Throughput** | $Throughput = \frac{\text{No. of Processes Completed}}{\text{Unit Time}}$ | Jobs completed per second |
| **Virtual Memory Size (logical)** | $Logical\_Address = \text{Page\_Number} \times \text{Page\_Size} + \text{Offset}$ | Address translation in paging systems |
| **Effective Access Time (EAT)** | $EAT = (1-p) \times 1 + p \times (1 + T_{miss})$ | Average memory access with TLB ($p$ = TLB hit rate) |
| **System Call Cost** | $T_{syscall} = T_{trap} + T_{service} + T_{return}$ | Overhead of mode switching |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern **cloud computing** (AWS, Azure, GCP), the OS is the **hypervisor** that allows thousands of virtual machines to share the same physical hardware. In **embedded systems** (your car's Anti-lock Braking System, an Arduino drone), a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** guarantees that critical functions (applying brakes) complete within a strict deadline (e.g., < 1 ms). In **smartphones**, Android (a Linux-based OS) manages the touchscreen, GPS, camera, battery, and millions of apps with strict permission boundaries. The functions of an OS are not academic — they are the reason your phone does not crash when you open 50 apps simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Where Each OS Function Operates

This matrix maps every OS function to the specific layer of the computer system it operates on. This is a **high-yield KTU 14-mark question framework**.

| **OS Function** | **Hardware Layer** | **Software Layer** | **Primary Data Structure** | **Failure Mode If Missing** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Process Management | CPU Registers, Program Counter | Job Queue, Ready Queue | **PCB (Process Control Block)** | System deadlock / uncontrolled process sprawl |
| Memory Management | RAM, MMU (Memory Management Unit) | Logical Address Space | **Page Tables / Segment Tables** | Programs overwrite each other; random crashes |
| File Management | Hard Disk / SSD | Directory Tree, Inodes | **File Control Block (FCB) / Inode** | Data corruption, file loss, unorganized storage |
| I/O Management | Device Controllers, DMA, Buffers | Device Driver Stack | **I/O Request Queue** | Printer starvation, slow disk access, device hangs |
| CPU Scheduling | CPU Pipeline, ALU | Dispatcher, Scheduler | **Ready Queue (Linked List / Heap)** | CPU idle, starvation, poor responsiveness |
| Protection/Security | MMU, Ring Registers | ACLs, Capability Lists | **Access Control Matrix** | Unauthorized data access, privilege escalation |
| Error Handling | Hardware Interrupts, Sensors | Log Files, Signal Handlers | **System Log / Event Queue** | Silent data corruption, unrecoverable crashes |
| Accounting | Performance Counters (TSC) | Log Files, Billing Records | **Usage Statistics Table** | Resource hogging, no accountability in multi-user systems |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Dual-Mode System Call Mechanism

This derivation shows **exactly** what happens when a user application makes a system call. This is a favorite 7-mark KTU question.

**Scenario:** A user application (e.g., MS Word) wants to read a file from disk.

**Step 1 — User Mode Request Initiation**
The application calls the high-level library function (e.g., `fread()` in C). This is a normal function call in **User Mode (Ring 3)**.

**Step 2 — Library Wraps with System Call Number**
The standard library prepares a **system call number** (e.g., `sys_read = 3` on Linux) and the arguments (file descriptor, buffer pointer, byte count). It then executes a special CPU instruction — typically `SYSCALL` (x86-64) or `INT 0x80` (legacy x86) — to transition to the kernel.

**Step 3 — Hardware Trap / Mode Switch**
The CPU detects the `SYSCALL` instruction. Hardware automatically performs the following in a single atomic cycle:
- Saves the current **User Mode registers** (especially the Instruction Pointer `EIP/RIP` and Flags) onto the **kernel stack**.
- Loads the **kernel entry point address** from a pre-configured **Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT)** register.
- **Flips the Mode Bit from 1 to 0**, transitioning the CPU to **Kernel Mode (Ring 0)**.

**Step 4 — Kernel System Call Dispatcher**
The kernel's **system call dispatcher** reads the system call number from a register (e.g., `EAX` on x86-32, `RAX` on x86-64) and indexes into the **System Call Table** to find the address of the appropriate handler function (e.g., `sys_read()`).

**Step 5 — Privileged Operation Execution**
The kernel handler executes the privileged operation:
- Validates the user buffer pointer (to prevent the user from tricking the kernel into reading kernel memory).
- Issues commands to the disk controller via the device driver.
- Waits for the I/O completion interrupt.
- Copies the data from the kernel buffer to the user buffer using safe kernel functions (e.g., `copy_to_user()`).

**Step 6 — Return to User Mode**
The handler calls `iret` (interrupt return), which:
- Restores the saved User Mode registers from the kernel stack.
- **Flips the Mode Bit from 0 to 1**, returning the CPU to **User Mode (Ring 3)**.

**Mathematical Cost Model of a System Call:**

Let $T_{trap}$ be the trap overhead, $T_{service}$ be the actual service execution time, and $T_{return}$ be the return overhead. The total system call cost is:

$$ T_{syscall} = T_{trap} + T_{service} + T_{return} $$

A typical Linux system call on a modern Intel CPU costs approximately **100–1000 nanoseconds**, dominated by $T_{trap}$ and $T_{return}$. This is why high-performance applications (databases, games) minimize system calls using techniques like **memory-mapped I/O** and **batched syscalls** (`readv`/`writev`).

## 3.3 Worked Example: CPU Utilization Calculation

**Problem:** A system has 3 processes, each spending 40% of its time in I/O wait. Calculate the CPU utilization.

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**
Number of processes $n = 3$. Probability of I/O wait per process $p = 0.40$.

**Step 2 — Apply the KTU Formula**

$$ \text{CPU Utilization} = 1 - p^{n} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$ \text{CPU Utilization} = 1 - (0.40)^{3} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the Exponent**

$$ (0.40)^{3} = 0.40 \times 0.40 \times 0.40 = 0.064 $$

**Step 5 — Final Subtraction**

$$ \text{CPU Utilization} = 1 - 0.064 = 0.936 = 93.6\% $$

**Interpretation:** The CPU is busy 93.6% of the time, and idle only 6.4% of the time. This is an excellent utilization figure. If we had 10 such processes, the utilization would drop to $1 - 0.4^{10} = 99.99\%$, which is why modern systems must support high concurrency.

## 3.4 Comparison: OS vs. Bare-Machine Computing

This is a classic 7-mark analytical question in KTU Module 1.

| **Aspect** | **Bare-Machine Computing (No OS)** | **OS-Managed Computing** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resource Sharing** | Only one program runs at a time; full hardware monopoly | Multiple programs share resources via time-sharing / multiplexing |
| **Programming Effort** | Programmer must write code to directly control disk, memory, I/O | Standardized API (`open`, `malloc`, `printf`); hardware details hidden |
| **Error Handling** | One bug crashes the entire machine | OS isolates processes; one crash does not affect others |
| **Security** | None — any program can access any memory | Memory protection rings, permission checks, authentication |
| **Performance** | Theoretically fastest (no OS overhead) | Slight overhead, but vastly higher aggregate throughput |
| **User Experience** | Requires deep hardware knowledge; command-line only | Graphical UI, plug-and-play devices, seamless multitasking |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Layered View of a Computer System with the OS

This block diagram illustrates the hierarchical relationship between hardware, the OS, system programs, and application software — a guaranteed KTU 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1[END USER LAYER]
    apps[APPLICATION SOFTWARE LAYER]
    sysprog[SYSTEM PROGRAMS LAYER SHELLS UTILITIES LIBRARIES]
    oslayer[OPERATING SYSTEM KERNEL PROCESS MEMORY FILE IO SCHEDULER]
    hard[COMPUTER HARDWARE LAYER CPU RAM DISK DEVICES]

    user1 --> apps
    apps --> sysprog
    sysprog --> oslayer
    oslayer --> hard
    apps -. syscall .-> oslayer
    oslayer -. driver .-> hard
```

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Architecture Flow of OS Functions

This Mermaid block diagram maps the **eight core OS functions** and their internal interactions, satisfying the "Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow" requirement for complex conceptual topics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER PROCESS REQUEST] --> B{SYSTEM CALL}
    B --> C[KERNEL DISPATCHER]

    C --> D[PROCESS MANAGER]

    C --> J[ERROR HANDLER]
    C --> K[ACCOUNTING MODULE]

    D --> D1[Process Control Block]
    D --> D2[Ready Queue]
    D --> D3[IPC Channels]

    E --> E1[Page Table]
    E --> E2[Swap Space Manager]
    E --> E3[MMU Interface]

    F --> F1[Directory Tree]
    F --> F2[Inode Table]
    F --> F3[Buffer Cache]

    G --> G1[Device Drivers]
    G --> G2[Disk Scheduler]
    G --> G3[DMA Controller]

    H --> H1[Long Term Scheduler]
    H --> H2[Short Term Scheduler]
    H --> H3[Dispatcher]

    I --> I1[Authentication]
    I --> I2[Authorization ACL]
    I --> I3[Encryption Layer]

    J --> J1[Interrupt Vector]
    J --> J2[System Log]
    J --> J3[Signal Handler]

    K --> K1[Usage Statistics]
    K --> K2[Billing Records]
    K --> K3[Performance Counters]

    D1 --> L[RESOURCE RETURN TO USER]
    E1 --> L
    F1 --> L
    G1 --> L
    H1 --> L
    I1 --> L
    J1 --> L
    K1 --> L
```

## 4.3 The Dual-Mode Operation State Diagram

This state diagram shows how the CPU alternates between User Mode and Kernel Mode via traps and interrupts.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UserMode
    UserMode --> KernelMode : System Call or Interrupt
    KernelMode --> UserMode : Return from Kernel
    KernelMode --> KernelMode : Nested Interrupt
    UserMode --> UserMode : Normal Execution
    note right of UserMode
        Privilege Level Ring 3
        Applications Run Here
        Hardware Access DENIED
    end note
    note right of KernelMode
        Privilege Level Ring 0
        OS Kernel Runs Here
        Hardware Access GRANTED
    end note
```

## 4.4 OS Evolution Timeline (KTU Frequently Asked)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1940s BARE MACHINES] --> B[1950s BATCH SYSTEMS]
    B --> C[1960s MULTIPROGRAMMING]
    C --> D[1970s TIME SHARING UNIX]
    D --> E[1980s PERSONAL COMPUTERS MS DOS]
    E --> F[1990s GUI WINDOWS LINUX]
    F --> G[2000s MOBILE ANDROID IOS]
    G --> H[2010s CLOUD VIRTUALIZATION]
    H --> I[2020s AI EDGE CONTAINERS]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define an Operating System. Explain its two main viewpoints.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
An Operating System is a system software program that acts as an interface between the user and the computer hardware. It manages all hardware and software resources of the computer system.

**Two Viewpoints:**
1. **Resource Manager View:** The OS is viewed as a manager of system resources (CPU, memory, I/O devices, files). It allocates, deallocates, and protects these resources among competing user processes efficiently.
2. **Extended Machine / Virtual Machine View:** The OS hides the complex, low-level hardware details and presents the user with a simple, friendly, high-level abstraction. For example, instead of forcing the user to deal with disk sectors and cylinder-head-sector addressing, the OS provides a simple concept of "files" and "folders."

### **Question 2: What is the difference between Kernel Mode and User Mode? Why are both required?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
- **Kernel Mode (Supervisor/Privileged Mode):** A privileged execution mode (Ring 0) in which the OS kernel and device drivers run. The CPU can execute any instruction, including privileged I/O and memory management instructions.
- **User Mode (Unprivileged Mode):** A restricted execution mode (Ring 3) in which user application software runs. The CPU cannot directly access hardware or sensitive kernel data structures.

**Why Both Are Required:**
This dual-mode architecture provides **system stability and security**. If a user application crashes or contains malicious code, it cannot directly damage hardware or other processes. The mode bit in the CPU enforces this isolation. All hardware access must go through controlled **system calls**, allowing the OS to validate requests and prevent unauthorized operations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the definitions and forget to explain *why* dual-mode is necessary. This costs 1 mark. Always mention **security, stability, and privilege separation**.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: With neat diagrams, explain the various functions of an Operating System. Discuss in detail the role of the OS as a resource manager.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Functions of the Operating System [7 Marks]**

The operating system performs eight major functions:

1. **Process Management:** Creates, schedules, suspends, resumes, and terminates processes. Maintains the **Process Control Block (PCB)** for each process, which contains the program counter, registers, memory maps, and open file descriptors. Provides primitives for **inter-process communication (IPC)** like shared memory, message queues, and pipes.
2. **Memory Management:** Allocates and deallocates RAM to processes dynamically. Implements **virtual memory** using paging or segmentation, allowing programs to use more memory than physically available. Performs **swapping** to move inactive pages to disk.
3. **File Management:** Organizes data into hierarchical files and directories. Implements file operations (create, read, write, delete). Manages file permissions, access control lists, and disk free-space tracking via the **File Allocation Table** or **inode** structure.
4. **I/O Device Management:** Manages all input/output devices via **device drivers**. Performs **buffering** (temporarily holding data during transfers), **caching** (storing frequently accessed data in faster memory), and **spooling** (queueing output for devices like printers).
5. **CPU Scheduling:** Selects which process from the ready queue should run on the CPU. Uses algorithms like FCFS, SJF, Round Robin, and Priority. The dispatcher hands control of the CPU to the selected process.
6. **Protection and Security:** Enforces access control to resources. Verifies user identity (**authentication**) and checks permissions (**authorization**). Uses techniques like firewalls, encryption, and access control matrices.
7. **Error Detection:** Monitors both hardware (memory parity errors, disk failures) and software (divide by zero, null pointer) errors. Generates appropriate **interrupts, signals, or system logs**.
8. **Accounting:** Tracks resource usage per user/process for billing, performance tuning, and capacity planning.

**(b) OS as a Resource Manager [7 Marks]**

The OS as a resource manager is responsible for the orderly, efficient, and fair distribution of four primary resources:

- **Processor (CPU) Time:** Through **CPU scheduling algorithms**, the OS multiplexes the CPU among multiple processes. Even on a single-core system, it creates the **illusion of parallelism** via rapid context switching (e.g., every 10–100 ms).
- **Main Memory:** Through **memory allocation strategies** (contiguous, paging, segmentation), the OS decides which process occupies which physical memory region. The **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** translates logical addresses to physical addresses.
- **I/O Devices:** Through **device drivers and I/O schedulers**, the OS handles the enormous speed mismatch between the CPU (nanoseconds) and I/O devices (milliseconds to seconds). It uses **DMA (Direct Memory Access)** to offload data transfer from the CPU.
- **File/Storage Space:** Through the **file system**, the OS organizes secondary storage into a hierarchical structure. It tracks free blocks, maintains metadata, and ensures data integrity through journaling (ext4, NTFS) or copy-on-write (ZFS, Btrfs).

The OS must balance **efficiency** (maximizing resource utilization) with **fairness** (preventing starvation of any single process) and **deadlock avoidance** (preventing circular wait conditions). A well-designed resource manager is what distinguishes a usable OS from a research prototype.

---

### **Question B: Explain the dual-mode operation of an Operating System with a neat diagram. How does a system call work? What is its cost?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dual-Mode Operation of the OS [7 Marks]**

Modern CPUs support at least two distinct execution modes, identified by a special hardware register called the **Mode Bit** (or **Current Privilege Level, CPL**).

- When the mode bit is **0**, the CPU is in **Kernel Mode** (also called Supervisor Mode, Privileged Mode, or System Mode). In this mode, the CPU can execute every instruction in its ISA, including privileged I/O, memory management, and interrupt control instructions. The OS kernel and device drivers run here.
- When the mode bit is **1**, the CPU is in **User Mode**. In this mode, the CPU can only execute a restricted subset of instructions. Privileged instructions cause a **trap** (general protection fault) if attempted. Application software runs here.

**Diagram of Dual Mode:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KM[KERNEL MODE RING 0 MODE BIT 0]
        K1[OS Kernel]
        K2[Device Drivers]
        K3[Kernel Data Structures]
    end
    subgraph UM[USER MODE RING 3 MODE BIT 1]
        U1[Application A]
        U2[Application B]
        U3[Application C]
    end
    UM -->|System Call SYSCALL| KM
    KM -->|Return IRET| UM
    K1 -. privileged instruction .-> K2
```

**Why Dual Mode is Essential:**
- Prevents user applications from directly controlling hardware, which would crash the system.
- Provides **memory protection**: one process cannot read/write another process's memory.
- Enables **privilege separation**: the OS can revoke or grant capabilities dynamically.

**(b) System Call Mechanism and Cost [7 Marks]**

A **system call** is the controlled, well-defined mechanism by which a user-mode application requests a service from the kernel. Common examples: `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `fork()`, `exec()`.

**Detailed Steps of a System Call (e.g., `read()`):**

1. **User Application Calls Library Wrapper:** The C program calls `read(fd, buf, n)`. The `glibc` library prepares the system call number and arguments.
2. **Execute SYSCALL Instruction:** The library executes the `SYSCALL` CPU instruction, passing the system call number in a register (e.g., `RAX = 0` on Linux for `read`).
3. **Hardware Trap and Mode Switch:** The CPU automatically saves the user-mode state, switches the mode bit to 0, and jumps to the kernel's **system call entry point** (registered in the **IDT** or **MSR**).
4. **Kernel Dispatcher:** The kernel's `system_call()` routine reads the system call number and dispatches to the correct handler via the **System Call Dispatch Table**.
5. **Validate Arguments:** The kernel checks that the user buffer pointer is in valid user-space memory (using `access_ok()`) to prevent kernel exploitation.
6. **Execute Privileged Operation:** The actual read operation is performed — the disk driver issues a DMA command and waits for the I/O completion interrupt.
7. **Return to User Mode:** The handler sets the return value in a register, executes `SYSRET` (or `iret`), which restores user state and flips the mode bit back to 1.

**System Call Cost:**

The cost of a system call is the sum of the trap, service, and return overheads:

$$ T_{syscall} = T_{trap} + T_{service} + T_{return} $$

On a modern 3 GHz x86-64 CPU:
- $T_{trap} \approx 100\text{ ns}$
- $T_{return} \approx 100\text{ ns}$
- $T_{service}$ depends on the operation (e.g., 1 µs for a simple `getpid`, 10 ms for a disk `read`)

The **trap and return overhead are pure waste** — they exist only to switch privilege levels. This is why OS designers minimize context switches in performance-critical code (e.g., database engines use **user-space drivers** like DPDK to bypass the kernel entirely).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Common 14-mark Trap):** When asked "What is the cost of a system call?", do **not** answer "A system call takes 1 microsecond." This is wrong because it depends on the operation. Always give the formula $T_{syscall} = T_{trap} + T_{service} + T_{return}$ and explain that $T_{service}$ dominates for slow I/O operations, while $T_{trap} + T_{return}$ dominates for trivial operations like `getpid()`. This shows the examiner you truly understand the layered cost structure.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — OS Overview & Functions**

- **OS Definition:** System software that acts as an interface between user and hardware. Two views: **Resource Manager** + **Extended Machine**.
- **Eight Core Functions:** Process Management, Memory Management, File Management, I/O Management, CPU Scheduling, Protection/Security, Error Handling, Accounting.
- **Process Control Block (PCB):** The "ID card" of every process — contains PID, PC, registers, memory maps, state, scheduling info.
- **Memory Management Unit (MMU):** Hardware that translates logical addresses to physical addresses — enables virtual memory.
- **File Control Block (FCB) / Inode:** Metadata structure for each file (size, permissions, timestamps, disk block pointers).
- **Dual Mode Operation:** Kernel Mode (Ring 0, mode bit 0, full privileges) vs User Mode (Ring 3, mode bit 1, restricted).
- **System Call:** The only legal way for user programs to request kernel services. Triggered by `SYSCALL`/`INT 0x80`, causes a trap to kernel mode.
- **Mode Bit:** Hardware register in the CPU, flipped during every system call/interrupt.
- **CPU Utilization Formula:** $U = 1 - p^{n}$ (where $p$ is I/O-wait probability, $n$ is number of processes).
- **Turnaround Time:** $TAT = \text{Completion} - \text{Arrival}$. **Waiting Time:** $WT = TAT - \text{Burst Time}$.
- **OS as Hotel Manager:** Hotel = Hardware, Manager = OS, Guests = Processes, Rooms = Memory, Food = CPU Time, Kitchen = Disk.
- **Evolution Order (KTU favorite):** Bare Machine → Batch → Multiprogramming → Time-Sharing → PC → GUI → Mobile → Cloud → Containers.
- **Three OS Purposes:** Convenience, Efficiency, Evolution/Abstraction. Memorize this triad for short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boot Process - Process States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Boot Process - Process States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Boot Process (Booting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Boot Process** (or Booting) is the sequence of operations that a computer system performs from the moment power is applied (cold boot) or the reset button is pressed (warm boot) until the Operating System (OS) kernel is fully loaded into main memory and a login prompt (or GUI) is presented to the user. The term is derived from the concept of "pulling oneself up by one's bootstraps."

**Conceptual Analogy — The Morning Routine of a Computer:**
Imagine a person who has just woken up from deep sleep (power-on). They cannot immediately start working. First, they must:
1. Open their eyes and check if they are alive (Power-On Self-Test - POST).
2. Recall their identity and basic instructions (BIOS/UEFI Firmware execution).
3. Get dressed in their work clothes (Loading the Bootloader).
4. Wake up their brain and load their daily tasks (Loading the OS Kernel into RAM).
5. Finally, sit at their desk, ready to work (User login / Shell prompt).

This entire chain of "waking up" is the **Boot Process**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 explicitly expects students to identify the *four classical stages* — **Power-On Self-Test (POST), Bootloader, Kernel Loading, and Init/User Space**.

---

## 1.2 Process States

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Process** is a program in execution. As a process executes, it changes state. A **Process State** is a precise snapshot of the current activity of the process and the resources it currently holds. The classic 5-state model defines them as: **New, Ready, Running, Blocked (Waiting), and Terminated (Exit)**.

**Conceptual Analogy — A Patient in a Hospital:**
Think of a process as a patient moving through a hospital:
- **New** = Patient is being registered at the reception (just arrived).
- **Ready** = Patient is healthy, sitting in the waiting room, ready to see the doctor.
- **Running** = Patient is *currently* inside the doctor's consultation room (using the CPU).
- **Blocked/Waiting** = Patient is sent for an X-ray or blood test (waiting for I/O, cannot proceed).
- **Terminated** = Treatment is complete; patient has been discharged.

Just like a patient moves between waiting room → doctor's room → X-ray lab → waiting room → doctor → discharge, a process moves between its five states.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The transition between these states is managed by the **Scheduler** and **Dispatcher** components of the OS. The maximum number of processes that can be in the *Ready* state at any instant is bounded by the size of the **Ready Queue**, typically expressed in terms of memory capacity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Stages of the Boot Process — Structured Logic

### Stage 1: Power-On Self-Test (POST)
- **Why?** To verify the integrity of the hardware before trusting it to load software.
- **How?**
  - The **Power Supply Unit (PSU)** sends a "Power Good" signal to the motherboard once voltages stabilize (**+12V, +5V, +3.3V** are standard rails).
  - The **CPU** is reset and begins executing firmware instructions stored in **ROM/Flash** (specifically the BIOS in legacy systems or UEFI in modern systems).
  - POST tests: **RAM memory test, video card initialization, keyboard controller, storage controllers, and connected peripherals**.
  - Beep codes (1 short beep = success in Award BIOS) signal the result.

### Stage 2: Bootloader Execution
- **Why?** Firmware is too small (typically **1 MB to 16 MB**) to hold a full OS. A tiny program must locate and load the bigger OS.
- **How?**
  - The firmware scans the configured **Boot Device Order** (configured in CMOS/UEFI NVRAM, powered by a **CR2032** lithium battery rated at **~3V**).
  - It reads the **Master Boot Record (MBR)** — the first **512 bytes** of the disk — or the **GUID Partition Table (GPT)** for UEFI.
  - The bootloader (e.g., **GRUB2** on Linux, **Windows Boot Manager**) is loaded into RAM and executed.

### Stage 3: Kernel Loading
- **Why?** The kernel is the core of the OS that controls the CPU, memory, and devices. It must be in RAM for the CPU to execute it.
- **How?**
  - The bootloader reads the **kernel image** (e.g., `vmlinuz` for Linux, `ntoskrnl.exe` for Windows) from the boot partition.
  - **Initial RAM Disk (`initrd` or `initramfs`)** is also loaded — a temporary root filesystem containing drivers needed to access the real root filesystem.
  - The kernel decompresses itself, initializes core data structures, and prints the "Linux version" banner.

### Stage 4: Init / User Space
- **Why?** Once the kernel is running, it must start the *first user-space process*, which in turn starts all system services.
- **How?**
  - The kernel executes the first process, historically **`/sbin/init`**, now replaced by **systemd** (PID 1 — Process Identifier 1) in modern Linux.
  - System services, networking, login manager (GDM/LightDM) and the GUI/desktop environment are started.
  - A login screen appears, signaling the end of the boot process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Boot-time analysis is critical in **embedded systems** (automotive ECUs, IoT devices, aerospace) where a deterministic boot time (e.g., < 2 seconds) is a safety requirement governed by standards like **ISO 26262**.

---

## 2.2 The 5-State Process Model — Transition Logic

| State | Meaning | Transition Trigger | Transition Destination |
|---|---|---|---|
| **New** | Process is being created; PCB is being built. | OS accepts the program. | $\rightarrow$ Ready |
| **Ready** | Process is in main memory, waiting for CPU assignment. | Scheduler dispatches it. | $\rightarrow$ Running |
| **Running** | Process instructions are being executed by the CPU. | (a) Time quantum expires; (b) I/O initiated; (c) Terminated. | $\rightarrow$ Ready / Blocked / Terminated |
| **Blocked** | Process cannot run until some event occurs (I/O, signal). | The awaited event completes. | $\rightarrow$ Ready |
| **Terminated** | Process has finished execution; PCB still exists. | Cleanup complete. | Memory freed. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Boundary Condition:** A process in the **Blocked** state *cannot* be scheduled directly to the CPU, even if the CPU is idle. It must first transition back to the **Ready** state. This is a common 1-mark KTU question trap.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Numerical Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| MBR Size | $\text{Size} = 512$ bytes | First sector of boot disk |
| MBR Layout | $446$ (bootloader) $+ 64$ (partition table) $+ 2$ (magic) $= 512$ | Must sum exactly |
| CPU Power Rails | $+3.3\text{V}, +5\text{V}, +12\text{V}$ | Standard ATX specification |
| CMOS Backup Battery | $\approx 3\text{V}$ (CR2032) | Retains BIOS settings |
| Context Switch Cost | $T_{\text{switch}} \approx 1 \text{ to } 10\ \mu\text{s}$ | Depends on architecture |
| Dispatch Latency | $T_{\text{dispatch}} = T_{\text{stop}()} + T_{\text{load}()} + T_{\text{start}()}$ | Time to switch context |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Mark-Saver Rule:** Never use the vertical pipe character $\vert$ inside KTU answer sheets. The university uses a **right-angled bracket notation** $\mid$ for conditionals like $P \mid \text{Running}$ to mean "P is in the Running state."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: From Power-Off to Login Prompt

We model the boot process as a deterministic finite state machine. Each state $B_i$ represents a stage; the system moves from $B_0$ (off) to $B_4$ (login).

### Step 1 — Power Application
When the user presses the power button, the PSU begins to ramp up the DC rails. The motherboard chipset holds the CPU in **RESET** until the **+5V** rail is within a $\pm 5\%$ tolerance band.

$$
V_{\text{tolerance}} = V_{\text{nominal}} \times 0.05 = 5\text{V} \times 0.05 = 0.25\text{V}
$$

Thus the CPU is released only when $4.75\text{V} \le V_{+5\text{V}} \le 5.25\text{V}$. This prevents the CPU from executing instructions on unstable voltage (which causes data corruption).

### Step 2 — Firmware Hand-off
The CPU's instruction pointer (Program Counter, PC) is hardwired to a specific physical address: **0xFFFFFFF0** in legacy BIOS systems (top of the **4 GB** address space). It begins executing the firmware byte-by-byte.

$$
\text{Address}_{\text{firmware}} = 0x\text{FFFFFFF0}_{16} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}280_{10} \quad (\text{near top of 32-bit space})
$$

### Step 3 — POST Execution
The firmware runs a battery of checks. The total POST time $T_{\text{POST}}$ is approximately:

$$
T_{\text{POST}} \approx T_{\text{RAM}} + T_{\text{VIDEO}} + T_{\text{DRIVES}} + T_{\text{KEYBOARD}}
$$

For a modern system with **8 GB** of DDR4 RAM, the RAM test alone may take **2–3 seconds**. The video card initialization takes another **1–2 seconds** while the GPU firmware loads.

### Step 4 — Bootloader Handoff
The firmware reads sector **0** of the configured boot device into RAM at physical address $0x7C00$ (a 32-bit real-mode convention). The total data read is exactly $512$ bytes.

$$
\text{Bytes}_{\text{read}} = 1 \text{ sector} \times 512 \frac{\text{bytes}}{\text{sector}} = 512 \text{ bytes}
$$

The CPU then jumps to $0x7C00$, transferring control to the bootloader.

### Step 5 — Kernel Decompression
The bootloader (e.g., GRUB2) reads the compressed kernel image, often named `vmlinuz` — a **gzip-compressed** Linux kernel. Typical sizes:

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{compressed}} \approx 8 \text{ MB} \qquad \text{Size}_{\text{uncompressed}} \approx 40 \text{ MB}
$$

The compression ratio is therefore:

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{Compressed}}{\text{Uncompressed}} = \frac{8}{40} = 0.20 \quad \text{or} \quad 20\%
$$

### Step 6 — First User Process
After kernel initialization, it spawns **PID 1**. The total boot time $T_{\text{boot}}$ is:

$$
T_{\text{boot}} = T_{\text{POST}} + T_{\text{bootloader}} + T_{\text{kernel}} + T_{\text{init}}
$$

For a modern Linux system on an SSD, typical values are:

$$
T_{\text{boot}} \approx 2\text{s} + 0.5\text{s} + 1.5\text{s} + 1\text{s} = 5\text{ seconds}
$$

---

## 3.2 Process State Transition Analysis

Consider a process $P$ that begins in **New**, transitions to **Ready**, gets the CPU (**Running**), requests I/O (**Blocked**), gets the I/O completion signal (**Ready**), gets the CPU again (**Running**), and finally exits (**Terminated**).

The state sequence $S(t)$ can be written as a piecewise function of time $t$:

$$
S(t) =
\begin{cases}
\text{New}, & 0 \le t < t_1 \\
\text{Ready}, & t_1 \le t < t_2 \\
\text{Running}, & t_2 \le t < t_3 \\
\text{Blocked}, & t_3 \le t < t_4 \\
\text{Ready}, & t_4 \le t < t_5 \\
\text{Running}, & t_5 \le t < t_6 \\
\text{Terminated}, & t \ge t_6
\end{cases}
$$

The **CPU burst time** $B_{\text{CPU}}$ is the total time $P$ spent in the Running state:

$$
B_{\text{CPU}} = (t_3 - t_2) + (t_6 - t_5)
$$

The **I/O wait time** $W_{\text{IO}}$ is the total time in Blocked:

$$
W_{\text{IO}} = (t_4 - t_3)
$$

The **Turnaround Time** $T_{\text{turn}}$ is the total time from creation to termination:

$$
T_{\text{turn}} = t_6 - 0 = t_6
$$

The **Waiting Time** $T_{\text{wait}}$ is time spent in the Ready queue (not the Blocked queue):

$$
T_{\text{wait}} = (t_2 - t_1) + (t_5 - t_4)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuator's Insight:** Examiners frequently award marks for distinguishing *Waiting* (Ready queue time) from *Blocked* (I/O wait). Confusing them costs **2 marks** on average.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boot Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PowerOff State] --> B[Power Button Pressed]
    B --> C[PSU Stabilizes DC Rails]
    C --> D[CPU Released from RESET]
    D --> E[Execute Firmware from ROM]
    E --> F[POST Hardware Tests]
    F --> G{POST Passed?}
    G -- No --> H[Halt and Beep Error Code]
    G -- Yes --> I[Scan Boot Device Order]
    I --> J[Read MBR or GPT Sector]
    J --> K[Load Bootloader into RAM]
    K --> L[Execute Bootloader GRUB]
    L --> M[Load Kernel Image]
    M --> N[Load Initramfs]
    N --> O[Decompress Kernel to RAM]
    O --> P[Kernel Initializes Drivers]
    P --> Q[Spawn PID 1 Init or Systemd]
    Q --> R[Start System Services]
    R --> S[Display Login Prompt]
    S --> T[Boot Complete]
```

## 4.2 Process State Transition Diagram (5-State Model)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> NewState
    NewState --> ReadyState : Admission
    ReadyState --> RunningState : Scheduler Dispatch
    RunningState --> TerminatedState : Exit or Abort
    RunningState --> ReadyState : Quantum Expired
    RunningState --> BlockedState : I/O or Event Wait
    BlockedState --> ReadyState : I/O Completion or Event Signal
    TerminatedState --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Context Switching Timeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant Sched as Scheduler
    participant Q1 as Ready Queue
    participant P1 as Process P1
    participant P2 as Process P2
    participant IO as I/O Device

    Note over CPU,P1: Time T0 : P1 is Running
    CPU->>P1: Execute Instruction
    P1->>Sched: System Call - Initiate I/O
    Sched->>Q1: Save P1 PCB to Blocked Queue
    Sched->>P2: Load P2 PCB from Ready Queue
    Note over CPU,P2: Time T1 : P2 is now Running
    IO-->>Sched: I/O Completion Interrupt
    Sched->>Q1: Move P1 from Blocked to Ready
    Sched->>P1: Dispatch P1 when quantum arrives
```

## 4.4 Process Control Block (PCB) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PCB [Process Control Block]
    A1[Process ID PID]
    A2[Process State]
    A3[Program Counter PC]
    A4[CPU Registers]
    A5[Memory Mgmt Info]
    A6[Scheduling Info Priority]
    A7[Accounting Info]
    A8[I/O Status Info]
    A9[Open File List]
    end
    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A3 --- A4
    A4 --- A5
    A5 --- A6
    A6 --- A7
    A7 --- A8
    A8 --- A9
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Bootloader and the BIOS/UEFI firmware.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BIOS / UEFI Firmware | Bootloader |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Stored permanently in ROM/Flash on the motherboard | Stored in the first sector(s) of the boot disk (MBR/GPT) |
| **Function** | Initializes hardware, runs POST, locates the bootloader | Locates and loads the OS kernel into RAM |
| **Stage** | Stage 2 of the boot process | Stage 3 of the boot process |
| **Size** | Typically 1 MB to 16 MB | Typically 512 B to a few hundred KB |
| **Examples** | Award BIOS, AMI UEFI, Insyde H2O | GRUB2, LILO, Windows Boot Manager, systemd-boot |

*(1 mark each for: Location, Function, and Examples)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Why is a process not directly scheduled from the Blocked state to the Running state? Explain.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A process enters the **Blocked state** because it is waiting for some event to occur (e.g., I/O completion, a signal, or resource availability). The event that unblocks it is asynchronous and is signaled via an **interrupt**.

When the interrupt occurs, the OS moves the process from the **Blocked queue to the Ready queue** (this is a *software action* performed by the interrupt handler).

Only the **Scheduler** — which runs at fixed intervals or on specific events — can select a Ready process and Dispatch it to the CPU. A Blocked process has no entry in the Ready queue; therefore, the Scheduler cannot select it.

**Conclusion:** A Blocked process **must** first transition to **Ready** before it can become **Running**. This is enforced to maintain scheduler correctness and avoid race conditions. *(Full 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**a) Describe the complete boot process of a Linux-based computer system, with a neat flowchart. Mention the role of POST, MBR, GRUB, and initramfs.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Power-On and POST (2 marks)**
When power is applied, the PSU ramps the DC rails (**+3.3V, +5V, +12V**). The chipset holds the CPU in RESET until voltages stabilize. The CPU then fetches its first instruction from physical address **0xFFFFFFF0** (BIOS) or the UEFI equivalent. The **POST** checks the integrity of the **RAM, video card, keyboard, and storage controllers**. A single short beep indicates success in legacy systems.

**Step 2 — MBR and Bootloader (2 marks)**
The firmware reads the configured boot device (HDD/SSD configured in CMOS NVRAM) and loads its first sector — the **Master Boot Record (MBR)**, exactly **512 bytes** — into memory at address **0x7C00**. The MBR contains a tiny first-stage bootloader and the partition table. In modern UEFI systems, this is replaced by the **EFI System Partition (ESP)** with files like `EFI/ubuntu/grubx64.efi`.

**Step 3 — GRUB and Kernel Loading (2 marks)**
The first-stage bootloader is too small to load the OS. It invokes the second-stage bootloader, **GRUB2** (GRand Unified Bootloader). GRUB2 reads its configuration file (`grub.cfg`), presents a menu to the user, and then loads:
- The compressed **Linux kernel** (`vmlinuz`)
- The **Initial RAM Filesystem** (`initramfs`) — a compressed cpio archive containing essential block-device drivers needed to mount the real root filesystem.

**Step 4 — initramfs and Kernel Handoff (1 mark)**
The kernel decompresses itself, mounts `initramfs` as a temporary root, loads necessary modules (e.g., SATA, NVMe, EXT4 drivers), and finally pivots to the real root filesystem.

**Flowchart (built into the answer — 1 mark):**
Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the canonical flowchart.

---

**b) Explain the 5-state process model with a state transition diagram. How does the Scheduler differ from the Dispatcher?** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**The 5 States (3 marks):**

1. **New** — The process is being created. The OS allocates a **Process Control Block (PCB)**, a unique PID, and prepares the address space.
2. **Ready** — The process has all resources *except* the CPU. It resides in the **Ready Queue**, a doubly-linked list of PCBs ordered by priority.
3. **Running** — Exactly one process per CPU core is in the Running state. Its instructions are being fetched, decoded, and executed.
4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — The process cannot proceed because it is awaiting an I/O completion, a semaphore, or a signal from another process. It is in the Blocked Queue, *not* the Ready Queue.
5. **Terminated** — The process has finished execution (via `exit()` system call) or was killed. The PCB remains for accounting purposes until the parent process reaps it.

**State Transition Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes. Transitions occur due to:
- **Admission:** New $\rightarrow$ Ready
- **Dispatch:** Ready $\rightarrow$ Running
- **Timeout (Quantum Expiry):** Running $\rightarrow$ Ready
- **I/O or System Call:** Running $\rightarrow$ Blocked
- **Interrupt / I/O Done:** Blocked $\rightarrow$ Ready
- **Exit:** Running $\rightarrow$ Terminated

**Scheduler vs. Dispatcher (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Scheduler | Dispatcher |
|---|---|---|
| **Role** | *Decides* which process to run next (long-term, short-term, medium-term) | *Performs* the actual context switch |
| **Frequency** | Less frequent (e.g., every 10 ms on a Linux CFS) | Very frequent — invoked immediately after scheduler decides |
| **Algorithm** | FCFS, SJF, RR, Priority, MLFQ, CFS | Fixed 3-step procedure |
| **Output** | Selects a PID | Loads PCB, switches stacks, jumps to PC |
| **Cost** | Algorithm-dependent | Measured as **Dispatch Latency** |

The **Dispatcher** performs exactly three steps:
1. **Context switch** — save state of old process, load state of new process.
2. **Switch to user mode** — drop privilege level.
3. **Jump to the new process's Program Counter (PC)** to start execution.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**a) With neat diagrams, explain the structure of a Process Control Block (PCB). List any six fields stored in it.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **Process Control Block (PCB)** — also called a Task Control Block — is a kernel data structure that stores all the information required to manage a particular process. It is the *identity card* of a process. When a context switch occurs, the CPU registers are saved into the PCB of the old process and the PCB of the new process is loaded.

**Six Essential PCB Fields (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Process ID (PID):** A unique positive integer (e.g., 1, 2, 3, ..., 32768 on Linux).
2. **Process State:** Current state from {New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated}.
3. **Program Counter (PC):** The address of the next instruction to execute.
4. **CPU Registers:** Accumulator, Stack Pointer (SP), Base Register, General-Purpose Registers (R0–R15 on ARM).
5. **Memory Management Information:** Page tables, base/limit registers, segment tables.
6. **Scheduling Information:** Priority, accounting data (CPU time used, time limits).
7. *(Bonus)* **I/O Status Information:** List of open files, allocated I/O devices, pending I/O requests.
8. *(Bonus)* **Accounting Information:** User CPU time, kernel CPU time, exit status.

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.4** of these notes for the canonical PCB block diagram. A student who draws a labeled rectangular block containing all eight fields earns full marks.

---

**b) Compare the Long-Term, Short-Term, and Medium-Term Schedulers. Which one is most important in a time-sharing system, and why?** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**The Three Schedulers (6 marks):**

| Parameter | Long-Term Scheduler (LTS) | Short-Term Scheduler (STS) | Medium-Term Scheduler (MTS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Also called** | Job Scheduler / Admission Scheduler | CPU Scheduler | Swapping Scheduler |
| **Frequency** | Very low (seconds to minutes) | Very high (every 10–100 ms) | Moderate (seconds) |
| **Function** | Decides which *jobs* from the disk are admitted into the Ready queue | Decides which *process* from the Ready queue gets the CPU next | Decides which processes to **swap out** to disk to reduce multiprogramming degree |
| **Controls** | Degree of Multiprogramming (DMP) | CPU utilization, throughput, turnaround | Memory pressure, swapping |
| **Speed** | Slowest | Fastest | Medium |
| **Exists in** | Batch systems mainly | All systems (especially time-sharing) | Systems with virtual memory |

**Degree of Multiprogramming (DMP) — a key metric:**

$$
\text{DMP} = \frac{\text{Number of processes in Ready} + \text{Running}}{\text{Unit time}}
$$

**Most Important in a Time-Sharing System (1 mark):**
The **Short-Term Scheduler (STS)** is the most critical in a time-sharing system (e.g., Linux, Windows, macOS). Reason: Time-sharing systems must give the *illusion* of simultaneous execution to multiple users/processes. This illusion is sustained only by extremely rapid CPU switches — typically every **10–100 ms** — performed by the STS. Without a fast STS, the system would feel "laggy" to the user, and response time would exceed human-perceptible thresholds (about **100 ms**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A very common mistake is to confuse the **Scheduler** (which *decides*) with the **Dispatcher** (which *executes* the context switch). If you interchange them in your answer, you will lose **2 marks outright**. Also, students often write "**Waiting**" when they mean "**Blocked**" — these are NOT synonyms in KTU's marking scheme. *Waiting* = Ready queue, *Blocked* = I/O wait. Memorize this distinction.

---

## 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Boot Process Stages:** Power-On $\rightarrow$ POST $\rightarrow$ Firmware (BIOS/UEFI) $\rightarrow$ Bootloader (GRUB2/Windows Boot Manager) $\rightarrow$ Kernel Loading $\rightarrow$ init/systemd $\rightarrow$ Login Prompt.
- **POST** verifies hardware integrity. Failure = beep codes.
- **MBR** is exactly **512 bytes** at sector 0 of the boot disk.
- **GRUB2** is the default Linux bootloader; it handles multi-OS selection.
- **initramfs** is a *temporary* root filesystem containing drivers needed to mount the real root.
- **5 Process States:** New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated.
- **Key Rule:** Blocked $\nrightarrow$ Running. Always Blocked $\rightarrow$ Ready $\rightarrow$ Running.
- **PCB** is the *identity card* of a process — contains PID, State, PC, Registers, Memory info, Scheduling info, I/O info.
- **Scheduler** decides; **Dispatcher** executes the context switch.
- **Dispatch Latency** $T_{\text{dispatch}} \approx 1 \text{ to } 10\ \mu\text{s}$ on modern systems.
- **Degree of Multiprogramming (DMP)** is controlled by the **Long-Term Scheduler**; **CPU allocation** is the **Short-Term Scheduler's** job.
- **Cold boot** = power applied from off. **Warm boot** = reset button; skips some POST steps.
- **CMOS battery** (CR2032, **~3V**) retains BIOS settings when mains power is off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Control Block
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Control Block

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Process Control Block — Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Control Block (PCB)** — Also known as a *Task Control Block* or *Switchframe*, the PCB is a kernel-level **data structure** maintained by the operating system for every active process. It acts as the *physical identity card* of a process, holding all the state information required to **suspend, resume, schedule, and terminate** the process during its lifetime in main memory.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the PCB is the single most important *kernel data structure* that enables **multiprogramming** and **CPU virtualization**. When a process is *not* in the Running state, the OS *cannot* leave process information scattered in CPU registers and kernel memory; it must persist this information somewhere — the PCB is that location.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **hospital admission file** for an ICU patient.

| Hospital File | Equivalent OS Concept |
|---|---|
| Patient's **Unique Hospital ID** | **Process ID (PID)** |
| Current ward (ICU / General / Recovery) | **Process State** (Running / Ready / Waiting) |
| Doctor's notes on current vitals & treatment | **CPU Registers & Program Counter** |
| Insurance & billing records | **Accounting Information** |
| List of visitors & assigned nurse | **Parent / Children Pointers** |
| Allocated room number & bed | **Memory Management Info** |
| Scheduled next check-up time | **Scheduling Information** |
| Attached IV lines & monitors | **I/O Status / Open Files** |

Just as a patient is *not* the file — the file merely *describes* the patient — a process is *not* the PCB; the PCB merely *describes* the process. Even when the patient is shifted to a different ward, the file follows them. The PCB behaves the same way: it follows the process across all state transitions.

## 1.3 Why the PCB Exists (The Core "Why")

> [!NOTE]
> The CPU can execute **only one instruction stream at a time per core**. When the OS decides to *preempt* a process and give the CPU to another, every single piece of volatile state — registers, flags, the instruction pointer — must be preserved. Without the PCB, the resumed process would have **no memory of who it was**.

The PCB solves three fundamental problems:

1. **State Persistence** — Registers, program counter, and stack pointer must be saved across context switches.
2. **Scheduling Decision Support** — Priority, burst time estimates, and wait times must be retained for the scheduler.
3. **Resource Tracking** — The OS must know which files, memory regions, and devices a process owns for cleanup during termination.

## 1.4 Lifecycle Tie-In

A PCB is **created** when a process is created, **updated** at every state transition, and **destroyed** when the process terminates (its memory is then returned to the kernel free list). It resides in **kernel-address space** (protected from user-mode access) to maintain system integrity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the PCB — Structured Component Breakdown

The PCB is conventionally organized into **three logical categories**: *Process Identification*, *CPU State*, and *Process Control*. The bullet logic below traces the *why* and *how* behind every field.

### 2.1.1 Process Identification Block
- **PID (Process Identifier)** — A unique positive integer (e.g., on Linux, default max **32768** for 32-bit, **4194304** for 64-bit kernels). PID **0** is the *swapper* (idle process) and PID **1** is typically *init* (systemd on modern distros).
- **PPID (Parent Process ID)** — Required for forming a process tree; used during `wait()`/`waitpid()` syscalls and orphan-process reparenting.
- **UID / GID** — Real and effective user/group IDs for access control (Unix DAC model).
- **Why?** — Distinguishes the process globally and determines its privilege boundary.

### 2.1.2 CPU State Block (Volatile State)
- **Program Counter (PC / EIP / RIP)** — Address of the *next* instruction to execute.
- **General Purpose Registers (AX, BX, CX, DX, etc.)** — Working variables of the process.
- **Stack Pointer (SP / ESP / RSP)** — Points to the top of the process's kernel/user stack.
- **Base Pointer (BP / EBP / RBP)** — Frame reference for the current function.
- **Status Flags / Condition Codes** — Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow flags in the FLAGS/EFLAGS/RFLAGS register.
- **How?** — On every context switch, the OS executes a *save* of these registers into the PCB and a *restore* on resumption — using privileged instructions (e.g., `PUSHAD`/`POPAD` on x86, or compiler-generated inline code in modern kernels).

### 2.1.3 Process Control Block (Persistent State)
- **Process State** — One of *New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated*.
- **Scheduling Information** — Priority (static + dynamic), recent CPU burst, accumulated wait time, quantum remaining.
- **Memory Management Info** — Base/Limit registers, Segment Table pointer, Page Table pointer, swap device info.
- **I/O Status Info** — List of open file descriptors, I/O devices allocated, pending I/O requests.
- **Accounting Info** — CPU time consumed (user + kernel), wall-clock time, time limits, signal delivery history.
- **Links / Pointers** — Pointer to parent PCB, pointer to first child PCB, pointer to younger/older sibling PCB, pointer to ready-queue/waits-queue entry.

## 2.2 PCB Field Dependency Graph — The "How"

The following dependency logic governs what the OS does during every scheduler tick:

1. Scheduler reads **Scheduling Info** of each PCB on the Ready queue.
2. Picks a winner using the policy (FCFS, RR, SJF, Priority, MLFQ).
3. Calls `context_switch(current_PCB, next_PCB)`.
4. The `context_switch` routine saves the current **CPU State** into *current_PCB* and loads the **CPU State** from *next_PCB*.
5. The **MMU** is reloaded using the new PCB's **Memory Management Info** (page-table base register).
6. The **I/O subsystem** may flush or invalidate caches tied to the old process.
7. Execution resumes at the **Program Counter** of the new process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pointer field within the PCB allows the OS to walk the process tree in **O(depth)** time for `kill -<pid>`, orphan adoption, and zombie reclamation — without searching a flat process table linearly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes high-yield equations and metrics that frequently appear in KTU 2024 ESE derivations and numericals.

| Symbol / Term | Formula or Value | Description | Units / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $T_{cs}$ | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{MMU}$ | Total context switch overhead | **microseconds ($\mu s$)** |
| $U_{CPU}$ | $U_{CPU} = \dfrac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu} + T_{cs}}$ | Effective CPU utilization during a burst | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $N_{cs}$ | $N_{cs} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{q}$ | Number of context switches in Round Robin (quantum $q$) | Integer count |
| $T_{turnaround}$ | $T_{turnaround} = T_{waiting} + T_{burst}$ | Standard turnaround identity | **ms** or **s** |
| $T_{throughput}$ | $T_{throughput} = \dfrac{N_{completed}}{T_{unit}}$ | Processes completed per unit time | **processes / s** |
| PCB Memory | $S_{PCB} \approx 300 - 1500$ bytes | Typical PCB footprint in modern Linux | Bytes per PCB |
| Max PIDs | $2^{15} = 32768$ (legacy) / $2^{22} = 4194304$ (modern) | PID space ceiling | Integer |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 derivations, **never** use the pipe character `$\vert$` ambiguously inside prose. In the formula sheet above, vertical bars are deliberately avoided by writing $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{MMU}$ in expanded form to prevent LaTeX/markdown table conflicts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linux** stores the PCB equivalent as a `task_struct` (≈ 4 KB on x86_64) in the kernel's slab allocator.
- **Windows** uses an **Executive Process (EPROCESS)** block (≈ 8 KB+) plus a **Kernel Process (KPROCESS)** substructure.
- **Embedded RTOSs** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) use a much smaller **TCB** — often 50–200 bytes — because memory is scarce and processes are fewer.
- **Hypervisors (Xen, KVM)** keep an additional *Virtual PCB* per vCPU to multiplex guest operating systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Effective CPU Utilization Under Round Robin

The single most testable derivation in this module asks: *Given a CPU burst of duration $T_{cpu}$ and a per-switch overhead of $T_{cs}$, what fraction of time is the CPU doing useful work under Round Robin?*

### Step 1: Define the useful work interval

The process is in the **Running** state for a *net* duration of $T_{cpu}$ during one quantum. During the quantum, the CPU is not context-switching.

$$\text{Useful work per quantum} = T_{cpu}$$

### Step 2: Account for the overhead interval

When the timer fires, the OS preempts the process. The context switch consists of three sequential sub-phases:

1. **Save phase** — Push all general-purpose registers, PC, and flags into the outgoing PCB.
2. **Load phase** — Pop the same fields from the incoming PCB.
3. **MMU switch** — Reload the page-table base register (CR3 on x86) and flush the TLB if the new address space differs.

$$\text{Overhead per switch} = T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{MMU}$$

### Step 3: Write the total time slice

The combined duration of one quantum, including the trailing context switch, is the sum of useful work and overhead:

$$T_{slot} = T_{cpu} + T_{cs}$$

### Step 4: Compute utilization as a ratio

CPU utilization is the ratio of useful work to total wall-clock time. Substituting $T_{slot}$:

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{T_{cpu}}{T_{slot}}$$

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu} + T_{cs}}$$

### Step 5: Sanity check the limits

**Limit Case 1** — If $T_{cs} \to 0$ (ideal zero-overhead switch), then:

$$\lim_{T_{cs} \to 0} U_{CPU} = \lim_{T_{cs} \to 0} \frac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu} + T_{cs}} = \frac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu} + 0} = 1$$

This represents **100% utilization**, as expected.

**Limit Case 2** — If $T_{cs} \to \infty$ (degenerate overhead), then:

$$\lim_{T_{cs} \to \infty} U_{CPU} = \lim_{T_{cs} \to \infty} \frac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu} + T_{cs}} = 0$$

This represents **0% utilization** — the CPU is permanently context-switching.

**Limit Case 3** — If $T_{cpu} = T_{cs}$ (overhead equals work), then:

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{T_{cs}}{T_{cs} + T_{cs}} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

The CPU wastes **half** of its time on housekeeping. This is the *crossover threshold* the KTU examiner loves to test.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Context Switch Cost vs. Quantum

The following table shows how $U_{CPU}$ varies with quantum size $q$ when $T_{cs}$ is held constant. Assume $T_{cs} = 10\ \mu s$:

| Quantum $q$ (ms) | Switches per burst $N_{cs}$ | Total time per slot (ms) | $U_{CPU}$ | Verdict |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | 10 | 1.010 | $1 / 1.010 \approx 0.990$ | High overhead |
| 10 | 1 | 10.010 | $10 / 10.010 \approx 0.999$ | Sweet spot |
| 100 | 0.1* | 100.010 | $\approx 0.9999$ | Diminishing returns |
| 0.05 (50 $\mu s$) | 200 | 0.052 | $0.05 / 0.052 \approx 0.962$ | Overhead-dominated |

> *For $q = 100$ ms, the integer number of switches for a 10 ms burst is 0, hence the row uses a fractional value to illustrate the trend.

> [!NOTE]
> The "sweet spot" rule taught in KTU classrooms: **set the quantum $q \ge 10 \cdot T_{cs}$** to keep $U_{CPU} \ge 99\%$. Below this threshold, scheduling overhead begins to dominate real work.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Lifecycle of a PCB Through All Five States

1. **New → Ready**: A `fork()`/`exec()` system call triggers the kernel to allocate a fresh `task_struct` in kernel memory. The PID is assigned from a *pidmap* (a bitmap). The state field is set to `READY`. Pointers to parent and siblings are wired.
2. **Ready → Running**: The scheduler dequeues this PCB and calls `context_switch()`. CPU registers are loaded from the PCB. State field flips to `RUNNING`.
3. **Running → Ready (Preemption)**: The timer interrupt fires. The interrupt handler saves volatile state into the *current* PCB, then calls the scheduler. The PCB's state is set to `READY` and it is re-enqueued.
4. **Running → Waiting (I/O or Event)**: The process issues a blocking syscall (e.g., `read()` on a pipe with no data). The kernel saves state into the PCB, marks it `WAITING`, and enqueues it on the appropriate wait queue (e.g., the inodes's wait list).
5. **Waiting → Ready (I/O Completion)**: The device driver raises an interrupt on I/O completion. The driver dequeues the PCB from the wait list, flips its state to `READY`, and inserts it into the scheduler's run queue.
6. **Running → Terminated**: The process issues `exit()` or is killed. The kernel releases its file descriptors, frees its user-space pages, and finally frees the PCB itself — returning the slab to the kernel's free list.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Fields Inside a Single PCB

The diagram below maps the internal organization of a Process Control Block. The kernel views the PCB as a *container* of related sub-records; each sub-record is updated by a different kernel subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef coreStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef subStyle fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leafStyle fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0284c7,stroke-width:1px,color:#0c4a6e

    PCB["PCB PROCESS CONTROL BLOCK"]:::coreStyle

    PCB --> IDBlock["IDENTIFICATION UNIT"]:::subStyle
    PCB --> CPUBlock["CPU STATE UNIT"]:::subStyle
    PCB --> MemBlock["MEMORY MGMT UNIT"]:::subStyle
    PCB --> SchedBlock["SCHEDULING UNIT"]:::subStyle
    PCB --> IOBlock["I O STATUS UNIT"]:::subStyle
    PCB --> AcctBlock["ACCOUNTING UNIT"]:::subStyle
    PCB --> LinkBlock["PROCESS LINKS UNIT"]:::subStyle

    IDBlock --> PID["PID NUMBER"]:::leafStyle
    IDBlock --> PPID["PARENT PID"]:::leafStyle
    IDBlock --> UID["USER ID"]:::leafStyle
    IDBlock --> GID["GROUP ID"]:::leafStyle

    CPUBlock --> PC["PROGRAM COUNTER"]:::leafStyle
    CPUBlock --> REG["GENERAL REGISTERS"]:::leafStyle
    CPUBlock --> SP["STACK POINTER"]:::leafStyle
    CPUBlock --> FL["STATUS FLAGS"]:::leafStyle

    MemBlock --> PTBL["PAGE TABLE POINTER"]:::leafStyle
    MemBlock --> STBL["SEGMENT TABLE POINTER"]:::leafStyle
    MemBlock --> BASE["BASE LIMIT REGISTERS"]:::leafStyle

    SchedBlock --> PRIO["PRIORITY"]:::leafStyle
    SchedBlock --> QNT["QUANTUM REMAINING"]:::leafStyle
    SchedBlock --> BURST["LAST CPU BURST"]:::leafStyle

    IOBlock --> FDS["OPEN FILE DESCRIPTORS"]:::leafStyle
    IOBlock --> DEV["ALLOCATED DEVICES"]:::leafStyle
    IOBlock --> PND["PENDING I O REQUESTS"]:::leafStyle

    AcctBlock --> CTIME["CPU TIME USED"]:::leafStyle
    AcctBlock --> TLIM["TIME LIMITS"]:::leafStyle
    AcctBlock --> SIG["SIGNAL HISTORY"]:::leafStyle

    LinkBlock --> PAR["PARENT POINTER"]:::leafStyle
    LinkBlock --> CHLD["CHILD POINTER"]:::leafStyle
    LinkBlock --> SIB["SIBLING POINTER"]:::leafStyle
    LinkBlock --> QLINK["QUEUE LINK POINTER"]:::leafStyle
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — The Context Switch Flow

This diagram illustrates the precise sequence of events when the timer interrupt forces the OS to swap two processes (P1 and P2) on the CPU. Each numbered step corresponds to a *kernel action* in the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU CORE
    participant TIMER as HARDWARE TIMER
    participant KS as KERNEL SCHEDULER
    participant PCB1 as PCB OF P1
    participant PCB2 as PCB OF P2
    participant MMU as MMU CR3

    CPU->>TIMER: 1. Execute user code of P1
    TIMER-->>KS: 2. Interrupt fires quantum expired
    KS->>CPU: 3. Save PC flags registers into PCB1
    KS->>PCB1: 4. Update state field to READY
    KS->>PCB1: 5. Enqueue PCB1 to ready queue tail
    KS->>PCB2: 6. Dequeue PCB2 from ready queue head
    KS->>PCB2: 7. Update state field to RUNNING
    KS->>CPU: 8. Load PC flags registers from PCB2
    KS->>MMU: 9. Reload page table base from PCB2 memory info
    KS-->>CPU: 10. Resume execution at P2 program counter
    CPU->>CPU: 11. Begin executing P2
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — PCB Pointer Tree (Process Genealogy)

This diagram shows how the *link* fields of PCBs are wired together to form the process tree. The `init` process is the ancestor of every user process on Unix-like systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef procStyle fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef pcbStyle fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937

    INIT["INIT PROCESS PID 1"]:::procStyle
    SHELL["USER SHELL PID 500"]:::procStyle
    EDIT["TEXT EDITOR PID 510"]:::procStyle
    COMP["COMPILER PID 511"]:::procStyle
    DAEMON["LOG DAEMON PID 120"]:::procStyle

    INIT_PCB["PCB 1 PARENT CHILD"]:::pcbStyle
    SHELL_PCB["PCB 500 PARENT CHILD"]:::pcbStyle
    EDIT_PCB["PCB 510 PARENT SIBLING"]:::pcbStyle
    COMP_PCB["PCB 511 PARENT SIBLING"]:::pcbStyle
    DAEMON_PCB["PCB 120 PARENT CHILD"]:::pcbStyle

    INIT -.->|linked via| INIT_PCB
    SHELL -.->|linked via| SHELL_PCB
    EDIT -.->|linked via| EDIT_PCB
    COMP -.->|linked via| COMP_PCB
    DAEMON -.->|linked via| DAEMON_PCB

    INIT_PCB -->|child pointer| SHELL_PCB
    INIT_PCB -->|child pointer| DAEMON_PCB
    SHELL_PCB -->|child pointer| EDIT_PCB
    SHELL_PCB -->|child pointer| COMP_PCB
    EDIT_PCB -.->|sibling pointer| COMP_PCB
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Q: Define Process Control Block. List any FIVE fields stored in a PCB and briefly explain the role of the Program Counter field.**

> **Model Answer:**
> A Process Control Block (PCB) is a kernel-resident data structure that stores all information necessary to manage a process, including its identification, state, registers, memory maps, and resource allocations. *(1 mark)*
>
> Five fields stored in a PCB are: *(1 mark)*
> 1. Process ID (PID)
> 2. Process State
> 3. Program Counter
> 4. CPU Registers
> 5. Memory Management Information (Page Table Pointer)
>
> **Role of Program Counter:** The Program Counter (PC) stores the address of the *next* instruction to be executed by the process. When a process is preempted, the PC is saved into its PCB so that when the process is rescheduled, the CPU can resume execution from exactly the same instruction. Without the PC, the process would have no record of *where it left off*. *(1 mark)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is meant by context switching? How is the PCB used during a context switch?**

> **Model Answer:**
> Context switching is the mechanism by which the operating system saves the state of a currently running process and loads the state of the next process to be executed, effectively switching the CPU from one process to another. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> During a context switch, the OS performs the following PCB-based steps: *(1.5 marks)*
> 1. The CPU registers, Program Counter, and status flags of the outgoing process are *saved* into its PCB.
> 2. The outgoing PCB's state field is updated to *Ready* (or *Waiting*) and it is enqueued onto the appropriate queue.
> 3. The scheduler selects the next PCB and updates its state field to *Running*.
> 4. The saved CPU state is *restored* from the new PCB, and the MMU is reprogrammed using the new PCB's memory management fields.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE norms, Part B Module-1 questions are 14-mark problems with internal choice. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### ❖ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` — 14 Marks

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a Process Control Block. Describe the role of each field in detail. Also, explain how the PCB enables context switching in a multiprogrammed operating system.**

#### (a) Structure of the PCB with Diagram — 7 Marks

The PCB can be divided into three logical units:

**1. Process Identification Unit**
- **PID (Process ID):** Unique integer assigned by the OS at creation.
- **PPID (Parent PID):** Points to the creator process for the process tree.
