| Linux default PID max | `32768` (legacy) or `/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max` | $\text{pid}_{\max} = 32768$ | Configurable up to $4{,}194{,}304$ |
| Total processes after `fork()` then child `exec()` | Still $2$ (parent + child) | — | `exec()` does not duplicate, it replaces |
| Shared memory address space | $A_{shared} = A_{p1} \cap A_{p2}$ | — | Common mapped region |
| Producer waiting condition | $\text{full} \geq N$ | — | $N$ = bounded buffer size |
| Consumer waiting condition | $\text{full} = 0$ | — | Buffer empty |

### Real-World Utility

| **Domain** | **Application of Shared Memory IPC** |
|---|---|
| **Databases** (Oracle, PostgreSQL) | Shared memory caches the buffer pool for concurrent queries |
| **Web servers** (NGINX worker processes) | Shared memory for shared configuration and statistics counters |
| **Game engines** (multi-threaded renderers) | Shared frame buffers between render and physics threads |
| **High-frequency trading** | Lowest possible IPC latency between strategy and execution threads |
| **Operating Systems themselves** | Kernel subsystems communicate via shared in-memory data structures |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Counting Total Processes After a Chain of `fork()` Calls

**Problem Statement:** A process executes the following code:

```
int main() {
    fork();
    fork();
    fork();
    return 0;
}
```

**How many distinct processes are created in total?**

### Analytical Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

We model this using a binary tree. Each `fork()` doubles the number of processes currently alive, because each existing process invokes `fork()` once.

Let $N_i$ denote the number of processes alive immediately *after* the $i$-th `fork()` call (including the original parent).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state (before any fork)}: \quad & N_0 = 1 \\
\text{After } 1^{\text{st}} \text{ fork}: \quad & N_1 = 2 \cdot N_0 = 2 \cdot 1 = 2 \\
\text{After } 2^{\text{nd}} \text{ fork}: \quad & N_2 = 2 \cdot N_1 = 2 \cdot 2 = 4 \\
\text{After } 3^{\text{rd}} \text{ fork}: \quad & N_3 = 2 \cdot N_2 = 2 \cdot 4 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**General formula** for $n$ sequential `fork()` calls:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 2^{n}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{total}} = 2^{n} = 2^{3} = 8 \text{ processes}}
$$

### Why does each fork double the count?

Because the original process (parent) and every newly created child both continue execution from the line *after* `fork()`. So when the second `fork()` is reached, there are now 2 processes (parent + first child) that each execute `fork()`, yielding 4. The pattern is exponential.

---

## 3.2 Fork / Exec / Wait — Step-by-Step Execution Walkthrough

Consider the following canonical C code snippet (parent waits, child executes a new program):

```
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(void) {
    pid_t pid;
    int status;

    pid = fork();             // STEP 1
    if (pid < 0) {
        perror("fork"); exit(1);
    }
    if (pid == 0) {           // STEP 2 — child branch
        execlp("/bin/ls", "ls", "-l", NULL);   // STEP 3
        perror("execlp");     // only reached on exec failure
        _exit(127);
    } else {                  // STEP 4 — parent branch
        waitpid(pid, &status, 0);  // STEP 5
        if (WIFEXITED(status))
            printf("Child exited with code %d\n", WEXITSTATUS(status));
    }
    return 0;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Detailed Walkthrough

**STEP 1 — `fork()` invocation**
The kernel allocates a new PCB, assigns a new PID, sets up Copy-on-Write page tables, copies the parent's file descriptor table, and schedules the child. `fork()` returns in **two places**:
- In the parent: returns the child's PID (a positive integer).
- In the child: returns `0`.

**STEP 2 — Child branch (`pid == 0`)**
The child takes this branch because `fork()` returned `0` to it. The child process is now running concurrently with the parent.

**STEP 3 — `execlp("/bin/ls", "ls", "-l", NULL)`**
The `exec` system call **overlays** the child's address space with the `/bin/ls` binary. On success, the old program (`main()` of this file) is gone forever — execution continues inside `ls`. On failure, control returns to the line below and `perror` is invoked.

**STEP 4 — Parent branch (`pid > 0`)**
The parent takes this branch because `fork()` returned the child's PID. The parent proceeds immediately to `waitpid()`.

**STEP 5 — `waitpid(pid, &status, 0)`**
The parent blocks (suspends) until the child (whose PID matches) terminates. When the child exits, the kernel:
- Marks the child as a **zombie**.
- Stores the exit status in the PCB.
- Wakes the parent.
- The parent reads `status`, parses it with `WIFEXITED` / `WEXITSTATUS`, and prints the result.
- The kernel then **reaps** the zombie child, freeing its PCB.

### Logical Condition Evaluation

$$
\text{Process lifetime} = T_{\text{create}} + T_{\text{ready}} + T_{\text{run}} + T_{\text{exit}} + T_{\text{zombie}}
$$

where:
- $T_{\text{create}}$ = PCB + address space setup time
- $T_{\text{ready}}$ = time spent in ready queue
- $T_{\text{run}}$ = time executing on CPU
- $T_{\text{exit}}$ = time in exit system call (cleanup)
- $T_{\text{zombie}}$ = time as zombie (until parent `wait()`s)

For the child in our code: $T_{\text{zombie}} \to 0$ because the parent explicitly calls `waitpid()`.

---

## 3.3 Shared Memory Producer–Consumer — Analytical Walkthrough

### Bounded Buffer Setup

Assume:
- Buffer size $N = 5$ slots.
- `in` pointer (next write position) initialized to $0$.
- `out` pointer (next read position) initialized to $0$.
- All three are placed in a shared memory segment.

### Producer Pseudocode with Synchronization

```
Producer:
    while (true) {
        // 1. Produce an item (local work)
        item = produce();

        // 2. Wait for an empty slot
        sem_wait(&empty);   // empty--

        // 3. Lock the buffer (mutual exclusion)
        sem_wait(&mutex);

        // 4. Critical Section: write item
        buffer[in] = item;
        in = (in + 1) mod N;

        // 5. Unlock
        sem_post(&mutex);

        // 6. Signal that one more slot is full
        sem_post(&full);    // full++
    }
```

### Consumer Pseudocode with Synchronization

```
Consumer:
    while (true) {
        // 1. Wait for a full slot
        sem_wait(&full);    // full--

        // 2. Lock the buffer
        sem_wait(&mutex);

        // 3. Critical Section: read item
        item = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) mod N;

        // 4. Unlock
        sem_post(&mutex);

        // 5. Signal that one more slot is empty
        sem_post(&empty);   // empty++

        // 6. Consume the item
        consume(item);
    }
```

### Mathematical Invariant

At any point during correct execution, the following invariant must hold:

$$
\text{empty} + \text{full} = N
$$

$$
\boxed{0 \leq \text{empty} \leq N \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \leq \text{full} \leq N}
$$

### Why Must `mutex` Be Acquired AFTER Waiting on `empty` / `full`?

**Critical insight for KTU exams:**

$$
\text{Order of sem\_wait calls} \neq \text{Order of sem\_post calls}
$$

- The ordering of `sem_wait` calls matters to **prevent deadlock**.
- The ordering of `sem_post` calls is flexible (but conventionally reversed of wait order).
- Acquiring `mutex` first would lead to **deadlock** if another process holds `mutex` and is waiting on `empty` or `full`.

### Step-by-Step State Trace Table

| **Step** | **Action** | **empty** | **full** | **buffer state** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | Semaphores created | $5$ | $0$ | All slots empty |
| Producer produces item A | `sem_wait(empty)` | $4$ | $0$ | — |
| Producer enters CS | `sem_wait(mutex)` | $4$ | $0$ | Write A at `in=0` |
| Producer leaves CS | `sem_post(mutex)` | $4$ | $0$ | — |
| Producer signals | `sem_post(full)` | $4$ | $1$ | 1 full slot |
| Consumer requests item | `sem_wait(full)` | $4$ | $0$ | — |
| Consumer enters CS | `sem_wait(mutex)` | $4$ | $0$ | Read A at `out=0` |
| Consumer leaves CS | `sem_post(mutex)` | $4$ | $0$ | — |
| Consumer signals | `sem_post(empty)` | $5$ | $0$ | All slots empty again |

---

## 3.4 Shared Memory System Calls — POSIX vs System V Comparison

### System V Shared Memory API

| **Function** | **Purpose** | **Returns** |
|---|---|---|
| `shmget(key_t key, size_t size, int shmflg)` | Create or get access to a shared memory segment | Shared memory ID |
| `shmat(int shmid, const void *shmaddr, int shmflg)` | Attach the segment to the process's address space | Pointer to attached segment |
| `shmdt(const void *shmaddr)` | Detach the segment from the process | `0` on success, `-1` on error |
| `shmctl(int shmid, int cmd, struct shmid_ds *buf)` | Control operations (e.g., `IPC_RMID` to remove) | Depends on `cmd` |

### POSIX Shared Memory API

| **Function** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|
| `shm_open(const char *name, int oflag, mode_t mode)` | Open/create a POSIX shared memory object (returns fd) |
| `ftruncate(int fd, off_t length)` | Set size of the shared memory object |
| `mmap(void *addr, size_t length, int prot, int flags, int fd, off_t offset)` | Map the object into address space |
| `munmap(void *addr, size_t length)` | Unmap the region |
| `shm_unlink(const char *name)` | Remove the shared memory object |

### Derived Relationship

$$
\text{Process Virtual Address of Shared Region} = \text{mmap return} = A_{\text{base}} + \text{offset}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{shared}} = \text{mmap}(\text{NULL}, N, \text{PROT\_READ} \mid \text{PROT\_WRITE}, \text{MAP\_SHARED}, \text{fd}, 0)}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain how a process is created using the `fork()` system call in UNIX.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When a parent process invokes `fork()`:
1. The kernel creates a **new PCB** for the child with a unique PID.
2. The child's **address space is a logical copy** of the parent's. Modern systems use **Copy-on-Write (COW)** — physical pages are shared until either process modifies them.
3. The child inherits **open file descriptors** of the parent, sharing the underlying kernel `file` structures (and thus the same file offsets).
4. `fork()` returns in **two places** — the child's PID to the parent and `0` to the child — enabling the program to distinguish parent from child code paths.

Diagram (textual):
```
   Parent (PID 1001)
        |
        | fork()
        v
   +----+----+
   |         |
Parent    Child (PID 1002)
returns   returns
child PID     0
```

**(3 marks: 1 mark for listing steps, 1 mark for COW concept, 1 mark for diagram)**

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: Differentiate between shared memory and message passing as IPC mechanisms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Shared Memory** | **Message Passing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Fast — no kernel mediation after `shmat`/`mmap` setup | Slower — every `send`/`receive` is a system call |
| **Setup** | Requires explicit creation (`shmget`/`shm_open`) and attachment (`shmat`/`mmap`) | Simpler — just open a pipe/FIFO/socket |
| **Synchronization** | Programmer's responsibility (must use semaphores) | Implicit — kernel synchronizes send/receive |
| **Best for** | Large data, high-throughput | Small data, distributed systems, security |
| **Conflicts** | Need mutual exclusion (race conditions possible) | No conflicts — kernel serializes |

**(3 marks: 1 mark for each valid distinguishing pair; minimum 3 contrasts needed)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1]

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the various operations on processes including **process creation, process termination, and process hierarchy**. Include in your answer the role of `fork()`, `exec()`, `wait()`, `exit()`, and the concept of **zombie and orphan processes**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a C program that demonstrates a **producer–consumer** system using **POSIX shared memory** and **POSIX semaphores** for a bounded buffer of size 5. Explain the role of each semaphore.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Process Creation:**
- A new process is created by the `fork()` system call. (1 mark)
- The creating process is the **parent**; the new process is the **child**. Each process is uniquely identified by a **PID** (typically `0` to `32768`). (1 mark)
- `fork()` returns the child's PID to the parent and `0` to the child. After `fork()`, both processes continue execution concurrently from the same point in the code. (1 mark)

**Process Termination:**
- A process terminates either **normally** (via `exit()` or `return` from `main`) or **abnormally** (killed by a signal such as `SIGKILL`, `SIGSEGV`). (1 mark)
- After termination, the process becomes a **zombie** — its PCB remains until the parent calls `wait()` to reap it. (1 mark)

**Process Hierarchy & Orphans:**
- In UNIX, processes form a **tree** rooted at `init`/`systemd` (PID 1). Every child has exactly one parent.
- If a parent terminates before its child, the child becomes an **orphan** and is adopted by `init`, which periodically `wait()`s on it to prevent zombie accumulation. (1 mark)

**The `exec()` Family:**
- After `fork()`, the child typically calls `exec()` to replace its address space with a new program. The PID is unchanged; the program image is fully replaced. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**C Program — Producer Process (`producer.c`):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/mman.h>
#include <sys/stat.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <semaphore.h>
#include <string.h>

#define BUFFER_SIZE 5
#define SHM_NAME "/my_shared_buffer"

typedef struct {
    int buffer[BUFFER_SIZE];
    int in;
    int out;
} shared_data;

int main(void) {
    int shm_fd = shm_open(SHM_NAME, O_CREAT | O_RDWR, 0666);
    ftruncate(shm_fd, sizeof(shared_data));

    shared_data *data = mmap(NULL, sizeof(shared_data),
                             PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE,
                             MAP_SHARED, shm_fd, 0);

    sem_t *mutex = sem_open("/mutex_sem", O_CREAT, 0666, 1);
    sem_t *empty = sem_open("/empty_sem", O_CREAT, 0666, BUFFER_SIZE);
    sem_t *full  = sem_open("/full_sem",  O_CREAT, 0666, 0);

    data->in = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < 20; i++) {
        sem_wait(empty);                 // (1) wait for empty slot
        sem_wait(mutex);                 // (2) enter critical section
        data->buffer[data->in] = i;      // (3) produce item
        data->in = (data->in + 1) % BUFFER_SIZE;
        printf("Produced %d\n", i);
        sem_post(mutex);                 // (4) leave critical section
        sem_post(full);                  // (5) signal new full slot
    }

    munmap(data, sizeof(shared_data));
    close(shm_fd);
    return 0;
}
```

**C Program — Consumer Process (`consumer.c`):**

```c
/* Similar setup with mmap ... */
    for (int i = 0; i < 20; i++) {
        sem_wait(full);
        sem_wait(mutex);
        int item = data->buffer[data->out];
        data->out = (data->out + 1) % BUFFER_SIZE;
        printf("Consumed %d\n", item);
        sem_post(mutex);
        sem_post(empty);
    }
```

**Role of Each Semaphore (1 mark per explanation):**
- **`mutex`** (initial = 1): Ensures **mutual exclusion** — only one process enters the critical section (writing/reading the buffer) at a time.
- **`empty`** (initial = N = 5): Counts the number of empty slots. Producer blocks here when buffer is full.
- **`full`** (initial = 0): Counts the number of full slots. Consumer blocks here when buffer is empty.

**Cleanup:**
```c
shm_unlink(SHM_NAME);
sem_unlink("/mutex_sem");
sem_unlink("/empty_sem");
sem_unlink("/full_sem");
```

**(7 marks: 2 marks program structure, 2 marks for producer code, 2 marks for consumer code, 1 mark for semaphore explanation)**

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2]

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of relevant system calls and code snippets, explain the **System V shared memory IPC mechanism**. Show how a process creates, attaches, writes to, reads from, and detaches a shared memory segment.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **POSIX shared memory** with **System V shared memory**. Also discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of shared memory IPC.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Include headers and define the segment**

```c
#include <sys/ipc.h>
#include <sys/shm.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

#define KEY 1234
#define SIZE 1024
```

**Step 2: Create the shared memory segment** (1 mark)

```c
int shmid = shmget(KEY, SIZE, IPC_CREAT | 0666);
if (shmid < 0) { perror("shmget"); return 1; }
```
`shmget()` allocates a shared memory segment of `SIZE` bytes identified by the integer key `KEY` and returns a shared memory ID.

**Step 3: Attach the segment to the address space** (1 mark)

```c
char *shm_ptr = (char *) shmat(shmid, NULL, 0);
if (shm_ptr == (char *) -1) { perror("shmat"); return 1; }
```
`shmat()` maps the segment into the calling process's virtual address space and returns a pointer to it. From now on, the process can read/write using ordinary memory operations.

**Step 4: Write data to shared memory** (1 mark)

```c
strcpy(shm_ptr, "Hello from shared memory!");
printf("Writer wrote: %s\n", shm_ptr);
```

**Step 5: Read data from shared memory (in a different process)** (1 mark)

```c
int shmid = shmget(KEY, SIZE, 0666);             // get existing segment
char *shm_ptr = (char *) shmat(shmid, NULL, 0);   // attach
printf("Reader read: %s\n", shm_ptr);
```

**Step 6: Detach from address space** (1 mark)

```c
shmdt(shm_ptr);
```

**Step 7: Remove the segment (typically done by one designated process)** (1 mark)

```c
shmctl(shmid, IPC_RMID, NULL);
```
`IPC_RMID` marks the segment for removal. The segment is destroyed after the last process detaches from it.

**Working principle:** (1 mark)
- The two cooperating processes must agree on a common `key` (e.g., `KEY = 1234`).
- `shmget()` looks up or creates the segment. If `IPC_CREAT` is not set, it fails when the segment does not exist.
- `shmat` returns a pointer that both processes use to access the same physical memory.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table — POSIX vs System V (4 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **System V Shared Memory** | **POSIX Shared Memory** |
|---|---|---|
| **API Origin** | Older (1980s), AT&T System V UNIX | Newer (IEEE 1003.1b), POSIX standard |
| **Identifiers** | Integer `key_t` + `shmid` | File system path name (e.g., `/my_shm`) |
| **Creation** | `shmget()` with `IPC_CREAT` flag | `shm_open()` returns file descriptor |
| **Attachment** | `shmat()` returns pointer directly | `mmap()` of the file descriptor |
| **Removal** | `shmctl(shmid, IPC_RMID, NULL)` | `shm_unlink(name)` |
| **Size Setting** | At creation via `shmget()` | `ftruncate(fd, size)` after open |
| **Portability** | Widely supported | Preferred on modern Linux/macOS |
| **Persistence** | Survives process termination until `IPC_RMID` | Survives until `shm_unlink` |
| **Namespace** | IPC namespace (visible via `ipcs`) | Virtual filesystem (`/dev/shm`) |

**Advantages of Shared Memory IPC (2 marks):**
1. **Speed** — Once the region is mapped into both processes, all accesses are normal memory operations. No kernel intervention is needed for each data exchange. This is the **fastest IPC mechanism**.
2. **Convenient for large data** — Sharing a large structure (e.g., a buffer, a tree) is trivial — just place a pointer in shared memory.
3. **Natural for shared data structures** — Linked lists, hash tables, and matrices can be built cooperatively.

**Disadvantages of Shared Memory IPC (1 mark):**
1. **Synchronization burden** — Without careful synchronization (semaphores, mutexes), race conditions and data corruption can occur.
2. **No protection** — A buggy or malicious cooperating process can corrupt the shared region.
3. **Difficult to use across networks** — Only works between processes on the same machine with a shared address space; not suitable for distributed systems without additional layers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing `exit()` vs `_exit()`:** Using `exit()` in a child after `fork()` causes the parent's `stdio` buffers to be flushed **twice** (once by the child and once by the parent), producing duplicated output. Always use `_exit()` in the child after `fork()` unless you really want cleanup.
> 2. **Forgetting `wait()`:** If the parent doesn't call `wait()`, terminated children remain as **zombies** (`Z` state in `ps`). Over time, the process table fills and `fork()` fails with `EAGAIN`.
> 3. **Deadlock on semaphores:** Always acquire semaphores in the **same order** in both producer and consumer. The standard order is `empty/full` *then* `mutex`, never the reverse — otherwise deadlock can occur.
> 4. **Missing `IPC_RMID` or `shm_unlink`:** Shared memory segments are **not automatically cleaned up** when processes exit. Lingering segments in `/dev/shm` (POSIX) or `ipcs` (System V) consume RAM until manually removed.
> 5. **Counting `fork()` processes wrong:** Students often forget that `fork()` runs in BOTH parent and child. If `n` sequential `fork()` calls appear, the answer is **$2^n$**, not $n+1$.
> 6. **Drawing fork() as a single execution line:** Always draw `fork()` as a **bifurcation** with two arrows — one to the parent path (returns child PID) and one to the child path (returns 0).

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **process** = program in execution + context (registers, stack, heap, PC, PCB).
- ✅ `fork()` **duplicates** the calling process — returns child PID to parent, `0` to child, `-1` on error.
- ✅ `exec()` **replaces** the current process image with a new program — PID unchanged.
- ✅ `wait()` **blocks** the parent until a child terminates; reaps the zombie.
- ✅ `exit()` runs cleanup (flushes buffers, `atexit` handlers); `_exit()` is the raw kernel exit.
- ✅ **Zombie** = terminated but not yet reaped (PCB held by kernel).
- ✅ **Orphan** = parent has exited; reparented to `init` (PID 1).
- ✅ Shared memory IPC uses a **common region of physical memory** mapped into each process's address space.
- ✅ **Two flavors**: System V (`shmget/shmat/shmdt/shmctl`) and POSIX (`shm_open/mmap/munmap/shm_unlink`).
- ✅ Shared memory is the **fastest IPC** but requires **explicit synchronization** (typically semaphores).
- ✅ Producer–Consumer uses three semaphores: `mutex` (mutual exclusion, init = 1), `empty` (init = N), `full` (init = 0).
- ✅ Critical invariant: $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$ at all times.
- ✅ Always acquire semaphores in consistent order to avoid deadlock.
- ✅ `n` sequential `fork()` calls yield $2^n$ total processes.
- ✅ Default Linux `pid_max` is **32768**, configurable up to 4,194,304.
- ✅ Shared memory is **not auto-cleaned**; must call `IPC_RMID` (System V) or `shm_unlink` (POSIX).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Passing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Message Passing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Message Passing System** is an inter-process communication (IPC) mechanism in which cooperating processes exchange information by sending and receiving messages through the operating system kernel, without sharing any common address space. It is a synchronization and communication facility provided by the OS where two or more processes communicate by passing structured messages via primitives such as `send(message)` and `receive(message)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Message passing is an alternative to shared memory for inter-process communication. It is useful for processes running on different hosts connected by a network, and is typically implemented via system calls provided by the operating system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are in a corporate office where two employees, **Alice** and **Bob**, work in completely separate, soundproof rooms. They cannot see each other, cannot access each other's desks, and have no common whiteboard. However, they need to exchange information.

- **Shared Memory Analogy**: If there was a common glass window between their rooms, they could write and read notes on it (this is shared memory).
- **Message Passing Analogy**: Instead, they use the **office postal/courier system**. Alice writes a letter, addresses it to Bob, and hands it to the receptionist (the **OS Kernel**). The receptionist delivers it through a mail slot to Bob. Bob reads it and may reply through the same system.

In this analogy:
- The **letter** = the **message**
- The **receptionist** = the **operating system / kernel**
- The **mail slot / mailbox** = the **message queue / port**
- **Alice and Bob** = **cooperating processes**

This approach is **inherently safe** because processes never share memory directly, eliminating the risk of race conditions, deadlocks, and data corruption common in shared memory systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Message passing is the **only viable IPC method** when processes reside on different, geographically distributed systems connected via a network. Shared memory is fundamentally impossible in such distributed environments.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Characteristics

The following are standard KTU-recognized characteristics of a robust message passing system:

- **Direct vs. Indirect Communication**: How processes name each other.
- **Synchronous vs. Asynchronous**: The timing relationship between `send` and `receive`.
- **Automatic vs. Explicit Buffering**: How messages are queued before delivery.
- **Fixed-size vs. Variable-size Messages**: The flexibility of the message format.
- **Unicast, Broadcast, Multicast**: The number of intended recipients.
- **Endianness Standard**: **Big-Endian** or **Little-Endian** byte ordering for network compatibility.
- **Maximum Message Size**: Often bounded by the kernel's internal buffer (typically **4 KB** to **64 KB**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Message passing is implemented by the OS as a **system call**, which means it involves a **kernel mode switch** (context switch from user mode to kernel mode). This makes it inherently **slower** than shared memory but **safer and more portable** across distributed systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Components of a Message Passing System

A message passing system can be broken down into the following structured components:

1. **Communication Primitives (System Calls)**
   - `send(destination, message)` — used by the sender process to transmit a message.
   - `receive(source, message)` — used by the receiver process to accept a message.
   - These are **kernel-mediated system calls** that ensure data integrity.

2. **Naming/Addressing Scheme**
   - Determines *how* a sender identifies the receiver (Direct or Indirect).

3. **Synchronization Mechanism**
   - Determines *when* the message is delivered and acknowledged (Blocking/Non-Blocking).

4. **Buffering Strategy**
   - Determines *where* the message waits between send and receive (Zero/Finite/Infinite capacity).

5. **Message Format**
   - Typically a structured tuple: `<message_type, sender_id, payload, checksum>`.

### 2.2 Communication Primitives — The "Why" and "How"

#### 2.2.1 Direct Communication

In **direct communication**, every process must explicitly name its communication partner. This is the most rigid but most secure form of IPC.

**Operations:**
- `send(P, message)` — Send a message to process **P**.
- `receive(Q, message)` — Receive a message from process **Q**.

**Properties:**
- A communication link is established **automatically and uniquely** between every pair of processes that wish to communicate.
- The link is **bidirectional**, but a pair of processes can have **only one link** between them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Direct Communication?** It offers **strong authentication** — the receiver knows *exactly* who sent the message. However, it suffers from **limited scalability** because changing a process's identifier (PID) requires updating all other processes' code.

#### 2.2.2 Indirect Communication

In **indirect communication**, messages are sent to and received from **mailboxes** (also called **ports**). Processes communicate by posting to and retrieving from shared mailboxes.

**Operations:**
- `send(A, message)` — Send a message to mailbox **A**.
- `receive(A, message)` — Receive a message from mailbox **A**.

**Properties:**
- The link is established **only if processes share a common mailbox**.
- A link may be associated with **two or more processes**.
- Several different links may exist between each pair of processes, each corresponding to a different mailbox.
- **Who can receive from a mailbox?** This is a design choice:
  - Allow only **one process** to receive (the mailbox is private to that process).
  - Allow **multiple processes** to receive (the OS arbitrarily selects one — risk of **message loss** if no synchronization is enforced).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Port Concept (KTU 2024 Specific):** Modern operating systems like **Windows NT** use the term **port** instead of mailbox. A port is essentially a strongly-typed mailbox that can only accept messages of a specific structure, providing compile-time type safety.

#### 2.2.3 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Communication

This is the most critical design decision in any message passing implementation:

| Type | Sender Behavior | Receiver Behavior | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous (Blocking)** | `send` blocks until message is received | `receive` blocks until message arrives | Phone call (both must be online) |
| **Asynchronous (Non-Blocking)** | `send` returns immediately | `receive` returns null/fails if no message | Email (send and forget) |
| **Rendezvous (CSP Model)** | `send` blocks until `receive` executes | `receive` blocks until `send` executes | Both wait at a meeting point |

### 2.3 Buffering Strategies

A buffer is a temporary storage area in the kernel where messages wait between `send` and `receive`. There are three buffering strategies:

- **Zero Capacity (No Buffering):** The `send` operation cannot complete until the `receive` operation occurs. The link cannot hold any messages in transit. This is essentially **rendezvous synchronization**.
- **Bounded Capacity (Finite Buffering):** The link has a finite queue length **N**. The `send` blocks when the queue is full; the `receive` blocks when the queue is empty. This is the **most common implementation**.
- **Unbounded Capacity (Infinite Buffering):** The queue can grow without limit. The `send` never blocks; the `receive` only blocks if the queue is empty. This is theoretically ideal but **practically infeasible** due to memory constraints.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Message Latency | $L = t_{send} + t_{transit} + t_{receive}$ | Total time from `send` initiation to `receive` completion | Network performance analysis |
| Throughput | $T = \frac{N_{messages}}{\Delta t}$ | Number of messages processed per unit time | IPC system benchmarking |
| Bounded Buffer Constraint | $0 \leq Q_{size} \leq N$ | Queue size must be between zero and capacity **N** | Finite buffering design |
| Communication Links (Direct) | $L = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Number of unique links among **n** processes | Direct IPC scalability |
| Mailboxes (Indirect) | $M \geq n$ for full connectivity | At least one mailbox per process for full connectivity | Indirect IPC scalability |
| Blocking Probability | $P_{block} = 1 - P_{empty}$ | Probability that `send` blocks when buffer is full | Bounded buffer performance |
| Context Switch Overhead | $C = 2 \times t_{switch}$ | Two context switches per message (user→kernel→user) | Message passing cost |
| Effective Bandwidth | $B_{eff} = \frac{S_{msg}}{L + C}$ | Useful data rate accounting for latency and overhead | Distributed system design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The overhead of message passing is dominated by the **two context switches** and **at least two copies** of the message (user space → kernel buffer → receiver user space). This is why for **high-throughput, same-host IPC**, shared memory is often preferred. For **cross-host or low-trust communication**, message passing is the only safe choice.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Message passing is the foundation of modern distributed computing. Its applications include:
- **Distributed Operating Systems** (e.g., Amoeba, Sprite)
- **Microkernel Architectures** (e.g., QNX, MINIX, macOS XNU)
- **Network Protocols** (e.g., TCP/IP sockets, HTTP, gRPC)
- **Parallel Computing** (e.g., MPI — Message Passing Interface)
- **Operating System Microkernel IPC** (e.g., seL4, Mach)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Communication Links for Direct Communication

Let **n** be the number of cooperating processes. In a direct communication scheme, every pair of processes requires a dedicated bidirectional link.

**Step 1:** Define the combinatorial setup.

$$
\text{We have } n \text{ processes, and we need to count the number of unique unordered pairs.}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the combination formula.

$$
L = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the factorial expression.

$$
L = \frac{n \times (n-1) \times (n-2)!}{2 \times 1 \times (n-2)!}
$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the common term $(n-2)!$ from numerator and denominator.

$$
L = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.

$$
\boxed{L = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}}
$$

This shows that direct communication has **quadratic complexity** $O(n^2)$ with respect to the number of processes — a key scalability bottleneck.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bounded Buffer Behavior (Producer-Consumer with Message Passing)

Consider a bounded buffer of capacity **N** where a producer process sends messages and a consumer receives them. Let the arrival rate be $\lambda$ messages/second and service rate be $\mu$ messages/second.

**Step 1:** Define the utilization factor (traffic intensity).

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}
$$

For the system to be stable, we require $\rho < 1$.

**Step 2:** The probability that the buffer is empty (consumer waits).

$$
P_{empty} = (1 - \rho)
$$

**Step 3:** The probability that the buffer is full (producer blocks).

$$
P_{full} = \rho^{N}
$$

**Step 4:** Average number of messages in the buffer (steady-state).

$$
E[Q] = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} - \frac{(N+1)\rho^{N+1}}{1 - \rho^{N+1}}
$$

**Step 5:** Average message latency in the queue.

$$
W_q = \frac{E[Q]}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} - \frac{N}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\rho^{N}}{1 - \rho^{N}}
$$

**Step 6:** Total expected latency including service time.

$$
W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{N \rho^{N}}{1 - \rho^{N}}\right)
$$

This is the **M/M/1/N** queueing model applied to IPC buffer analysis.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Trade-offs

| Parameter | Synchronous (Blocking) | Asynchronous (Non-Blocking) |
|---|---|---|
| Sender Blocking | Yes — until ACK | No — returns immediately |
| Receiver Blocking | Yes — until message | Optional — may poll |
| Throughput | Lower (waiting) | Higher (pipelining) |
| Complexity | Simpler (sequential) | More complex (buffer mgmt) |
| Memory Usage | Low (no buffering) | High (buffering required) |
| Failure Detection | Immediate | Delayed (timeout needed) |
| Programming Model | CSP, Ada rendezvous | Unix pipes, sockets |
| Use Case | Critical control signals | High-volume data streams |
| Order Preservation | Guaranteed | Guaranteed (with FIFO queue) |
| KTU Examiner Focus | **High** — appears frequently | Medium — for design questions |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Message Lifecycle in Kernel

**Step 1: Sender Process invokes `send()` system call.**

The process transitions from **user mode** to **kernel mode** (first context switch).

**Step 2: Kernel validates parameters.**

The kernel checks: Does the destination process/mailbox exist? Is the message size within limits? Does the sender have permission?

**Step 3: Kernel copies the message from sender's user space to a kernel buffer.**

This is a **secure copy** — the sender cannot modify the message after this point.

**Step 4: Kernel locates the receiver.**

For direct communication, the kernel looks up the PID. For indirect, it locates the mailbox descriptor.

**Step 5: Kernel enqueues the message.**

The message is placed in the receiver's message queue (or mailbox) according to the buffering policy.

**Step 6: Kernel may wake up the receiver.**

If the receiver is blocked in a `receive()` call, the kernel may copy the message directly to the receiver's user buffer and wake it up (an **optimization** known as **direct handoff**).

**Step 7: Sender returns from system call.**

The sender transitions back from kernel mode to user mode (second context switch).

**Step 8: Receiver process invokes `receive()`.**

The receiver transitions to kernel mode (third context switch), copies the message to its user buffer, and returns (fourth context switch).

$$
\text{Minimum context switches per message} = 4
$$

$$
\text{Cost} = 4 \times t_{ctx} + 2 \times t_{copy}
$$

where $t_{ctx}$ is the context switch time and $t_{copy}$ is the memory copy time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Direct Communication Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process P1] -->|"send(P2, msg)"| K1[Kernel Mail Slot]
    K1 -->|"deliver"| B[Process P2]
    B -->|"send(P1, reply)"| K1
    K1 -->|"deliver"| A
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style K1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
```

### 4.2 Indirect Communication via Mailbox

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process P1] -->|"send(M, msg)"| M[Mailbox M]
    P2[Process P2] -->|"send(M, msg)"| M
    P3[Process P3] -->|"receive(M, msg)"| M
    M -.->|"FIFO delivery"| P3
    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style P2 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style P3 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style M fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
```

### 4.3 Message Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Process calls send] --> B[User to Kernel Mode Switch]
    B --> C[Kernel Validates Message]
    C --> D[Copy to Kernel Buffer]
    D --> E{Receiver Waiting?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Direct Handoff to Receiver Buffer]
    E -- No --> G[Enqueue in Mailbox]
    G --> H[Receiver Calls receive]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Kernel to User Mode Switch for Receiver]
    I --> J[Message Delivered to User Space]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style E fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc
    style F fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style G fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style H fill:#ffe1f0,stroke:#cc0066
    style I fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style J fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

### 4.4 Synchronization Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Synchronous
        S1[Sender Blocks] --> S2[Wait for Receive]
        S2 --> S3[Handshake Complete]
        S3 --> S4[Both Continue]
    end
    subgraph Asynchronous
        AS1[Sender Returns] --> AS2[Message in Buffer]
        AS2 --> AS3[Receiver Polls or Waits]
        AS3 --> AS4[Independent Execution]
    end
    style S1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style S2 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style S3 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style S4 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style AS1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style AS2 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style AS3 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style AS4 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define message passing. List any two advantages of message passing over shared memory communication.**

**Model Answer:**

**Message Passing** is a method of inter-process communication in which cooperating processes exchange information by sending and receiving messages through the operating system kernel, without sharing any common address space.

**Two Advantages:**

1. **Suitable for Distributed Systems:** Message passing is the only viable IPC mechanism for processes running on different hosts connected by a network, since shared memory requires a common address space which does not exist across machines.

2. **Simpler Synchronization:** The kernel inherently handles synchronization, eliminating the need for explicit semaphore or mutex primitives. The `send` and `receive` operations are atomic from the user's perspective.

*(Additional valid advantages: no race conditions, no need for mutual exclusion, easier to implement in microkernels, cleaner API.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023] Differentiate between direct and indirect communication in message passing systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Direct Communication | Indirect Communication |
|---|---|---|
| Naming | Processes explicitly name each other (e.g., `send(P, msg)`) | Processes communicate via shared mailboxes (e.g., `send(M, msg)`) |
| Link Establishment | Automatic, unique per pair | Manual, requires shared mailbox creation |
| Number of Processes per Link | Exactly two (one-to-one) | Two or more (one-to-many or many-to-one) |
| Scalability | Poor — $O(n^2)$ links | Better — fewer mailboxes needed |
| Decoupling | Tightly coupled | Loosely coupled |
| Example | Mach microkernel IPC | Unix pipes, Windows mailslots |

### Part B Questions (14 Marks)

**Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the different buffering strategies used in message passing systems with suitable diagrams.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare synchronous and asynchronous message passing with examples.**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Buffering in message passing** refers to the temporary storage of messages in the kernel between the `send` and `receive` operations. The choice of buffering strategy affects system throughput, memory usage, and synchronization semantics. There are three strategies:

**1. Zero Capacity (No Buffering) / Rendezvous:**

- The `send` operation blocks until the corresponding `receive` operation is executed by the receiver.
- The communication link cannot hold any messages in transit.
- The two processes must "meet" at a synchronization point.
- **Advantage:** Minimal memory usage, immediate delivery.
- **Disadvantage:** Poor performance due to tight synchronization; sender and receiver must be ready simultaneously.
- **Use case:** Control signals, critical handshakes.

$$
\text{Buffer size} = 0
$$

**2. Bounded Capacity (Finite Buffering):**

- The link has a finite queue of length **N**.
- `send` blocks when the queue is **full**.
- `receive` blocks when the queue is **empty**.
- This is the **most common implementation** in real systems (e.g., Unix pipes with a 64 KB buffer).

$$
0 < \text{Buffer size} \leq N
$$

**3. Unbounded Capacity (Infinite Buffering):**

- The queue can grow without limit.
- `send` never blocks (always succeeds).
- `receive` blocks only if the queue is empty.
- **Advantage:** Maximum sender throughput.
- **Disadvantage:** Unpredictable memory consumption; practically infeasible.

**Diagram (textual):**

```
Sender  --send--> [ Buffer (capacity N) ] --receive--> Receiver
                    | M1 | M2 | M3 | ... | MN |
```

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Synchronous Message Passing:**

In synchronous (blocking) message passing, both `send` and `receive` operations block until the message exchange is complete. The sender waits until the receiver has accepted the message, and the receiver waits until a message is available.

**Example (Ada Rendezvous):**

```
task Sender;
task Receiver;

task body Sender is
begin
   Receiver.Greet;  -- blocks until Receiver accepts
end Sender;

task body Receiver is
begin
   accept Greet;  -- blocks until Sender calls
   Put_Line("Hello!");
end Receiver;
```

**Asynchronous Message Passing:**

In asynchronous (non-blocking) message passing, the `send` operation returns immediately after copying the message to the kernel buffer, without waiting for the receiver. The `receive` may block or be non-blocking.

**Example (Unix Sockets):**

```
sendto(socket_fd, buffer, len, MSG_DONTWAIT, ...);  // non-blocking send
```

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Blocking | Both sender and receiver | Sender does not block |
| Throughput | Lower | Higher |
| Memory | Low | High (buffering) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (buffer management) |
| Failure Detection | Immediate | Requires timeouts |
| Use Case | Control signals | High-volume data |

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Jun 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the message passing system with neat diagram showing direct communication. List its limitations.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain indirect communication using mailboxes. What are the issues with mailbox ownership?**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Direct Communication System:**

In direct communication, each process that wishes to communicate must explicitly name the recipient (for `send`) or the sender (for `receive`). The communication primitives are:

- `send(P, message)` — Send `message` to process **P**.
- `receive(Q, message)` — Receive a message from process **Q**.

**Properties:**

1. A communication link is **automatically established** between every pair of processes that wish to communicate.
2. The link is **bidirectional** — both processes can send and receive.
3. The link is **unique** — only one link exists between any pair of processes.
4. The processes need to **know each other's identity** (PID).

**Diagram:**

```
Process P1                    Process P2
    |                             ^
    |--- send(P2, "Hello") ------>|
    |                             |
    |<-- send(P1, "Hi back") -----|
    v                             |
Process P1                    Process P2
```

**Limitations of Direct Communication:**

1. **Limited Modularity:** Changing a process's identifier (PID) requires modifying all other processes that communicate with it.
2. **Poor Scalability:** With **n** processes, the number of communication links is $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$, leading to $O(n^2)$ complexity.
3. **Tight Coupling:** Processes are tightly coupled; they must know each other explicitly.
4. **No Decoupling:** A process cannot communicate with a group of processes simultaneously without addressing each one individually.
5. **Security Risk:** Exposing PIDs to other processes creates a potential security vulnerability.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Indirect Communication using Mailboxes:**

In indirect communication, messages are sent to and received from **mailboxes** (also called **ports**). A mailbox can be viewed as an object into which messages can be placed and from which messages can be removed. Each mailbox has a unique identification.

**Primitives:**

- `send(A, message)` — Send `message` to mailbox **A**.
- `receive(A, message)` — Receive a message from mailbox **A**.

**Properties:**

1. A communication link is established **only if processes share a common mailbox**.
2. A link may be associated with **two or more processes**.
3. Multiple links may exist between the same pair of processes, each using a different mailbox.
4. Mailboxes provide **decoupling** — sender and receiver need not know each other.

**Diagram:**

```
Process P1  ---send(M, msg)---> [ Mailbox M ] ---receive(M, msg)---> Process P2
Process P3  ---send(M, msg)---> [          ] ---receive(M, msg)---> Process P2
```

**Issues with Mailbox Ownership:**

1. **Single Owner Problem:** If a mailbox is owned by a single process, when that process terminates or crashes, the mailbox and all its messages are destroyed. This can cause **message loss**.

2. **Multiple Receivers Problem:** If multiple processes are allowed to receive from the same mailbox, the OS must decide **which process gets which message**. This non-determinism can lead to:
   - **Race conditions** between receivers.
   - **Message loss** if no receiver is ready when a message arrives.
   - **Starvation** of certain receivers.

3. **Mailbox Creation/Deletion:** Who has the right to create and destroy a mailbox? The creator? The OS? This raises **resource management** and **security** concerns.

4. **Port Concept in Windows NT:** Windows addresses these issues by associating a **port** with a process but allowing messages to be forwarded to a **replacement port** if the owner dies, providing fault tolerance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake students make is to **confuse mailboxes with message queues**. A mailbox is a **named communication endpoint** (an abstract object), while a message queue is a **data structure** used to implement the mailbox internally. Also, do NOT confuse **port** (Windows) with **socket** (Unix) — both are mailbox implementations but have different APIs and semantics.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Message passing is IPC via kernel-mediated `send` and `receive` system calls without shared address space.
- **Two Types:** Direct (process-to-process) and Indirect (process-to-mailbox).
- **Synchronization:** Synchronous (blocking/rendezvous) vs. Asynchronous (non-blocking/buffered).
- **Buffering:** Zero (rendezvous), Bounded (most common), Unbounded (theoretical).
- **Cost:** Minimum **4 context switches** per message exchange.
- **Scalability:** Direct has $O(n^2)$ link complexity; Indirect uses mailboxes for decoupling.
- **Use Cases:** Distributed systems, microkernels (Mach, QNX), network protocols, MPI.
- **Advantages:** No race conditions, works across networks, inherent synchronization, portable.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher overhead than shared memory, context switch cost, buffer management complexity.
- **Mailbox Issues:** Ownership, multiple receivers, message loss on process termination.
- **KTU Focus:** Know the primitive signatures, buffering trade-offs, and be able to draw the communication link diagrams.
- **Exam Tip:** Always mention the **two context switches** (user→kernel→user) per `send`/`receive` when discussing performance.
- **Remember:** Message passing is implemented by the **kernel**, making it a **system call** — this is why it is slower but safer than shared memory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Scheduling - Basic Concepts, Scheduling Criteria, Scheduling Algorithms - First Come First Served
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Scheduling - Basic Concepts, Scheduling Criteria, Scheduling Algorithms - First Come First Served

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Process Scheduling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Scheduling** is the activity of the **Operating System (OS)** that decides which of the several ready-to-run processes in the **Ready Queue** is to be allocated the **CPU** at a given point in time, and for how long. It is the central mechanism by which the OS achieves **multiprogramming** and **resource maximization**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the OS component that performs this decision-making is called the **Scheduler** (also referred to as the *CPU Scheduler* or *Short-Term Scheduler*). The actual hardware-level act of physically loading the chosen process's context into the CPU registers is handled by the **Dispatcher**, which then performs a **Context Switch**.

### 1.2 Types of Schedulers (Mandatory KTU Concept)

The KTU syllabus categorizes schedulers into three distinct types, distinguished by the *frequency* of their execution:

| Scheduler Type | Also Called | Decision Frequency | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Long-Term Scheduler (LTS)** | Job Scheduler / Admission Scheduler | Very low (seconds to minutes) | Controls the **degree of multiprogramming** by selecting jobs from disk to memory |
| **Short-Term Scheduler (STS)** | CPU Scheduler | Very high (milliseconds — every CPU burst) | Selects one process from the Ready Queue for CPU execution |
| **Medium-Term Scheduler (MTS)** | Swapper | Intermediate | Temporarily removes (**swaps out**) processes from memory to reduce contention; later **swaps them back in** |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern KTU exam questions, when someone says "scheduler" without a qualifier, they almost always mean the **Short-Term Scheduler (CPU Scheduler)**.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Token Counter"

Imagine you walk into a busy government bank on a Monday morning:

1. There is **one** service counter (the **CPU**).
2. There are **many** customers waiting (the **Ready Queue of processes**).
3. Only **one** customer can be served at a time.
4. A manager (the **Scheduler**) stands at the gate and decides: *"Who goes next to the counter?"*

- If the manager follows **FCFS (First Come First Served)**, the customer with the earliest token number enters the counter first, regardless of how long their work will take.
- The **Dispatcher** is the assistant who actually walks the customer to the counter, hands them the form, and sets up their chair (context loading).

> [!TIP]
> A **Context Switch** is the cost you pay when the manager shouts *"Next please!"* — the previous customer leaves the chair, and the new one sits down. The bank loses a few seconds every time this happens. This is the **Context Switch Overhead**.

## 1.4 FCFS — The Simplest Scheduling Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First Come First Served (FCFS)** is a **non-preemptive** CPU scheduling algorithm in which the process that requests the CPU **first** (i.e., arrives in the ready queue first) is allocated the CPU. It runs to **completion** of its CPU burst before any other waiting process is allowed in.

- It is implemented using a simple **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue**.
- It is the **default** policy of most batch operating systems and is the basis of every other algorithm.
- It is **non-preemptive** → once a process starts, it cannot be forcibly removed from the CPU.

## 1.5 Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive Scheduling

This distinction is a **high-weight KTU favorite question** and must be memorized:

| Property | Non-Preemptive | Preemptive |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Once allocated CPU, process keeps it until burst completion or I/O | CPU can be forcibly taken away from a running process |
| **Decision Points** | Process termination or voluntary I/O wait | Clock interrupts, I/O completion, new arrivals, etc. |
| **Algorithm Examples** | FCFS, Non-Preemptive SJF, Non-Preemptive Priority | Round Robin, Preemptive SJF (SRTF), Preemptive Priority |
| **System Responsiveness** | Poor — long jobs block short ones | Better — supports time-sharing |
| **Context Switch Overhead** | **Low** | **High** |
| **Convoy Effect** | **Yes — severe** | Largely avoided |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CPU–I/O Burst Cycle (Foundational Concept)

Every process alternates between two states during its lifetime:

- **CPU Burst** — The interval during which the process actively executes instructions on the CPU (computationally intensive work).
- **I/O Burst** — The interval during which the process waits for an I/O device (keyboard, disk, network) and the CPU is idle for that process.

> [!NOTE]
> A typical process begins with a **CPU burst**, followed by an **I/O burst**, then alternates. The **distribution** of CPU burst lengths is a key factor — most bursts are short, but a few are very long (a *hyperexponential* distribution).

This burst alternation is the reason scheduling exists at all — the OS must decide *which ready process* gets the CPU during the next CPU burst window.

## 2.2 Scheduling Criteria — The Five Performance Metrics (Mandatory KTU)

The KTU syllabus and Silberschatz's *Operating System Concepts* (the canonical KTU reference) mandate the study of **five scheduling criteria**. These are used to **compare and evaluate** any scheduling algorithm.

### 2.2.1 CPU Utilization
- **Definition:** The fraction of time the CPU is doing useful work (not idle).
- **Goal:** Keep it as **high** as possible (theoretical ideal: **40% to 90%** in real systems, with **100%** being the perfect theoretical ceiling).
- **Unit:** Percentage (%).
- **Engineering utility:** Maximizing CPU utilization directly reduces the cost-per-transaction in data centers and cloud servers.

### 2.2.2 Throughput
- **Definition:** The number of processes completed per unit of time.
- **Goal:** **Maximize** it.
- **Formula:** $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Number of processes completed}}{\text{Total time elapsed}}$
- **Unit:** Processes/second.

### 2.2.3 Turnaround Time (TAT)
- **Definition:** The total time from process **arrival in the ready queue** to its **completion**.
- **Goal:** **Minimize** it.
- **Formula:** $TAT = \text{Completion Time (CT)} - \text{Arrival Time (AT)}$
- **Unit:** Time units (ms, seconds).

### 2.2.4 Waiting Time (WT)
- **Definition:** The total time a process spends in the **ready queue** waiting for the CPU.
- **Goal:** **Minimize** it.
- **Formula:** $WT = TAT - \text{Burst Time (BT)}$
- **Alternative:** $WT = \text{Start Time (ST)} - \text{Arrival Time (AT)}$
- **Unit:** Time units.

### 2.2.5 Response Time (RT)
- **Definition:** The time from process **arrival** until it gets the CPU **for the first time**.
- **Goal:** **Minimize** it.
- **Formula:** $RT = \text{First CPU Allocation Time} - \text{Arrival Time}$
- **Relevance:** Critical in **time-sharing / interactive** systems (e.g., FCFS has poor response time, RR has good response time).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commit this table to memory before any numerical KTU question.** Examiners award partial marks only if intermediate steps use these formulas correctly.

| Metric | Formula | Goal | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Average Turnaround Time | $AvgTAT = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(CT_i - AT_i)}{n}$ | Minimize | Performance |
| Average Waiting Time | $AvgWT = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(TAT_i - BT_i)}{n}$ | Minimize | Performance |
| Average Response Time | $AvgRT = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(FirstAlloc_i - AT_i)}{n}$ | Minimize | User-perceived |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{n}{Last\ CT - First\ AT}$ | Maximize | Performance |
| CPU Utilization | $\dfrac{\text{Busy CPU time}}{\text{Total time}} \times 100\%$ | Maximize (%) | Resource |
| Normalized TAT | $\dfrac{TAT}{\text{Service Time (BT)}}$ | Minimize ($\geq 1$) | Fairness |

> [!TIP]
> **Normalized TAT (NTAT)** $\geq 1$ always. A value of **1.0** means the process had **zero wait time** (perfect). FCFS typically produces **higher** normalized TAT for short processes because they wait behind long ones.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Centers (AWS, Azure, GCP):** FCFS is unsuitable because it causes "head-of-line blocking." Modern cloud schedulers (Kubernetes, YARN) use variants of fair-share or preemptive algorithms.
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems:** A predictable non-preemptive FCFS variant (sometimes called *Co-operative scheduling*) is used in **Arduino, automotive ECUs** because it has **zero preemption overhead** and is easy to verify for safety certification.
- **Batch Processing:** FCFS is still used in **HPC and supercomputer batch queues** where fairness and simplicity matter more than turnaround.

## 2.5 The Convoy Effect — FCFS's Fatal Flaw

> [!WARNING]
> The **Convoy Effect** is the phenomenon in FCFS where a single long process arriving first causes all short processes behind it to **wait disproportionately**, even though they need only a fraction of the CPU time.

**Real-world example:** A single truck (long burst) on a narrow mountain road blocks 20 motorcycles (short bursts) behind it. This is precisely why FCFS is rarely used in modern interactive systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Standard FCFS Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
Consider the following four processes with their Arrival Times (AT) and Burst Times (BT). Calculate **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using **FCFS**.

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 5 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 8 |
| P4 | 3 | 6 |

### Step 1 — Build the Gantt Chart

Under FCFS, processes are executed in the order of arrival. The CPU executes each process to completion (non-preemptive).

```
| P1  | P2  |  P3   |  P4  |
0     5     8      16      22
```

**Detailed explanation of the chart construction:**

- **Time 0 → 5:** P1 arrives first (AT=0) and is the only one in the queue. P1 runs for 5 units, completing at time **5**.
- **Time 5 → 8:** P2 arrived at time 1, has been waiting. P2 starts at time 5, runs for 3 units, completing at time **8**.
- **Time 8 → 16:** P3 arrived at time 2. P3 starts at time 8, runs for 8 units, completing at time **16**.
- **Time 16 → 22:** P4 arrived at time 3. P4 starts at time 16, runs for 6 units, completing at time **22**.

### Step 2 — Extract Completion Times (CT)

From the Gantt chart:

$$CT_{P1} = 5, \quad CT_{P2} = 8, \quad CT_{P3} = 16, \quad CT_{P4} = 22$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Turnaround Time (TAT) for each process

Using the formula $TAT = CT - AT$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= CT_{P1} - AT_{P1} = 5 - 0 = 5 \\
TAT_{P2} &= CT_{P2} - AT_{P2} = 8 - 1 = 7 \\
TAT_{P3} &= CT_{P3} - AT_{P3} = 16 - 2 = 14 \\
TAT_{P4} &= CT_{P4} - AT_{P4} = 22 - 3 = 19
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Waiting Time (WT) for each process

Using the formula $WT = TAT - BT$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= TAT_{P1} - BT_{P1} = 5 - 5 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= TAT_{P2} - BT_{P2} = 7 - 3 = 4 \\
WT_{P3} &= TAT_{P3} - BT_{P3} = 14 - 8 = 6 \\
WT_{P4} &= TAT_{P4} - BT_{P4} = 19 - 6 = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate the Averages

$$
\begin{aligned}
AvgTAT &= \frac{TAT_{P1} + TAT_{P2} + TAT_{P3} + TAT_{P4}}{4} = \frac{5 + 7 + 14 + 19}{4} = \frac{45}{4} = 11.25 \text{ units} \\
AvgWT &= \frac{WT_{P1} + WT_{P2} + WT_{P3} + WT_{P4}}{4} = \frac{0 + 4 + 6 + 13}{4} = \frac{23}{4} = 5.75 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Throughput Calculation

The total elapsed time = 22 units (from first arrival at time 0 to last completion at time 22). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} = \frac{4 \text{ processes}}{22 \text{ time units}} = 0.1818 \text{ processes/unit time}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Demonstrating the Convoy Effect

**Problem:** Demonstrate the convoy effect using the following processes.

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 100 |
| P2 | 1 | 1 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 |
| P5 | 4 | 1 |

### Step 1 — Build the Gantt Chart

Under FCFS, P1 gets the CPU first and runs for **100 units** despite needing only **1 unit** of work each.

```
|  P1 (long)   |P2|P3|P4|P5|
0             100 101 102 103 104
```

### Step 2 — Completion Times and TAT/WT

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} = 100, \quad TAT_{P1} = 100 - 0 = 100, \quad WT_{P1} = 100 - 100 = 0 \\
CT_{P2} = 101, \quad TAT_{P2} = 101 - 1 = 100, \quad WT_{P2} = 100 - 1 = 99 \\
CT_{P3} = 102, \quad TAT_{P3} = 102 - 2 = 100, \quad WT_{P3} = 100 - 1 = 99 \\
CT_{P4} = 103, \quad TAT_{P4} = 103 - 3 = 100, \quad WT_{P4} = 100 - 1 = 99 \\
CT_{P5} = 104, \quad TAT_{P5} = 104 - 4 = 100, \quad WT_{P5} = 100 - 1 = 99
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Observe the Catastrophe

$$
\begin{aligned}
AvgTAT = \frac{100+100+100+100+100}{5} = 100 \text{ units} \\
AvgWT = \frac{0+99+99+99+99}{5} = \frac{396}{5} = 79.2 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Four short processes (each needing only 1 unit) were forced to wait **99 units** each. The **Average Waiting Time of 79.2 units** is one of the worst possible for any scheduling algorithm — this is the *Convoy Effect* in action, and is the primary reason FCFS is unsuitable for time-sharing systems.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Example 1 (Balanced) | Example 2 (Convoy) |
|---|---|---|
| Average TAT | **11.25** units | **100** units |
| Average WT | **5.75** units | **79.2** units |
| Convoy Effect | Mild | **Severe** |
| Suited for | Batch systems | Unsuitable for time-sharing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CPU Scheduling Dispatcher Cycle

The following block diagram illustrates the closed-loop interaction between the **Scheduler**, **Dispatcher**, **CPU**, and **Ready Queue** — the canonical architecture for *any* CPU scheduling algorithm (FCFS included).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Memory[MAIN MEMORY]
        RQ[Ready Queue FIFO]
        P1[Process P1 PCB]
        P2[Process P2 PCB]
        P3[Process P3 PCB]
        IO[Waiting Queue I O]
    end

    CPU[CPU Registers]
    Sched[Short Term Scheduler]
    Disp[Dispatcher]
    CS[Context Switch]

    Sched -. selects next .-> RQ
    Sched == hands control ==> Disp
    Disp == loads PCB into ==> CPU
    CPU == runs for burst ==> CS
    CS == saves old PCB ==> P1
    CS == loads new PCB ==> P2
    P1 -. arrived .-> RQ
    P2 -. arrived .-> RQ
    P3 -. arrived .-> RQ
    CPU -. I O request .-> IO
    IO -. completion interrupt .-> RQ
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The **Ready Queue** holds PCBs (Process Control Blocks) of all ready-to-run processes, maintained in **arrival order** for FCFS.
- The **Short-Term Scheduler** peeks at the head of the queue and selects one process.
- The **Dispatcher** performs the actual hardware-level **Context Switch**: it saves the old process state into its PCB, loads the new PCB into the CPU registers, and jumps to the program counter.
- The **CPU** then executes the chosen process for its entire CPU burst (FCFS = no preemption).
- If the process needs I/O, it is moved to the **Waiting Queue** and returns to the Ready Queue upon I/O completion.

## 4.2 Process State Transition Diagram (KTU Mandatory)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready : Long Term Scheduler admits
    Ready --> Running : Short Term Scheduler dispatches
    Running --> Waiting : I O or event wait
    Waiting --> Ready : I O completion interrupt
    Running --> Ready : Preemption occurs only for preemptive
    Running --> Terminated : Process exits
    Terminated --> [*]
```

**Note on FCFS:** Because FCFS is **non-preemptive**, the transition `Running → Ready` (preemption) **never** occurs. Every process that enters the `Running` state exits only through either the `Waiting` state (voluntary I/O) or the `Terminated` state (completion).

## 4.3 FCFS Gantt Chart Visual for Example 1

```mermaid
gantt
    title FCFS Gantt Chart Example 1
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section CPU
    P1 (BT 5)       :a1, 0, 5
    P2 (BT 3)       :a2, 5, 3
    P3 (BT 8)       :a3, 8, 8
    P4 (BT 6)       :a4, 16, 6
```

The horizontal bars represent each process's execution window on the CPU's timeline. The **idle gap is zero** in this example because a process was always available, which gives **100% CPU utilization** in the interval [0, 22].

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q: Define the term "CPU Scheduler". Differentiate between a Long-Term Scheduler and a Short-Term Scheduler.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **CPU Scheduler** (also called a *Short-Term Scheduler*) is a component of the operating system that selects one of the processes residing in the **Ready Queue** for execution on the CPU. It makes this decision very frequently — typically every few milliseconds, at the end of every CPU burst.

**Difference Table (Mandatory in KTU Answers):**

| Basis | Long-Term Scheduler (LTS) | Short-Term Scheduler (STS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Alternative Name** | Job / Admission Scheduler | CPU Scheduler |
| **Selection From** | Job Pool (Disk) | Ready Queue (Main Memory) |
| **Execution Frequency** | Very low (seconds to minutes) | Very high (milliseconds) |
| **Controls** | Degree of Multiprogramming | CPU allocation |
| **Present in** | Mainly Batch systems | All modern OS (incl. time-sharing) |
| **Can be Absent?** | Yes (in pure time-sharing) | Never — must exist |

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Q: What is the "Convoy Effect" in the context of FCFS scheduling? Illustrate with a small example.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Convoy Effect** is a phenomenon specific to **FCFS** (and other non-preemptive algorithms) where the presence of one or more **long CPU-bound processes** at the head of the ready queue causes all the shorter, I/O-bound or quick processes behind them to **wait excessively long**, even though they need very little CPU time. This degrades the **average waiting time** and **throughput** drastically.

**Illustration:**

If `P1 (BT=20)` arrives at t=0, and `P2 (BT=1), P3 (BT=1)` arrive at t=1 and t=2, then under FCFS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} = 20, \quad CT_{P2} = 21, \quad CT_{P3} = 22 \\
WT_{P2} = 21 - 1 - 1 = 19 \text{ units} \\
WT_{P3} = 22 - 2 - 1 = 19 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Two tiny 1-unit jobs were forced to wait **19 time units** each — this is the convoy effect. In a real system, this would freeze the UI or cause severe user-visible lag.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Q(a)** [7 Marks] Consider the following set of processes with their Arrival Time and Burst Time. Draw the **Gantt Chart** and compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using **FCFS** scheduling.

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gantt Chart Construction:**

Under FCFS, the process which arrives first is executed first. Once started, the process runs to completion (non-preemptive).

```
| P1  | P2  | P3 |  P4  |
0     7    11   13    18
```

- P1: starts at 0, completes at 7 (BT = 7)
- P2: started waiting at t=1, gets CPU at 7, runs for 4, completes at 11
- P3: started waiting at t=2, gets CPU at 11, runs for 2, completes at 13
- P4: started waiting at t=3, gets CPU at 13, runs for 5, completes at 18

**Step 2 — Completion Times, TAT, and WT Table:**

| Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT = CT − AT | WT = TAT − BT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 | 7 | 7 | 0 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 | 11 | 10 | 6 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 | 13 | 11 | 9 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 | 18 | 15 | 10 |

**Step 3 — Averages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AvgTAT &= \frac{7+10+11+15}{4} = \frac{43}{4} = 10.75 \text{ units} \\
AvgWT &= \frac{0+6+9+10}{4} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Q(b)** [7 Marks] List and briefly explain the **five CPU scheduling criteria** used to compare scheduling algorithms. State the goal of each (maximize or minimize).

**Model Answer:**

1. **CPU Utilization** — Fraction of time CPU is busy. **Goal: Maximize** (ideal 40%–90%).
2. **Throughput** — Number of processes completed per unit time. **Goal: Maximize**.
3. **Turnaround Time (TAT)** — Total time from arrival to completion. **Goal: Minimize**.
4. **Waiting Time (WT)** — Total time spent waiting in ready queue. **Goal: Minimize**.
5. **Response Time (RT)** — Time from arrival to first CPU allocation. **Goal: Minimize** (critical for interactive systems).

> FCFS is **optimal** in terms of *Average Waiting Time only when all processes arrive at the same time (AT=0)*, and even then only when sorted by burst time (which then becomes SJF). In the general case, FCFS is **suboptimal**.

---

### **Question B — Option 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**Q(a)** [7 Marks] For the following processes, calculate the **Average Turnaround Time** and **Average Waiting Time** under FCFS. Also compute the **Throughput**.

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 4 |
| P2 | 2 | 6 |
| P3 | 4 | 2 |
| P4 | 6 | 3 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gantt Chart:**

```
| P1  |   P2    | P3 |  P4  |
0     4        10   12    15
```

**Step 2 — Detailed Calculation Table:**

| Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT = CT − AT | WT = TAT − BT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| P2 | 2 | 6 | 10 | 8 | 2 |
| P3 | 4 | 2 | 12 | 8 | 6 |
| P4 | 6 | 3 | 15 | 9 | 6 |

**Step 3 — Averages and Throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AvgTAT &= \frac{4+8+8+9}{4} = \frac{29}{4} = 7.25 \text{ units} \\
AvgWT &= \frac{0+2+6+6}{4} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.5 \text{ units} \\
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{4 \text{ processes}}{15 \text{ time units}} = 0.2667 \text{ processes/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Q(b)** [7 Marks] What is a **Dispatcher**? Explain the **role of the Dispatcher** and the concept of **Dispatch Latency**. Why is dispatch latency a critical concern in real-time systems?

**Model Answer:**

> A **Dispatcher** is the OS module that gains control of the CPU from the **Scheduler** and performs the actual hand-off to the selected process. Its three main functions are:
>
> 1. **Context Switching** — Saving the state (registers, PC, etc.) of the old process into its PCB, and loading the saved state of the new process into the CPU.
> 2. **Switching to user mode** — Setting the CPU's privilege level to user mode for the new process.
> 3. **Jumping to the correct location** — Setting the Program Counter to the saved PC of the new process, so it resumes execution where it left off.

> **Dispatch Latency** is the **time taken by the Dispatcher** to stop one process and start another running. It is essentially the **duration of the context switch overhead**.
>
> In **real-time systems**, dispatch latency is a critical concern because:
> - A process may have a **hard deadline** (e.g., anti-lock braking must respond within 5 ms).
> - If dispatch latency is unpredictable or too long, the deadline is **missed**, which can cause system failure (e.g., airbag fails to deploy).
> - Therefore, real-time OSes (RTOS) like **VxWorks, FreeRTOS, QNX** are designed to have **bounded, minimal dispatch latency** — often in the order of **microseconds**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students lose marks in FCFS questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the Arrival Time in TAT.** Many students compute $TAT = CT - BT$ instead of $TAT = CT - AT$. This costs **3 marks minimum** in any 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Ignoring the Arrival Time in the Gantt Chart construction.** Students often sort by process ID (P1, P2, P3...) but **FCFS orders by Arrival Time**, not by name. If P3 arrives before P2, P3 runs first — period.
> 3. **Not converting CT − AT properly for the Waiting Time formula.** Writing $WT = CT - BT$ instead of $WT = TAT - BT$ gives wrong values when AT ≠ 0. Always go through the intermediate $TAT$ step.
> 4. **Ignoring units in the final answer.** Writing "Average WT = 3.5" without "units" or "ms" is considered incomplete in strict KTU valuation.
> 5. **Confusing FCFS (non-preemptive) with Round Robin (preemptive).** In RR, you must also account for the **time quantum** and preemption points — these do not exist in FCFS.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this checklist in your mind 10 minutes before the exam:**

- [ ] **FCFS = FIFO queue = non-preemptive** — once started, the process runs to completion of its CPU burst.
- [ ] **Gantt Chart order is determined by Arrival Time (AT), not by process name.**
- [ ] **Three key formulas** — memorize verbatim:
  - $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$
  - $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$
  - $Avg = \dfrac{\sum \text{values}}{n}$
- [ ] **Five scheduling criteria** — CPU Utilization, Throughput, TAT, WT, RT. First two are **maximized**; the last three are **minimized**.
- [ ] **Three schedulers** — Long-Term (job admission), Short-Term (CPU selection), Medium-Term (swapping).
- [ ] **FCFS advantage** — extremely simple, fair, no starvation, low overhead, predictable.
- [ ] **FCFS disadvantage** — Convoy Effect, poor response time, poor average TAT/WT for mixed workloads.
- [ ] **FCFS is non-preemptive** — no `Running → Ready` transition in the state diagram for FCFS processes.
- [ ] **Dispatcher ≠ Scheduler** — Scheduler *decides*, Dispatcher *acts* (performs context switch).
- [ ] **Dispatch Latency** = time of context switch — must be minimized in real-time systems.
- [ ] **Throughput** = number of processes / total elapsed time.
- [ ] **Average TAT and Average WT** are the two quantities the KTU examiner *always* asks in numerical questions.
- [ ] **Always draw the Gantt Chart first** — it is the official method and earns the first 2–3 marks of any 7-mark calculation sub-question.
- [ ] For problems with **AT = 0 for all processes**, FCFS is still suboptimal compared to **SJF (Shortest Job First)** — a frequent KTU comparison question.
- [ ] **FCFS cannot cause starvation** because every process that arrives will eventually run — this is a direct exam-answer phrase worth 2 marks in any "Discuss the advantages" question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shortest Job First
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shortest Job First

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shortest Job First (SJF)** is a **non-priority, greedy CPU scheduling algorithm** that selects the waiting process with the **smallest next CPU burst length** for execution. When multiple processes are in the ready queue, the dispatcher always picks the one whose predicted next CPU burst is the **minimum**.

The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) classifies SJF into two functional variants:

| Variant | Alternate Name | Preemption Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-Preemptive SJF** | Shortest Job Next (SJN) | Once a process begins execution on the CPU, it runs to **completion** of its current burst, even if a shorter job arrives. |
| **Preemptive SJF** | Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) | If a newly arriving process has a **CPU burst strictly smaller** than the **remaining time** of the currently running process, the CPU is **preempted** and assigned to the new arrival. |

> [!NOTE]
