| $P_4$ | 3 | 1 | 15 | 12 | 11 |
| $P_5$ | 4 | 2 | 17 | 13 | 11 |

**Step 4 — Averages:**

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{18 + 13 + 7 + 12 + 13}{5} = \frac{63}{5} = 12.6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{11 + 8 + 4 + 11 + 11}{5} = \frac{45}{5} = 9.0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Mark Distribution:** Correct Gantt chart (2), correct CT (1), correct TAT (2), correct WT (1), averages (1) = 7 marks.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**
Define a **race condition**. Consider two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ that share a variable `balance` (initial value 1000). Both processes execute the following code concurrently:

```
Process Pi:                      // i = 1 or 2
  temp = balance;
  temp = temp + 500;
  balance = temp;
```

Show how a **race condition** can occur, and propose **two software-level remedies** (excluding hardware locks).

**Model Solution:**

**Race Condition Demonstration (4 marks):**

The operation $balance = balance + 500$ is *not atomic*; it compiles to three machine instructions: `LOAD → ADD → STORE`. Consider this interleaving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Time 1: } P_1 \text{ executes } temp_1 = \text{balance}; \quad (temp_1 = 1000) \\
&\text{Time 2: } P_2 \text{ executes } temp_2 = \text{balance}; \quad (temp_2 = 1000) \\
&\text{Time 3: } P_1 \text{ executes } temp_1 = 1500; \\
&\text{Time 4: } P_2 \text{ executes } temp_2 = 1500; \\
&\text{Time 5: } P_1 \text{ executes } balance = 1500; \\
&\text{Time 6: } P_2 \text{ executes } balance = 1500;
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final balance = 1500**, but the **expected balance = 2000** (each process added 500). The increment by $P_1$ was lost. The exact outcome depends on the **interleaving order**, which is the essence of a race condition (1 mark for identifying the lost update, 2 marks for the trace, 1 mark for the expected vs actual).

**Two Software Remedies (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

1. **Peterson's Algorithm** (software-only, two processes) — wrap the three statements in an *entry section* that busy-waits until mutual exclusion is granted.
2. **Mutex / Semaphore** (software construct implemented by the OS) — acquire a `mutex_lock` before the read, release after the write. The OS internally enforces atomicity using hardware primitives (e.g., `xchg` on x86).
3. **(Alternative)** **Monitor / Condition Variables** in higher-level languages (Java `synchronized` block, C++ `std::lock_guard`).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - For part (a), **forgetting to update the ready queue** during the trace loses 1 mark. Always show the queue evolution.
> - For part (b), the trace **must use at least 6 time steps** to be valid. A 3-step trace is rejected as it does not actually demonstrate a race.
> - "Use a lock" without specifying *which lock* (mutex, semaphore, spinlock) loses 0.5 marks for imprecision.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Critical Section + Peterson's Solution**

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEET632, 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]**
*State the **three formal requirements** that any correct solution to the Critical Section Problem must satisfy. With a neat block diagram, describe the **general structure of a process** $P_i$ and label the four regions of execution.*

**Model Solution:**

**Three Requirements (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** If process $P_i$ is executing in its critical section, then no other process $P_k$ ($k \neq i$) can simultaneously be in its critical section.
2. **Progress:** If no process is in the critical section and some processes wish to enter, the selection of the next entrant is made in finite time, restricted to only those processes that are *not* in their remainder section.
3. **Bounded Waiting:** There exists a bound on the number of times other processes can enter the critical section after a process has announced its intention to enter and *before* it is granted entry.

**Block Diagram of $P_i$ (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i \rightarrow \text{[Entry Section]} \rightarrow \text{[Critical Section]} \rightarrow \text{[Exit Section]} \rightarrow \text{[Remainder Section]} \\
\quad\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\uparrow \qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\downarrow \\
\quad\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\text{loop back to Entry Section after Remainder} \qquad\qquad\qquad\;\;\uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

* **Entry Section:** Code that requests permission (sets `flag[i] = true`, `turn = j` in Peterson).
* **Critical Section:** Code that accesses shared data.
* **Exit Section:** Code that relinquishes permission (sets `flag[i] = false` in Peterson).
* **Remainder Section:** All other (non-critical) code.

**Mark split:** 3 marks for the three requirements, 1 mark for correctly naming the four regions, 3 marks for explaining the role of each region.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**
*Explain **Peterson's Solution** to the Critical Section Problem for two processes $P_0$ and $P_1$. Write the complete pseudocode for both processes. Show by a **proof by contradiction** that the solution guarantees **mutual exclusion**.*

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Description (2 marks):**
Peterson's Solution is a software-only synchronisation algorithm for **exactly two processes** ($P_0$ and $P_1$). It uses two shared variables: `int turn` and `boolean flag[2]`. It guarantees all three CSP requirements without hardware `test_and_set` or OS mutex support, but requires **atomic loads and stores** on the shared variables.

**Pseudocode (3 marks):**

```
// SHARED VARIABLES
int turn;              // 0 or 1
boolean flag[2];       // initially false

// PROCESS Pi (i = 0 or 1)
do {
    flag[i] = true;           // "I want to enter"
    turn = j;                  // "But you go first, j = 1-i"
    while (flag[j] == true && turn == j)
        ;   // busy wait
    
    // ===== CRITICAL SECTION =====
    // shared resource accessed here
    // =============================
    
    flag[i] = false;          // "I am done"
    
    // remainder section
} while (true);
```

**Proof of Mutual Exclusion (2 marks):**

Assume for contradiction that both $P_0$ and $P_1$ are inside their critical sections at the same instant $t$. For each process to have entered, it must have passed the `while` test:

- For $P_0$: `(flag[1] == false) ∨ (turn ≠ 1)`.
- For $P_1$: `(flag[0] == false) ∨ (turn ≠ 0)`.

Since both are inside the CS, neither has executed `flag[i] = false` yet. So `flag[0] = flag[1] = true`, reducing the predicates to:
- From $P_0$: `turn ≠ 1`.
- From $P_1$: `turn ≠ 0`.

Since `turn ∈ {0, 1}`, exactly one of these is false — **contradiction**. Hence both cannot be in the CS simultaneously. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In part (a), students often confuse **Progress** with **Bounded Waiting**; the difference is *system-wide liveness* vs *per-process starvation freedom*. Listing them with the same wording loses 1 mark.
> - In part (b), the pseudocode **must** include `turn = j;` (not `turn = i;`) — a common slip-up that breaks correctness. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> - The proof **must end with the explicit contradiction** (e.g., $\Rightarrow\!\Leftarrow$ or "this is impossible"). Stopping at the two inequalities loses 0.5 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Wall):**

- **Round Robin** = circular FIFO with pre-emption by **Time Quantum $T_q$**.
- RR is **fair** (no starvation) but has **higher context-switch overhead**.
- Empirical rule: $T_q$ should be **≫ context-switch time** (rule of thumb: $T_q \geq 10 \times T_{cs}$).
- Key RR metrics: $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$, $\;WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$, $\;RT_i = \text{First CPU} - AT_i$.
- A **race condition** needs **shared data + concurrent access + non-atomic operations** — the holy trinity.
- **Critical Section** = the code segment that touches shared resources.
- CSP has **3 requirements**: **Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting**.
- **Peterson's Solution** is a **2-process, software-only** CSP solution using `flag[2]` and `turn`.
- Peterson's **Entry Section** = `flag[i] = true; turn = j; while (flag[j] && turn == j) ;`
- Peterson's **Exit Section** = `flag[i] = false;`
- The **courtesy flip `turn = j`** is the secret sauce that makes bounded waiting finite.
- Mutual-exclusion proof uses **contradiction + predicate logic** with the fact that `turn ∈ {0,1}`.
- Peterson requires **atomic LOAD/STORE**; on modern weakly-ordered CPUs, explicit **memory barriers** (`mfence`) are needed for portability.
- Average metrics are *normalised by $n$* — the dividing-by-$n$ step is the most-skipped step in KTU valuation (loses 0.5 mark if forgotten).
- Always **update the ready queue after each quantum** in RR traces — examiners cross-check this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Synchronization Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronization Hardware** refers to the set of special **atomic (uninterruptible) machine-level instructions** provided directly by the processor architecture to support process/thread synchronization, mutual exclusion, and the construction of higher-level synchronization primitives (such as locks, semaphores, and monitors) without relying purely on software-based busy-waiting solutions.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Process Synchronization – Module 2)**, synchronization hardware addresses the fundamental limitations of purely software solutions (such as Dekker's or Peterson's algorithms), namely:
- Inability to guarantee atomicity in high-level languages
- Dependence on busy-wait loops
- Vulnerability on modern pipelined, multi-core architectures

> [!NOTE]
> **Atomic Instruction**: A single machine instruction that the CPU executes as a **single, indivisible, uninterruptible bus transaction**. From the perspective of other processors/cores, it either happens completely or not at all.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "One-Token Lockbox" Intuition

Imagine a **single public lockbox** placed in a hallway, containing **only one physical key** to a restricted laboratory.

- Any student who wants to enter the lab must walk up to the lockbox.
- The act of **opening the box, checking if the key is there, and taking it out** happens as a **single, instantaneous, un-splittable motion** — nobody can interrupt you mid-action.
- If the key is there, you take it and enter the lab (you now hold the resource).
- If the key is **not** there, you must wait outside (busy-wait) until the student inside returns the key.
- The "check-and-take" motion is the **Test-and-Set** — atomic, single, and decisive.

> [!NOTE]
> This lockbox model directly mirrors how a processor provides a special instruction that reads and modifies a memory word in a **single bus cycle**, immune to context switches and interrupts.

---

## 1.3 Why Hardware Support is Needed — Key Motivation

| Problem in Pure Software Solutions | Hardware-Level Remedy |
| :--- | :--- |
| Context switches can split a critical section | Atomic instructions cannot be interrupted |
| Non-atomic `load` and `store` race conditions | `compare_and_swap` performs read-modify-write atomically |
| Out-of-order execution on pipelined CPUs | **Memory barriers** enforce ordering |
| Memory caching inconsistencies across cores | Atomic operations use **locked bus cycles** or **cache coherence** protocols |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Answers**: The hardware-level atomic instruction must execute in **exactly one memory bus cycle** (or one cache-coherent transaction) for true atomicity on a single-processor system.

---

## 1.4 Common Hardware Primitives Discussed in KTU Syllabus

1. **`Test-and-Set`** — Reads and sets a flag in one atomic step
2. **`Swap`** — Atomically exchanges two memory values
3. **`Compare-and-Swap (CAS)`** — Updates a value only if it matches an expected old value
4. **`Fetch-and-Add`** — Atomically increments a counter and returns the old value
5. **Memory Barriers (`fence` instructions)** — Force ordering of memory operations

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### 🔹 A. The `TestAndSet` Instruction

A **single, atomic, indivisible** machine instruction that performs:

$$
\text{TestAndSet}(\text{target}) \rightarrow \text{returns } \text{old\_value} \text{ of target AND sets target = TRUE}
$$

**Logic Steps** (as the CPU executes it):
1. The CPU places the memory address of `target` on the address bus.
2. The CPU asserts the **LOCK#** signal — locking the memory bus.
3. In a **single bus transaction**, it reads the current value of `target`.
4. It then writes `TRUE` (`1`) back to the same location.
5. The lock is released only after the write completes.

Because Steps 3 and 4 are inseparable, no other process or interrupt handler can interleave a read/write of `target`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On modern x86 systems, this is realized as the `XCHG` instruction with the `LOCK` prefix, or as the `BTS` (Bit Test and Set) instruction.

---

### 🔹 B. The `Swap` Instruction

Atomically exchanges the contents of two memory locations:

$$
\text{Swap}(a, b) \longrightarrow (a \leftarrow b, \ b \leftarrow a) \ \text{atomically}
$$

It is functionally similar to `TestAndSet` for mutual exclusion, and can be used as a building block for spinlocks.

---

### 🔹 C. The `CompareAndSwap` (CAS) Instruction

A **three-operand** atomic instruction, the most powerful of the three:

$$
\text{CAS}(\text{memory}, \text{expected}, \text{new}) \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{memory} = \text{new}, \ \text{return TRUE} & \text{if memory} = \text{expected} \\ \text{no change}, \ \text{return FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Key Insight for KTU**:
- CAS is the **foundation of all modern lock-free data structures** (lock-free queues, stacks, counters).
- It was popularized by the **IBM System/370** architecture and is now ubiquitous (`CMPXCHG` on x86).

---

### 🔹 D. Memory Barriers (Fences)

A **memory barrier** is not a synchronization instruction on data per se, but a **compiler/CPU ordering constraint** that ensures all memory operations before the barrier complete before any operation after the barrier begins.

- **`MFENCE`** — Full memory barrier (x86)
- **`SFENCE`** — Store fence
- **`LFENCE`** — Load fence

> [!NOTE]
> Without memory barriers, modern **out-of-order execution** pipelines may reorder loads and stores, violating the sequential consistency required for correct synchronization.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM INSTRUCTION**: The following table is the high-density reference for solving any 7/14-mark KTU question on Synchronization Hardware.

| # | Atomic Instruction | Mathematical / Logical Form | Key Property | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `TestAndSet(target)` | $T = \text{target}; \ \text{target} = 1; \ \text{return } T$ | Read + Write in 1 cycle | Spinlock construction |
| 2 | `Swap(a, b)` | $\text{temp} = a; \ a = b; \ b = \text{temp}$ (atomic) | Mutual value exchange | Process-level flag exchange |
| 3 | `CompareAndSwap(m, e, n)` | $\text{if } m = e \text{ then } m = n; \ \text{return match}$ | Optimistic concurrency | Lock-free algorithms |
| 4 | `FetchAndAdd(p, v)` | $\text{old} = \ast p; \ \ast p = \text{old} + v; \ \text{return old}$ | Atomic counter increment | Ticket locks, fair scheduling |
| 5 | `Memory Barrier` | $\text{op}_1 \prec \text{BARRIER} \prec \text{op}_2$ | Enforces ordering | Cross-core visibility |
| 6 | Bounded Wait Spinlock Cost | $T_{\text{wait}} \le (n-1) \cdot T_{\text{CAS}}$ | $n$ processes, max $n-1$ waits | KTU critical section proofs |

> **Notation Used**:
> - $T$ → temporary register / return value
> - $m$ → memory address
> - $e$ → expected old value
> - $n$ → new value
> - $\ast p$ → value at pointer $p$
> - $\prec$ → "happens-before" relation
> - $T_{\text{CAS}}$ → execution time of one CAS instruction

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB)**: Use `CompareAndSwap` to implement **latch-free B-tree updates** for high-throughput transactions.
- **Linux Kernel `cmpxchg`**: Used in RCU (Read-Copy-Update) and lock-free linked list manipulation.
- **Java `AtomicInteger`, C++ `std::atomic`**: All implemented atop hardware CAS.
- **High-Frequency Trading Systems**: Use `FetchAndAdd` for **deterministic-fair** ticket locks to guarantee order-of-arrival fairness.
- **Multiprocessor Boot Synchronization (BIOS/UEFI)**: AP (Application Processor) startup uses memory barriers and atomic flags.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Implementing Mutual Exclusion Using `TestAndSet`

The goal is to design a **mutual-exclusion algorithm** (a spinlock) for $n$ concurrent processes that satisfies:
- **Mutual Exclusion** — at most one process in the critical section
- **Progress** — no process outside CS blocks others
- **Bounded Waiting** — a process waits at most a finite, bounded number of turns

### Step 1 — Define the Shared Variable

Let `lock` be a single shared Boolean variable, **initialized to FALSE** ($0$).

$$
\text{lock} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \text{lock}_{\text{initial}} = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Atomic Hardware Instruction

$$
\text{TestAndSet}(\text{target}) \rightarrow \text{return } \text{old\_value} \text{ AND set target} = 1
$$

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
T = \text{TestAndSet}(\&\text{lock}) \equiv \begin{cases} T \leftarrow \text{lock} \\ \text{lock} \leftarrow 1 \\ \text{return } T \end{cases} \quad \text{(atomic)}
$$

### Step 3 — Algorithm Skeleton for Process $P_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{do} \{ \\
&\quad \text{// ENTRY SECTION} \\
&\quad T \leftarrow \text{TestAndSet}(\&\text{lock}) \\
&\ \text{while } (T == 1); \quad \text{// busy-wait if lock was already held} \\
&\quad \text{// --- CRITICAL SECTION ---} \\
&\quad \text{// ... execute protected code ...} \\
&\quad \text{// EXIT SECTION} \\
&\quad \text{lock} \leftarrow 0; \\
&\quad \text{// --- REMAINDER SECTION ---} \\
&\} \text{while} (\text{true});
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Proof of Mutual Exclusion

Assume, for contradiction, that two processes $P_i$ and $P_j$ are **simultaneously** in the critical section.

1. For $P_i$ to enter the CS, the **last** `TestAndSet` it executed must have returned $0$.
2. This means **immediately after** $P_i$'s atomic instruction, `lock` was set to $1$.
3. For $P_j$ to also enter, **after** $P_i$ set `lock = 1`, $P_j$ must have read `lock = 0` and set it to $1$.
4. But step 2 and step 3 are **causally contradictory** — `lock` was $1$ when $P_i$ finished; the atomicity of `TestAndSet` guarantees that $P_j$ cannot have observed `lock = 0` after that moment unless $P_i$ **exited** (which would have set `lock = 0`, violating the "simultaneous" assumption).

$$
\boxed{\therefore \text{Mutual Exclusion is preserved.}}
$$

### Step 5 — Proof of Bounded Waiting

The simple `TestAndSet` solution shown above **fails bounded waiting** because the order of acquiring the lock depends on arbitrary CPU scheduling. To fix this, we use the **extended TestAndSet with waiting array**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Shared:} \quad \text{bool lock} = 0; \quad \text{bool waiting}[n] = \{0, 0, \ldots, 0\}; \\
&\text{For process } P_i: \\
&\text{do} \{ \\
&\quad \text{waiting}[i] = 1; \quad T \leftarrow 1; \quad \text{key} = 1; \\
&\quad \text{while (waiting[i] \&\& key)} \{ \text{key} = \text{TestAndSet}(\&\text{lock}); \} \\
&\quad \text{waiting}[i] = 0; \\
&\quad \text{// CRITICAL SECTION} \\
&\quad j \leftarrow (i + 1) \mod n; \\
&\quad \text{while ((j != i) \&\& !waiting[j]) } \{ j \leftarrow (j+1) \mod n; \} \\
&\quad \text{if (j == i)} \{ \text{lock} = 0; \} \text{ else } \{ \text{waiting}[j] = 0; \} \\
&\quad \text{// REMAINDER SECTION} \\
&\} \text{while (true});
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bounded Waiting Analysis**: When $P_i$ exits the CS, it scans the array `waiting[]` starting at index $(i+1) \mod n$. It finds the **first** waiting process $P_j$ and grants it the lock by setting `waiting[j] = 0`. Hence $P_j$ enters the CS in its **next turn**, satisfying bounded waiting.

$$
\boxed{\text{Maximum wait time for any process} \le (n-1) \cdot T_{\text{TestAndSet}}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Hardware vs. Software Synchronization

> [!NOTE]
> **High-yield comparison table** — frequently asked in **7-mark KTU questions**.

| Criterion | Software Solutions (Peterson, Dekker) | Synchronization Hardware (TestAndSet, CAS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Atomicity Guarantees | Relies on compiler/memory model | Guaranteed by CPU hardware |
| Performance on Single CPU | Reasonable | Faster (1 instruction vs. many) |
| Performance on Multiprocessor | **Fails** (memory reordering) | Robust (with memory barriers) |
| Portability | High (pure C/Java) | Architecture-specific |
| Busy-Wait | Yes (spin) | Yes (spin) |
| Bounded Waiting Achievable | Yes (Peterson) | Yes (with waiting array) |
| Modern Industry Use | Educational only | **Standard (used in OS kernels)** |
| Susceptible to Cache Coherence Bugs | Yes | No (uses locked atomic ops) |

---

## 3.3 Derivation — CompareAndSwap as Universal Synchronization Primitive

We prove CAS can implement **any** atomic read-modify-write operation.

**Theorem**: For any function $f: \mathbb{N} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$, the operation $x \leftarrow f(x)$ can be implemented atomically using CAS.

**Proof by Construction**:
1. Read current value of $x$ into local register `old`.
2. Compute `new = f(old)` locally (no concurrency issues — local computation).
3. Execute $\text{CAS}(\&x, \text{old}, \text{new})$.
4. If CAS returns `TRUE`, the update succeeded — exit.
5. If CAS returns `FALSE`, another process changed $x$ — re-read, recompute, retry.

$$
\boxed{\text{This loop terminates because } x \text{ can only be modified finitely many times.}}
$$

This is the formal basis of **optimistic concurrency control** used in every modern lock-free algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow — Mutual Exclusion with `TestAndSet`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Pi Starts] --> B[Set waiting i = 1]
    B --> C[key = TestAndSet and lock]
    C --> D{key == 0?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Enter Critical Section]
    D -- No --> F[Check waiting i == 1 AND key == 1]
    F --> G{Loop Condition True?}
    G -- Yes --> C
    G -- No --> E
    E --> H[Execute Protected Code]
    H --> I[Exit CS: Find Next Waiting Process]
    I --> J{Scan j from i+1 mod n}
    J --> K{Found waiting j?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Set waiting j = 0]
    K -- No --> M[Set lock = 0]
    L --> N[Remainder Section]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Loop Forever?}
    O -- Yes --> A
```

---

## 4.2 Atomic Operation Architecture — CompareAndSwap Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instruction Fetch] --> B[Decode CAS Opcode]
    B --> C[Read Memory m into Register]
    C --> D{Register == Expected e?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Write New Value n to Memory m]
    D -- No --> F[No Write: Memory Unchanged]
    E --> G[Return TRUE to Processor]
    F --> H[Return FALSE to Processor]
    G --> I[Commit: Pipeline Stage Complete]
    H --> I
```

---

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

    end
    subgraph L1[CPU and Bus Layer]
        C1[X86 LOCK Prefix]
        C2[ARM LDREX and STREX]
        C3[Memory Coherence - MESI]
        C4[Locked Bus Cycle]
    end
    U1 --> R1
    U2 --> R2
    U3 --> R3
    R1 --> K1
    R2 --> K2
    R3 --> K3
    K1 --> H1
    K1 --> H2
    K1 --> H3
    K2 --> H1
    K3 --> H2
    H1 --> H4
    H2 --> H4
    H3 --> H4
    H1 --> C1
    H2 --> C1
    H3 --> C2
    H4 --> C3

```

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Race Condition Resolution with CAS

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P1 as Process P1
    participant P2 as Process P2
    participant MEM as Shared Memory (lock)
    Note over P1,P2: Both processes attempt to enter Critical Section
    P1->>MEM: CAS(lock, 0, 1) [expects 0]
    MEM-->>P1: Return TRUE (lock was 0)
    Note over P1: P1 enters Critical Section
    P2->>MEM: CAS(lock, 0, 1) [expects 0]
    MEM-->>P2: Return FALSE (lock is now 1)
    Note over P2: P2 busy-waits (spins)
    P1->>MEM: lock = 0 (Exit Section)
    P2->>MEM: CAS(lock, 0, 1) [retry]
    MEM-->>P2: Return TRUE
    Note over P2: P2 enters Critical Section
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
> What is an **atomic operation**? Why is `TestAndSet` considered atomic in a multiprocessor system? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer**:
An atomic operation is a machine-level instruction that executes as a **single, indivisible, uninterruptible unit** with respect to all other processes, processors, and interrupts. The `TestAndSet` instruction is considered atomic because the read of the current flag value and the write of the new value (`TRUE`) occur within **one locked bus transaction** — no other processor can observe an intermediate state of the flag. On x86 systems, this is implemented via the `LOCK#` pin which locks the memory bus, ensuring hardware-level mutual exclusion. (3 Marks)

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
> Differentiate between `TestAndSet` and `CompareAndSwap` instructions. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | `TestAndSet` | `CompareAndSwap` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of Operands | 1 (target) | 3 (memory, expected, new) |
| Conditional Update | No (always sets) | Yes (only if match) |
| Return Value | Old value (boolean) | Boolean (match status) |
| Use Case | Simple spinlocks | Lock-free algorithms |
| Power | Limited | Universal (can simulate any RMW) |

`CompareAndSwap` is more powerful because it supports optimistic concurrency — it only updates when a precondition holds, making it the basis of lock-free data structures. (3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester]**

#### (a) Explain the `TestAndSet` instruction with its hardware implementation. How does it solve the critical section problem? Discuss its limitation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Definition (2 Marks)**:
The `TestAndSet` instruction is a single, atomic, hardware-level instruction defined as:

$$
T \leftarrow \text{lock}; \quad \text{lock} \leftarrow 1; \quad \text{return } T
$$

It returns the old value of `lock` and simultaneously sets it to `1`, all within one bus cycle.

**Hardware Implementation (3 Marks)**:
On x86 multiprocessors, atomicity is achieved via the `LOCK#` signal:

1. The CPU asserts `LOCK#` on the system bus **before** initiating the read.
2. This prevents all other processors and DMA controllers from accessing memory.
3. The CPU reads the current value of `lock` into a register.
4. The CPU writes `1` back to `lock` in the **same bus cycle**.
5. `LOCK#` is de-asserted, releasing the bus.

Modern processors use **cache line locking** (MESI protocol invalidation) rather than physical bus locking for scalability, but the semantic guarantee remains the same.

**Solution to Critical Section (1 Mark)**:
A process loops on `TestAndSet` until it returns `0` (lock was free), enters the CS, and sets `lock = 0` on exit.

**Limitation (1 Mark)**:
The simple `TestAndSet` spinlock **does not guarantee bounded waiting** — a process may starve indefinitely under unfair scheduling. This is fixed by adding the `waiting[]` array.

---

#### (b) Write the algorithm for mutual exclusion using `TestAndSet` with **bounded waiting** for $n$ processes. Explain how bounded waiting is achieved. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Algorithm (5 Marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Shared: } \text{bool lock} = 0; \quad \text{bool waiting}[0 \ldots n-1] = \{0, 0, \ldots, 0\}; \\
&\text{For process } P_i, \quad i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-1: \\
&\text{do} \{ \\
&\quad \text{waiting}[i] = 1; \quad \text{key} = 1; \\
&\quad \text{while (waiting}[i] \ \&\& \ \text{key)} \{ \\
&\quad\quad \text{key} = \text{TestAndSet}(\&\text{lock}); \\
&\quad \} \\
&\quad \text{waiting}[i] = 0; \\
&\quad \text{// --- CRITICAL SECTION ---} \\
&\quad j \leftarrow (i + 1) \mod n; \\
&\quad \text{while } (j \neq i \ \&\& \ !\text{waiting}[j]) \{ j \leftarrow (j+1) \mod n; \} \\
&\quad \text{if } (j == i) \{ \text{lock} = 0; \} \text{ else } \{ \text{waiting}[j] = 0; \} \\
&\quad \text{// --- REMAINDER SECTION ---} \\
&\} \text{while } (\text{true});
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of Bounded Waiting (2 Marks)**:
- Each process announces its intent via `waiting[i] = 1`.
- When a process exits the CS, it scans the `waiting[]` array cyclically from index $(i+1) \mod n$ and grants the lock to the **first** waiting process it finds by setting `waiting[j] = 0`.
- If no other process is waiting (`j == i`), it sets `lock = 0` (free lock).
- A process that just failed to enter will be picked up in at most $(n-1)$ iterations by the scanning process. Hence, **maximum wait is bounded by $(n-1)$ CS executions**.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester]**

#### (a) Explain the `CompareAndSwap` (CAS) instruction. How is it used to implement a **lock-free counter**? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Definition (2 Marks)**:
`CompareAndSwap(memory, expected, new)` atomically performs:

$$
\begin{cases}
\text{memory} \leftarrow \text{new}, \ \text{return TRUE} & \text{if } \ast\text{memory} = \text{expected} \\
\text{return FALSE} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Lock-Free Counter Implementation (4 Marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Shared: int counter} = 0; \\
&\text{// Atomically increment counter} \\
&\text{void increment()} \{ \\
&\quad \text{int old} = \text{counter}; \\
&\quad \text{int new\_val} = \text{old} + 1; \\
&\quad \text{while (!CAS(\&counter, old, new\_val))} \{ \\
&\quad\quad \text{old} = \text{counter}; \quad \text{new\_val} = \text{old} + 1; \\
&\quad \} \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation (1 Mark)**:
- The thread reads the current value (`old`), computes the new value locally, and tries to swap.
- If another thread changed the counter in the meantime, CAS fails — the thread re-reads, recomputes, and retries.
- **No lock is held**; threads do not block each other — hence "lock-free."
- This guarantees progress because the system as a whole makes forward progress (at least one CAS succeeds per retry cycle).

---

#### (b) What are **memory barriers**? Why are they necessary even when using atomic instructions like `TestAndSet`? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Definition (3 Marks)**:
A memory barrier (also called a *fence*) is a CPU instruction that forces **ordering constraints** on memory operations issued before and after it. There are four types:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Load Barrier} \ (\text{LFENCE}): \quad \text{All loads before must complete before any load after.} \\
&\text{Store Barrier} \ (\text{SFENCE}): \quad \text{All stores before must complete before any store after.} \\
&\text{Full Barrier} \ (\text{MFENCE}): \quad \text{Combines both load and store ordering.} \\
&\text{Compiler Barrier}: \quad \text{Prevents compiler reordering of instructions.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Necessary with Atomic Instructions (4 Marks)**:

1. **Out-of-Order Execution**: Modern CPUs execute instructions out of order for performance. Even if `TestAndSet` is atomic, *adjacent* loads/stores may be reordered past it.

2. **Store Buffering**: When a CPU writes to memory, the write may sit in a **store buffer** before reaching the cache. Other CPUs may not see the latest value immediately.

3. **Cache Coherence Delay**: Even with MESI, propagation of cache invalidations across cores is not instantaneous.

4. **Example Failure**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Thread 1: } \text{data} = 42; \quad \text{ready} = 1; \\
&\text{Thread 2: } \text{while (!ready); } \quad \text{print(data);}
\end{aligned}
$$
   Without a barrier, Thread 2 may see `ready = 1` but read stale `data` because the store to `data` was reordered after the store to `ready`. Inserting `MFENCE` (or using `_mm_mfence()`) between the two stores forces correct ordering.

5. **Conclusion**: Atomic instructions ensure *single-variable* atomicity; memory barriers ensure *multi-variable ordering* — both are essential for correct synchronization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**:
>
> 1. **Confusing atomicity with ordering** — Writing that `TestAndSet` alone guarantees mutual exclusion is **correct**, but claiming it guarantees ordering of *other* memory operations is **wrong**. Use memory barriers for ordering.
> 2. **Forgetting the `waiting[]` array in bounded-waiting questions** — Many students write the simple spinlock and lose 2-3 marks for not proving bounded waiting.
> 3. **Not stating initial conditions** — Always mention `lock = 0` and `waiting[] = {false}` in the shared data section.
> 4. **Mixing up CAS with TestAndSet** — CAS is conditional; TestAndSet is unconditional. Marks are lost if the pseudocode does not match the instruction semantics.
> 5. **Ignoring the multiprocessor concern** — On a single-core system, disabling interrupts is sufficient. The whole point of hardware synchronization is **multiprocessor** support — always mention this.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention `MFENCE` / `LOCK#` prefix** — These are the actual *hardware mechanisms* that make instructions atomic. KTU examiners specifically look for these terms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Synchronization Hardware**

- ✅ **Atomicity** = single, indivisible bus transaction; cannot be interrupted.
- ✅ **TestAndSet** = unconditional read-and-set in one cycle.
- ✅ **Swap** = atomic two-way value exchange.
- ✅ **CompareAndSwap (CAS)** = conditional update; foundation of lock-free programming.
- ✅ **FetchAndAdd** = atomic increment; used in ticket locks.
- ✅ **Memory Barriers** = enforce ordering across cores (LFENCE, SFENCE, MFENCE).
- ✅ **Simple TestAndSet spinlock fails bounded waiting** — must add `waiting[]` array.
- ✅ **Bounded wait proof**: max $(n-1)$ CS executions before a process enters.
- ✅ **Hardware mechanism on x86**: `LOCK#` prefix / cache line locking / MESI coherence.
- ✅ **Multiprocessor cache coherence** is essential — single-CPU disabling interrupts is **not** sufficient on multi-core.
- ✅ **Atomic instructions solve mutual exclusion; memory barriers solve visibility & ordering.**
- ✅ **CAS is the universal primitive** — can implement any Read-Modify-Write operation optimistically.
- ✅ **Industry relevance**: Linux kernel `cmpxchg`, Java `AtomicInteger`, C++ `std::atomic`, database MVCC, RCU.
- ✅ **Modern alternative**: `futex` in Linux combines userspace spinning with kernel blocking for efficiency.
- ✅ **Architecture-specific names to remember**:
  - x86 → `CMPXCHG`, `XCHG`, `BTS`, `LOCK` prefix
  - ARM → `LDREX`/`STREX` (Load/Store Exclusive)
  - PowerPC → `lwarx`/`stwcx` (Load/Store Word Conditional)
  - IBM 370 → `CS` (Compare and Swap)
  - MIPS → `ll`/`sc` (Load Linked / Store Conditional)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mutex Locks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mutex Locks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Mutex (Mutual Exclusion Object)** > A **Mutex Lock** is a synchronization primitive provided by the operating system (and supported by hardware atomic instructions) that enforces *mutual exclusion* on a shared critical resource. It is essentially a special variable (commonly of type `pthread_mutex_t` in POSIX systems) that can be in one of two logical states: **locked (1)** or **unlocked (0)**. A thread must *acquire* the mutex before entering a critical section and *release* it on exit. Only **one** thread may hold the lock at any instant. Mathematically, the state of a mutex at time $t$ can be represented as: $$M(t) \in \{0, 1\}$$ where $M(t) = 1$ denotes the **locked** state and $M(t) = 0$ denotes the **unlocked** state. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Public Bathroom Key" Imagine a public restroom in a bus stand with a single physical **key hanging on the door**. - When the key is **on the door** (unlocked, $M = 0$) — the bathroom is free. Any person (thread) walking by can take the key. - The person **holding the key** (locked, $M = 1$) has exclusive access. No one else can enter. - The first person who notices the key is missing *queues up and waits* until the key is returned. - When the person is done, they **hang the key back on the door** (release) so the next person can take it. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway for Examiners** > Unlike a *binary semaphore*, a **mutex** has the concept of **ownership** — only the thread that acquired (locked) the mutex is permitted to release (unlock) it. A binary semaphore has no such ownership constraint. This distinction is a **favourite KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question**. ### 1.3 The Critical Section Problem Recap The classic problem that mutex locks solve is the **Critical Section Problem (CSP)**, originally formulated by **Edsger Dijkstra (1965)**. A solution to CSP must satisfy three formal properties: | Property | Description | Metric / Standard | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Mutual Exclusion** | No two processes are inside the critical section (CS) simultaneously. | Hard guarantee, **mandatory** | | **Progress** | If no process is in CS and some processes *want* to enter, only those not in remainder section decide who enters next. | Liveness guarantee | | **Bounded Waiting** | A bound exists on the number of times other processes can enter CS after a process has requested entry, before this process gets its turn. | Starvation-freedom | > [!NOTE] > Mutex locks provide **Mutual Exclusion** deterministically. The other two properties (Progress, Bounded Waiting) depend on the *waiting strategy* used (busy-wait vs. blocking). ### 1.4 Why Hardware Atomic Instructions Are Needed A naive software-only solution fails because checking the value of $M$ and setting it is **not a single step** — it is two separate machine instructions. Between them, a context switch can occur, breaking mutual exclusion. Mutexes therefore rely on **hardware-supported atomic instructions** such as: - `test_and_set()` — atomically reads and writes a value - `compare_and_swap()` — atomically compares and conditionally swaps These execute as a **single, indivisible** CPU instruction, which is the foundation for correct mutex implementation. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Operations of a Mutex A mutex object exposes **exactly two atomic operations** to user-space threads. The naming convention follows the **POSIX pthread standard** widely adopted in KTU-referenced textbooks (Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne; Stallings). | Operation | POSIX API | Effect on State | Owner Constraint | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Acquire / Lock** | `pthread_mutex_lock(&m)` | If $M = 0 \rightarrow M := 1$, thread proceeds. If $M = 1 \rightarrow$ thread blocks (or spins). | None on acquire | | **Release / Unlock** | `pthread_mutex_unlock(&m)` | If $M = 1 \rightarrow M := 0$, wakes one waiter. | **Only the owning thread may call this** | The combined state transition can be expressed as: $$M_{new} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \texttt{acquire()} \text{ succeeds} \\ 0 & \text{if } \texttt{release()} \text{ is called} \\ \text{(unchanged)} & \text{if } \texttt{acquire()} \text{ blocks} \end{cases}$$ ### 2.2 Structural Logic of Acquire / Release **Step 1 — The `acquire()` Path** - Thread $T_i$ evaluates the lock state. - If the mutex is **unlocked**, $T_i$ atomically claims ownership, sets state to locked, and enters the critical section. - If the mutex is **already locked**, $T_i$ is **descheduled** by the OS scheduler and placed in the mutex's wait queue. **Step 2 — Inside the Critical Section** - Only the lock-holding thread executes the protected code. All other contenders remain suspended (or spinning). **Step 3 — The `release()` Path** - The owning thread finishes its work and calls `release()`. - The OS atomically sets the lock to **unlocked**. - A **single** waiting thread (chosen by scheduler policy) is woken up and transitions to the *ready* queue. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ownership is a SAFETY property.** Allowing thread $T_j$ to release a mutex owned by $T_i$ would create an unauthenticated handoff, opening the door to deadlocks and double-entry scenarios. POSIX explicitly enforces this — calling `unlock` on a mutex you do not own results in undefined behaviour. ### 2.3 Hardware Building Blocks: `test_and_set()` and `compare_and_swap()` #### 2.3.1 `test_and_set()` — The Atomic Test-and-Set The instruction is defined as a single atomic operation that returns the *old* value while simultaneously writing the *new* value. $$ \text{test\_and\_set}(\text{target}, \text{new}) \;\rightarrow\; \text{returns old\_value, sets target := new} $$ In standard textbook pseudocode (C-style atomic): ```c int test_and_set(int *target) { int old = *target; // read current value *target = 1; // unconditionally set to 1 (locked) return old; // return what we saw } ``` The execution of these three lines is guaranteed by hardware to be **atomic** (bus-locked on x86, `ldaxr/stlxr` on ARM). #### 2.3.2 `compare_and_swap()` — The Atomic Compare-and-Swap A more powerful primitive that compares with an *expected* value before writing: $$ \text{CAS}(\text{ptr},\; \text{expected},\; \text{new}) \;\rightarrow\; \begin{cases} \text{true, *ptr := new} & \text{if } *\text{ptr} = \text{expected} \\ \text{false, *ptr unchanged} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ CAS is the building block of **lock-free data structures** and is preferred on modern multi-core CPUs because it allows optimisations like back-off and version counters. ### 2.4 Types of Mutex Implementations | Type | Waiting Strategy | CPU Usage | Suitable For | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Spinlock (Busy-Wait Mutex)** | Loops testing the lock | **Wastes CPU cycles** | Very short critical sections, multi-core, kernel space | | **Blocking Mutex (Sleeping Mutex)** | Deschedules the thread via OS | **Releases CPU** | Longer critical sections, user space | | **Adaptive Mutex** | Spins briefly, then blocks | Hybrid | Mixed workloads | | **Recursive Mutex** | Same thread may lock multiple times | Tracks owner + count | Recursive functions calling locked routines | > [!NOTE] > POSIX names a recursive mutex `PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE`. Without it, a thread that tries to lock the same mutex twice **deadlocks** itself. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Property Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is examiner-favourite and must be memorised verbatim for KTU ESE.** | Property / Equation | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Mutex state space | $M(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ | Binary variable | | test_and_set effect | $\text{TAS}(M) = M_{\text{old}}$, $M \leftarrow 1$ | Atomic claim | | CAS effect | $\text{CAS}(M, 0, 1) = \text{true} \iff M = 0$ | Conditional claim | | Mutual exclusion condition | $\forall i \neq j: \big( \text{in\_CS}(T_i) \land \text{in\_CS}(T_j) \big) = \text{false}$ | Hard safety rule | | Bounded waiting bound | $\text{entries}(T_j) \le N$ before $T_i$ enters | Starvation-freedom | | Throughput upper bound | $\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{\text{CS}} + t_{\text{context\_switch}}}$ | Best case (no contention) | | Spinlock CPU waste | $W_{\text{CPU}} = n_{\text{spinners}} \times P_{\text{core}} \times t_{\text{wait}}$ | Energy / thermal cost | *(Note: `\land` is logical AND, `\iff` is "if and only if" — the vertical bar `|` is intentionally avoided in cell contents.)* ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Mutex locks are the *backbone of every multi-threaded application* in production: - **Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB)** — protect buffer-pool pages and B-tree node updates. - **Operating system kernels (Linux `futex`, Windows `ERESOURCE`)** — synchronise scheduler, VFS, and memory-management subsystems. - **Embedded RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks)** — coordinate ISR-to-task hand-offs in automotive ECUs. - **High-frequency trading systems** — use spinlock-based mutexes (often `pthread_spinlock_t`) to avoid syscall latency on hot paths. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: `acquire()` and `release()` using `test_and_set()` We now show, line-by-line, how the two atomic operations of a mutex are constructed from the hardware primitive `test_and_set()`. > [!NOTE] > This is the standard KTU 2024-Style derivation. You will be expected to write this (or the equivalent with `CAS`) in 14-mark answers. **Algorithm 1 — `acquire()` using `test_and_set()`** ```c void acquire(int *mutex) { while (test_and_set(mutex) == 1) { // spin / block here } // mutex is now 0, and we have set it to 1 } ``` **Step-by-step trace for two competing threads $T_1$ and $T_2$:** Let initial state: $M(0) = 0$ (unlocked). **Step 1** — $T_1$ calls `acquire()`. It executes `test_and_set(&M)`. This is **one atomic CPU instruction**: - Reads $M = 0$ (the *old* value). - Sets $M := 1$. - Returns $0$ to $T_1$. Because the returned value is $0$, the `while` loop condition `0 == 1` is **false**, so the loop exits. $T_1$ proceeds to the critical section. **Step 2** — $T_2$ (running on a different core) calls `acquire()`. It executes `test_and_set(&M)`: - Reads $M = 1$ (the *old* value, because $T_1$ just set it). - Sets $M := 1$ (no change). - Returns $1$ to $T_2$. Now the `while` condition `1 == 1` is **true**, so $T_2$ enters the loop body — it **spins** (or is descheduled, depending on the mutex type) and immediately retries `test_and_set`. **Step 3** — $T_1$ finishes the critical section and calls `release(&M)`. This is typically implemented as a **plain atomic store** (because no value to test): $$ M \xleftarrow{\text{atomic}} 0 $$ At this point, $T_2$'s next `test_and_set` returns $0$, exits the loop, and enters the critical section. **Algorithm 2 — `release()` using plain atomic store** ```c void release(int *mutex) { *mutex = 0; // atomic write enforced by hardware memory model } ``` **Correctness Argument (Sketch)** We can formalise the mutual-exclusion property as follows. Let $\text{IN\_CS}(T_i, k)$ be the predicate "thread $T_i$ is in the critical section at instant $k$." For any two distinct threads $T_i, T_j$ and any instant $k$: $$ \text{IN\_CS}(T_i, k) \land \text{IN\_CS}(T_j, k) = \text{false} $$ This holds because the only way to transition from $\text{outside} \rightarrow \text{inside}$ is to call `acquire()`, and `acquire()` can succeed **iff** `test_and_set` returned $0$, which happens **iff** the previous owner has called `release()` (i.e., $M = 0$). Since `test_and_set` is atomic, two threads cannot both observe $M = 0$ simultaneously. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Comparison: Mutex vs. Binary Semaphore > [!IMPORTANT] > This table is the single most-tested comparison in KTU ESE Module 2. | Attribute | **Mutex Lock** | **Binary Semaphore** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | State space | $M \in \{0, 1\}$ | $S \in \{0, 1\}$ | | **Ownership** | Yes — only owner can `unlock` | No — any thread can `signal()` | | Initialiser | Often default **unlocked** ($M = 0$) | Often initialised to **0** or **1** | | Waiting | Blocks (or spins) | Blocks on `wait()` | | Use case | Mutual exclusion (protect CS) | Either mutual exclusion **or** signalling between threads | | Standard type | `pthread_mutex_t` | `sem_t` (with `pshared = 0`) | | POSIX API | `pthread_mutex_lock` / `unlock` | `sem_wait` / `sem_post` | | Suitable for producer-consumer | Only partially (needs additional condition variable) | Yes — directly supports signalling semantics | | Priority inversion handling | Supports **priority inheritance protocol (PIP)** | Generally does not support PIP | ### 3.3 The Producer–Consumer Problem with Mutex + Condition Variable A common KTU question asks why a **mutex alone is insufficient** to solve the bounded-buffer producer-consumer problem. The answer: a mutex provides *mutual exclusion* but no *synchronisation* (the consumer does not know when the buffer becomes non-empty). The complete POSIX solution therefore uses **two components**: 1. A **mutex** for the buffer (mutual exclusion). 2. A **condition variable** for signalling (synchronisation). The pseudocode skeleton is: ```c pthread_mutex_t m = PTHREAD_MUTEX_INITIALIZER; pthread_cond_t c = PTHREAD_COND_INITIALIZER; int count = 0; // number of items in buffer const int N = 10; // buffer capacity void *producer(void *arg) { pthread_mutex_lock(&m); while (count == N) // full pthread_cond_wait(&c, &m); // releases m, blocks on c // ... produce item, append to buffer ... count++; pthread_cond_signal(&c); // wake one consumer pthread_mutex_unlock(&m); } void *consumer(void *arg) { pthread_mutex_lock(&m); while (count == 0) // empty pthread_cond_wait(&c, &m); // releases m, blocks on c // ... remove item from buffer ... count--; pthread_cond_signal(&c); // wake one producer pthread_mutex_unlock(&m); } ``` Notice the **critical observation**: `pthread_cond_wait` *atomically* releases the mutex and suspends the thread, then *re-acquires* the mutex before returning. Without this atomicity, a missed signal could deadlock the system. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Lock Variant > [!NOTE] > This is a KTU-favourite analytical question. Understand the **trade-off**, not just the names. | Workload Profile | Recommended Mutex Type | Justification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Critical section < 100 ns, multi-core | **Spinlock** | Context-switch cost (~1–10 µs) exceeds CS time | | Critical section > 1 µs, single-core or contended | **Blocking mutex** | Avoid wasting CPU; OS can schedule another thread | | Recursive call chain through locked routine | **Recursive mutex** | Same thread re-entry must be allowed | | Real-time system with strict deadlines | **Priority-inheritance mutex** | Prevents unbounded priority inversion | | Hybrid (unknown CS length) | **Adaptive mutex** | Linux `PTHREAD_MUTEX_ADAPTIVE_NP` style behaviour | ### 3.5 The Priority Inversion Problem (Advanced KTU Topic) Priority inversion is a **liveness hazard** that mutex locks can introduce in real-time systems. **The scenario with three threads:** - $T_H$ (high priority) — needs mutex $M$. - $T_M$ (medium priority) — does *not* need $M$. - $T_L$ (low priority) — holds $M$. **The bad sequence:** 1. $T_L$ acquires $M$ and enters its CS. 2. $T_H$ becomes runnable, preempts $T_L$, tries to lock $M$, **blocks** on $M$. 3. $T_M$ becomes runnable, preempts $T_L$ (which is still holding $M$), and runs to completion. 4. $T_L$ finally resumes, finishes CS, releases $M$. $T_H$ can now proceed. **Result:** $T_H$ waited not just for $T_L$ (expected) but also for *all of $T_M$* (unbounded). This is **unbounded priority inversion**, famously documented in the **Mars Pathfinder** (1997) spacecraft incident. **The solution — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** When a high-priority thread $T_H$ blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority thread $T_L$, the OS **temporarily raises** $T_L$'s effective priority to $\max(\text{priority}(T_L), \text{priority}(T_H))$ for the duration that $T_L$ holds the mutex. This prevents medium-priority threads from preempting $T_L$ and bounding the inversion length. > [!IMPORTANT] > POSIX supports PIP via the mutex attribute `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT` (set with `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol`). This is the answer expected for 14-mark KTU questions on this topic. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram: Mutex Lifecycle This diagram maps every valid state transition of a single mutex object across the actions of multiple threads. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Unlocked : init M := 0 Unlocked --> Locked : acquire() by thread T_i TAS returns 0 Locked --> Locked : acquire() by T_j blocks / spins TAS returns 1 Locked --> Unlocked : release() by T_i M := 0 Unlocked --> [*] : destroy Locked --> [*] : destroy forbidden only after release note right of Locked : Owner pointer = T_i note right of Unlocked : No owner wait queue may be non empty ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The `Locked` state has a self-loop representing **contention**. The `Unlocked` state's outgoing arrow is the *only* legal transition that releases ownership. ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mutex Lock Functional Architecture This is the **block-level functional architecture** of a mutex implementation, showing the interactions between hardware, OS kernel, and user threads. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Thread T1
application code] --> B[POSIX API Layer
pthread_mutex_lock] C[User Thread T2
application code] --> D[POSIX API Layer
pthread_mutex_lock] B --> E{Glibc Mutex Wrapper
fast path CAS on word} D --> E E --> F[Hardware Atomic Primitive
test_and_set OR compare_and_swap] F --> G[Kernel Wait Queue
struct futex_wait] G --> H[Scheduler
blocks T2 and resumes T1] H --> I[Critical Section
shared resource access] I --> J[POSIX API Layer
pthread_mutex_unlock] J --> K[Hardware Atomic Primitive
atomic store M := 0] K --> L[Kernel Wakeup
futex_wake one waiter] L --> C ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Modern Linux mutexes (since glibc 2.11+) use a **futex-based hybrid** — a *fast path* that uses a single `compare_and_swap` in user space (no syscall), and a *slow path* that calls into the kernel only when contention occurs. This is why uncontended mutex lock/unlock on Linux is **~20 ns**. ### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Producer–Consumer with Mutex + Condvar ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P as Producer Thread participant Mtx as Mutex m participant Buf as Bounded Buffer participant CV as Condvar c participant C as Consumer Thread P->>Mtx: pthread_mutex_lock activate Mtx P->>Buf: check count == N P->>CV: pthread_cond_wait releases m deactivate Mtx Note over CV: Consumer arrives C->>Mtx: pthread_mutex_lock activate Mtx C->>Buf: remove item count minus minus C->>CV: pthread_cond_signal C->>Mtx: pthread_mutex_unlock deactivate Mtx Note over P: woken reacquires m atomically P->>Buf: append item count plus plus P->>CV: pthread_cond_signal P->>Mtx: pthread_mutex_unlock ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Normal["Normal Priority State"] L_norm[Low Priority Thread T_L
priority = 1] M_norm[Medium Priority Thread T_M
priority = 5] H_norm[High Priority Thread T_H
priority = 9] end subgraph Inverted["Priority Inversion Scenario"] L_inv[T_L holds mutex M
T_H blocked on M] M_inv[T_M preempts T_L
delays release] end subgraph PIP_Fix["PIP Solution"] L_pip[T_L boosted to priority 9
matches T_H] M_block[T_M cannot preempt
scheduled after T_L] end Normal --> Inverted --> PIP_Fix L_pip --> Finish[Release M T_L drops to priority 1 T_H proceeds] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]** **Q1. Differentiate between a mutex lock and a binary semaphore. Which one is preferred for protecting a critical section and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A mutex lock and a binary semaphore both have a binary state $\{0, 1\}$, but differ in the **ownership** property. - **Mutex lock** has *ownership*: only the thread that performed the `lock()` operation is allowed to perform the corresponding `unlock()`. This prevents accidental or malicious unlocking by another thread. - **Binary semaphore** has *no ownership*: any thread can perform `signal()` (V operation) regardless of who performed `wait()` (P operation). - **Critical section protection** ⇒ **Mutex lock is preferred**, because the ownership constraint makes it safer and avoids scenarios where a thread unlocks a resource it does not logically "own", which can lead to race conditions and priority inversions. *(1 mark for state, 1 mark for ownership, 1 mark for justification.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]** **Q2. Why is `test_and_set()` instruction needed for implementing mutex locks? What would happen if we used a simple non-atomic `if (lock == 0) lock = 1;` check?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `test_and_set()` instruction is needed because **checking** the lock value and **setting** it must be a **single, indivisible** (atomic) operation. If these two steps are separated: ```c if (lock == 0) // Step 1: read lock = 1; // Step 2: write ``` then between Step 1 and Step 2, a context switch can occur, and another thread can read the same value $0$, set it to $1$, and both threads would enter the critical section — **violating mutual exclusion**. `test_and_set()` executes the read and write as one hardware-atomic bus-locked instruction (e.g., `xchg` on x86, `ldaxr/stlxr` on ARM), preventing the race entirely. *(3 marks — 1 for problem statement, 1 for race explanation, 1 for atomic solution.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common error 1:** Students write `acquire` and `release` as separate *non-atomic* statements ⇒ loses 3–4 marks. Always emphasise **atomicity**. > - **Common error 2:** Confusing **binary semaphore** with **mutex** in the ownership context ⇒ loses 2 marks. > - **Common error 3:** Forgetting to mention **busy-waiting vs. blocking** distinction in `acquire()` ⇒ loses 1–2 marks. > - **Common error 4:** Writing a producer-consumer solution using **only a mutex** (no condition variable) — this is a *famous* KTU trap question; the correct answer must highlight the need for a condition variable. > - **Common error 5:** Skipping the **hardware support** angle (test-and-set / CAS) in derivation questions ⇒ loses 3 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **(a)** With the help of the `test_and_set()` instruction, explain how **mutual exclusion** is achieved using a mutex lock. Provide the pseudocode for `acquire()` and `release()` and show a trace for two processes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **Priority Inversion problem** in real-time systems using mutexes. Explain the **Priority Inheritance Protocol** with a suitable example, and show how it solves the Mars-Pathfinder-style scenario. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Mutual exclusion using `test_and_set()` — 7 marks** The `test_and_set()` instruction atomically reads a memory location and writes a new value, returning the old value. It is implemented in hardware as a single bus-locked instruction (e.g., `XCHG` on x86, or `LDAXR/STLXR` pair on ARM) and therefore cannot be interrupted. **Pseudocode (1 mark):** ```c int test_and_set(int *target) { int old = *target; // atomic *target = 1; // atomic (single instruction) return old; } void acquire(int *lock) { while (test_and_set(lock) == 1) ; // spin or block } void release(int *lock) { *lock = 0; // atomic store } ``` **Trace for two processes $P_0$ and $P_1$ (4 marks):** | Time | Event | State of $M$ | `test_and_set` return | Who is in CS? | | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $t_0$ | Initial state | $0$ | — | None | | $t_1$ | $P_0$ calls `acquire()` | $0 \to 1$ | $0$ | $P_0$ enters | | $t_2$ | $P_1$ calls `acquire()` | $1$ (no change) | $1$ | $P_0$ still; $P_1$ spins | | $t_3$ | $P_0$ calls `release()` | $1 \to 0$ | — | $P_0$ exits | | $t_4$ | $P_1$'s next `test_and_set` | $0 \to 1$ | $0$ | $P_1$ enters | **Correctness argument (2 marks):** Mutual exclusion holds because the only path from $M=0$ to $M=1$ passes through `test_and_set`, which is atomic. Two threads cannot both observe $M=0$ simultaneously. --- **(b) Priority Inversion and Priority Inheritance Protocol — 7 marks** **Problem definition (2 marks):** Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority thread $T_H$ is forced to wait for a low-priority thread $T_L$ that holds a shared mutex, *and* a medium-priority thread $T_M$ preempts $T_L$, indefinitely delaying $T_L$'s release of the mutex. The result is that $T_H$'s effective wait time becomes **unbounded**, violating real-time deadlines. **Classic example (Mars Pathfinder, 1997):** The spacecraft experienced system resets because a high-priority meteorological task was blocked on a shared bus mutex held by a low-priority task, while medium-priority tasks ran to completion. The fix applied was the **Priority Inheritance Protocol**. **PIP description (3 marks):** When a high-priority thread $T_H$ blocks on a mutex $M$ held by a lower-priority thread $T_L$, the OS dynamically raises the priority of $T_L$ to the priority of $T_H$ (or to the maximum priority of all threads waiting on $M$) for the entire duration that $T_L$ holds $M$. When $T_L$ releases $M$, its priority is restored to its original value. **PIP trace with $T_H > T_M > T_L$ in priority:** 1. $T_L$ acquires $M$, enters CS. 2. $T_H$ arrives, preempts $T_L$, tries `lock(M)`, **blocks**. 3. OS raises $T_L$'s priority to $\text{priority}(T_H)$. 4. $T_M$ arrives, but **cannot** preempt $T_L$ anymore (priority lower or equal). 5. $T_L$ finishes CS, releases $M$, priority restored. 6. $T_H$ immediately acquires $M$ and proceeds. **Conclusion (2 marks):** PIP bounds the inversion length to **one CS execution time of $T_L$**, making the system predictable and avoiding the unbounded delay seen in Pathfinder. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]* **(a)** Explain the producer–consumer (bounded buffer) problem. Why is a **mutex alone insufficient** to solve it? Provide a complete POSIX solution using **mutex + condition variable**, and explain the role of `pthread_cond_wait`. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **blocking mutex**, **spinlock**, **recursive mutex**, and **adaptive mutex**. For each, state a specific scenario from the Linux kernel or a real-time system where it is the appropriate choice. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Producer–Consumer and Mutex + Condvar — 7 marks** **Problem (1 mark):** Multiple producer threads generate data items and append them to a shared buffer of fixed capacity $N$. Multiple consumer threads remove and process them. The buffer access must be mutually exclusive, and producers must block when the buffer is full, while consumers must block when the buffer is empty. **Why mutex alone is insufficient (2 marks):** A mutex provides only *mutual exclusion* (atomicity of buffer access). It does **not** provide *synchronisation* (the ability for a consumer to be notified that an item has been produced, or for a producer to be notified that a slot is free). Without a signalling mechanism, a consumer that finds the buffer empty would have to *busy-wait* (wasting CPU) or block *without* ever being woken (deadlock). **Complete solution (3 marks):** (Refer to Section 3.3 above for the full code. Briefly recap the key calls: `pthread_mutex_lock`, `pthread_cond_wait`, `pthread_cond_signal`, `pthread_mutex_unlock`.) **Role of `pthread_cond_wait` (1 mark):** This function performs an **atomic** release of the mutex and suspends the calling thread on the condition variable. When the thread is later woken via `pthread_cond_signal` (or `broadcast`), it **re-acquires the mutex** before returning. This atomic release-block-reacquire sequence is essential to avoid a missed-signal race. --- **(b) Comparison of Mutex Variants — 7 marks** | Variant | Mechanism | Linux Kernel / RTOS Use Case | Trade-off | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Blocking mutex** | Deschedules contending thread via OS scheduler | `inode->i_mutex` protecting VFS inode fields | Latency on entry; frees CPU | | **Spinlock** | Tightly loops on `test_and_set` | `spinlock_t` protecting run-queue, scheduler data on multi-core | Wastes CPU; no syscall overhead | | **Recursive mutex** | Allows same thread to lock N times; tracked via counter | Used in `drm_lock` style scenarios where driver calls may be nested | Slight overhead; prevents self-deadlock | | **Adaptive mutex** | Spins briefly, then blocks if no progress | Linux `pthread_mutex_t` with `PTHREAD_MUTEX_ADAPTIVE_NP`; FreeRTOS `xSemaphoreCreateMutex` with priority inheritance | Tunable for mixed workloads | (Allocate ~1.5 marks per variant, with 1 mark for the trade-off summary.) **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The choice of mutex variant depends on **critical section duration**, **contention level**, **system criticality**, and **hardware support** (multi-core vs. single-core). No single variant is universally best. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mutex Locks (Module 2)** - **Definition:** A mutex is a binary synchronisation primitive ($M \in \{0, 1\}$) with **ownership** semantics, used to enforce mutual exclusion on a critical section. - **Two operations:** `acquire()` and `release()`. Only the owner can `release()`. - **Hardware support mandatory:** Implementation requires atomic instructions — `test_and_set()` (TAS) or `compare_and_swap()` (CAS). - **State transition:** $M = 0 \xrightarrow{\text{acquire}} M = 1 \xrightarrow{\text{release}} M = 0$. - **Three CSP requirements solved:** Mutual Exclusion (definitively), Progress (depends on waiting strategy), Bounded Waiting (depends on waiting strategy). - **Spinlock vs. Blocking Mutex:** Spinlock burns CPU but has no syscall overhead — use for *short* CS in *multi-core* systems. Blocking mutex sleeps the thread — use for *longer* CS or *single-core* systems. - **Recursive mutex:** Same thread can lock multiple times. Counter tracks depth. Mandatory for recursive code. - **Mutex vs. Binary Semaphore:** Mutex has *ownership*; semaphore does *not*. Use mutex for CS protection, semaphore for signalling. - **Producer–Consumer needs both:** Mutex (for buffer atomicity) **+** Condition variable (for signalling full/empty). - **Priority Inversion:** High-priority thread blocked behind low-priority mutex holder, with medium-priority threads preempting. - **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** OS boosts the priority of the mutex-holding thread to the highest waiting thread's priority. Bounded inversion length. - **POSIX API:** `pthread_mutex_t`, `pthread_mutex_init`, `pthread_mutex_lock`, `pthread_mutex_unlock`, `pthread_mutex_destroy`. PIP via `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(attr, PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT)`. - **Linux futex:** Modern glibc mutexes use a fast-path CAS in user space and only enter the kernel on contention — gives ~20 ns uncontended latency. - **Examiner favourites:** (i) Trace table with TAS, (ii) Mutex vs. semaphore comparison, (iii) Producer-consumer with condvar, (iv) Priority inversion + PIP. --- > **End of Module 2 — Mutex Locks Notes** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education guidelines.*

topics.title: Semaphores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semaphores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Semaphore** is a non-negative integer synchronization variable, proposed by Dutch computer scientist **Edsger W. Dijkstra** in **1965**, that is accessed only through two standard, atomic (indivisible) operations known as **Wait** (also called **P** or `proberen` meaning "to test") and **Signal** (also called **V** or `verhogen` meaning "to increment"). Semaphores provide a robust mechanism for solving the **Critical Section Problem** and for enforcing process synchronization without the drawbacks of busy-waiting at the application level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A semaphore is essentially an **integer variable accessed via two atomic operations only**. KTU examiners often give full marks only when students explicitly mention the *atomicity* of the P and V operations.

## 1.2 Types of Semaphores

There are two fundamental types of semaphores that every KTU student must commit to memory:

| Type | Value Range | Primary Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Binary Semaphore (Mutex)** | Only **0** or **1** | Mutual Exclusion (locking a single resource) |
| **Counting Semaphore** | Any non-negative integer **N** | Controlling access to a finite pool of **N** identical resources |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Counting Semaphore** initialized to **1 behaves exactly like a Binary Semaphore**. However, a true binary semaphore enforces the value range $\in \{0, 1\}$ strictly, whereas a counting semaphore can take any value $\geq 0$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### Analogy 1: The Bathroom Key System 🚻
Imagine a public washroom with **3 identical stalls** (counting semaphore = 3). Hanging outside is a key-rack:
- A person arriving must **take a key (P operation)**. If keys are available (value $\geq 1$), they decrement the counter and enter.
- If no keys are available (value = 0), they must **wait** outside (blocked state).
- A person leaving must **return the key (V operation)**, incrementing the counter. This may wake up a waiting person.

### Analogy 2: Toll Plaza with Limited Lanes 🛣️
Consider a toll booth with **N lanes** open. Each arriving vehicle must pass through a counter. If all lanes are occupied, vehicles queue up. As each vehicle exits, the counter increments, allowing the next waiting vehicle to pass.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever you write a semaphore definition in the KTU answer script, ALWAYS draw a small box showing the value and label the two arrows as **P** and **V**. This visual cue fetches extra marks from the evaluator.

## 1.4 Key Operational Properties

- **Atomicity:** The P and V operations are *uninterruptible*. On a uniprocessor system, they can be implemented by disabling interrupts. On a multiprocessor, hardware support (atomic instructions like `TestAndSet`) is required.
- **No Busy Waiting (Ideal):** In a properly designed OS, blocked processes are moved to a **waiting queue** associated with the semaphore, freeing the CPU.
- **Independence from Process Count:** Semaphores are OS-managed kernel variables — processes do not own them, they merely manipulate them.
- **Implicit Blocking:** A process performing a P operation on a zero-valued semaphore is **automatically blocked** without any explicit check.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Atomic Operations — Formal Semantics

### 2.1.1 The Wait (P / `wait()`) Operation
The **P** operation (or `proberen` / `wait()` / `down()`) executes the following sequence atomically:

- **Step 1:** The semaphore value `S` is **decremented by 1**.
- **Step 2:** If the new value of `S` becomes **negative** (or in some formulations, *less than 0*), the executing process is **blocked** and added to the semaphore's waiting queue.
- **Step 3:** Otherwise, the process is permitted to proceed.

### 2.1.2 The Signal (V / `signal()`) Operation
The **V** operation (or `verhogen` / `signal()` / `up()`) executes the following sequence atomically:

- **Step 1:** The semaphore value `S` is **incremented by 1**.
- **Step 2:** If the new value of `S` becomes **less than or equal to 0** (meaning at least one process is waiting), then **one blocked process is removed** from the waiting queue and marked as *ready*.
- **Step 3:** The signalling process continues execution immediately.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design

> **Why the negative value convention?**
> The classic Dijkstra formulation used a *non-negative* initial value, and the P operation would block if the result was **negative**. The negation of the value represents the **number of processes waiting** on the semaphore. This elegant property allows the kernel to know exactly how many processes to wake up during cascading signals.

> **Why atomicity?**
> If two processes simultaneously executed `S = S - 1` without atomicity, both could read the same value, both could decrement, and only one decrement would effectively occur — a classic **race condition**. Atomicity is non-negotiable in semaphore design.

## 2.3 Critical Section Protection with a Semaphore

To protect a critical section, we use a **Binary Semaphore `mutex`** initialized to **1**:

$$S_{mutex} = 1$$

The process structure becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Entry Protocol:} \quad P(mutex) \\
&\text{Critical Section: Access shared resource} \\
&\text{Exit Protocol:} \quad V(mutex) \\
&\text{Remainder Section: Continue execution}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **examination gold**. Memorize it verbatim for the **Process Synchronization** module. In KTU answer scripts, a well-formatted table is worth 1–2 extra marks.

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Expression | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Semaphore Initialization** | $S \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N\}$ | Set to the number of free resources |
| **P Operation Result** | $S \leftarrow S - 1$ | Decrement, then test for blocking |
| **V Operation Result** | $S \leftarrow S + 1$ | Increment, then test for wakeup |
| **Blocking Condition (Dijkstra)** | $S < 0 \implies$ block process | Classical formulation |
| **Blocking Condition (Common)** | $S < 0 \implies$ block process | Same as above |
| **Number of Waiting Processes** | $\vert S \vert$ | Magnitude of negative value |
| **Mutual Exclusion Guarantee** | $S_{mutex} = 1$ | Only one process enters at a time |
| **Resource Pool Size** | $S_{count} = N$ | Allows N concurrent accesses |
| **Deadlock Condition (Circular Wait)** | $\exists \; p_i, p_j : $ waiting on each other indefinitely | Violation of circular wait |
| **Priority Inversion** | High-priority task blocked by lower-priority task | Solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol** |
| **Starvation Condition** | Process waits indefinitely in the queue | Avoided by **FIFO queue** discipline |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the pipe character `|` inside markdown tables. This prevents the table from breaking visually.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Concurrency Control:** Semaphores govern simultaneous read/write locks in DBMS engines.
- **Embedded RTOS (VxWorks, FreeRTOS):** Binary semaphores synchronize ISR-to-task communication.
- **Multithreaded Web Servers:** Counting semaphores limit the number of concurrent database connections.
- **Industrial Automation (PLC Systems):** Semaphores coordinate robotic arm access to shared conveyor belts.
- **Linux Kernel Internals:** The `down()` and `up()` functions on `struct semaphore` protect kernel data structures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Pseudocode Models & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Classical Pseudocode of Semaphore Operations

### 3.1.1 `wait(S)` — The P Operation

```
wait(Semaphore S):
    S.value = S.value - 1
    if (S.value < 0):
        block this process
        enqueue it onto S.waiting_queue
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Line-by-Line Explanation:**
> - **Line 1:** The semaphore value is atomically decremented. This single line is the *heart* of the P operation.
> - **Line 2:** The *test* part. If the value has become negative, it means more processes requested access than the resource allows.
> - **Line 3:** The calling process is suspended by the OS scheduler.
> - **Line 4:** The process descriptor is added to the FIFO waiting queue attached to the semaphore.

### 3.1.2 `signal(S)` — The V Operation

```
signal(Semaphore S):
    S.value = S.value + 1
    if (S.value <= 0):
        dequeue a process P from S.waiting_queue
        wakeup(P)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Line-by-Line Explanation:**
> - **Line 1:** The semaphore value is atomically incremented.
> - **Line 2:** The wakeup test. A non-positive value means at least one process is currently waiting.
> - **Line 3:** Remove one process from the semaphore's waiting queue (FIFO order is standard).
> - **Line 4:** Transition the process state from *Blocked* to *Ready*, making it eligible for CPU scheduling.

## 3.2 Implementation: Disabling Interrupts (Uniprocessor)

The atomicity of P and V is enforced by temporarily disabling hardware interrupts:

```
wait(S):
    disable_interrupts()
    S.value = S.value - 1
    if (S.value < 0):
        enqueue_and_block()
    enable_interrupts()
```

**Why this works:** On a uniprocessor, only one process can be executing at a time. By disabling interrupts, no context switch can occur mid-operation, making the decrement-and-test effectively atomic.

> [!WARNING]
> This technique **does not work on multiprocessors**. Modern systems use atomic hardware instructions like `compare_and_swap()` or `test_and_set()` for synchronization across cores.

## 3.3 Solving the Producer–Consumer (Bounded Buffer) Problem

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup
- **Producer:** Generates data items and places them into a shared buffer of size **N**.
- **Consumer:** Removes data items from the buffer and processes them.
- **Constraint:** The buffer has finite capacity **N** (bounded). Producer must block when buffer is full. Consumer must block when buffer is empty.

### 3.3.2 Semaphore Declaration and Initialization

We need **three semaphores**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&mutex \; = \; 1 \quad &&\text{(for buffer access mutual exclusion)} \\
&empty \; = \; N \quad &&\text{(counts empty slots in the buffer)} \\
&full \; = \; 0 \quad &&\text{(counts filled slots in the buffer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.3 Full Solution Code Structure

**Producer Process:**
```
Producer:
    while (true):
        produce_item(&item)
        wait(empty)              // Wait for an empty slot
        wait(mutex)              // Lock the buffer
        buffer[in] = item        // Place item in buffer
        in = (in + 1) % N        // Update circular index
        signal(mutex)            // Unlock the buffer
        signal(full)             // Indicate a new full slot
```

**Consumer Process:**
```
Consumer:
    while (true):
        wait(full)               // Wait for a filled slot
        wait(mutex)              // Lock the buffer
        item = buffer[out]       // Remove item from buffer
        out = (out + 1) % N      // Update circular index
        signal(mutex)            // Unlock the buffer
        signal(empty)            // Indicate a new empty slot
        consume_item(item)       // Process the item
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of `wait()` calls is critical in KTU exams.** The order `wait(empty)` then `wait(mutex)` is **deadlock-free** in the standard formulation. Reversing them (i.e., `wait(mutex)` first) is also deadlock-free here, but for *other* problems like the Dining Philosophers, the order matters enormously. The examiner will check this ordering.

### 3.3.4 Logical Verification

At any time, the following invariant **must hold** for the bounded buffer:
