$$\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$$

This can be proved by induction: the initial state has $\text{empty} = N$ and $\text{full} = 0$, satisfying the relation. Every producer iteration increments `full` by 1 and decrements `empty` by 1; every consumer iteration does the opposite. Hence the sum remains $N$ forever.

## 3.4 Solving the Dining Philosophers Problem (Dijkstra, 1965)

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement
- **5 philosophers** sit around a circular table.
- Between each pair of adjacent philosophers is **1 chopstick** (5 chopsticks total).
- Each philosopher alternates between **Thinking** and **Eating**.
- To eat, a philosopher must acquire **both** the left and right chopsticks.
- A chopstick can be held by only one philosopher at a time.

### 3.4.2 Semaphore Declaration

$$
\begin{aligned}
&chopstick[0..4] \; = \; \{1, 1, 1, 1, 1\} \quad &&\text{(One per chopstick)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.3 Naive Solution (and Why It Fails)

```
Philosopher i:
    while (true):
        wait(chopstick[i])
        wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])
        eat()
        signal(chopstick[i])
        signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])
        think()
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Deadlock Scenario:** If all 5 philosophers simultaneously execute `wait(chopstick[i])` and pick up their *left* chopstick, then ALL `chopstick[i]` values become 0. When each tries `wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])`, they block. **Circular wait = Deadlock.**

### 3.4.4 Deadlock-Free Solution

Break the circular symmetry by having **one specific philosopher (say, the 4th, index 4)** pick up chopsticks in **reverse order**:

```
Philosopher i (for i = 0, 1, 2, 3):
    wait(chopstick[i])
    wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])
    eat()
    signal(chopstick[i])
    signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])

Philosopher 4 (special):
    wait(chopstick[0])              // Right chopstick first
    wait(chopstick[4])              // Left chopstick second
    eat()
    signal(chopstick[0])
    signal(chopstick[4])
```

**Logical Proof of Deadlock-Freeness:**

By having philosopher 4 reach for chopstick[0] *first*, we break the circular chain. There can never be a cycle where every chopstick is held by a different philosopher waiting for another. At most 4 philosophers can be holding chopsticks simultaneously, leaving at least one chopstick free for the 5th philosopher to eventually eat.

## 3.5 The Readers–Writers Problem

### 3.5.1 Problem Statement
A shared database must allow **multiple concurrent readers** (since reading is non-destructive) but **only ONE writer at a time**, and a writer must have **exclusive access** (no readers allowed while writing).

### 3.5.2 Semaphores and Variables Used

$$
\begin{aligned}
&mutex \; = \; 1 \quad &&\text{(Protects readcount variable)} \\
&wrt \; = \; 1 \quad &&\text{(Ensures exclusive writer access)} \\
&readcount \; = \; 0 \quad &&\text{(Tracks active readers)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5.3 The Solution

**Reader Process:**
```
Reader:
    wait(mutex)
    readcount = readcount + 1
    if (readcount == 1):
        wait(wrt)                 // First reader blocks writers
    signal(mutex)
    // ---- Critical Section: Reading ----
    perform_reading()
    // ---- End Critical Section ----
    wait(mutex)
    readcount = readcount - 1
    if (readcount == 0):
        signal(wrt)               // Last reader unblocks writers
    signal(mutex)
```

**Writer Process:**
```
Writer:
    wait(wrt)
    // ---- Critical Section: Writing ----
    perform_writing()
    // ---- End Critical Section ----
    signal(wrt)
```

> [!NOTE]
> This solution **favors readers**: if readers keep arriving, a waiting writer can starve indefinitely. A "writer-preference" version reverses this by having writers block new readers upon arrival.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Lifecycle of a Semaphore Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Process Executes wait P on Semaphore S] --> nodeB[Atomically Decrement S by 1]
    nodeB --> nodeC{New value of S less than 0?}
    nodeC -->|No, S greater or equal 0| nodeD[Process Enters Critical Section]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Execute Protected Code]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Process Executes signal V on Semaphore S]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Atomically Increment S by 1]
    nodeG --> nodeH{New value of S less than or equal 0?}
    nodeH -->|No| nodeI[Continue Normal Execution]
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeJ[Dequeue One Process from Waiting Queue]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Transition Process to Ready State]
    nodeK --> nodeI
    nodeC -->|Yes, S less than 0| nodeL[Block Current Process]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Enqueue Process onto S Waiting Queue]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Place Process in Blocked State]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Producer–Consumer Synchronization with Semaphores

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Producer Thread] --> nodeA1[wait empty]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[wait mutex]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Insert Item into Buffer]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[signal mutex]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[signal full]
    nodeA5 --> nodeP
    nodeC[Consumer Thread] --> nodeB1[wait full]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[wait mutex]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Remove Item from Buffer]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4[signal mutex]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5[signal empty]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6[Consume Item]
    nodeB6 --> nodeC
    nodeA3 -.-> nodeShared[(Shared Circular Buffer of Size N)]
    nodeB3 -.-> nodeShared
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Dining Philosophers Chopstick Access

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP0[Philosopher 0] -.->|waits on| nodeCS0[Chopstick 0]
    nodeP0 -.->|waits on| nodeCS4[Chopstick 4]
    nodeP1[Philosopher 1] -.->|waits on| nodeCS0
    nodeP1 -.->|waits on| nodeCS1[Chopstick 1]
    nodeP2[Philosopher 2] -.->|waits on| nodeCS1
    nodeP2 -.->|waits on| nodeCS2[Chopstick 2]
    nodeP3[Philosopher 3] -.->|waits on| nodeCS2
    nodeP3 -.->|waits on| nodeCS3[Chopstick 3]
    nodeP4[Philosopher 4] -.->|waits on| nodeCS3
    nodeP4 -.->|waits on| nodeCS4
    nodeCS0[Chopstick 0 Binary Semaphore]:::sem
    nodeCS1[Chopstick 1 Binary Semaphore]:::sem
    nodeCS2[Chopstick 2 Binary Semaphore]:::sem
    nodeCS3[Chopstick 3 Binary Semaphore]:::sem
    nodeCS4[Chopstick 4 Binary Semaphore]:::sem
    classDef sem fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Semaphore Implementation via Test-and-Set

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeT1[Hardware Level: Atomic Test and Set Instruction] --> nodeT2[Disables Interrupts on Uniprocessor]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Executes P Operation Code]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4{Semaphore value check}
    nodeT4 -->|Negative| nodeT5[Add Process to Kernel Waiting Queue]
    nodeT4 -->|Non-negative| nodeT6[Return to User Process]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT7[Scheduler Picks Next Ready Process]
    nodeT7 --> nodeT8[Re-enable Interrupts]
    nodeT8 --> nodeT6
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 marks / 3 marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a semaphore. With the help of neat diagrams, explain the working of `wait()` and `signal()` operations on a semaphore.**

**Model Answer:**

A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer synchronization variable that can be accessed and modified only through two standard atomic operations, namely `wait()` (P) and `signal()` (V). It was proposed by Edsger W. Dijkstra in 1965 to solve the critical section problem and enforce process synchronization in multiprogrammed systems.

**Working of `wait(S)`:**
- Decrement the semaphore value S by 1 atomically: $S \leftarrow S - 1$.
- If the new value of S is less than 0, then block the calling process and enqueue it onto the semaphore's waiting queue.

**Working of `signal(S)`:**
- Increment the semaphore value S by 1 atomically: $S \leftarrow S + 1$.
- If the new value of S is less than or equal to 0, dequeue one process from the waiting queue and place it in the ready queue.

A diagram showing the decrement-test block-decision flow for `wait()` and the increment-test wakeup-decision flow for `signal()` should accompany this answer.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Binary Semaphore and Counting Semaphore.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Value Range** | Only 0 or 1 | Any non-negative integer N |
| **Also Known As** | Mutex lock | Resource-count semaphore |
| **Initialization** | Usually 1 | Set to the number of available resources |
| **Use Case** | Mutual exclusion of a single resource | Controlling access to N identical resources |
| **Special Note** | Enforces strict $\{0, 1\}$ | No upper bound, but typically $\leq N$ |
| **Example** | Locking a single printer | 5 parking slots in a parking lot |

> [!TIP]
> Always present comparison answers in a **tabular format**. KTU examiners reward organized presentation, often awarding the full 3 marks even if a single line of explanation is incomplete.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Module 2 questions typically follow a 14-mark pattern with internal choice. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)
**Q: What is a semaphore? Explain the Producer–Consumer (Bounded Buffer) problem. Write the semaphore-based solution with proper initialization of semaphores.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A semaphore is a non-negative integer variable used for process synchronization, accessed only through two atomic operations, `wait(S)` (also called P) and `signal(S)` (also called V). It was proposed by E.W. Dijkstra in 1965 to handle critical sections without busy-waiting at the user level.

**Problem Explanation (2 marks):**
The Producer–Consumer problem (also called the Bounded Buffer problem) involves one or more producer processes that generate data and place it into a shared buffer of fixed size N, and one or more consumer processes that remove data from the buffer and process it. The key synchronization requirements are:
- A producer must wait if the buffer is full.
- A consumer must wait if the buffer is empty.
- Buffer access must be mutually exclusive to prevent race conditions.

**Semaphore Initialization (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&mutex = 1 \quad &&\text{(binary semaphore for buffer access)} \\
&empty = N \quad &&\text{(counting semaphore for empty slots)} \\
&full = 0 \quad &&\text{(counting semaphore for full slots)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution Code (2 marks):**

**Producer Process:**
```
Producer:
    while (true):
        produce_item(&item)
        wait(empty)
        wait(mutex)
        buffer[in] = item
        in = (in + 1) % N
        signal(mutex)
        signal(full)
```

**Consumer Process:**
```
Consumer:
    while (true):
        wait(full)
        wait(mutex)
        item = buffer[out]
        out = (out + 1) % N
        signal(mutex)
        signal(empty)
        consume_item(item)
```

The invariant $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$ is maintained throughout the execution, ensuring correctness.

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)
**Q: Explain the Readers–Writers problem. Provide a semaphore-based solution. Mention the starvation issue and how it can be addressed.**

**Model Solution:**

**Problem Statement (1.5 marks):**
The Readers–Writers problem occurs when a shared resource (e.g., a database or file) is accessed concurrently by multiple processes. Multiple readers can access the resource simultaneously since reading does not modify the data. However, a writer requires **exclusive access** — no other reader or writer may access the resource while a write is in progress.

**Semaphores and Variable Used (0.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&mutex = 1 \\
&wrt = 1 \\
&readcount = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution Code (4 marks):**

**Writer Process:**
```
Writer:
    wait(wrt)
    // Writing performed
    signal(wrt)
```

**Reader Process:**
```
Reader:
    wait(mutex)
    readcount = readcount + 1
    if (readcount == 1):
        wait(wrt)
    signal(mutex)
    // Reading performed
    wait(mutex)
    readcount = readcount - 1
    if (readcount == 0):
        signal(wrt)
    signal(mutex)
```

**Starvation Issue and Solution (1 mark):**
In the above solution, if readers arrive continuously, a waiting writer may starve indefinitely. To resolve this:
- Use a **FIFO queue** for both readers and writers.
- Implement a **writer-preference policy** by having writers block new incoming readers using an additional `turnstile` semaphore.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the implementation of semaphores. How is atomicity guaranteed on a uniprocessor and on a multiprocessor?**

**Model Solution:**

**Implementation of Semaphores (3 marks):**
A semaphore can be implemented as a kernel data structure containing:
- An integer value `S.value`
- A list of process control blocks (PCBs) of processes currently blocked on the semaphore, i.e., `S.waiting_queue`

The structure in pseudocode:
```
typedef struct {
    int value;
    struct process *waiting_queue;
} Semaphore;
```

**Implementation of `wait(S)`:**
```
wait(S):
    S.value = S.value - 1
    if (S.value < 0):
        add this process to S.waiting_queue
        block()
```

**Implementation of `signal(S)`:**
```
signal(S):
    S.value = S.value + 1
    if (S.value <= 0):
        remove a process P from S.waiting_queue
        wakeup(P)
```

**Atomicity on a Uniprocessor (2 marks):**
On a uniprocessor, the simplest way to make `wait()` and `signal()` atomic is to **disable hardware interrupts** during the execution of these operations. This prevents a context switch from occurring in the middle of the operation. Since no other process can run on a single CPU during the disabled-interrupt window, the read-modify-write sequence becomes effectively atomic.

```
wait(S):
    disable_interrupts()
    S.value = S.value - 1
    if (S.value < 0):
        block_and_enqueue()
    enable_interrupts()
```

**Atomicity on a Multiprocessor (2 marks):**
On a multiprocessor (or multicore) system, disabling interrupts on one CPU does not prevent another CPU from accessing the semaphore. Hence, **atomic hardware instructions** are required. Common primitives include:
- `test_and_set()` — sets a memory location to 1 and returns its old value atomically.
- `compare_and_swap()` — atomically compares and swaps two values.
- `fetch_and_add()` — atomically reads and increments a memory location.

These are supported by most modern instruction set architectures (x86, ARM, RISC-V) and form the foundation of lock-free synchronization.

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)
**Q: Explain the Dining Philosophers problem. Why does the naive solution lead to deadlock? Provide a deadlock-free solution and prove its correctness.**

**Model Solution:**

**Problem Description (2 marks):**
The Dining Philosophers problem, formulated by E.W. Dijkstra in 1965, models resource contention in a concurrent system. Five philosophers sit around a circular table. Between each pair of adjacent philosophers lies one chopstick. There are exactly 5 chopsticks in total. Each philosopher alternates between **thinking** and **eating**. To eat, a philosopher must acquire both adjacent chopsticks. A chopstick can be held by only one philosopher at a time. The challenge is to design a protocol that allows all philosophers to eat without deadlock or starvation.

**Naive Solution and Why It Fails (2 marks):**
Each chopstick is modeled as a binary semaphore initialized to 1:

$$
chopstick[i] = 1, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

The naive solution has philosopher $i$ perform:
```
wait(chopstick[i])
wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])
eat()
signal(chopstick[i])
signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])
```

**Deadlock Scenario:** If all 5 philosophers simultaneously become hungry and each successfully performs `wait(chopstick[i])` (picking up their left chopstick), then every chopstick semaphore becomes 0. Now, when each philosopher attempts `wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])`, they all block. This creates a **circular wait** — the four Coffman conditions for deadlock are satisfied, and the system is deadlocked.

**Deadlock-Free Solution (2 marks):**
Break the circular symmetry by making the **last philosopher (index 4)** pick up chopsticks in **reverse order**:
```
Philosopher i (for i = 0, 1, 2, 3):
    wait(chopstick[i])
    wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])
    eat()
    signal(chopstick[i])
    signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5])
    think()

Philosopher 4 (special):
    wait(chopstick[0])        // Right chopstick first
    wait(chopstick[4])        // Left chopstick second
    eat()
    signal(chopstick[0])
    signal(chopstick[4])
    think()
```

**Correctness Proof (1 mark):**
With this change, it is impossible for all 5 philosophers to hold exactly one chopstick while waiting for another. Suppose philosophers 0, 1, 2, 3 all hold their left chopstick. Philosopher 4 will then attempt to acquire chopstick[0] (right first) — but it is held by philosopher 0, so philosopher 4 blocks **without** holding chopstick[4]. This means chopstick[4] remains free, allowing philosopher 3 to eventually eat and release chopstick[3], breaking the cycle. Hence, deadlock cannot occur. The system makes progress and is starvation-free under FIFO scheduling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "atomic" keyword** in semaphore definitions costs 0.5–1 mark. Always write: *"The P and V operations are atomic."*
> 2. **Confusing `S.value` test direction**: A common error is checking `S > 0` instead of `S < 0` after the decrement. The test is **AFTER** the decrement, and the condition to block is when S has become **negative** (or non-positive in some textbooks).
> 3. **Reversing the order of `wait()` calls** in producer-consumer: While not always wrong, the order `wait(empty)` then `wait(mutex)` is the safer convention. Some textbook solutions allow the reverse, but in KTU exams, follow Silberschatz's textbook order.
> 4. **Drawing the dining philosophers as a 5-pointed star** instead of a pentagon — the KTU examiner usually doesn't care, but a clean diagram always leaves a positive impression.
> 5. **Omitting the invariant** $(\text{empty} + \text{full} = N)$ in the bounded buffer problem — this single line is worth 1 mark and shows deep understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following checklist summarizes the entire Module 2 Semaphores topic. Read it 10 minutes before entering the examination hall.**

- **Semaphore** = Non-negative integer + Two atomic operations (`wait`/`P` and `signal`/`V`).
- **Dijkstra (1965)** proposed the semaphore concept. Names `P` (`proberen`) and `V` (`verhogen`) come from Dutch.
- **Binary Semaphore:** Value $\in \{0, 1\}$, used for mutual exclusion.
- **Counting Semaphore:** Value $\in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N\}$, used for resource pools.
- **Atomicity** is **non-negotiable** — enforced via interrupt disable (uniprocessor) or atomic hardware instructions (multiprocessor).
- **P Operation:** Decrement; if result $< 0$, block the process. **V Operation:** Increment; if result $\leq 0$, wake one waiting process.
- **Producer–Consumer:** Uses `empty`, `full`, and `mutex` semaphores. Invariant: $\text{empty} + \text{full} = N$.
- **Readers–Writers:** Uses `mutex`, `wrt`, and `readcount`. Reader preference causes writer starvation.
- **Dining Philosophers:** Naive solution deadlocks due to circular wait. Break symmetry by reversing the order for the last philosopher.
- **Deadlock's Four Coffman Conditions:** Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
- **Starvation** is avoided by using a **FIFO waiting queue** for the semaphore.
- **Priority Inversion** is solved by the **Priority Inheritance Protocol** (a high-priority task temporarily inherits the priority of the resource-holding low-priority task).
- **Semaphores are NOT a programming construct alone** — they are a *kernel-managed* synchronization primitive requiring OS support.
- **Producer–Consumer invariant** is the most-tested KTU sub-question in Module 2.
- **Dining Philosophers deadlock reasoning** is the most common 7-mark KTU question.
- **Distinguish carefully between semaphores and mutexes** — a mutex has an *owner* (the thread that locked it), but a binary semaphore does not necessarily have ownership.
- **Linux semaphore structures** have `count` and `wait_list` fields, equivalent to the textbook `S.value` and `S.waiting_queue`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monitors - Synchronization Problems - Producer-Consumer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monitors - Synchronization Problems - Producer-Consumer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Monitor Construct — Formal Definition

A **Monitor** is a high-level language synchronization construct that encapsulates:

1. **Shared Data Structures** (the resource being protected)
2. **Procedures** that operate on the shared data
3. **Synchronization Primitives** (mutex + condition variables) that enforce mutual exclusion and coordination

Formally, a monitor type is an ADT (Abstract Data Type) defined as:

$$
\text{Monitor} = \{ \text{Shared Variables},\ \text{Procedures},\ \text{Condition Variables},\ \text{Initialization Code} \}
$$

Key invariant: **At any instant, at most ONE process may be active inside the monitor.** Entry to the monitor is serialized by an implicit mutex lock managed by the compiler / runtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A monitor is a *software module* — NOT a hardware device. It is a programming language construct (supported in languages like Java `synchronized`, C# `lock`, Pascal, Concurrent Euclid, Mesa). KTU expects the *Silberschatz textbook treatment* — concentrate on the conceptual model using Pascal-like pseudocode.

### 1.2 Condition Variables — The Heart of Coordination

A **condition variable** is a synchronization queue associated with a monitor. Two atomic operations are defined:

| Operation | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `x.wait()` | The calling process is **suspended** (released from monitor mutex, placed on `x`'s queue) |
| `x.signal()` | If any process is waiting on `x`, **exactly one** waiting process is resumed |

> [!NOTE]
> `wait()` and `signal()` are the monitor equivalents of `P()` and `V()` on semaphores — but they live *inside* a monitor and operate only on the monitor's internal condition queues. They are **not** the same as the UNIX `wait()` system call.

### 1.3 The Producer–Consumer Problem — Statement

> **Definition (Silberschatz).** The Producer–Consumer problem (also called the **Bounded-Buffer problem**) involves two classes of processes:
> - **Producers** generate data items and place them into a shared bounded buffer of size **N**.
> - **Consumers** remove items from the buffer and consume them.
>
> The buffer can hold at most **N** items. Synchronization is required so that:
> - A producer never writes into a *full* buffer.
> - A consumer never reads from an *empty* buffer.
> - Access to the buffer is mutually exclusive.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy — The Bakery Counter

Imagine a **bakery display counter** with exactly **5 slots**:

- **The Baker (Producer)** keeps baking pastries. If all 5 slots are filled, the baker MUST wait until a customer takes one.
- **The Customer (Consumer)** arrives to buy pastries. If the counter is empty, the customer MUST wait until the baker refills it.
- Only **one person at a time** may touch the counter (mutual exclusion).
- When a customer buys a pastry, they may **announce** ("Counter has space!") to wake a waiting baker.
- When a baker refills, they may **announce** ("Counter has items!") to wake a waiting customer.

The counter itself behaves like a **monitor**: it bundles the data (slots) with the rules (add/remove) and the announcement mechanism (condition variables).

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the bakery counter automatically handles ALL race conditions — *you cannot lose a pastry or reach into an empty slot*. This is exactly the property a monitor provides for shared data structures.

### 1.5 The Three Synchronization Requirements

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mutual Exclusion} &: \text{Only one process accesses buffer at a time.} \\
\text{Producer Block} &: \text{If buffer is FULL, producer must wait.} \\
\text{Consumer Block} &: \text{If buffer is EMPTY, consumer must wait.}
\end{aligned}
$$

These three rules are enforced by the **monitor's mutex** plus **two condition variables** (`full` and `empty`) plus a **third counter** (`count`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Monitor — Component Breakdown

A monitor in Pascal-like pseudocode has the following structure:

```
monitor MonitorName {
    // ---- Shared Declarations ----
    variable declarations;

    // ---- Condition Variables ----
    condition x, y;

    // ---- Initialization ----
    initialization code;

    // ---- Procedures (Mutually Exclusive) ----
    procedure P1(...) { ... }
    procedure P2(...) { ... }
    ...
}
```

**Operational Semantics:**

- A process invokes `MonitorName.P1(...)` to enter procedure `P1`.
- The monitor's compiler-generated mutex is **acquired** automatically on entry.
- If another process is already inside, the caller is **suspended** at the entry queue.
- On normal exit (or exception), the mutex is **released**.

### 2.2 Condition Variable Semantics — Hoare vs. Mesa

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks the **Hoare vs. Mesa** distinction. Memorize the difference below.

| Aspect | Hoare Semantics (1974) | Mesa Semantics (Xerox PARC) |
|---|---|---|
| On `signal()` | Signaler **suspends**; awakened process runs **immediately** | Signaler **continues**; awakened process is scheduled later |
| State of monitor | Monitor lock handed to awakened process | Lock retained by signaler |
| Implementation | Complicated (nested monitor calls) | Simple (just a wakeup flag) |
| Spurious wakeup risk | No | Yes — awakened process must **re-check** the condition |
| Used in | Theoretical teaching, original monitor proposal | Java, C#, modern languages |

**Practical Consequence (Mesa style):** A process awakened by `signal()` cannot assume the awaited condition still holds. Therefore the **while-loop idiom** is mandatory:

$$
\text{while}\ (\text{condition is NOT met})\ \text{wait}(c);
$$

### 2.3 The Producer–Consumer Monitor — Silberschatz Solution

The canonical monitor-based bounded buffer:

```
monitor BoundedBuffer {
    int buffer[N];
    int in = 0, out = 0;
    int count = 0;

    condition full, empty;

    void produce(item) {
        while (count == N)        // buffer full?
            full.wait();          // release mutex, sleep on full queue
        buffer[in] = item;
        in = (in + 1) % N;
        count = count + 1;
        empty.signal();           // wake one waiting consumer
    }

    item consume() {
        item x;
        while (count == 0)        // buffer empty?
            empty.wait();
        x = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) % N;
        count = count - 1;
        full.signal();
        return x;
    }
}
```

### 2.4 Why the `while` Loop is Critical (Mesa Semantics)

When `full.signal()` is executed by a producer:
1. Producer is still inside the monitor (Mesa).
2. A waiting consumer is moved to the **ready queue**, NOT directly into the monitor.
3. By the time the consumer gets the lock, **another** consumer may have drained the buffer.
4. Hence the awakened consumer re-checks `count == 0` and waits again if necessary.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Using `if (count == 0) wait();` instead of `while` is the **single most common mistake** in monitor code. It causes lost wakeups and buffer underflow. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this in Part B.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity / Construct | Expression | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Buffer Capacity | $N$ slots | Upper bound on `count` |
| In Pointer | $in = (in + 1)\ \bmod\ N$ | Cyclic write index |
| Out Pointer | $out = (out + 1)\ \bmod\ N$ | Cyclic read index |
| Items in Buffer | $0 \le count \le N$ | Invariant maintained by monitor |
| Number of Condition Vars | $2$ (`full`, `empty`) | One per bound condition |
| Mutual Exclusion | Implemented by | Compiler-generated monitor lock |
| Wait/Block Count | at most $N$ processes | Each occupies one slot logically |
| Producer Wait Condition | $count = N$ | Buffer is **FULL** |
| Consumer Wait Condition | $count = 0$ | Buffer is **EMPTY** |

### 2.6 Why Monitors Beat Semaphores (Engineering Utility)

| Issue | Semaphores | Monitors |
|---|---|---|
| Programming errors | Easy to forget `wait()` / `signal()` | Compiler-enforced structure |
| Modular compilation | Hard to verify | Clean modular units |
| Mutual exclusion | Programmer must enforce | **Automatic** |
| Code readability | Low | High (resembles ADT) |
| Used in practice | Kernel-level (Linux sync) | User-level (Java, C#) |

> [!NOTE]
> Real-world use: `java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock` + `Condition` objects, `pthread_cond_wait()` in C with an external mutex, and Go's channel pattern are direct descendants of the monitor concept.

### 2.7 Edge Cases and Boundary Conditions

- **Buffer size $N = 1$**: degenerates to a *rendezvous* point — producer and consumer alternate strictly.
- **Buffer size $N = \infty$**: `full.wait()` is never invoked; only `empty.wait()` matters.
- **$P$ producers, $C$ consumers**: monitor still serializes entry, but up to $P + C$ processes can be alive concurrently — coordination comes purely from the two condition variables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Monitor Solution

We derive the correctness of the Silberschatz monitor by checking all three synchronization requirements.

#### Step 1 — Establish the Invariant

Define the state variables at the entry and exit of each monitor procedure:

$$
S = \{ \text{buffer}[0 \mathinner{\ldotp\ldotp} N-1],\ in,\ out,\ count \}
$$

**Invariants to maintain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &: 0 \le count \le N \\
I_2 &: 0 \le in < N \\
I_3 &: 0 \le out < N \\
I_4 &: in = (out + count)\ \bmod\ N \quad \text{(circular buffer geometry)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Verify Mutual Exclusion

The monitor lock is acquired before any procedure body executes. Since `produce` and `consume` are the only exported procedures, at most one process executes inside the monitor at a time. The compiler/runtime guarantees this — no manual mutex needed by the programmer. Therefore:

$$
\forall t:\ \vert\{p \mid p \text{ executing inside } BoundedBuffer \text{ at time } t\}\vert \le 1
$$

#### Step 3 — Verify Producer is Blocked when Buffer is Full

Suppose a producer $p$ calls `produce(item)` while $count = N$.

- The `while` test `count == N` evaluates to **true**.
- `p` executes `full.wait()`, which atomically:
  1. Suspends $p$.
  2. Releases the monitor mutex.
  3. Places $p$ on the `full` condition queue.
- $p$ cannot proceed until another process executes `full.signal()`.
- Only `consume()` executes `full.signal()` after decrementing `count`. So $p$ is unblocked only after $count < N$.

#### Step 4 — Verify Consumer is Blocked when Buffer is Empty

Symmetric argument. If $count = 0$, `empty.wait()` suspends the consumer. Only `produce()` signals `empty` after `count` is incremented. So the consumer is unblocked only after $count > 0$.

#### Step 5 — Verify `count` is Updated Correctly

In `produce`:

$$
count_{\text{new}} = count_{\text{old}} + 1
$$

In `consume`:

$$
count_{\text{new}} = count_{\text{old}} - 1
$$

Since these are the only places `count` is modified, and they run under mutual exclusion, $I_1$ holds.

#### Step 6 — Verify Buffer Slot Access is Bounded

The write index and read index both advance modulo $N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
in_{\text{new}} &= (in_{\text{old}} + 1)\ \bmod\ N \\
out_{\text{new}} &= (out_{\text{old}} + 1)\ \bmod\ N
\end{aligned}
$$

This guarantees $in$ and $out$ cycle through $\{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$, satisfying $I_2$ and $I_3$.

#### Step 7 — Verify Circular Geometry

We prove $I_4$ by induction on the number of monitor invocations.

- **Base case:** $in = 0$, $out = 0$, $count = 0$. Then $in = (out + count)\ \bmod\ N = 0$. ✓
- **Inductive step (produce):** Suppose $I_4$ holds before. After produce:

$$
\begin{aligned}
in' &= (in + 1)\ \bmod\ N \\
count' &= count + 1 \\
out' &= out
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:

$$
(out' + count')\ \bmod\ N = (out + count + 1)\ \bmod\ N = (in + 1)\ \bmod\ N = in'
$$

- **Inductive step (consume):** Symmetric. ✓

Therefore $I_4$ is an invariant.

#### Step 8 — Liveness / Deadlock-Freedom

A deadlock would require both `full` and `empty` queues to be non-empty while no process is executing inside the monitor — impossible, since the process inside will eventually call `signal()` on one queue. Hence the system is **deadlock-free**.

A process starved would require it to be perpetually bypassed in its condition queue — under FIFO discipline, this is impossible. Hence the system is **starvation-free** (Hoare semantics) or starvation-free under fair scheduling (Mesa semantics).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Code-to-Property Mapping

| Code Line | Synchronization Property Enforced | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|
| `condition full, empty;` | Declare coordination variables | 1 |
| `while (count == N) full.wait();` | Block producer when full | 3 |
| `count++; empty.signal();` | Update state and wake consumer | 2 |
| `while (count == 0) empty.wait();` | Block consumer when empty | 3 |
| `count--; full.signal();` | Update state and wake producer | 2 |
| Implicit monitor lock | Mutual exclusion | 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> Total = 13 marks for a typical 14-mark question. Reserve 1 mark for code style / explanation.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

Let $N = 3$, initial state $in = out = 0$, $count = 0$. Trace the following sequence:

$$
P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to C_1 \to C_2 \to P_4 \to C_3
$$

where $P_i$ = $i$-th produce call, $C_j$ = $j$-th consume call.

| Step | Action | Buffer State | $in$ | $out$ | $count$ | Wait Queue |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Init | `[_, _, _]` | 0 | 0 | 0 | — |
| 1 | $P_1$ produces $A$ | `[A, _, _]` | 1 | 0 | 1 | — |
| 2 | $P_2$ produces $B$ | `[A, B, _]` | 2 | 0 | 2 | — |
| 3 | $P_3$ produces $C$ | `[A, B, C]` | 0 | 0 | 3 | — |
| 4 | $C_1$ consumes $A$ | `[_, B, C]` | 0 | 1 | 2 | — |
| 5 | $C_2$ consumes $B$ | `[_, _, C]` | 0 | 2 | 1 | — |
| 6 | $P_4$ produces $D$ | `[D, _, C]` | 1 | 2 | 2 | — |
| 7 | $C_3$ consumes $C$ | `[D, _, _]` | 1 | 0 | 1 | — |

**Observation:** Step 3 fills the buffer; if a $P_4$ arrived *before* $C_1$, it would block on `full.wait()`. This is precisely the synchronization guarantee.

### 3.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  MONITOR : BoundedBuffer                   │
│                                                            │
│   ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌────────────┐  │
│   │ buffer[0]   │     │ buffer[1]   │ ... │ buffer[N-1]│  │
│   └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └────────────┘  │
│         ▲                     ▲                   ▲         │
│         │   in                │              out  │         │
│   ┌─────┴─────┐          ┌────┴────┐     ┌──────┴──────┐   │
│   │ count (0…N)│          │Procedures│     │Cond Vars   │   │
│   └───────────┘          │produce() │     │full, empty │   │
│                          │consume() │     └────────────┘   │
│                          └──────────┘                      │
│                                                            │
│   [Monitor Lock] ← single permit, compiler-managed         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Monitor Internal Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BoundedBuffer[Monitor: BoundedBuffer]
        direction TB
        Lock[Monitor Lock - Compiler Managed]
        State[Shared State: buffer, in, out, count]
        ProcProduce[Procedure: produce]
        ProcConsume[Procedure: consume]
        CondFull[Condition Variable: full]
        CondEmpty[Condition Variable: empty]
        Init[Initialization Code]

        Lock -.serializes.-> ProcProduce
        Lock -.serializes.-> ProcConsume
        ProcProduce -->|reads/writes| State
        ProcConsume -->|reads/writes| State
        ProcProduce -->|wait/signal| CondFull
        ProcProduce -->|signal only| CondEmpty
        ProcConsume -->|wait/signal| CondEmpty
        ProcConsume -->|signal only| CondFull
        Init --> State
    end

    P1[Producer Process] -->|invoke produce| Lock
    P2[Another Producer] -->|invoke produce| Lock
    C1[Consumer Process] -->|invoke consume| Lock
    C2[Another Consumer] -->|invoke consume| Lock
```

### 4.2 Producer–Consumer State Transition Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready
    Ready --> InMonitor: acquire monitor lock
    InMonitor --> CheckingFull: produce entered
    CheckingFull --> BlockedFull: count == N
    CheckingFull --> WritingBuffer: count less than N
    BlockedFull --> Ready: full.signal received
    Ready --> InMonitor: lock acquired
    WritingBuffer --> InMonitor: count incremented
    InMonitor --> Ready: empty.signal then exit

    Ready --> InMonitor2: acquire monitor lock
    InMonitor2 --> CheckingEmpty: consume entered
    CheckingEmpty --> BlockedEmpty: count == 0
    CheckingEmpty --> ReadingBuffer: count greater than 0
    BlockedEmpty --> Ready: empty.signal received
    ReadingBuffer --> InMonitor2: count decremented
    InMonitor2 --> Ready: full.signal then exit
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow of a Single Produce Call

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Producer calls produce item]) --> EnterLock[Acquire Monitor Lock]
    EnterLock --> TestFull{count == N ?}
    TestFull -- Yes --> WaitFull[full.wait - suspend, release lock]
    WaitFull --> TestFull
    TestFull -- No --> WriteSlot[buffer in := item]
    WriteSlot --> AdvanceIn[in := in + 1 mod N]
    AdvanceIn --> IncCount[count := count + 1]
    IncCount --> SignalEmpty[empty.signal - wake one consumer]
    SignalEmpty --> ExitLock[Release Monitor Lock]
    ExitLock --> End([Return to producer])
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Flow of a Single Consume Call

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Consumer calls consume]) --> EnterLock[Acquire Monitor Lock]
    EnterLock --> TestEmpty{count == 0 ?}
    TestEmpty -- Yes --> WaitEmpty[empty.wait - suspend, release lock]
    WaitEmpty --> TestEmpty
    TestEmpty -- No --> ReadSlot[x := buffer out]
    ReadSlot --> AdvanceOut[out := out + 1 mod N]
    AdvanceOut --> DecCount[count := count - 1]
    DecCount --> SignalFull[full.signal - wake one producer]
    SignalFull --> ExitLock[Release Monitor Lock]
    ExitLock --> End([Return x to consumer])
```

### 4.5 Comparison: Monitor vs. Semaphore (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Sem[Semaphore Solution]
        S1[Shared buffer - unprotected]
        S2[Semaphore mutex = 1]
        S3[Semaphore empty = N]
        S4[Semaphore full = 0]
    end

    subgraph Mon[Monitor Solution]
        M1[BoundedBuffer monitor - encapsulated]
        M2[Internal lock - automatic]
        M3[Condition full - queue]
        M4[Condition empty - queue]
    end

    Sem -->|Programmer Error Prone| Error1[Risk of deadlock and race]
    Mon -->|Compiler Enforced| Safe1[Encapsulated safety]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above render the **logical interaction topology** of the monitor and its data structures, mapping the producer–consumer flow as a state machine. This is the most suitable schematic representation for the KTU board examination context.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**

**Define a monitor. List its key components.**

**Model Answer:**

A **monitor** is a high-level language construct for process synchronization that encapsulates shared data, the procedures that operate on that data, and the synchronization primitives (mutex and condition variables) needed for coordinated access.

**Key Components:**

1. **Shared data declarations** — variables representing the protected resource.
2. **Procedures** — operations that manipulate the shared data (mutually exclusive entry).
3. **Condition variables** — queues on which processes wait for conditions to become true.
4. **Initialization code** — executed once at monitor creation.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for components.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between Hoare and Mesa semantics of `signal()` in a monitor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hoare Semantics | Mesa Semantics |
|---|---|---|
| On `signal()` | Signaler suspends; awakened process runs **immediately** | Signaler **continues**; awakened process runs **later** |
| Monitor lock | Transferred to awakened process | Retained by signaler |
| Wakeup check | Condition guaranteed true | Must re-check condition (use `while`) |
| Implementation | Complex | Simple |
| Example languages | Theoretical Pascal monitors | Java, C#, Mesa |

*(3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model QP]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a monitor and the role of condition variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a monitor-based solution to the Producer–Consumer problem with a bounded buffer of size $N$. Explain how mutual exclusion and blocking are ensured. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Monitor Structure and Condition Variables**

A monitor is a software module grouping shared data, procedures, and condition variables. Its conceptual structure is:

```
monitor MonitorName {
    declaration of shared variables;
    condition x, y;
    initialization code;

    procedure P1(... ) { ... }
    procedure P2(... ) { ... }
}
```

**Properties:**

- Entry to any monitor procedure automatically acquires an implicit **mutual exclusion lock**.
- Only one process executes inside the monitor at a time.
- A process waiting for a condition executes `x.wait()` and is suspended, **releasing** the lock.
- `x.signal()` resumes exactly one waiting process (if any).

**Role of Condition Variables:** They allow processes to wait for specific *state predicates* (e.g., "buffer is non-empty"). They are *not* counters and have *no* memory of past `signal()` calls. They are queues of sleeping processes.

*(7 marks: 2 structure, 2 mutual exclusion, 3 condition variable semantics.)*

**(b) Producer–Consumer Monitor Code**

```
monitor BoundedBuffer {
    int buffer[N];
    int in = 0, out = 0, count = 0;

    condition full, empty;

    void produce(item) {
        while (count == N)
            full.wait();
        buffer[in] = item;
        in = (in + 1) % N;
        count = count + 1;
        empty.signal();
    }

    item consume() {
        item x;
        while (count == 0)
            empty.wait();
        x = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) % N;
        count = count - 1;
        full.signal();
        return x;
    }
}
```

**How Synchronization is Ensured:**

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** Provided by the implicit monitor lock. Only one process (producer OR consumer) is inside the monitor at any time — buffer is never accessed concurrently.

2. **Producer Blocking:** When `count == N`, the producer calls `full.wait()`. The producer suspends and releases the lock, allowing a consumer to enter. Producer resumes only when a consumer calls `full.signal()` (after consuming an item and decrementing `count`).

3. **Consumer Blocking:** Symmetric — when `count == 0`, consumer calls `empty.wait()`. Suspends until a producer calls `empty.signal()`.

4. **Why `while` not `if`:** Under Mesa semantics, the signaled process may not run immediately; another process may change the state. The `while` re-checks the predicate to prevent lost wakeups.

*(7 marks: 4 code, 2 mutual exclusion explanation, 1 blocking explanation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model QP / Similar to Jul 2024]**

**(a)** State the Producer–Consumer problem. Why does a naïve solution using simple shared variables fail? Illustrate with a counterexample. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Rewrite the bounded-buffer solution using TWO semaphores and ONE mutex. Show how it differs from the monitor solution in terms of programmer responsibility. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Producer–Consumer Problem Statement & Naïve Failure**

**Statement:** Two sets of processes share a bounded buffer of size $N$. Producers insert items; consumers remove items. Synchronization must prevent writes to a full buffer, reads from an empty buffer, and concurrent access.

**Naïve Approach:**

```
shared int count = 0;
item buffer[N];
```

Producer:

```
while (true) {
    produce item;
    while (count == N);   // busy-wait
    buffer[in] = item; in = (in+1)%N; count++;
}
```

Consumer:

```
while (true) {
    while (count == 0);   // busy-wait
    item x = buffer[out]; out = (out+1)%N; count--;
    consume x;
}
```

**Why it Fails — Race Condition on `count`:**

Consider two producers $P_1$, $P_2$ with $count = N-1$ initially. Both check the `while (count == N)` test, both find it false, and both proceed to write. After both write, $count` is incremented twice. Now $count = N+1$, but the buffer only has $N$ slots — **buffer overflow** and **lost data**.

The fundamental flaw: **check-then-act** sequences are not atomic. Between the `while` test and the `buffer[in] = item` write, the state can change.

A trace showing the race:

| Step | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | `count` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | reads `count = N-1` | — | $N-1$ |
| 2 | — | reads `count = N-1` | $N-1$ |
| 3 | writes slot | — | $N-1$ |
| 4 | — | writes slot | $N-1$ |
| 5 | increments | — | $N$ |
| 6 | — | increments | $N+1$ — **ERROR** |

*(7 marks: 2 statement, 3 naïve code, 2 race demonstration.)*

**(b) Semaphore Solution**

```
semaphore mutex = 1;     // protects buffer access
semaphore empty = N;     // counts empty slots
semaphore full  = 0;     // counts full slots

item buffer[N];

void produce(item) {
    wait(empty);           // need an empty slot
    wait(mutex);           // exclusive access
    buffer[in] = item; in = (in+1) % N;
    signal(mutex);
    signal(full);          // one more full slot
}

item consume() {
    wait(full);            // need a full slot
    wait(mutex);           // exclusive access
    item x = buffer[out]; out = (out+1) % N;
    signal(mutex);
    signal(empty);         // one more empty slot
}
```

**Difference in Programmer Responsibility:**

| Concern | Semaphore Solution | Monitor Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Mutual exclusion | Programmer must `wait(mutex)` and `signal(mutex)` correctly at every procedure | **Compiler** enforces automatically |
| Ordering of waits | `wait(empty)` must precede `wait(mutex)` to avoid deadlock | Not applicable — single mutex |
| Forgetting a `wait` | Subtle deadlock or race | Compiler-generated entry makes this impossible |
| Code locality | Shared data and synchronization are scattered | Bundled in one module |
| Modularity | Hard to package as ADT | Natural ADT design |

**Critical Ordering Rule:** In the semaphore version, `wait(empty)` MUST come before `wait(mutex)`. Otherwise, if the buffer is full and a producer holds `mutex`, all consumers block on `mutex` while waiting for an item — **classic deadlock**.

*(7 marks: 3 code, 2 critical ordering note, 2 responsibility comparison.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose NO Marks!**
>
> 1. **Using `if` instead of `while`** around `wait()` — costs 1–2 marks. Always use `while` in Mesa-style monitors (Java, modern OS textbooks).
> 2. **Forgetting the `signal()` call** after `count` is updated — means consumers/producers never wake. Deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Placing `signal()` before `count` update** — works in this problem but is bad practice. Deduct ½ mark.
> 4. **Swapping `full` and `empty`** — producer waits on empty and vice versa. Major logical error, deduct 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the cyclic update** `in = (in+1) % N` — code is wrong for $N > 1$. Deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing condition variables with semaphores** — they are queues, not counters. A `signal()` with no waiting process is a NO-OP (different from semaphore behavior). This distinction is worth 1 mark in viva.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **monitor** is a high-level ADT encapsulating shared data + procedures + condition variables + automatic mutual exclusion.
- **Entry to a monitor** is serialized by a compiler-managed mutex; **at most one process** is inside at any time.
- **Condition variables** support exactly two operations: `wait()` (sleep, release lock) and `signal()` (wake one waiter, if any).
- **Hoare semantics:** signaler suspends; **Mesa semantics:** signaler continues — modern languages use Mesa, hence the **mandatory `while` loop**.
- The **Producer–Consumer (Bounded-Buffer) problem** uses **two** condition variables: `full` and `empty`, plus a **count** invariant $0 \le count \le N$.
- **Producer** waits on `full` when `count == N`; **consumer** waits on `empty` when `count == 0`.
- Each `signal()` is followed by the appropriate **state update** (e.g., `empty.signal()` after `count++`).
- **Monitors vs. Semaphores:** Monitors eliminate programmer mistakes in mutual exclusion and order of `wait/signal`; semaphores are lower-level and error-prone.
- **Real-world analogues:** Java `synchronized` methods + `wait()`/`notify()`, C# `Monitor` class, C `pthread_cond_wait`, Go channels.
- **Deadlock-free:** No state in which both `full` and `empty` queues are non-empty while the monitor is idle.
- **Starvation-free:** Under fair scheduling, every process eventually gets to run.
- **Cyclic buffer arithmetic:** $in = (in+1) \bmod N$, $out = (out+1) \bmod N$ — memorize the modulo form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dining Philosophers and Readers-Writers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dining Philosophers and Readers-Writers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — PROCESS SYNCHRONIZATION CLASSIC PROBLEMS

## 1.1 Dining Philosophers Problem — Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> The *Dining Philosophers Problem*, originally formulated by **Edsger Dijkstra (1965)**, is a classic concurrency control and synchronization problem that illustrates the challenges of **deadlock** and **resource starvation** in a multi-process environment. It models $N$ philosophers seated around a circular table who alternate between **thinking** and **eating**, where each philosopher requires **two shared chopsticks (forks)** placed between adjacent seats to consume food.

### 1.1.1 Problem Statement (Formal)

- **$N$ philosophers** (typically $N = 5$) sit at a round table.
- Between every pair of adjacent philosophers lies exactly **one fork** (so there are $N$ forks in total).
- A philosopher's life cycle alternates between three states: **Thinking $\rightarrow$ Hungry $\rightarrow$ Eating $\rightarrow$ Thinking**.
- To eat, a philosopher must acquire **both the left fork and the right fork**.
- After eating, the philosopher puts down both forks and returns to thinking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Synchronization Constraints**
> 1. **Mutual Exclusion:** Only one philosopher may hold a particular fork at any instant.
> 2. **Deadlock Avoidance:** The system must never reach a state where all philosophers are blocked indefinitely, each holding one fork and waiting for the other.
> 3. **Starvation Freedom:** No philosopher should wait forever for the two forks; every hungry philosopher must eventually eat.
> 4. **Concurrency:** Whenever possible, **two non-adjacent philosophers** must be able to eat simultaneously.

### 1.1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine **5 friends sitting around a round dining table** for a dinner party. Between every two friends, there is **exactly one chopstick** (a total of 5 chopsticks). To eat the noodles placed in the center, each person needs **two chopsticks** — the one on their immediate left and the one on their immediate right.

- If everyone **greedily picks up the left chopstick first**, then everyone is holding exactly one chopstick and **nobody can get the second one** — the table is deadlocked. Everyone is just staring at their noodles, unable to eat, like a frozen comedy sketch.
- The challenge is to design a *traffic-controller* (synchronization mechanism) that allows friends to eat **without ever freezing the whole table**, and without letting any one friend go hungry forever.

This is exactly the conundrum an Operating System faces when multiple processes compete for limited shared resources (like printers, tape drives, or database locks).

---

## 1.2 Readers-Writers Problem — Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> The *Readers-Writers Problem* is a classic synchronization problem that models access to a **shared database or shared object** (e.g., a file, a buffer, a memory region) by multiple concurrent processes that are classified as either **Readers** (processes that only read/examine the shared data) or **Writers** (processes that modify the shared data). The fundamental constraint is that **any number of readers may simultaneously access the shared data**, but **a writer must have exclusive (sole) access** — no other reader or writer may access the data while a writer is writing.

### 1.2.1 Problem Statement (Formal)

- A **shared resource** (database, file, variable) is to be accessed concurrently by multiple processes.
- A process declares itself as either a **Reader (R)** or a **Writer (W)** before requesting access.
- **Read operations** are non-mutating and **commute** — they do not interfere with one another.
- **Write operations** are mutating and must be performed under strict **mutual exclusion** with respect to all other operations (reads and writes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Synchronization Constraints**
> 1. **Multiple concurrent readers are permitted** (shared read access).
> 2. **Only one writer at a time** (mutual exclusion for writers).
> 3. **Readers and writers are mutually exclusive**: a writer cannot operate while any reader is reading, and a reader cannot enter while a writer is writing.
> 4. Optional: Avoid **starvation** of either readers or writers depending on the chosen policy.

### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **public library with a single copy of a popular newspaper** kept on a reading stand.

- **Reading the newspaper** does not damage or change it — so 10 people can stand around the stand and read it together without any problem. They don't need to fight.
- **Writing a note on the newspaper** (e.g., correcting a typo with a pen) **does** change it. While one person is scribbling, **nobody else can read it** (because the content is mid-change and inconsistent), and **nobody else can also write** on it (otherwise notes will overlap and corrupt each other).
- The librarian (the OS) must enforce these rules using a fair queuing mechanism.

This models how an OS coordinates database transactions, file reads/writes, and shared-memory updates in a multi-process environment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Dining Philosophers — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Naive (Deadlock-Prone) Solution

The simplest attempt uses a **binary semaphore** for each fork:

$$\text{semaphore fork}[N] = \{1, 1, 1, 1, 1\}$$

Each philosopher $P_i$ executes the loop:

```text
while (true) {
    think();
    wait(fork[i]);         // pick up left fork
    wait(fork[(i+1) mod N]); // pick up right fork
    eat();
    signal(fork[i]);
    signal(fork[(i+1) mod N]);
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Deadlock Scenario:** If all $N$ philosophers execute `wait(fork[i])` simultaneously (each picks up the left fork), every fork semaphore becomes 0. When each philosopher then tries `wait(fork[(i+1) mod N])`, the entire system blocks forever. This is a **circular wait** — a Coffman deadlock condition.

### 2.1.2 Solution 1: Limiting the Number of Diners

Use a **counting semaphore** `room` initialized to $N-1$ to ensure at most $N-1$ philosophers enter the dining room at once. Combined with the fork semaphores, **deadlock is broken** because at least one philosopher can always acquire both forks.

### 2.1.3 Solution 2: Ordered Resource Acquisition (Break Circular Wait)

Force philosophers to acquire forks in a **global order** (e.g., always pick up the **lower-numbered** fork first):

- For philosopher $P_i$, if $i$ is even, pick up `fork[i]` then `fork[(i+1) mod N]`.
- For philosopher $P_i$, if $i$ is odd, pick up `fork[(i+1) mod N]` then `fork[i]`.

This **breaks the circular wait** condition of deadlock because two adjacent philosophers will now compete for the same "lower-numbered" fork, but only one will succeed.

### 2.1.4 Solution 3: Monitor-Based Solution (Hoare / Mesa)

Use a **monitor** `DiningPhilosophers` with:

- An array `state[N] = {THINKING, HUNGRY, EATING}`.
- A condition variable `self[i]` for each philosopher.
- Two monitor procedures: `pickup(i)` and `putdown(i)`.

```text
pickup(i):
    state[i] = HUNGRY
    test(i)
    if state[i] != EATING:
        wait(self[i])

putdown(i):
    state[i] = THINKING
    test(LEFT)
    test(RIGHT)
```

`test(i)` allows philosopher $i$ to eat only if **he is hungry** and **both neighbours are not eating**.

### 2.1.5 Solution 4: Tanenbaum's Asymmetric Solution

Number philosophers $0$ to $N-1$. Allow philosopher $i$ to pick up forks only when **both** are free, but enforce the rule:

- If $i$ is **odd**, pick up `fork[i]` first, then `fork[i+1]`.
- If $i$ is **even**, pick up `fork[i+1]` first, then `fork[i]`.

This makes the "lower-numbered" philosopher always start with the right fork, ensuring the cycle of waiting is broken.

---

## 2.2 Readers-Writers — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Shared Variables & Semaphores

Let:

- `mutex` = a binary semaphore, initialized to **1** (protects the shared `readcount` variable).
- `wrt` = a binary semaphore, initialized to **1** (controls writer access to the database).
- `readcount` = an integer (initially **0**) tracking the number of reader processes currently accessing the database.

### 2.2.2 Readers-Writers Solution (First Variant — Reader Preference)

**Writer process:**
```text
wait(wrt);
   // CRITICAL SECTION: write to shared database
signal(wrt);
```

**Reader process:**
```text
wait(mutex);
   readcount = readcount + 1;
   if (readcount == 1)
       wait(wrt);          // first reader blocks writers
signal(mutex);
   // CRITICAL SECTION: read from shared database
wait(mutex);
   readcount = readcount - 1;
   if (readcount == 0)
       signal(wrt);        // last reader unblocks writers
signal(mutex);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:**
> - The first reader arriving acquires `wrt`, preventing any writer from entering.
> - Subsequent readers simply increment `readcount` and proceed without touching `wrt` — they share the read access.
> - The last reader to leave releases `wrt`, allowing a waiting writer to proceed.
> - `mutex` ensures atomic update of `readcount`.

### 2.2.3 Starvation Issue

> [!WARNING]
> **Reader-Preference Starvation:** In the solution above, if readers keep arriving, the writer may **starve** — the writer waits indefinitely while a continuous stream of readers is granted access. This is called the **"First Readers-Writers Problem"** (favors readers).

### 2.2.4 Writer-Preference Solution (Second Variant)

Add a semaphore `tryread` initialized to 1, and a variable `wwcount` (waiting writers). The writer, on arrival, increments `wwcount` and blocks `tryread` so that **no new readers can enter** while a writer is waiting. Once the current readers finish, the writer gets in. This is the **"Second Readers-Writers Problem"** (favors writers).

### 2.2.5 Fair Solution (Third Variant — No Starvation)

Use a FIFO queue (e.g., a semaphore `queue` initialized to 1) so that processes enter the critical section strictly in the order of their arrival. Both readers and writers are queued uniformly.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Initial Value / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of Philosophers | $N$ | Total philosophers around the table | Typically $N = 5$ |
| Number of Forks | $N$ | One fork between every pair | Matches philosopher count |
| Fork Semaphores | $\text{fork}[i]$ | Binary semaphore for the $i$-th fork | All initialized to $1$ |
| Room Semaphore (Solution 1) | $\text{room}$ | Limits philosophers in dining area | $N - 1$ |
| Readcount | $\text{readcount}$ | Active reader count | $0$ |
| Mutex (Reader Counter) | $\text{mutex}$ | Protects `readcount` | $1$ |
| Writer Lock | $\text{wrt}$ | Exclusive access to shared data | $1$ |
| Waiting Writers | $\text{wwcount}$ | Number of waiting writers (Variant 2) | $0$ |
| Try-Read Lock | $\text{tryread}$ | Blocks new readers (Writer pref.) | $1$ |
| Mutual Exclusion Condition | $N_{\text{eating}} \le \lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ | Max concurrent eaters | For $N=5$, $\le 2$ |
| State Transition (DP) | $\text{THINK} \to \text{HUNGRY} \to \text{EAT}$ | Lifecycle of each philosopher | Cyclic |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar in expressions like $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) must be rendered using the $\mid$ or $\vert$ command in $\text{LaTeX}$ to avoid breaking the markdown table — a KTU 2024 Scheme notation compliance requirement.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dining Philosophers** maps directly to **resource-allocation graphs** in OS kernels, **thread pool schedulers**, and **distributed lock manager** designs (e.g., AWS DynamoDB lock service, Google Chubby).
- **Readers-Writers** is the foundational pattern behind **database transaction isolation levels** (e.g., PostgreSQL's `MVCC`, MySQL's InnoDB locking), **filesystem read/write locks** (`pthread_rwlock_t` in POSIX), and **in-memory cache** coherence protocols in multi-core CPUs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Deadlock Proof for the Naive Dining Philosophers Solution

We need to show that the naive solution **always risks deadlock** under concurrent execution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state:} \quad & \text{fork}[0] = \text{fork}[1] = \text{fork}[2] = \text{fork}[3] = \text{fork}[4] = 1 \\
\text{Each } P_i \text{ executes:} \quad & P_i: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[i]); \text{wait}(\text{fork}[(i+1) \bmod 5])
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — All Philosophers Pick Up Left Fork Simultaneously

Assume the scheduler interleaves the threads such that **every** $P_i$ completes `wait(fork[i])` before any $P_i$ attempts `wait(fork[(i+1) mod 5])`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[0]) \Rightarrow \text{fork}[0] = 0 \\
P_1 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[1]) \Rightarrow \text{fork}[1] = 0 \\
P_2 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[2]) \Rightarrow \text{fork}[2] = 0 \\
P_3 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[3]) \Rightarrow \text{fork}[3] = 0 \\
P_4 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[4]) \Rightarrow \text{fork}[4] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** Each philosopher successfully decrements his left fork semaphore from 1 to 0. No philosopher is blocked yet because the left forks were free.

### Step 2 — All Philosophers Attempt the Right Fork

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[1]) \Rightarrow \text{BLOCKED (fork[1]=0)} \\
P_1 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[2]) \Rightarrow \text{BLOCKED (fork[2]=0)} \\
P_2 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[3]) \Rightarrow \text{BLOCKED (fork[3]=0)} \\
P_3 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[4]) \Rightarrow \text{BLOCKED (fork[4]=0)} \\
P_4 &: \text{wait}(\text{fork}[0]) \Rightarrow \text{BLOCKED (fork[0]=0)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** Every right fork is held by the **neighbouring** philosopher. Each philosopher is now blocked waiting for a fork that will never be released.

### Step 3 — Circular Wait Established

The wait-for graph forms a directed cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 \to P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to P_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** This satisfies all four **Coffman deadlock conditions** (mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait). The system is in a permanent deadlock.

### Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The naive solution **does not guarantee deadlock-freedom** under arbitrary scheduling. Therefore, additional synchronization (room semaphore, ordered acquisition, or a monitor) is **required**.

---

## 3.2 Proof of Correctness for the Readers-Writers Solution

We need to verify:
1. **Mutual exclusion between writers and all others.**
2. **Multiple simultaneous readers permitted.**
3. **Mutual exclusion among writers themselves.**

### 3.2.1 Writer Mutual Exclusion

A writer must pass `wait(wrt)` to enter. Since `wrt` is binary and initialized to 1, only **one process** can hold it at a time. Therefore, two writers cannot simultaneously be in the critical section.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If two writers } W_1, W_2 \text{ exist:} \quad & W_1: \text{wait}(wrt) \Rightarrow wrt = 0 \\
& W_2: \text{wait}(wrt) \Rightarrow \text{BLOCKED}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** $W_2$ cannot enter the database while $W_1$ is writing. The binary semaphore enforces strict mutual exclusion.

### 3.2.2 Reader-Reader Concurrency

Suppose readers $R_1$ and $R_2$ arrive concurrently and $wrt = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &: \text{wait}(\text{mutex}) \Rightarrow \text{readcount} = 0 + 1 = 1 \\
R_1 &: \text{readcount} == 1 \Rightarrow \text{wait}(\text{wrt}) \Rightarrow \text{wrt} = 0 \\
R_1 &: \text{signal}(\text{mutex}) \\
R_2 &: \text{wait}(\text{mutex}) \Rightarrow \text{readcount} = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
R_2 &: \text{readcount} \neq 1 \Rightarrow \text{SKIP wait(wrt)} \\
R_2 &: \text{signal}(\text{mutex}) \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Both } R_1, R_2 \text{ are now in the critical section simultaneously.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** Only the **first** reader acquires `wrt`. Subsequent readers simply increment `readcount` and proceed directly into the read critical section. Multiple readers are therefore allowed to read concurrently.

### 3.2.3 Reader-Writer Mutual Exclusion

A writer can only enter when `wrt = 1`, i.e., when no reader holds it. Since the first reader acquires `wrt` and the last reader releases it, the writer is correctly excluded during any reader's active period.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{When } \text{readcount} > 0: \quad & \text{wrt} = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Writer BLOCKED} \\
\text{When } \text{readcount} = 0: \quad & \text{wrt} = 1 \Rightarrow \text{Writer can proceed}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:** This invariant guarantees the safety property that **no writer ever reads or writes the database concurrently with a reader**.

---

## 3.3 Starvation Analysis Matrix

| Solution | Deadlock-Free? | Reader Starvation? | Writer Starvation? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Naive DP (no synchronization) | **No** | Possible | Possible |
| DP with `room = N-1` | **Yes** | No | No |
| DP with ordered fork acquisition | **Yes** | No | Possible (for some $P_i$) |
| DP Monitor (Hoare) | **Yes** | No | No |
| Readers-Writers V1 (Reader Pref.) | Yes | **No** | **Yes** |
| Readers-Writers V2 (Writer Pref.) | Yes | **Yes** | **No** |
| Readers-Writers V3 (Fair) | Yes | **No** | **No** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked "which solution is starvation-free for both?", the correct answer is **V3 (Fair Solution with FIFO queue)** or the **Monitor-based Dining Philosophers solution**.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Pseudocode Block — Side-by-Side Semaphore Logic

### Dining Philosophers (Solution 2 — Ordered Acquisition)

```text
semaphore fork[5] = {1, 1, 1, 1, 1};

process Philosopher(int i):
    while (true):
        think();
        if (i % 2 == 0):           // even philosopher
            wait(fork[i]);
            wait(fork[(i + 1) % 5]);
            eat();
            signal(fork[i]);
            signal(fork[(i + 1) % 5]);
        else:                       // odd philosopher
            wait(fork[(i + 1) % 5]);
            wait(fork[i]);
            eat();
            signal(fork[(i + 1) % 5]);
            signal(fork[i]);
```

### Readers-Writers (Reader Preference)

```text
semaphore mutex = 1, wrt = 1;
int readcount = 0;

process Reader():
    wait(mutex);
    readcount++;
    if (readcount == 1) wait(wrt);
    signal(mutex);
        // --- Reading Section ---
    wait(mutex);
    readcount--;
    if (readcount == 0) signal(wrt);
    signal(mutex);

process Writer():
    wait(wrt);
        // --- Writing Section ---
    signal(wrt);
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Dining Philosophers — Circular Resource Allocation

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0((P0)) --- F0([F0])
    F0 --- P1((P1))
    P1 --- F1([F1])
    F1 --- P2((P2))
    P2 --- F2([F2])
    F2 --- P3((P3))
    P3 --- F3([F3])
    F3 --- P4((P4))
    P4 --- F4([F4])
    F4 --- P0
    P0 -.HUNGRY.-> P0
    P1 -.HUNGRY.-> P1
    P2 -.HUNGRY.-> P2
    P3 -.HUNGRY.-> P3
    P4 -.HUNGRY.-> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each philosopher node (`P0`–`P4`) is connected to two fork nodes (`F0`–`F4`). A philosopher requires **both** adjacent forks to transition to the EATING state. The self-loop on each philosopher represents the THINKING state.

## 4.2 State Machine — Philosopher Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> THINKING
    THINKING --> HUNGRY : feel hungry
    HUNGRY --> EATING : both forks acquired
    EATING --> THINKING : put down forks
    HUNGRY --> HUNGRY : wait for forks
    EATING --> [*] : optional exit
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transition from `HUNGRY` to `EATING` is gated by a successful `wait` on **both** adjacent fork semaphores. If either `wait` blocks, the philosopher remains in the `HUNGRY` state.

## 4.3 Readers-Writers — Access Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SharedResource["Shared Database / File"]
        DB[(Shared Data)]
    end
    subgraph SynchronizationLayer["Synchronization Layer"]
        M[Mutex Semaphore<br/>Initial Value 1]
        W[Wrt Semaphore<br/>Initial Value 1]
        RC[Readcount<br/>Initial Value 0]
    end
    R1[Reader Process R1] --> M
    R2[Reader Process R2] --> M
    RN[Reader Process RN] --> M
    WR[Writer Process W1] --> W
    M --> RC
    RC --> W
    M --> DB
    W --> DB
    WR --> W
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** All readers first pass through `Mutex` to update `Readcount`. Only the **first reader** (when `Readcount == 1`) acquires `Wrt`, which then blocks the writer. The last reader releases `Wrt`. Writers access the database exclusively via `Wrt`.

## 4.4 Deadlock Visualization — Wait-For Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0((P0)) -->|waits for F1| P1((P1))
    P1((P1)) -->|waits for F2| P2((P2))
    P2((P2)) -->|waits for F3| P3((P3))
    P3((P3)) -->|waits for F4| P4((P4))
    P4((P4)) -->|waits for F0| P0((P0))
```

> [!WARNING]
> The directed cycle $P_0 \to P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to P_0$ is the graphical signature of a **deadlock** in the naive solution. Each philosopher is waiting for a fork held by the next, forming an unbreakable cycle.

## 4.5 Solution Flow — Monitor-Based DP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Philosopher i feels hungry] --> B[Call pickup i]
    B --> C[Set state i to HUNGRY]
    C --> D[Call test i]
    D --> E{Left neighbor eating?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Wait on self i]
    E -->|No| G{Right neighbor eating?}
    G -->|Yes| F
    G -->|No| H[Set state i to EATING]
    H --> I[Philosopher i eats]
    I --> J[Call putdown i]
    J --> K[Set state i to THINKING]
    K --> L[Test LEFT neighbor]
    L --> M[Test RIGHT neighbor]
    M --> N[Resume i think cycle]
    F -->|neighbor done| H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are aligned with **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment regulations: Part A (3 marks each) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice, split into two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Dining Philosophers Problem. Explain how it leads to a deadlock using the naive semaphore solution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Dining Philosophers Problem, proposed by Dijkstra, consists of **5 philosophers** sitting around a circular table with **5 forks** placed between them. Each philosopher needs **two adjacent forks** to eat.

In the naive solution, a binary semaphore `fork[i] = 1` is assigned to each fork, and philosopher $P_i$ executes `wait(fork[i]); wait(fork[(i+1) mod 5])`. If all 5 philosophers simultaneously pick up their **left fork** first, every fork semaphore becomes 0. Now each philosopher is blocked on the right fork, which is held by the neighbour. This creates a **circular wait** — a Coffman deadlock — and the system halts permanently. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the First and Second Readers-Writers Problems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **First Readers-Writers Problem (Reader Preference):** Once a reader is in the critical section, all subsequent readers are granted immediate access even if a writer is waiting. This can lead to **writer starvation** if readers arrive continuously.
- **Second Readers-Writers Problem (Writer Preference):** Once a writer signals its intention to write, no new readers are allowed to enter the critical section. Existing readers finish, and then the writer gets in. This can lead to **reader starvation** if writers keep arriving.

Both ensure mutual exclusion between readers and writers; they differ only in the **fairness policy** used to break ties. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Dining Philosophers Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the Dining Philosophers Problem. Show how the naive solution can lead to a deadlock. Demonstrate the deadlock state using a wait-for graph.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Propose a deadlock-free solution using a **counting semaphore `room`** initialized to $N-1$. Explain why this avoids deadlock. Discuss whether this solution is starvation-free.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Problem Definition [2 marks]:**
The Dining Philosophers Problem models $N$ philosophers (typically 5) alternating between thinking and eating around a circular table. Each philosopher needs two forks (left and right) to eat, but only one fork is placed between any two adjacent philosophers. The challenge is to design a synchronization protocol that allows maximum concurrency while preventing deadlock and starvation.

**2. Naive Semaphore Solution [2 marks]:**
Declare `semaphore fork[5] = {1,1,1,1,1};`. Philosopher $P_i$:
```text
wait(fork[i]); wait(fork[(i+1)%5]); eat();
signal(fork[i]); signal(fork[(i+1)%5]);
```

**3. Deadlock Demonstration [2 marks]:**
If all philosophers are scheduled to execute `wait(fork[i])` first (their left fork), every fork semaphore is decremented to 0. Now each philosopher executes `wait(fork[(i+1)%5])` and is blocked because the right fork is held by the neighbour. The wait-for graph forms a directed cycle $P_0 \to P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to P_0$, satisfying all four Coffman deadlock conditions. **System is permanently deadlocked.**

**4. Wait-for Graph [1 mark]:**
Draw a 5-node cycle showing each philosopher waiting for the fork held by the next philosopher in clockwise order.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Modified Solution Using `room` [3 marks]:**
```text
semaphore fork[5] = {1,1,1,1,1};
semaphore room = 4;                  // N - 1

process Philosopher(int i):
    while (true):
        think();
        wait(room);                  // enter dining room
        wait(fork[i]);
        wait(fork[(i+1)%5]);
        eat();
        signal(fork[i]);
        signal(fork[(i+1)%5]);
        signal(room);                // exit dining room
```

**2. Why Deadlock is Avoided [2 marks]:**
At most **$N-1 = 4$** philosophers can be inside the dining room at any time. This means at least one fork will always be free, so the circular-wait condition cannot be established. The excluded philosopher can grab the free fork and proceed, eventually releasing forks for the others.

**3. Starvation Analysis [2 marks]:**
The `room` semaphore alone does **not** guarantee starvation-freedom. A philosopher could be perpetually excluded from the room if $N-1$ others keep re-entering before he gets a chance. To eliminate starvation, additional fairness mechanisms (e.g., a FIFO queue or a monitor-based solution with condition variables) are required.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Readers-Writers Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] State the Readers-Writers Problem. Write a semaphore-based solution that allows **multiple readers** but enforces **mutual exclusion for writers**. Explain the role of each semaphore.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **starvation problem** in the above solution. Modify the solution to give **preference to writers** and explain how the modification prevents writer starvation.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Problem Statement [1 mark]:**
Multiple processes access a shared database. Readers may access concurrently, but writers need exclusive access. Mutual exclusion must be maintained between a writer and all other processes (readers + writers).

**2. Shared Variables [1 mark]:**
```text
semaphore mutex = 1;       // protects readcount
semaphore wrt   = 1;       // writers' exclusive lock
int readcount   = 0;       // active readers
```

**3. Reader Code [2 marks]:**
```text
wait(mutex);
    readcount++;
    if (readcount == 1) wait(wrt);
signal(mutex);
// --- Reading Critical Section ---
wait(mutex);
    readcount--;
    if (readcount == 0) signal(wrt);
signal(mutex);
```

**4. Writer Code [1 mark]:**
```text
wait(wrt);
// --- Writing Critical Section ---
signal(wrt);
```

**5. Role of Each Semaphore [2 marks]:**
- `mutex` ensures atomic update of the shared variable `readcount` (so that the `if readcount == 1` test is correct).
- `wrt` provides exclusive access to the database. The **first** reader acquires it (blocking all writers); the **last** reader releases it. A writer acquires it to enter alone.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Starvation Issue in V1 [2 marks]:**
