## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why Swapping Matters |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems** | IoT devices use swap to run larger apps on limited RAM |
| **Smartphones (Android/iOS)** | ZRAM and swap partitions allow more apps to stay "open" |
| **Cloud Servers (AWS/Azure)** | Swap space handles memory spikes in virtualized environments |
| **Real-Time Systems** | Swapping is often **disabled** because it introduces **non-deterministic latency** |
| **Compilers / IDEs** | Heavy codebases may use swap when RAM is exhausted |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** In **Hard Real-Time Systems**, swapping is **strictly prohibited** because the disk's worst-case access time is unbounded, breaking real-time guarantees.

## 2.4 Variants of Swapping

### A. Standard Swapping (Whole-Process Swapping)
- Moves **entire process** in and out.
- High overhead.
- Rarely used in modern systems.

### B. Swapping with Paging (Modern Approach)
- Only **inactive pages** are swapped, not the whole process.
- Used in **Linux (swap partitions)**, **Windows (pagefile.sys)**, **macOS (dynamic swap)**.
- Drastically reduces I/O.

### C. Swapping with Mobile Systems (iOS / Android)
- Uses **compressed swap in ZRAM** (Linux kernel feature) for faster access.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Standard Swap Time

We will derive the total time required to perform a complete Roll-Out + Roll-In operation.

### Given Assumptions:
- Process size: $S_{proc}$ (in KB or MB)
- Disk seek time: $T_{seek}$
- Rotational latency: $T_{rot}$
- Disk transfer rate: $R_{transfer}$ (in MB/s or KB/ms)
- Round trip: process is rolled out, then rolled back in

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Time to roll out the process to disk.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer-out}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The disk arm first seeks to the correct track ($T_{seek}$), waits for the correct sector to rotate under the read head ($T_{rot}$), and then transfers the entire process image.

**Step 2:** Calculate the data transfer time for rolling out.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{transfer-out} &= \frac{S_{proc}}{R_{transfer}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Transfer time equals the size of data divided by the rate at which the disk can push bytes.

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= T_{seek} + T_{rot} + \frac{S_{proc}}{R_{transfer}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** By symmetry, time to roll in the process.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in} &= T_{seek} + T_{rot} + \frac{S_{proc}}{R_{transfer}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The same operation happens in reverse — seek, rotate, transfer.

**Step 5:** Total swap time for a full Roll-Out + Roll-In.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{swap} &= T_{out} + T_{in} \\
&= 2 \times (T_{seek} + T_{rot}) + \frac{2 \times S_{proc}}{R_{transfer}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Generalized Formula:**

$$
\boxed{T_{swap} = 2 \cdot T_{seek} + 2 \cdot T_{rot} + \frac{2 \cdot S_{proc}}{R_{transfer}}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

### **Problem Statement:**
A system uses standard swapping. A process of size **100 KB** needs to be rolled out and rolled back in. The disk has:
- Average seek time = **8 ms**
- Rotational latency = **4 ms**
- Transfer rate = **1 MB/ms** (= 1024 KB/ms)

**Find the total swap time.**

### **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{proc} &= 100 \text{ KB} \\
T_{seek} &= 8 \text{ ms} \\
T_{rot} &= 4 \text{ ms} \\
R_{transfer} &= 1024 \text{ KB/ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the transfer time for one direction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{transfer} &= \frac{S_{proc}}{R_{transfer}} = \frac{100}{1024} \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Roll-Out time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer} \\
&= 8 + 4 + \frac{100}{1024} \\
&= 12.0976 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Roll-In time (same as Roll-Out).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in} &= 12.0976 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Total swap time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{swap} &= T_{out} + T_{in} \\
&= 2 \times 12.0976 \\
&= 24.1953 \text{ ms} \\
&\approx \mathbf{24.2 \text{ ms}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Even for a small 100 KB process, swapping takes **~24 ms** — this is **~24,000 times slower** than a typical RAM access (~100 ns). This is why swapping is avoided when possible.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Standard Swapping | Swapping with Paging | No Swapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Whole process | Individual pages | N/A |
| **I/O Volume** | Very High | Low (only dirty pages) | None |
| **Swap Time** | Very High | Low | 0 |
| **Complexity** | Low | High | None |
| **Used In** | Older OS (e.g., early UNIX) | Modern OS (Linux, Windows) | Real-time / Embedded |
| **Context Switch Impact** | High | Moderate | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Roll-Out and Roll-In Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Ready in Main Memory] --> B{Is Memory Full?}
    B -- No --> C[Continue Normal Execution]
    B -- Yes --> D[Medium-Term Scheduler Activated]
    D --> E[Select Victim Process - Lowest Priority]
    E --> F[Suspend the Process - State becomes Suspended]
    F --> G[Copy Entire Process Image to Swap Space on Disk]
    G --> H[Update PCB and Free Frame Table]
    H --> I[Free RAM Frames Now Available]
    I --> J{New Process Needs Memory?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Allocate Frames to New Process]
    J -- No --> L[Wait for Event Trigger]
    L --> M[Roll-In Selected Process from Swap Space]
    M --> N[Update PCB - State becomes Ready]
    N --> O[Process Resumes Execution in Main Memory]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Layout During Swapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAIN_MEMORY[MAIN MEMORY - RAM]
        A1[OS Kernel - Always Resident]
        A2[Process A - Active]
        A3[Process B - Active]
        A4[Free Frames]
    end

    subgraph SWAP[SECONDARY STORAGE - SWAP SPACE]
        B1[Process C - Swapped Out]
        B2[Process D - Swapped Out]
        B3[Free Swap Space]
    end

    A2 -.->|Roll Out if needed| B1
    B1 -.->|Roll In when scheduled| A4
    A3 -.->|Roll Out if needed| B2
    B2 -.->|Roll In when scheduled| A4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Swapping Decision Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready : Admitted
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preemption or Interrupt
    Running --> SuspendedReady : Roll Out Triggered
    Ready --> SuspendedReady : Memory Pressure
    SuspendedReady --> Ready : Roll In Triggered
    Running --> Terminated : Exit
    Ready --> Terminated : Exit
    SuspendedReady --> Terminated : Killed or Exit
    Terminated --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Suspended States:** Suspended-Ready and Suspended-Blocked (not shown) are two additional states introduced by **medium-term scheduling** to handle swapping. The process is **suspended** (not in RAM) but is either **ready to run** or **waiting for an I/O event**.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Swapping Subsystem Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph USER_LEVEL[User Space]
        U1[Application Processes]
    end

    subgraph OS_LEVEL[OS Kernel - Memory Management Subsystem]
        M1[Memory Manager]
        M2[Medium-Term Scheduler]
        M3[Swap Manager / Swapper]
        M4[Page Table Manager]
        M5[Free Frame Table]
    end

    subgraph HARDWARE_LEVEL[Hardware Layer]
        H1[Main Memory - RAM]
        H2[Swap Partition on Disk]
        H3[Disk Controller]
    end

    U1 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> H1
    M3 --> H2
    M3 --> M4
    M3 --> M5
    H2 --> H3
    H1 <--> H3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Swapping. What are Roll-Out and Roll-In operations?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Swapping is a memory management technique in which the OS moves **entire processes** between the **main memory (RAM)** and the **secondary storage (backing store)** to efficiently utilize the limited physical memory.

- **Roll-Out (Swap-Out):** The operation of moving a process **out of main memory** to the swap space on the disk, typically when memory is needed for higher-priority processes. The process state changes to `Suspended-Ready`.

- **Roll-In (Swap-In):** The operation of **bringing a process back from the disk swap space** into main memory for continued execution. The process state changes from `Suspended-Ready` to `Ready`.

> **KTU Key Point (1 mark):** Swapping allows the OS to support **more processes** than what can physically fit in RAM, thereby increasing the **degree of multiprogramming**.

---

### **Question 2: List any three advantages and two disadvantages of swapping.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Higher Degree of Multiprogramming:** More processes can be kept in the system simultaneously.
2. **Efficient Memory Utilization:** Inactive processes are moved out, freeing RAM for active ones.
3. **Priority-Based Execution:** Low-priority processes can be rolled out to make room for high-priority ones.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **High I/O Overhead:** Disk operations are extremely slow (~ms) compared to RAM (~ns).
2. **Context Switching Cost:** Extra context switches and PCB updates increase CPU overhead.

> **Bonus Point (1 mark):** In **real-time systems**, swapping is generally disabled because it introduces **non-deterministic delays**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **🅰️ QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the concept of swapping in operating systems. Discuss the standard swap time formula with a suitable example. How does swapping with paging differ from standard swapping?**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the concept of swapping and illustrate with a diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

Swapping is a memory management scheme in which the OS temporarily moves processes between main memory and a backing store (disk).

**Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.2):**
The diagram shows main memory with active processes (A, B) and free frames, while the swap space on disk contains rolled-out processes (C, D). Arrows depict Roll-Out and Roll-In operations.

**Operational Steps:**
1. The medium-term scheduler monitors the **degree of multiprogramming**.
2. When RAM is full, a **victim process** is selected.
3. The process is **suspended**, and its entire image is copied to the swap space.
4. The freed frames are allocated to a new or higher-priority process.
5. Later, the suspended process is **rolled back in** for continued execution.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the swap time formula and compare swapping with paging.**

**Model Answer:**

**Swap Time Derivation:**

$$
T_{swap} = 2 \times T_{seek} + 2 \times T_{rot} + \frac{2 \times S_{proc}}{R_{transfer}}
$$

(Detailed derivation as shown in SECTION 3.1.)

**Numerical Example:**
For a process of size 200 KB, with $T_{seek} = 5$ ms, $T_{rot} = 5$ ms, $R_{transfer} = 200$ KB/ms:

$$
T_{swap} = 2(5) + 2(5) + \frac{2 \times 200}{200} = 10 + 10 + 2 = \mathbf{22 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Standard Swapping | Swapping with Paging |
|---|---|---|
| Unit | Entire process | Page (4 KB typically) |
| I/O Volume | Very High | Low |
| Used in | Early OS | Modern OS |
| Address Binding | Runtime/Dynamic | Paging-based |

---

### **🅱️ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the role of the medium-term scheduler in swapping. Describe the suspended states of a process. Why is swapping unsuitable for real-time systems?**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the medium-term scheduler and suspended states.**

**Model Answer:**

The **medium-term scheduler** is a key OS component that handles the swapping of processes between main memory and the secondary store. It exists alongside the **long-term scheduler** (which controls the degree of multiprogramming) and the **short-term scheduler** (which selects processes for CPU execution).

**Suspended States (refer to SECTION 4.3 for state diagram):**
- **Suspended-Ready:** The process is **not in RAM** but is ready to execute as soon as it is rolled in. It is waiting only for the CPU.
- **Suspended-Blocked:** The process is **not in RAM** and is **waiting for some event** (e.g., I/O completion). It cannot be rolled in until that event occurs.

**Significance:**
- Reduces memory pressure without terminating processes.
- Allows the OS to hold a **larger process queue** than physical memory permits.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Why is swapping unsuitable for real-time systems? Discuss with supporting arguments.**

**Model Answer:**

Real-time systems have **strict timing deadlines** that **must be met** for correct operation. Swapping introduces **unpredictable and unbounded latency** because of the following reasons:

1. **Disk I/O Latency is Non-Deterministic:**
   The total swap time formula contains $T_{seek}$ and $T_{rot}$ — these depend on the **current position of the disk arm** and the **rotational position of the platter**, both of which are **variable**.

2. **Variable Process Sizes:**
   Larger processes require longer transfer times, making worst-case execution time analysis impossible.

3. **Hard Real-Time Constraint Violation:**
   A hard real-time system (e.g., missile control, pacemakers) cannot tolerate missing a deadline. Swapping can cause **priority inversion** and **unbounded blocking**, leading to potential **system failure**.

4. **Better Alternatives Exist:**
   Real-time systems use **static memory allocation**, **paging with locked pages**, or **no virtual memory at all** to guarantee timing predictability.

> **Conclusion:** Swapping is **forbidden** in safety-critical and hard real-time systems (e.g., **RTOS like VxWorks, QNX** disable swapping by design).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Swapping with Paging** — Swapping moves the **entire process**; paging moves only **pages**. Writing "swapping moves pages" will cost you **2-3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Factor of 2** — The total swap time is for **roll-out + roll-in**. Many students compute only one direction, losing **3 marks**.
> 3. **Not Mentioning Address Binding** — Swapping requires **dynamic/runtime binding**. Writing only the formula without this justification loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the Diagram** — In Part B questions (14 marks), a diagram is **MANDATORY**. Skipping it typically costs **2-3 marks**.
> 5. **Using "Swap Time = Process Size / Transfer Rate"** — This is **incomplete**. You MUST include $T_{seek}$ and $T_{rot}$ for full marks.
> 6. **Confusing Roll-In with Context Switch** — They are **different**. Roll-in is **disk-to-RAM**, context switch is **CPU-to-CPU** between processes.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔹 Quick Revision Bullet Points

- **Swapping** is the movement of **entire processes** between **main memory** and **backing store**.
- **Roll-Out** = RAM → Disk ; **Roll-In** = Disk → RAM.
- It is managed by the **Medium-Term Scheduler**.
- Suspended states introduced: **Suspended-Ready** and **Suspended-Blocked**.
- Requires **dynamic/runtime address binding** to work correctly.
- The **standard swap time formula** is:

$$
T_{swap} = 2 \times (T_{seek} + T_{rot}) + \frac{2 \times S_{proc}}{R_{transfer}}
$$

- Modern OS use **Swapping with Paging** (e.g., `pagefile.sys` in Windows, `swap partition` in Linux).
- **Disallowed in real-time systems** due to non-deterministic disk I/O latency.
- Swapping **increases the degree of multiprogramming** beyond physical RAM size.
- Swap space is typically **1x to 2x** the size of physical RAM (modern Linux).
- **Mobile OS** (Android, iOS) use **compressed swap in ZRAM** for performance.

### 🔹 KTU 2024 High-Yield Mnemonic: **"S.W.A.P."**
- **S** — Suspend the process
- **W** — Write the image to backing store
- **A** — Allocate freed frames to a new process
- **P** — Pull (roll-in) the process back when needed

### 🔹 Must-Remember Numbers for KTU Exams

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Standard Page Size | **4 KB** |
| HDD Average Seek | **8 ms** |
| HDD Rotational Latency | **4 ms** (7200 RPM) |
| RAM Access Time | **~100 ns** |
| SSD Access Time | **~0.1 ms** |
| Swap Space (Linux default) | **1x–2x RAM** |

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contiguous Memory Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Contiguous Memory Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Contiguous Memory Allocation** is a classical memory management scheme in which the operating system assigns each process a single, continuous block of physical memory (RAM) of fixed or variable size. Every logical address generated by the process maps to exactly one physical address lying within that single contiguous region. Two hardware registers, the **Base Register** (starting physical address) and the **Limit Register** (size of the allocated region), are used by the Memory Management Unit (MMU) to validate and translate every memory reference at run time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEEET632, Module 3):**
> Contiguous memory allocation covers *Fixed & Variable partitioning*, *First/Best/Worst-Fit allocation*, *Internal vs External Fragmentation*, *Compaction*, and *Address translation using base & limit registers*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Hotel Floor Plan

Imagine a 20-room single-floor hotel. When a guest (a *process*) arrives, the receptionist (the *OS*) must hand over **consecutive rooms** — guest 1 gets rooms 1–4, guest 2 gets rooms 5–6, and so on. The guest is **never** allowed to occupy room 5 here, room 12 there, and room 18 somewhere else. The "starting room" of each guest is stored in a register (Base Register) and the "number of rooms booked" is stored in another (Limit Register). Any guest trying to peek into a room beyond their booking is immediately caught — this is the essence of the *Base–Limit protection scheme*.

## 1.3 Why Contiguous Allocation Matters (KTU Context)

| Perspective | Importance |
|---|---|
| **Hardware** | The simplest memory-protection hardware: just 2 registers per process. |
| **Performance** | Fast address translation — single addition, no page-table lookup. |
| **Pedagogy** | Foundation for understanding paging, segmentation, and virtual memory. |
| **Exam weightage** | A **high-yield topic** — every KTU ESE paper from 2020–2024 has a 7 or 14 mark question on this. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU ESE question on this topic tests **one of three** skills:
> 1. Computing **base + offset** addresses from logical addresses.
> 2. Tracing **First-Fit / Best-Fit / Worst-Fit** allocation on a given memory map.
> 3. Calculating **internal/external fragmentation** and explaining **compaction**.

## 1.4 Key Terms Box

> **[!NOTE]**
> - **Logical (Relative) Address** — Address generated by the CPU; ranges from `0` to `max limit`.
> - **Physical (Absolute) Address** — Real address seen by the memory bus after MMU translation.
> - **Base Register (Relocation Register)** — Holds the starting physical address of the process.
> - **Limit Register** — Holds the size (length) of the allocated memory block.
> - **Hole** — A contiguous block of free memory available for allocation.
> - **Fragmentation** — Wasted memory that cannot be (or is not) used effectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Major Schemes of Contiguous Allocation

### 2.1.1 Fixed (Static) Partitioning
- Memory is divided into a **fixed number of partitions** at system boot.
- Each partition can hold **exactly one process**.
- A queue of processes waits for a free partition.
- **Drawback:** Wastes memory whenever a process is smaller than its partition (Internal Fragmentation). Process size cannot exceed the largest partition.

### 2.1.2 Variable (Dynamic) Partitioning
- Partitions are created **on demand** — exactly the size requested by the process.
- Holes of varying sizes are scattered in memory.
- No internal fragmentation, but over time, free holes become too small to satisfy requests → **External Fragmentation**.

## 2.2 Allocation Strategies (The "Big Three" – KTU Favourite)

| Strategy | Selection Rule | Speed | Memory Utilisation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Fit** | Allocate the **first** hole that is large enough | **Fastest** | Moderate |
| **Best-Fit** | Allocate the **smallest** hole that is large enough | Slow (searches whole list) | Worst (leaves tiny unusable holes) |
| **Worst-Fit** | Allocate the **largest** hole available | Slow | Moderate (leaves large remaining holes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical fact used in KTU answers:** *First-Fit* is generally **faster** and **better** in practice. *Best-Fit* produces the **smallest leftover hole** (often unusable). *Worst-Fit* is theoretically justified only for very long process sequences.

## 2.3 Fragmentation — The Two Villains

### Internal Fragmentation
Memory **wasted inside** an allocated partition because the partition is bigger than the process it holds.

$$I_{frag} = \text{Partition Size} - \text{Process Size}$$

### External Fragmentation
Memory wasted **between** allocated blocks — total free memory exists, but it is **not contiguous**, so it cannot satisfy a single large request.

$$E_{frag} = \left(\sum \text{Hole Sizes}\right) - \text{Largest Single Request that fails}$$

### The 50-Percent Rule (KTU Short-Answer Favourite)
Statistical result by Knuth & Knuth: with *N* allocated blocks, approximately **1/3 of memory becomes externally fragmented** on average. For checking whether a request of size *S* can be satisfied:

$$\text{If total free memory} \geq S \text{ and largest single hole} \geq S \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{May still fail}!$$

## 2.4 Compaction (Defragmentation)

The OS slides all allocated processes toward one end of memory to **merge** scattered holes into a single large hole. Solutions are:
- **Relocation** at run time (requires base-register hardware support — which we already have ✔).
- **Expensive** if done frequently; usually done only at shutdown or at idle time.
- **Not possible** if processes are pinned to physical addresses (e.g., I/O using DMA).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Physical Address | $PA = \text{Base Register} + \text{Logical Address}$ | Bytes; if $LA > \text{Limit}$, **TRAP** |
| 2 | Logical Address Range | $0 \leq LA \leq \text{Limit Register} - 1$ | Inclusive range |
| 3 | Internal Fragmentation | $I = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(P_i - M_i)$ where $P_i$ = partition, $M_i$ = process | Bytes |
| 4 | External Fragmentation | $E = \text{Total Free Memory} - \text{Largest Contiguous Free Block}$ | Bytes |
| 5 | Memory Utilisation | $U = \dfrac{\sum \text{Process Sizes}}{\text{Total Memory}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 6 | Number of Holes after *N* allocations | Roughly $\dfrac{N}{2}$ (statistical) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Compaction feasibility | Possible ⇔ all processes are relocatable | Boolean |
| 8 | 50% Rule check | A request of size *S* succeeds if largest hole $\geq S$ | Boolean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation:** In all LaTeX, the absolute-value symbol is rendered as `\vert \cdot \vert` to keep markdown tables safe.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded systems** (no MMU, no paging) use contiguous allocation with a single static partition.
- **RTOS kernels** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) use fixed-size block allocators (a variant of fixed partitioning).
- **GPU memory pools** (CUDA, OpenCL) allocate large contiguous slabs because their DMA engines require physically contiguous buffers.
- The Base–Limit scheme is the **direct ancestor** of modern segment-based protection in x86 (real mode) and ARM TrustZone.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Address Translation

### 3.1.1 Logical vs Physical Address Mapping

Let:
- $B$ = contents of the **Base (Relocation) Register** = starting physical address of the process.
- $L$ = contents of the **Limit Register** = length of the process's allocated region (in bytes).
- $LA$ = Logical Address (offset) generated by the CPU.
- $PA$ = Physical Address sent to memory.

**Hardware rule enforced by the MMU on every memory access:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 (Protection check)} &\quad : \quad 0 \leq LA < L \\
\text{Step 2 (Address translation)} &\quad : \quad PA = B + LA
\end{aligned}
$$

If the check in Step 1 fails, a **trap** (segmentation fault) is raised to the OS, and the instruction is aborted.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — Address Translation

> **Problem:** A process has Base Register = **3000** and Limit Register = **1200**. Find the physical address corresponding to logical address **700**. State what happens for logical address **1500**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given} & : \quad B = 3000,\; L = 1200,\; LA = 700 \\
\text{Step 1 (Check)} & : \quad 0 \leq 700 < 1200 \;\; \text{(TRUE, proceed)} \\
\text{Step 2 (Translate)} & : \quad PA = B + LA = 3000 + 700 = 3700 \\
\text{Result} & : \quad \text{Physical Address} = 3700
\end{aligned}
$$

For $LA = 1500$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 (Check)} & : \quad 0 \leq 1500 < 1200 \;\; \text{(FALSE)} \\
\text{Result} & : \quad \text{TRAP – address-out-of-bound error} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples for Each Allocation Strategy

> **Common memory map used in all three cases (memory is a linear list of holes):**
>
> | Hole # | Start Address | Size (KB) |
> |---|---|---|
> | H1 | 100 | 50 |
> | H2 | 200 | 200 |
> | H3 | 450 | 80 |
> | H4 | 600 | 120 |
>
> **Requests arriving in order:** P1 needs **60 KB**, P2 needs **150 KB**, P3 needs **90 KB**.

### 3.2.1 First-Fit Allocation Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{P1 (60 KB):} &\quad \text{Scans H1 (50 — too small), H2 (200 — fits).} \\
                     &\quad \text{Allocate in H2. H2 becomes } 200-60 = 140 \text{ KB remaining.} \\
                     &\quad \text{Hole list: H1(100,50), H2(200,140), H3(450,80), H4(600,120)} \\
\textbf{P2 (150 KB):} &\quad \text{Scans H1 (50 — no), H2 (140 — no), H3 (80 — no), H4 (120 — no).} \\
                     &\quad \text{P2 must WAIT — no hole is large enough.} \\
\textbf{P3 (90 KB):} &\quad \text{Scans H1 (50 — no), H2 (140 — fits).} \\
                     &\quad \text{Allocate in H2. H2 becomes } 140-90 = 50 \text{ KB remaining.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Best-Fit Allocation Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{P1 (60 KB):} &\quad \text{Find smallest hole that fits. H1=50 (no), H3=80 (yes — smallest).} \\
                     &\quad \text{Allocate in H3. H3 becomes } 80-60 = 20 \text{ KB remaining.} \\
                     &\quad \text{Hole list: H1(100,50), H2(200,200), H3(450,20), H4(600,120)} \\
\textbf{P2 (150 KB):} &\quad \text{Smallest fitting hole: H2 (200) wins. Allocate. H2 becomes } 50 \text{ KB.} \\
                     &\quad \text{Hole list: H1(100,50), H2(200,50), H3(450,20), H4(600,120)} \\
\textbf{P3 (90 KB):} &\quad \text{No hole fits. WAIT.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Worst-Fit Allocation Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{P1 (60 KB):} &\quad \text{Largest hole overall is H2 (200). Allocate. H2 becomes } 140 \text{ KB.} \\
\textbf{P2 (150 KB):} &\quad \text{Largest remaining is H4 (120 — no), H2 (140 — no). WAIT.} \\
\textbf{P3 (90 KB):} &\quad \text{Largest is H2 (140). Allocate. H2 becomes } 50 \text{ KB.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In all three strategies, P2 (150 KB) was **starved** — a classic KTU illustration of how a request can fail even when total free memory is sufficient (i.e., **external fragmentation**).

## 3.3 Fragmentation Calculation — Compact Worked Example

> **Fixed partitioning scenario:** Memory = 256 KB, divided into 4 partitions of 64 KB each. Processes P1(50 KB), P2(40 KB), P3(60 KB), P4(45 KB) arrive.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total internal fragmentation} &= \sum (\text{Partition Size} - \text{Process Size}) \\
&= (64-50) + (64-40) + (64-60) + (64-45) \\
&= 14 + 24 + 4 + 19 \\
&= 61 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory Utilisation } U &= \frac{50+40+60+45}{4 \times 64} \times 100 = \frac{195}{256} \times 100 \approx 76.17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the *fixed* scheme, the **61 KB** lost is *internal fragmentation*. Notice that even the 19 KB wasted by P4 is *inside* its own partition and can never be used by another process.

## 3.4 Compaction Derivation

Let the hole list before compaction be $H = \{h_1, h_2, ..., h_k\}$ in address order, separated by processes $P_1, P_2, ..., P_n$. After compacting all processes toward the **low end** of memory:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New address of } P_i &= \text{End of } P_{i-1} \\
\text{Total new largest hole} &= \text{Total Memory} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Size of } P_i) \\
\text{Compaction cost} &= O\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Size of } P_i\right) \;\; \text{bytes to be moved}
\end{aligned}$$

In practice, only **relocatable** processes (those whose addresses go through the base register) can be moved.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Memory Layout under Variable Partitioning

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Low Address 0]:::addr --> B[Operating System 0 to 100 KB]:::os
    B --> C[Hole 100 KB - 140 KB]:::hole
    C --> D[Process P1 140 KB - 240 KB]:::proc
    D --> E[Hole 240 KB - 260 KB]:::hole
    E --> F[Process P2 260 KB - 410 KB]:::proc
    F --> G[Hole 410 KB - 440 KB]:::hole
    G --> H[Process P3 440 KB - 500 KB]:::proc
    H --> I[Hole 500 KB - 640 KB]:::hole
    I --> J[High Address 640 KB]:::addr

    classDef addr fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef os fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef proc fill:#548235,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef hole fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

> The **red blocks** are holes (free memory) and the **green blocks** are allocated processes. The OS occupies the low-address region in this example.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Address Translation using Base and Limit Registers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU[CPU generates Logical Address LA]:::cpu --> CHK{Is LA less than Limit Register}:::gate
    CHK -->|NO| TRAP[Raise Trap - Segmentation Fault]:::trap
    CHK -->|YES| ADD[Compute PA = Base + LA]:::arith
    ADD --> MEM[Send PA to Physical Memory]:::mem
    TRAP --> OS[OS Handles Fault - Kill or Recover]:::osbox

    classDef cpu fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef gate fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef trap fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef arith fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef osbox fill:#404040,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Contiguous Allocator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REQ[Request Pipeline]
        R1[New Process Arrives]:::step --> R2[Read Requested Size S]:::step
        R2 --> R3[Walk Hole List]:::step
    end

    REQ --> STRAT{Choose Strategy}:::gate
    STRAT -->|First Fit| FF[Allocate First Hole greater than S]:::step
    STRAT -->|Best Fit| BF[Allocate Smallest Hole greater than S]:::step
    STRAT -->|Worst Fit| WF[Allocate Largest Hole]:::step

    FF --> UPD1[Split Hole - Update Hole List]:::step
    BF --> UPD2[Split Hole - Update Hole List]:::step
    WF --> UPD3[Split Hole - Update Hole List]:::step

    UPD1 --> LOAD[Load Base and Limit Registers]:::step
    UPD2 --> LOAD
    UPD3 --> LOAD
    LOAD --> RUN[Process Begins Execution]:::step

    classDef step fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A request flows through the *Request Pipeline*, picks a strategy at the *Strategy gate*, splits a hole, loads the Base–Limit registers, and finally launches the process. The arrows form a *single execution thread* with no concurrency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between internal and external fragmentation. Which one occurs in fixed partitioning and which in variable partitioning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Internal fragmentation** is the wasted space *inside* an allocated partition when the partition is larger than the process. It occurs in **fixed (static) partitioning** because each partition is of a fixed size larger than the process.
- **External fragmentation** is the wasted free memory scattered as small holes *between* allocated blocks. It occurs in **variable (dynamic) partitioning** because free space is broken up as processes leave.
- *Awarding:* 1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for correct association.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the base register and limit register in contiguous memory allocation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Base (Relocation) Register** stores the *starting physical address* of the process in memory. It is used by the MMU to translate every logical address to a physical address via the formula $PA = B + LA$.
- The **Limit Register** stores the *size* of the memory region allocated to the process. Before translation, the MMU checks $0 \leq LA < L$; if the check fails, a trap is raised, protecting other processes.
- *Awarding:* 1 mark for base-register role, 1 mark for limit-register role, 1 mark for protection purpose.

---

## Part B — Full-Question Set (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** A 14-mark question has two sub-parts — typically (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. The student must answer *either* the entire Question A *or* the entire Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

> Consider a system with 640 KB of user memory. The memory is allocated using **variable partitioning**. At a particular instant, the hole list is: `Hole1: 100 KB starting at address 200 KB`, `Hole2: 250 KB starting at address 500 KB`, `Hole3: 90 KB starting at address 100 KB`, `Hole4: 150 KB starting at address 700 KB`. **Hole3 appears first** in the address order. (All holes are below 800 KB.)
>
> **(a)** Allocate the following processes in order using **First-Fit**: P1 = 120 KB, P2 = 80 KB, P3 = 160 KB. Show the resulting hole list after each allocation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Repeat the allocation using **Best-Fit** on the same initial hole list, for the same three processes. Compare the quality of the two strategies in this example. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — First-Fit (7 marks)

**Initial sorted hole list** (in address order):

| Hole | Start | Size |
|---|---|---|
| H3 | 100 KB | 90 KB |
| H1 | 200 KB | 100 KB |
| H2 | 500 KB | 250 KB |
| H4 | 700 KB | 150 KB |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{P1 = 120 KB:} &\quad \text{Scan from H3. H3=90 (no), H1=100 (no), H2=250 (YES).} \\
                      &\quad \text{Allocate in H2. Remaining H2 size} = 250 - 120 = 130 \text{ KB.} \\
                      &\quad \text{New hole list: H3(100,90), H1(200,100), H2(630,130), H4(700,150)} \\
\textbf{P2 = 80 KB:}  &\quad \text{Scan from H3. H3=90 (YES — first fit!).} \\
                      &\quad \text{Allocate in H3. Remaining H3 size} = 90 - 80 = 10 \text{ KB.} \\
                      &\quad \text{New hole list: H3(100,10), H1(200,100), H2(630,130), H4(700,150)} \\
\textbf{P3 = 160 KB:} &\quad \text{Scan from H3. H3=10 (no), H1=100 (no), H2=130 (no), H4=150 (no).} \\
                      &\quad \text{P3 must WAIT (external fragmentation).}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Mark split:* 1 mark for each successful allocation + 1 mark for correctly identifying that P3 waits + 1 mark for showing the final hole list.

#### Part (b) — Best-Fit (7 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{P1 = 120 KB:} &\quad \text{Smallest hole that fits. Scan: H3=90 (no), H1=100 (no), H2=250 (yes), H4=150 (yes).} \\
                      &\quad \text{Smallest fitting = H4 (150 KB). Allocate in H4. Remaining H4} = 30 \text{ KB.} \\
                      &\quad \text{New hole list: H3(100,90), H1(200,100), H2(500,250), H4(730,30)} \\
\textbf{P2 = 80 KB:}  &\quad \text{Smallest hole that fits. H3=90 (yes), H1=100 (yes), H2=250 (yes), H4=30 (no).} \\
                      &\quad \text{Smallest fitting = H3 (90 KB). Allocate. Remaining H3} = 10 \text{ KB.} \\
                      &\quad \text{New hole list: H3(100,10), H1(200,100), H2(500,250), H4(730,30)} \\
\textbf{P3 = 160 KB:} &\quad \text{Scan: H3=10 (no), H1=100 (no), H2=250 (YES), H4=30 (no).} \\
                      &\quad \text{Smallest fitting = H2 (250 KB). Allocate. Remaining H2} = 90 \text{ KB.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table for Full Marks (2 marks):**

| Metric | First-Fit | Best-Fit |
|---|---|---|
| P3 status | **Waits (starved)** | **Allocated** ✔ |
| Successful allocations | 2/3 | **3/3** ✔ |
| Average leftover hole size | 70 KB | 43.3 KB |
| Number of processes served | 2 | 3 |

**Conclusion:** In this example, **Best-Fit succeeded** in serving all three processes while First-Fit starved P3, despite both leaving scattered small holes. This contradicts the textbook generalisation that *First-Fit* is always better — and is exactly the kind of nuance a KTU examiner rewards.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** A process is loaded with **Base Register = 5000** and **Limit Register = 1500**. Compute the physical addresses for the following logical addresses, and indicate any traps. **(7 marks)**
>
> | Logical Address | Compute Physical Address |
> |---|---|
> | (i) 0 | |
> | (ii) 500 | |
> | (iii) 1499 | |
> | (iv) 1500 | |
> | (v) 999 | |
> | (vi) 1501 | |
> | (vii) 1000 | |

> **(b)** Define **compaction**. Explain why it may be infeasible in some systems. Also write one alternative to compaction for handling external fragmentation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Address Computation (7 marks)

Using $PA = B + LA = 5000 + LA$, with the protection check $0 \leq LA < 1500$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) LA=0}    &\;\Rightarrow\; 0 \leq 0 < 1500 \;\checkmark\; \Rightarrow\; PA = 5000 \\
\text{(ii) LA=500}  &\;\Rightarrow\; 0 \leq 500 < 1500 \;\checkmark\; \Rightarrow\; PA = 5500 \\
\text{(iii) LA=1499} &\;\Rightarrow\; 0 \leq 1499 < 1500 \;\checkmark\; \Rightarrow\; PA = 6499 \\
\text{(iv) LA=1500}  &\;\Rightarrow\; 0 \leq 1500 < 1500 \;\mathbf{\times} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{TRAP} \\
\text{(v) LA=999}   &\;\Rightarrow\; 0 \leq 999 < 1500 \;\checkmark\; \Rightarrow\; PA = 5999 \\
\text{(vi) LA=1501}  &\;\Rightarrow\; 0 \leq 1501 < 1500 \;\mathbf{\times} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{TRAP} \\
\text{(vii) LA=1000} &\;\Rightarrow\; 0 \leq 1000 < 1500 \;\checkmark\; \Rightarrow\; PA = 6000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Mark split:* 1 mark for each correctly computed address, with 0 marks awarded if the protection check is omitted for the boundary cases (LA = 1500, 1501).

#### Part (b) — Compaction (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Compaction is a technique in which the OS shifts all allocated processes to one end of memory so that all free holes are merged into a single large contiguous free block, thereby eliminating external fragmentation.

**Why infeasible in some systems (3 marks):**
- **I/O via DMA / memory-mapped I/O:** Some I/O devices perform direct memory access using *physical addresses*. Moving a process moves its physical addresses, breaking the I/O transfer in progress.
- **Cost:** Compaction requires copying all process memory, which is $O(\text{total memory size})$ — prohibitive in real-time or high-utilisation systems.
- **Non-relocatable processes:** If a process was linked to run at fixed physical addresses (rare, but possible in older embedded binaries), it cannot be moved.

**Alternative (2 marks):** The most widely used alternative is **Paging**, which eliminates external fragmentation entirely by allowing a process to occupy non-contiguous physical frames. **Swapping** (moving entire processes to secondary storage) and **segmentation** are also accepted alternatives.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (MUST AVOID):**
> - **Mistake 1:** In Part (a), students often forget the protection check and blindly compute $PA = 5000 + 1500 = 6500$. The correct answer is **TRAP**. Loss: **1 mark per such case**.
> - **Mistake 2:** In Part (b), many students write "compaction rearranges memory" without stating *what* it rearranges (processes) and *why* (to merge holes). Loss: **1 mark** for vague definition.
> - **Mistake 3:** Writing *virtual memory* as the alternative to compaction is **too broad** — virtual memory is a *family* of techniques, not a single one. Mention **paging** specifically for full credit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Contiguous allocation gives each process **one** continuous physical block.
- ✅ Two registers: **Base** (start) and **Limit** (size). Translation is $PA = B + LA$.
- ✅ **Fixed partitioning** → **Internal** fragmentation only.
- ✅ **Variable partitioning** → **External** fragmentation only.
- ✅ **First-Fit** is fastest; **Best-Fit** minimises leftover size; **Worst-Fit** is rarely best.
- ✅ A process may **wait** even if total free memory is enough — that is external fragmentation in action.
- ✅ **Compaction** is the brute-force fix; **Paging** is the elegant fix.
- ✅ The MMU performs the limit check **before** the base addition on every memory reference.
- ✅ Boundary case rule: $0 \leq LA < L$ (strictly less than the limit).
- ✅ **50% Rule:** ~1/3 of memory is externally fragmented on average (Knuth).
- ✅ Compaction is impossible for processes doing DMA-based I/O at fixed physical addresses.
- ✅ Address translation is **dynamic** — the same logical address yields different physical addresses in different processes.
- ✅ Logical address space is **process-specific**; physical address space is **system-global**.
- ✅ KTU 2024 examiners love: a 7-mark numerical on First-Fit + a 7-mark theoretical on the Base–Limit scheme.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Students:** In every answer, **show the protection check explicitly** when computing addresses, **sort holes by address** before allocation, and **explicitly state** whether fragmentation is internal or external. These three habits alone will lift a 10-mark answer to a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed and Variable Partitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fixed and Variable Partitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Memory Management Schemes

In multiprogrammed operating systems, the main memory must be divided among multiple competing processes. **Memory Management** is the functional responsibility of the OS that handles allocation and deallocation of memory blocks to processes. Among the earliest and most fundamental memory partitioning strategies are **Fixed (Static) Partitions** and **Variable (Dynamic) Partitions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Fixed Partitioning** is a memory management technique in which the main memory is divided into a fixed number of partitions (of equal or unequal size) at system boot/initialization time. Each partition can hold exactly **one** process.
> **Variable Partitioning** is a memory management technique in which partitions are created dynamically based on the exact size requirement of the incoming process. The number, size, and location of partitions vary with time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Hotel Room" Analogy**
> Imagine a hotel (Main Memory) with rooms (Partitions) and guests (Processes).
>
> 1. **Fixed Partitioning** is like a hotel that pre-decides room sizes — say 10 rooms of 200 sq.ft, 10 rooms of 400 sq.ft, and 5 rooms of 600 sq.ft. If a solo traveler (small process) is allotted a 600 sq.ft room, the remaining space (say 550 sq.ft) is **wasted** — this is *Internal Fragmentation*. Rooms cannot be merged or split dynamically.
>
> 2. **Variable Partitioning** is like an Airbnb-style rental where a room is cut exactly to the size of the guest — a guest needing 150 sq.ft gets exactly 150 sq.ft, leaving the rest available. However, over time, many small gaps appear between allocated blocks, leaving no single *contiguous* space for a large guest. This is *External Fragmentation*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Terms (Must Memorize for KTU Exam)**
> - **Internal Fragmentation**: Wasted memory *inside* an allocated partition (Fixed Partitions).
> - **External Fragmentation**: Wasted memory *between* allocated partitions (Variable Partitions).
> - **Compaction**: OS technique to combine scattered free holes into one contiguous block.
> - **Placement Strategy / Allocation Algorithm**: The policy used to choose *which* free hole to allocate to a process (First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit).
> - **Hole**: A contiguous block of free (unallocated) memory.

The standard metric used in KTU numerical problems is the **degree of multiprogramming** (number of processes resident in memory) and the **memory utilization efficiency**, typically expressed as a percentage. The unit of memory is conventionally expressed in **KB (Kilobytes)** or **MB (Megabytes)**, with a standard page size of **4 KB** in modern systems (though not required for this module).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fixed (Static) Partitions — Operational Breakdown

In a fixed partition system, the OS designer pre-divides memory at **system generation time**. Two variants exist:

### 2.1.1 Equal-Size Fixed Partitions
- Memory is sliced into **N** partitions of identical size.
- **Advantage**: Simple design, easy to implement.
- **Disadvantage**: Severe **internal fragmentation** when small processes occupy large partitions.
- **Restriction**: A process whose size exceeds the partition size **cannot be loaded**.

### 2.1.2 Unequal-size Fixed Partitions
- Memory is divided into partitions of varying sizes (e.g., 100 KB, 200 KB, 300 KB, 500 KB).
- Each process is assigned to the smallest partition that can accommodate it (queue-based).
- **Disadvantage**: Still suffers internal fragmentation, but at a lower rate than equal-size.

### 2.1.3 Operational Steps (Fixed Partition)
1. The OS maintains a **partition table** (base register + limit register per partition).
2. A process is loaded into a free partition matching its size.
3. After execution, the partition is marked **free** and the next waiting process is loaded.
4. The OS never re-sizes partitions dynamically.

## 2.2 Variable (Dynamic) Partitions — Operational Breakdown

Here, no pre-defined partition boundaries exist. A new process is allocated exactly the memory it requests.

### 2.2.1 Operational Steps
1. OS maintains a **linked list of holes** (free memory blocks).
2. On a memory request of size *N*, OS scans the hole list using a placement algorithm.
3. The chosen hole is split — *N bytes* are allocated, and the remaining (hole-size − N) bytes become a new (smaller) hole.
4. On process termination, the freed block is **coalesced** (merged) with adjacent free holes if possible.
5. If no single hole is large enough, the process must wait (or compaction occurs).

## 2.3 Placement Strategies (Allocation Algorithms)

| Strategy | Logic | Strength | Weakness |
|----------|-------|----------|----------|
| **First-Fit** | Allocate the **first** hole large enough | Fastest; simple | May leave small unusable holes at the front |
| **Best-Fit** | Allocate the **smallest** hole that fits | Minimizes wasted space per allocation | Slow search; produces tiny unusable fragments |
| **Worst-Fit** | Allocate the **largest** hole available | Leaves large remaining holes | Slowest; can break large holes prematurely |

## 2.4 Fragmentation — The Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Fragmentation** occurs in **Fixed Partitions** because the allocated partition is *larger* than the process, leaving unused space *inside* the boundary.
>
> **External Fragmentation** occurs in **Variable Partitions** because free memory exists but is *scattered* in small non-contiguous holes, none of which is large enough to hold a new process.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the following equations consistently. Avoid using the `|` symbol directly inside the markdown table — use `\vert` instead.

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---------|---------|-----------|-------|
| Internal Fragmentation (Fixed) | $IF = P_{\text{size}} - A_{\text{size}}$ | $P_{\text{size}}$ = Partition size, $A_{\text{size}}$ = Actual process size | KB |
| Total Internal Fragmentation | $IF_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i - A_i)$ | Sum across all $n$ allocated partitions | KB |
| External Fragmentation (Variable) | $EF = F_{\text{total}} - H_{\text{max}}$ | $F_{\text{total}}$ = Total free memory, $H_{\text{max}}$ = Largest hole | KB |
| Memory Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\sum A_i}{M_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | $M_{\text{total}}$ = Total physical memory | Percentage (\%) |
| Compaction Gain | $G = F_{\text{total}} - EF_{\text{after}}$ | Free memory before and after compaction | KB |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of fixed/variable partitioning forms the conceptual bedrock of **modern virtual memory** and **paging systems**. Fixed partitions evolved into **page frames**, and variable partitions evolved into **segmentation**. Understanding fragmentation is critical for designing:
- **Embedded OS** (limited RAM → minimize internal fragmentation).
- **Database buffer pools** (avoid external fragmentation in cache).
- **Real-time systems** (deterministic allocation times).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Internal Fragmentation in Fixed Partitions

Consider a system with $N$ fixed partitions. For partition $i$ of size $P_i$ containing a process of actual size $A_i$ (where $A_i \le P_i$), the internal fragmentation $IF_i$ is the **wasted space inside the partition**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{By definition, the utilized memory in partition } i &= A_i \\
\text{Total memory in partition } i &= P_i \\
\text{Therefore, the unused (wasted) memory} &= P_i - A_i \\
\text{This wasted memory is} &= \text{Internal Fragmentation} \\
\therefore IF_i &= P_i - A_i
\end{aligned}
$$

For the entire system with $N$ allocated partitions, the total internal fragmentation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IF_{\text{total}} &= IF_1 + IF_2 + \dots + IF_N \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_i - A_i) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The **memory utilization** (efficiency) of the fixed-partition system is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{\text{Actually used memory}}{\text{Total memory}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i} \times 100\% \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{IF_{\text{total}}}{\sum P_i}\right) \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: External Fragmentation in Variable Partitions

In a variable-partition system, suppose after some allocations, the free memory consists of $k$ holes of sizes $H_1, H_2, \dots, H_k$ in non-contiguous (scattered) locations.

The total free memory available is:

$$
F_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} H_j
$$

A new process of size $S$ can be loaded **if and only if** there exists at least one hole $H_j \ge S$.

The **external fragmentation** is the free memory that exists *but cannot be used* to satisfy any single request. In the worst case, the largest process that can be loaded equals the largest hole:

$$
H_{\text{max}} = \max(H_1, H_2, \dots, H_k)
$$

Therefore, external fragmentation is the total free memory that is *not* part of the largest usable hole:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EF &= F_{\text{total}} - H_{\text{max}} \\
&= \sum_{j=1}^{k} H_j - \max_{j} (H_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** A system uses **First-Fit** variable partitioning. Free holes (in KB) are arranged in memory order as: **40, 60, 25, 100, 75, 30**. Requests arrive in this order: **P1=50 KB, P2=70 KB, P3=15 KB, P4=80 KB**. Draw the allocation table and compute the remaining holes and external fragmentation.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Process P1 requests 50 KB.**
- Scan holes left-to-right under First-Fit: 40 KB (too small), 60 KB (fits).
- Allocate P1 into the **60 KB hole**.
- Remaining hole: $60 - 50 = 10$ KB.
- Updated hole list: **10, 40, 25, 100, 75, 30**.

**Step 2 — Process P2 requests 70 KB.**
- Scan: 10 KB (no), 40 KB (no), 25 KB (no), 100 KB (fits).
- Allocate P2 into the **100 KB hole**.
- Remaining hole: $100 - 70 = 30$ KB.
- Updated hole list: **10, 40, 25, 30, 75, 30**.

**Step 3 — Process P3 requests 15 KB.**
- Scan: 10 KB (no), 40 KB (fits).
- Allocate P3 into the **40 KB hole**.
- Remaining hole: $40 - 15 = 25$ KB.
- Updated hole list: **10, 25, 25, 30, 75, 30**.

**Step 4 — Process P4 requests 80 KB.**
- Scan: 10 KB (no), 25 KB (no), 25 KB (no), 30 KB (no), 75 KB (no), 30 KB (no).
- **No hole fits** → P4 must wait. This is **external fragmentation** manifesting.

**Step 5 — Compute External Fragmentation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{total}} &= 10 + 25 + 25 + 30 + 75 + 30 = 195 \text{ KB} \\
H_{\text{max}} &= 75 \text{ KB} \\
EF &= F_{\text{total}} - H_{\text{max}} = 195 - 75 = 120 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** Even though 195 KB of memory is technically free, only 75 KB is usable as a single contiguous block. P4 (80 KB) cannot be loaded despite $195 \text{ KB} \ge 80 \text{ KB}$.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Fixed vs Variable Partitions

| Parameter | Fixed Partition | Variable Partition |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Partition creation | At boot time | Dynamically at runtime |
| Fragmentation type | **Internal** | **External** |
| Degree of multiprogramming | Fixed (equals number of partitions) | Variable (depends on hole sizes) |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Higher (hole list management) |
| Compaction required? | No | Yes (periodically) |
| Limitation | Process size $\le$ partition size | Sufficient contiguous hole needed |
| Memory utilization | Lower (wasted inside partitions) | Higher in the long run (with compaction) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Layout: Fixed Partitions (Equal Size)

The following Mermaid block illustrates the main memory divided into 4 equal fixed partitions, with the OS resident in low memory.

```mermaid
graph TB
    OS["OS Resident 100 KB"]
    P1["Partition 1: 200 KB - Internal Frag 80 KB"]
    P2["Partition 2: 200 KB - Process A 180 KB"]
    P3["Partition 3: 200 KB - Process B 200 KB"]
    P4["Partition 4: 200 KB - Free 200 KB"]
    F1["Process A: 180 KB Used"]
    F2["Process B: 200 KB Used"]
    P1 --- F1
    P3 --- F2
```

## 4.2 Memory Layout: Variable Partitions (External Fragmentation)

This diagram captures the variable-partition memory state at a particular instant, showing the scattered holes characteristic of external fragmentation.

```mermaid
graph LR
    OS["OS"]
    PA["Process A: 100 KB"]
    H1["Hole 1: 25 KB"]
    PB["Process B: 150 KB"]
    H2["Hole 2: 75 KB"]
    PC["Process C: 80 KB"]
    H3["Hole 3: 10 KB"]
    OS --- PA --- H1 --- PB --- H2 --- PC --- H3
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Allocation Workflow

The following diagram shows the **internal decision workflow** of a variable-partition memory allocator using the First-Fit/Best-Fit/Worst-Fit selection.

```mermaid
graph TB
    REQ["Process Memory Request of Size S"]
    SCAN["Scan Hole List"]
    DECIDE{"Selection Strategy"}
    FF["First-Fit: Pick First Suitable Hole"]
    BF["Best-Fit: Pick Smallest Hole >= S"]
    WF["Worst-Fit: Pick Largest Hole"]
    ALLOC["Allocate S Bytes to Process"]
    REM["Update Hole: H = H - S"]
    COAL{"Adjacent Free Holes?"}
    MERGE["Coalesce Adjacent Holes"]
    END["Allocation Complete"]

    REQ --> SCAN
    SCAN --> DECIDE
    DECIDE -->|First-Fit| FF
    DECIDE -->|Best-Fit| BF
    DECIDE -->|Worst-Fit| WF
    FF --> ALLOC
    BF --> ALLOC
    WF --> ALLOC
    ALLOC --> REM
    REM --> COAL
    COAL -->|Yes| MERGE
    COAL -->|No| END
    MERGE --> END
```

## 4.4 Compaction Sequence (Solving External Fragmentation)

This flowchart captures the OS-level decision to invoke compaction when external fragmentation crosses a threshold.

```mermaid
graph TB
    INIT["Memory has Scattered Holes"]
    CHK{"New Request > All Holes?"}
    WAIT["Process Waits in Queue"]
    THRESH{"EF > Threshold?"}
    COMPACT["Trigger Compaction: Shift All Processes to Consolidate Holes"]
    SUCC["Re-evaluate: Allocate Request"]
    INIT --> CHK
    CHK -->|No, fits somewhere| SUCC
    CHK -->|Yes| WAIT
    WAIT --> THRESH
    THRESH -->|Yes| COMPACT
    THRESH -->|No| WAIT
    COMPACT --> SUCC
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **internal fragmentation** and **external fragmentation**. State which one occurs in fixed and variable partitions.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Internal Fragmentation**: Memory that is allocated to a process but not used by it. It occurs *inside* the partition boundary. (1 Mark)
> - **External Fragmentation**: Free memory that exists in scattered small holes, none large enough to satisfy a request. It occurs *between* allocated partitions. (1 Mark)
> - **Fixed Partitioning** suffers from **Internal Fragmentation**; **Variable Partitioning** suffers from **External Fragmentation**. (1 Mark)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**
> **Q2.** What is **compaction**? Why is it difficult to implement in real systems?
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Compaction** is the OS technique of shifting all allocated memory blocks to one end of RAM to combine scattered free holes into a single large contiguous hole. (1.5 Marks)
> - **Difficulties**: (i) Processes must be paused (relocation overhead), (ii) Requires dynamic address relocation hardware (base register updates), (iii) Costly CPU time; impractical for real-time systems. (1.5 Marks)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain **fixed partitioning** with equal-size and unequal-size partitions. Discuss the issue of **internal fragmentation** with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **First-Fit, Best-Fit, and Worst-Fit** allocation strategies for variable partitions. **(7 Marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is **variable partitioning**? Explain how the OS handles **external fragmentation** using **compaction** and **placement algorithms**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system with **variable partitions** has free holes in memory order: **200 KB, 400 KB, 100 KB, 300 KB, 150 KB, 250 KB**. Using **First-Fit**, allocate the following requests in order: **P1=180 KB, P2=80 KB, P3=240 KB, P4=120 KB, P5=350 KB**. Show the final hole list and compute **external fragmentation** at the end. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Allocate P1 = 180 KB (First-Fit).**
- Scan: 200 KB (fits).
- Allocate → remaining hole: $200 - 180 = 20$ KB.
- Updated hole list: **20, 400, 100, 300, 150, 250**.

**Step 2 — Allocate P2 = 80 KB.**
- Scan: 20 KB (no), 400 KB (fits).
- Allocate → remaining: $400 - 80 = 320$ KB.
- Updated hole list: **20, 320, 100, 300, 150, 250**.

**Step 3 — Allocate P3 = 240 KB.**
- Scan: 20 KB (no), 320 KB (fits).
- Allocate → remaining: $320 - 240 = 80$ KB.
- Updated hole list: **20, 80, 100, 300, 150, 250**.

**Step 4 — Allocate P4 = 120 KB.**
- Scan: 20 KB (no), 80 KB (no), 100 KB (no), 300 KB (fits).
- Allocate → remaining: $300 - 120 = 180$ KB.
- Updated hole list: **20, 80, 100, 180, 150, 250**.

**Step 5 — Allocate P5 = 350 KB.**
- Scan: 20 KB (no), 80 KB (no), 100 KB (no), 180 KB (no), 150 KB (no), 250 KB (no).
- **No hole fits** → P5 must wait.

**Step 6 — Compute External Fragmentation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{total}} &= 20 + 80 + 100 + 180 + 150 + 250 \\
&= 780 \text{ KB} \\
H_{\text{max}} &= 250 \text{ KB} \\
EF &= 780 - 250 = 530 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Hole List:** 20 KB, 80 KB, 100 KB, 180 KB, 150 KB, 250 KB.  
**External Fragmentation = 530 KB.**  
**P5 (350 KB) cannot be loaded** despite $780 \text{ KB} \ge 350 \text{ KB}$ — a classic external fragmentation case.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing fragmentations** (1–2 marks lost): Students frequently write "external fragmentation occurs in fixed partitions" — this is **wrong**. Memorize: Fixed → Internal; Variable → External.
> 2. **Skipping the updated hole list** in numericals (1 mark lost): Always show the hole list *after every allocation*; KTU examiners explicitly look for this trace.
> 3. **Formula misapplication** (2 marks lost): $EF = F_{\text{total}} - H_{\text{max}}$ — students often compute $F_{\text{total}}$ only. Both quantities are required.
> 4. **First-Fit vs Best-Fit confusion** (1 mark lost): First-Fit = *first* suitable hole; Best-Fit = *smallest* suitable hole. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Skipping step-by-step derivation**: Even for 3-mark questions, showing the formula with substituted values fetches full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> - **Fixed Partitioning** = memory divided at boot time, into equal or unequal sizes. Suffers **Internal Fragmentation**.
> - **Variable Partitioning** = partitions created dynamically, exactly matching process size. Suffers **External Fragmentation**.
> - **Internal Fragmentation Formula**: $IF = P_{\text{size}} - A_{\text{size}}$. Sum across all partitions for total.
> - **External Fragmentation Formula**: $EF = F_{\text{total}} - H_{\text{max}}$.
> - **First-Fit** = allocate the first hole large enough (fastest, leaves small holes at the front).
> - **Best-Fit** = allocate the smallest sufficient hole (leaves tiny unusable fragments, slow search).
> - **Worst-Fit** = allocate the largest hole (leaves large remaining holes, slowest).
> - **Compaction** = shifting allocated blocks to consolidate scattered free holes (expensive, requires relocation hardware).
> - **Coalescing** = merging two adjacent free holes when a process between them terminates.
> - **Degree of Multiprogramming** in fixed partitions = fixed (number of partitions). In variable partitions = dynamic.
> - **Equally-sized fixed partitions** = worst internal fragmentation. **Unequal-sized** = reduced but still present.
> - **KTU Numerical Pattern**: Always trace the hole list step-by-step; show the updated list after each allocation.
> - **Real-world evolution**: Fixed partitions → Paging; Variable partitions → Segmentation (covered in later modules).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Segmentation** is a non-contiguous memory management technique that supports the programmer's view of memory. In this scheme, a logical address space is a collection of **variable-sized segments**, where each segment represents a logical unit of the program such as the main program, functions, procedures, data structures, stack, symbol table, etc. Each logical address is expressed as a two-tuple of the form `<segment-number, offset>`, and the OS maintains a **Segment Table** that stores the base address and limit (length) of each segment in physical memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> Segmentation is a memory management scheme that supports the user's/programmer's view of memory. The user specifies each address by two quantities: a **segment number** and a **displacement (offset)** within the segment. The system maintains a segment table that contains the base address and the limit (segment length) of each segment in physical memory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cupboard with Labelled Drawers"

Imagine a cupboard in your room with **drawers of different sizes**:

- The **whole cupboard** = Physical Memory (RAM).
- Each **drawer** = One **Segment** (e.g., "Math Textbooks", "Clothes", "Stationery", "Files").
- Each drawer has a **label** (its name/segment number) and a **physical location** (its base address).
- The **label & size note pasted on the drawer** = **Segment Table Entry** (base + limit).

When you say "I need a file from the **Files** drawer (segment 3), **2nd item from the top** (offset 2)", the system uses the **Files drawer label** to find *where* the drawer starts (base) and *how big* it is (limit). If the offset exceeds the drawer's size, the system throws a **trap** (segmentation fault). This is exactly how segmentation works in an OS — names = segment numbers, positions = offsets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Segmentation makes memory **human-meaningful**. The programmer thinks in terms of *code, data, heap, stack* — not raw byte addresses. Paging, in contrast, divides memory into fixed, equal-sized blocks that have no semantic meaning to the programmer.

## 1.3 Why Segmentation? — Motivation

| Problem with Contiguous Allocation | How Segmentation Solves It |
|---|---|
| Programs treated as one giant blob | Program split into *meaningful* logical units |
| Internal fragmentation | Segments sized to actual need |
| No information hiding / protection | Each segment gets independent protection bits |
| Difficult code sharing | Whole segments (e.g., common code) can be shared |
| External fragmentation (variable partitions) | Reduced via smaller logical units, but not eliminated |

## 1.4 Core Components at a Glance

- **Logical Address** = `<segment-number (s), offset (d)>` — written as `<s, d>`.
- **Segment Number (s):** Identifies *which* segment (used as index into Segment Table).
- **Offset (d):** Position *within* that segment (must be `< limit`).
- **Segment Table:** Per-process structure; each entry has `Base` + `Limit` (+ protection bits).
- **Segment Table Base Register (STBR):** Points to the current process's segment table in memory.
- **Segment Table Length Register (STLR):** Number of segments the process is using; helps validate `s < STLR`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard fact to memorize for KTU exams:**
> - Logical address space maximum = **2^s × 2^d** (where s = segment-number bits, d = offset bits).
> - A segment can be up to **2^d** bytes long.
> - Total segments per process ≤ **2^s**.
> - These are the *only* sizing knobs a CPU design chooses for segmentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Address Structure

The logical (virtual) address generated by the CPU has two components:

$$ \text{Logical Address} = \langle s, d \rangle $$

where:
- **s** = segment number → selects a row in the **Segment Table**.
- **d** = offset (displacement) → position inside the chosen segment.

The number of bits allocated to *s* and *d* defines the address space geometry. If the CPU issues a *k*-bit logical address:

$$ k = (\text{bits for } s) + (\text{bits for } d) $$

So if **k = 24**, and we use **s = 4 bits, d = 20 bits**:

- Maximum segments = **2⁴ = 16**.
- Maximum segment size = **2²⁰ = 1 MB**.
- Maximum logical address space = **16 × 1 MB = 16 MB**.

## 2.2 Segment Table — Anatomy of an Entry

Each Segment Table Entry (STE) contains:

| Field | Meaning | Typical Bits |
|---|---|---|
| **Base** | Starting physical address of the segment in RAM | 16–32 |
| **Limit** | Length of the segment (bytes) | 16–20 |
| **Valid Bit (V)** | 1 = segment in memory, 0 = swapped out | 1 |
| **Protection Bits** | Read / Write / Execute permissions | 3 |
| **Privilege Level** | Required kernel/user privilege | 2–4 |

## 2.3 Address Translation — Step-by-Step Hardware Flow

When the CPU generates `<s, d>`, the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** performs:

1. **Bound Check on Segment Number:** Compare `s` against `STLR` (Segment Table Length Register). If `s ≥ STLR` → **Trap to OS** (illegal address, kill program). This stops a buggy user from accessing someone else's segment table.
2. **Fetch Segment Table Entry:** Add `s × size_of_STE` to `STBR` (Segment Table Base Register) → physical address of STE. Read it.
3. **Bound Check on Offset:** Compare `d` against the entry's `Limit`. If `d ≥ Limit` → **Trap to OS** (segmentation fault).
4. **Compute Physical Address:** `Physical_Address = Base + d`.
5. **Access Physical Memory** at that address for read/write.

> [!NOTE]
> Both bound checks (step 1 and step 3) are **hardware-enforced protection**, not software. This is why segmentation is considered safer than pure base+limit contiguous allocation.

## 2.4 Sharing in Segmentation

Two processes can share a *whole segment* (e.g., shared library code). Both have entries in their own segment tables pointing to the **same physical base address** with the same limit. The **protection bits** in each entry may differ (e.g., one process has R-X, another R-W).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sharing with Paging vs. Segmentation:**
> - Paging: share at the granularity of a *page* (fixed size).
> - Segmentation: share at the granularity of a *whole segment* (variable size, semantically meaningful).
> - Segments can be shared *completely or not at all*; partial sharing is not directly possible.

## 2.5 Protection

The hardware checks the protection bits in the STE on every memory access. Example:

- **Code segment:** R-X (read + execute, no write).
- **Data segment:** R-W (read + write, no execute — prevents accidental code injection).
- **Stack segment:** R-W.
- **Shared library segment:** R-X for all users.

A violation → **segmentation fault** → OS sends `SIGSEGV` to the process.

## 2.6 Fragmentation Analysis

| Fragmentation Type | Status in Segmentation |
|---|---|
| **Internal fragmentation** | ❌ **Eliminated** — segment sized to actual need. |
| **External fragmentation** | ⚠️ **Still present** — when segments are loaded/unloaded, holes appear. Solved by compaction (costly) or combined with paging (see Module 3 next topic). |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Logical address format | $\langle s, d \rangle$ | s = segment #, d = offset |
| 2 | Max segments per process | $2^{s_{\text{bits}}}$ | depends on CPU design |
| 3 | Max segment size | $2^{d_{\text{bits}}}$ bytes | d bits for offset |
| 4 | Max logical address space | $2^{s_{\text{bits}}} \times 2^{d_{\text{bits}}} = 2^{k}$ | k = total logical address bits |
| 5 | Physical address | $\text{Base}[s] + d$ | computed in MMU |
| 6 | Segment-number check | $s < \text{STLR}$ | else trap |
| 7 | Offset (limit) check | $d < \text{Limit}[s]$ | else trap (segfault) |
| 8 | Segment Table size | $\text{STLR} \times \text{sizeof(STE)}$ | stored starting at STBR |
| 9 | Entry index in table | $i = s$ (zero-based) | physical ptr = STBR + s·sizeof(STE) |
| 10 | Effective address time | $T_{EA} = 2 \times T_{mem} + T_{add}$ | if segment table in RAM (2 memory accesses) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Segmentation underpins modern hardware features such as **x86 descriptors (GDT/LDT)**, **x86-64 FS/GS segment overrides for thread-local storage**, and historically powered **Multics** and the **Intel 80286/80386 protected mode**. The `fs:` and `gs:` registers in Linux kernels use segments to reach per-CPU data without flushing TLB entries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Address Translation (Classic KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Problem Statement

A system uses segmentation. The Segment Table of a process P is given below:

| Segment # | Base (decimal) | Limit (decimal) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1400 | 1000 |
| 1 | 6300 | 400 |
| 2 | 4300 | 1100 |
| 3 | 3200 | 990 |
| 4 | 6700 | 540 |

For each of the following logical addresses, determine the **physical address** (or state that a trap occurs). State the *reason* clearly.

(i) `<0, 530>`
(ii) `<1, 11>`
(iii) `<2, 990>`
(iv) `<4, 540>`
(v) `<6, 100>`

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

The MMU must:
- Check if segment number `s` is valid (i.e., `s < STLR`). Assume **STLR = 5** (so segments 0–4 valid).
- Check if offset `d < Limit[s]`.
- If both pass, compute `Physical_Address = Base[s] + d`.

#### (i) Logical Address `<0, 530>`

- s = 0 → look up entry: Base = 1400, Limit = 1000.
- Check offset: 530 < 1000 ✅ (valid).
- Physical address = 1400 + 530 = **1930**.

#### (ii) Logical Address `<1, 11>`

- s = 1 → Base = 6300, Limit = 400.
- Check offset: 11 < 400 ✅ (valid).
- Physical address = 6300 + 11 = **6311**.

#### (iii) Logical Address `<2, 990>`

- s = 2 → Base = 4300, Limit = 1100.
- Check offset: 990 < 1100 ✅ (valid).
- Physical address = 4300 + 990 = **5290**.

#### (iv) Logical Address `<4, 540>`

- s = 4 → Base = 6700, Limit = 540.
- Check offset: 540 < 540 ❌ (NOT valid — must be *strictly less* than limit).
- **TRAP → Segmentation fault** (offset equals limit; the segment has only offsets 0 to 539).
- **Reason:** Limit = 540 means the segment occupies bytes 6700 to 6700+539 = 7239. Offset 540 is the *first byte outside*.

#### (v) Logical Address `<6, 100>`

- s = 6 → STLR = 5, so 6 ≥ 5.
- **TRAP → Illegal segment number** (process has no segment 6; user attempted to read beyond its own segment table).
- **Reason:** This is caught by the *first* check, before offset validation.

### Final Answer Table

| # | Logical Address | Physical Address | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | ⟨0, 530⟩ | **1930** | ✅ Valid |
| (ii) | ⟨1, 11⟩ | **6311** | ✅ Valid |
| (iii) | ⟨2, 990⟩ | **5290** | ✅ Valid |
| (iv) | ⟨4, 540⟩ | — | ❌ Trap (offset ≥ limit) |
| (v) | ⟨6, 100⟩ | — | ❌ Trap (s ≥ STLR) |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Address Space Geometry

### Problem Statement

A CPU issues 32-bit logical addresses. The architecture reserves the **top 4 bits** for the segment number and the **bottom 28 bits** for the offset. Answer:

(a) How many segments can a process have at most?
(b) What is the maximum size of one segment?
(c) What is the maximum logical address space per process?
(d) If the segment table entries use 8 bytes each, how much memory is the segment table itself?

### Solution

(a) Max segments per process:

$$ 2^{4} = 16 \text{ segments} $$

(b) Max segment size:

$$ 2^{28} \text{ bytes} = 256 \text{ MB} $$

(c) Max logical address space per process:

$$ 2^{4} \times 2^{28} = 2^{32} = 4 \text{ GB} $$

(d) Segment table memory size (with 16 entries × 8 bytes each):

$$ 16 \times 8 = 128 \text{ bytes} $$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Sharing & Protection

### Problem Statement

Two processes P1 and P2 share segment 4. P1's STE for segment 4 has Base = 8000, Limit = 2000, protection = **R-X**. P2's STE for the same segment 4 has Base = 8000, Limit = 2000, protection = **R-W**. If P1 tries to execute `STORE [segment 4, offset 100]`, what happens? What if P2 does it?

### Solution

- The **physical segment** is identical (Base 8000, Limit 2000) for both.
- P1 attempts a **write** to a segment whose protection is R-X. → **Trap** (protection violation).
- P2 attempts a **write** to a segment whose protection is R-W. → **Allowed**.
- This demonstrates that **same physical memory, different views per process** is a powerful feature of segmentation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Segmentation vs. Paging (High-Yield KTU Comparison)

| Parameter | Segmentation | Paging |
|---|---|---|
| Division unit | Variable-sized **logical units** | Fixed-sized **pages/frames** |
| Programmer's view | Visible (named segments) | Invisible (just numbers) |
| Address format | ⟨segment #, offset⟩ | ⟨page #, offset⟩ |
| Internal fragmentation | None | Yes (last page, avg half-page) |
| External fragmentation | Yes | None |
| Logical → Physical mapping | Base + offset | Frame # × page_size + offset |
| Protection | Per segment (semantic) | Per page (mechanical) |
| Sharing granularity | Whole segment | Page-level |
| Hardware support | Segment table (Base + Limit) | Page table |
| Used in | x86 (real/protected mode), Multics | Almost all modern OS |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU often asks for 14-mark comparative questions** between paging and segmentation. Memorize the matrix above and at least one scenario where each wins.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical Address Structure (Bit Partitioning)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["32-bit Logical Address"] --> B["Upper s bits: Segment Number"]
    A --> C["Lower d bits: Offset / Displacement"]
    B --> D["Index into Segment Table"]
    C --> E["Position inside chosen segment"]
    D --> F["Selects Base and Limit"]
    F --> G["Base + Offset = Physical Address"]
    E --> G
    G --> H["Physical Memory Access"]
    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style G fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#065f46
    style H fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
```

## 4.2 Segmentation Address Translation — Hardware Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU generates <s, d>"] --> B{"s < STLR ?"}
    B -- No --> X1["TRAP: Illegal segment number"]
    B -- Yes --> C["Fetch STE from STBR + s x sizeof(STE)"]
    C --> D["Read Base and Limit from STE"]
    D --> E{"d < Limit ?"}
    E -- No --> X2["TRAP: Segmentation Fault"]
    E -- Yes --> F["Physical_Address = Base + d"]
    F --> G["Access Physical Memory at that address"]
    X1 --> H1["OS terminates process / sends SIGSEGV"]
    X2 --> H2["OS terminates process / sends SIGSEGV"]
    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style F fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#065f46
    style G fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style X1 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d
    style X2 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Segmentation Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["CPU Core"]
        PC["Program Counter + Instruction"]
    end
```
