| NFSv2 | UDP | Stateless | Baseline |
| NFSv3 | TCP/UDP | Mostly stateless | Improved writes |
| NFSv4 | TCP | **Stateful** | Strong security, locking |
| NFSv4.1+ | TCP | Stateful | Parallel sessions (pNFS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NFS Statelessness Insight**: In NFSv2/v3, the server does not maintain client state. If a request is lost, the client retries. This makes recovery simple but complicates file locking (handled separately by NLM — Network Lock Manager).

---

## 2.3 Type 3 — Distributed File Systems (DFS)

A **DFS** stores files across *multiple* networked servers, presenting them as a single unified namespace. Examples: **HDFS (Hadoop)**, **GFS (Google File System)**, **CephFS**, **GlusterFS**.

### The GFS/HDFS Design Principle

Files are split into **chunks/blocks** (typically **64 MB in HDFS**):

$$ N_{\text{chunks}} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{file}}}{S_{\text{chunk}}} \right\rceil $$

Each chunk is **replicated** (default replication factor $\rho = 3$ in HDFS):

$$ S_{\text{physical}} = \rho \times S_{\text{logical}} $$

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Used by **Google Search Index**, **Facebook photos**, **Netflix streaming pipelines**, and **banking transaction logs**. The trade-off: **high availability and parallelism** vs. **eventual consistency**.

---

## 2.4 Type 4 — Flash File Systems

Flash memory has unique constraints:
- **No in-place writes** (must erase a block before rewriting).
- **Limited erase cycles** (~10,000 for MLC, ~100,000 for SLC).
- **Asymmetric read/write/erase speeds**.

Flash file systems (e.g., **JFFS2, YAFFS, UBIFS, F2FS**) implement **wear-leveling** to distribute writes evenly.

### Garbage Collection & Wear Leveling

$$ W_{\text{avg per cell}} = \frac{T_{\text{total writes}}}{N_{\text{cells}}} $$

A well-designed flash FS keeps $W_{\text{avg per cell}}$ uniform across all cells to maximize device lifetime.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often claim that flash file systems are *faster* than disk FS. In fact, random writes are **slower** than HDDs because of the erase-before-write requirement. They excel at **read performance** and **sequential writes**.

---

## 2.5 Type 5 — Database File Systems

A **DBFS** stores file metadata in a **relational database** rather than in flat inodes. Examples: **WinFS (Microsoft, cancelled)**, **DBea**, **ext3cow**, **S3-backed DBFS**.

### Advantages for KTU Examination
1. **Queryable metadata**: SQL queries on file attributes.
2. **ACID guarantees** on metadata updates.
3. **Easy replication** using database replication.

---

## 2.6 Type 6 — Special-Purpose / Virtual File Systems

Virtual file systems do not store real files; they provide a **file-like interface** to kernel or system information.

| Virtual FS | Path | Purpose |
|------------|------|---------|
| procfs | `/proc` | Process & kernel info (e.g., `/proc/cpuinfo`) |
| sysfs | `/sys` | Device & driver info |
| tmpfs | `/dev/shm`, `/tmp` | RAM-backed temporary storage |
| devfs | `/dev` | Device file abstraction |
| cgroupfs | `/sys/fs/cgroup` | Resource control hierarchies |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Question**: *"What is the difference between procfs and sysfs?"* — Answer: `/proc` exposes **process-specific** information (PID-based), while `/sys` exposes **kernel object hierarchy** (device, bus, class).

---

## 2.7 Type 7 — Journaling File Systems

A **journaling file system** maintains a **log (journal)** of metadata changes *before* they are applied. On crash recovery, the OS replays the journal to restore consistency.

### Three Journaling Modes

| Mode | What is Journaled | Recovery Speed | Risk |
|------|-------------------|----------------|------|
| **Writeback** | Only metadata | Fastest | Data loss possible |
| **Ordered** (default in ext3) | Metadata + flush data first | Balanced | Limited data loss |
| **Data** | Both metadata & data | Slowest | No data loss |

### Journal Recovery Equation

$$ T_{\text{recovery}} = O(J_{\text{size}}) $$

Where $J_{\text{size}}$ is the journal size (typically **32 MB to 128 MB**). Recovery is **bounded** by journal size — not by full FS size — which is the *defining advantage*.

**Examples**: ext3, ext4, NTFS, XFS, JFS, ReiserFS.

---

## 2.8 Type 8 — Log-Structured File System (LFS)

Originally proposed by **Rosenblum and Ousterhout (1991)**, LFS treats the entire disk as an **append-only log**. All writes (both data and metadata) are appended sequentially to the end of the log. This eliminates seek overhead for writes.

### The Free Space Problem in LFS

Because LFS never overwrites in place, old data becomes garbage. A **segment cleaner** runs in the background:

$$ \text{SegUtilization} = \frac{\text{Live data in segment}}{\text{Segment size}} $$

When utilization drops below a threshold (e.g., **50%**), live data is compacted and old segments are freed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LFS Equation for Total Disk Writes**:
> $$ W_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{user}} + W_{\text{cleaner}} = W_{\text{user}} \times \left(1 + \frac{1}{U_{\text{seg}}}\right) $$
> where $U_{\text{seg}}$ is the segment utilization ratio.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master These Five Equations for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | $S_{\text{max,file}} = K_p B_s (1 + K_p + K_p^2)$ | Max file size with triple indirection | Disk FS |
| 2 | $N_{\text{chunks}} = \lceil S_{\text{file}} / S_{\text{chunk}} \rceil$ | Chunk count in DFS | DFS |
| 3 | $S_{\text{physical}} = \rho \cdot S_{\text{logical}}$ | Replication storage overhead | DFS |
| 4 | $W_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{user}} (1 + 1/U_{\text{seg}})$ | Total writes with cleaner | LFS |
| 5 | $T_{\text{recovery}} = O(J_{\text{size}})$ | Bounded journal recovery | Journaling FS |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Decision Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Mapping (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva)**
> - **Android phone storage** → F2FS or ext4 on flash
> - **Linux boot partition** → ext4 (journaling)
> - **Embedded IoT sensor** → JFFS2 or UBIFS
> - **Data center analytics** → HDFS
> - **Cloud object store** → S3-backed virtual FS
> - **Windows C: drive** → NTFS (journaling)
> - **Live USB stick** → FAT32 (universally readable, no journaling)
> - **High-write database server** → LFS or XFS

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum File Size in a UNIX-style Inode FS

We derive the formula for the maximum file size supported by an inode-based disk file system using single, double, and triple indirect block pointers — a classic KTU 14-mark derivation question.

### Step 1: Define the Block Pointer Count

Assume each block has size $B_s$ bytes, and each block pointer is a 32-bit unsigned integer (4 bytes). The number of block pointers that fit in one block is:

$$
K_p = \frac{B_s}{4}
$$

**Substituting** $B_s = 4096$ bytes:

$$
K_p = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 \text{ pointers per block}
$$

### Step 2: Decompose the Inode Structure

A UNIX-style inode contains 15 block pointers:
- **12 direct pointers** → point directly to data blocks.
- **1 single-indirect pointer** → points to a block containing $K_p$ more pointers.
- **1 double-indirect pointer** → points to a block whose $K_p$ pointers each point to $K_p$ data blocks.
- **1 triple-indirect pointer** → points to a block whose $K_p$ pointers each point to blocks that point to $K_p$ blocks that point to $K_p$ data blocks.

### Step 3: Count Maximum Number of Data Blocks

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{max}} &= 12 \quad &&\text{(direct)} \\
&\quad + K_p \quad &&\text{(single indirect)} \\
&\quad + K_p^2 \quad &&\text{(double indirect)} \\
&\quad + K_p^3 \quad &&\text{(triple indirect)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $K_p = 1024$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{max}} &= 12 + 1024 + 1024^2 + 1024^3 \\
&= 12 + 1024 + 1{,}048{,}576 + 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \\
&= 1{,}074{,}791{,}436 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Maximum File Size

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{max,file}} &= N_{\text{max}} \times B_s \\
&= 1{,}074{,}791{,}436 \times 4096 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 4{,}402{,}345{,}881{,}856 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 4 \text{ TB (terabytes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: With a 4 KB block size, a single inode can address up to **~4 TB** of file data. This is why ext4 defaults to a 4 TB max file size with this indirection scheme. The actual theoretical limit (using 48-bit block pointers) is **1 EB (exabyte)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: HDFS Replication Storage Overhead

We derive the relationship between logical and physical storage when using a replication factor $\rho$.

### Step 1: Define Variables

- $S_{\text{logical}}$ = size of file as seen by the user.
- $\rho$ = replication factor (default $\rho = 3$ in HDFS).
- $S_{\text{physical}}$ = total disk space consumed across the cluster.

### Step 2: Apply Replication

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = \rho \times S_{\text{logical}}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example

Let $S_{\text{logical}} = 1 \text{ GB}$, $\rho = 3$:

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = 3 \times 1 \text{ GB} = 3 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Chunk Count

With chunk size $S_{\text{chunk}} = 64 \text{ MB} = 64 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$:

$$
N_{\text{chunks}} = \left\lceil \frac{1 \times 2^{30}}{64 \times 2^{20}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1024}{64} \right\rceil = 16 \text{ chunks}
$$

### Step 5: Total Chunks After Replication

$$
N_{\text{chunks,total}} = \rho \times N_{\text{chunks}} = 3 \times 16 = 48 \text{ physical chunks}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters**: HDFS places the 3 replicas of each chunk on **3 different racks** (rack-aware placement) to survive a full rack failure. This is a frequent 7-mark KTU sub-question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: LFS Total Write Amplification

We derive the cleaner overhead in a Log-Structured File System.

### Step 1: Setup

Let $W_{\text{user}}$ be the total bytes written by user applications. The segment cleaner reads and rewrites live data, generating additional writes.

### Step 2: Cleaner Overhead

If the segment utilization is $U_{\text{seg}}$ (fraction of segment holding live data), the cleaner must write $W_{\text{user}} / U_{\text{seg}}$ bytes to preserve the live data during cleaning.

$$
W_{\text{cleaner}} = \frac{W_{\text{user}}}{U_{\text{seg}}}
$$

### Step 3: Total Writes

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{total}} &= W_{\text{user}} + W_{\text{cleaner}} \\
&= W_{\text{user}} + \frac{W_{\text{user}}}{U_{\text{seg}}} \\
&= W_{\text{user}} \left(1 + \frac{1}{U_{\text{seg}}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Write Amplification Factor (WAF)

$$
\text{WAF} = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{W_{\text{user}}} = 1 + \frac{1}{U_{\text{seg}}}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example

If $U_{\text{seg}} = 0.5$ (50%):

$$
\text{WAF} = 1 + \frac{1}{0.5} = 1 + 2 = 3
$$

This means **3× the user writes** actually hit the disk — a critical performance bottleneck.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often forget the +1 in the WAF formula. If the cleaner writes back exactly the live data, you still have **one** pass of user data plus the cleaner pass — the +1 represents the original user writes themselves.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Attribute | Disk FS (ext4) | NFS | HDFS | Flash FS (UBIFS) | LFS |
|-----------|----------------|-----|------|------------------|-----|
| **Storage Medium** | HDD/SSD | Remote server | Cluster of nodes | NAND flash | HDD/SSD |
| **Access Pattern** | Random + Sequential | Network RPC | Sequential streaming | Random read, sequential write | Sequential only |
| **Failure Recovery** | Journal replay | Stateless retry | Chunk replication | Wear-leveling + GC | Segment cleaning |
| **Consistency Model** | Strong (POSIX) | Close-to-open | Eventual | Strong | Strong |
| **Typical Block/Chunk Size** | 4 KB | 8 KB (NFSv3) | 64 MB / 128 MB | 128 KB (erase block) | 512 KB – 1 MB segments |
| **Best Use-Case** | OS installation | Shared home dirs | Big data batch | Smartphones, IoT | Write-heavy mail servers |
| **Biggest Limitation** | Seek time | Network latency | Small files (NameNode bottleneck) | Garbage collection cost | Cleaner overhead |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*With a suitable example, explain any THREE types of file systems used in modern operating systems.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Disk File System (e.g., ext4):** A file system designed for block storage devices like HDDs and SSDs. It organizes data into inodes, directories, and data blocks within a fixed partition. Used in Linux desktops and servers as the default root file system.

2. **Network File System (e.g., NFS):** A client-server file system that allows a user on a client machine to access files over a network as if they were mounted locally. It uses RPC over TCP/UDP and is widely used in enterprise environments for shared home directories and centralized storage.

3. **Flash File System (e.g., JFFS2, F2FS):** A file system optimized for flash memory devices, which have erase-before-write constraints. It uses wear-leveling algorithms to extend device lifetime and is used in smartphones, SSDs, and embedded Linux devices.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between Journaling File System and Log-Structured File System (LFS).*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Journaling FS | Log-Structured FS |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Write Strategy** | Modifies in-place but logs intent first | Append-only; never overwrites in place |
| **Journal** | Small fixed-size circular log | Entire disk acts as a log |
| **Recovery** | Replay journal on crash | Segment cleaner compacts live data |
| **Read Performance** | Excellent (in-place data) | Good (requires segment summary lookup) |
| **Write Performance** | Good (one journal + one write) | Excellent sequential writes |
| **Example** | ext3, ext4, NTFS | Sprite LFS, NetApp WAFL |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Distributed & Flash File Systems (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model | CO4 | Blooms: Analyze, Apply]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of HDFS with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the NameNode and DataNode. How does HDFS achieve fault tolerance through replication?**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture of HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System):**

HDFS follows a **master-slave architecture** with the following components:

1. **NameNode (Master)**: Runs on a high-end machine. Stores all **metadata** — file names, directory structure, block-to-DataNode mapping, replication factor, permissions. It does NOT store actual file data.

2. **DataNodes (Slaves)**: Run on commodity hardware. Store the **actual data blocks** (default chunk size = **64 MB in HDFS v2, 128 MB in HDFS v3**). They send heartbeats to the NameNode every 3 seconds.

3. **Secondary NameNode**: Periodically merges the **EditLog** with the **FsImage** to prevent the edit log from growing unbounded. (Note: It is NOT a backup NameNode.)

**Block Diagram:**

```
Client → NameNode (metadata) → DataNode1 (Chunk replica 1)
                            → DataNode2 (Chunk replica 2)
                            → DataNode3 (Chunk replica 3)
```

**Role of NameNode:**
- Manages the file system **namespace**.
- Regulates **client access** to files.
- Executes file system operations like open, close, rename.
- Maps blocks to DataNodes.

**Role of DataNode:**
- Stores and retrieves blocks as instructed.
- Sends **block reports** to NameNode periodically.
- Performs block creation, deletion, and replication on instruction.

**Fault Tolerance via Replication (Default $\rho = 3$):**

When a file is written:
- The file is split into 64 MB (or 128 MB) chunks.
- Each chunk is replicated to 3 different DataNodes.
- **Rack-aware placement**: One replica on a local rack node, one on a different rack node, and one on the same different rack (to balance reliability and network bandwidth).

On DataNode failure:
- The NameNode detects via **missed heartbeats** (timeout: 10 minutes).
- It instructs another DataNode to **re-replicate** the lost chunks to maintain $\rho = 3$.
- Total physical storage:

$$S_{\text{physical}} = 3 \times S_{\text{logical}}$$

(3 marks for architecture + 2 marks for NameNode/DataNode roles + 2 marks for replication mechanism)

---

**(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Flash File System. Why is wear leveling important? Compare JFFS2 and F2FS.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of a Flash File System:**

Flash memory is organized into **blocks**, each containing multiple **pages**. The constraints are:
- **Read**: page-level (fast).
- **Write**: page-level (only to erased pages).
- **Erase**: block-level (slow, ~1 ms).

A flash file system maintains a **mapping table** that translates logical block addresses (used by the OS) to physical flash locations. When a page is updated, the FS:
1. Writes the new version to a **free page**.
2. Marks the old page as **stale** (invalid).
3. Updates the **mapping table**.

A background **garbage collector** reclaims stale pages by copying live data to a new block and erasing the old block.

**Why Wear Leveling is Important:**

Each flash cell supports a finite number of **program-erase (P/E) cycles** (typically 10,000 for MLC, 100,000 for SLC). Without wear leveling, frequently updated cells (e.g., a log file's inode) would wear out long before the rest of the disk, causing premature failure.

Wear leveling ensures that writes are **distributed evenly** across all cells:

$$W_{\text{avg per cell}} = \frac{T_{\text{total writes}}}{N_{\text{cells}}}$$

**Comparison: JFFS2 vs F2FS:**

| Feature | JFFS2 | F2FS |
|---------|-------|------|
| **Designed for** | Raw NAND flash (MTD) | Flash-friendly SSDs and eMMC |
| **Mount Time** | Slow (scans entire flash) | Fast (uses checkpoint) |
| **Garbage Collection** | In-place | Background thread |
| **Compression** | Yes (zlib, LZO) | No |
| **Use-Case** | Embedded Linux (OpenWrt) | Android devices, modern smartphones |

(3 marks for flash FS working + 2 marks for wear leveling math + 2 marks for JFFS2 vs F2FS comparison)

---

### Question B — Journaling FS, LFS, and Virtual FS (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model | CO4 | Blooms: Analyze, Apply, Evaluate]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the three journaling modes (writeback, ordered, data journaling) in ext3/ext4. When is each mode preferred? Why was journaling necessary after the introduction of large file systems?**

**Model Solution:**

**Need for Journaling:**

Before journaling, on a crash, `fsck` (file system consistency check) had to scan the **entire file system** to locate and fix inconsistencies. With multi-GB drives, this took hours. Journaling reduces recovery time to **seconds** by maintaining a **transaction log**.

**Three Journaling Modes:**

1. **Writeback Mode (Fastest, Riskiest):**
   - Only the **metadata changes** are journaled.
   - The corresponding **data blocks are NOT guaranteed to be flushed** to disk before the metadata.
   - **Risk**: After a crash, files may contain **stale (old) data** because the metadata says "this inode points to block X" but block X contains an older version of the file.
   - **Use-case**: When performance matters more than data integrity (rare in production).

2. **Ordered Mode (Default, Balanced):**
   - Only **metadata** is journaled, BUT the file's data blocks are **flushed to disk first** before the metadata change is committed to the journal.
   - **Guarantee**: No file will ever contain data that does not belong to it (no garbage data inside a file).
   - **Risk**: New file creation may leave orphan data blocks (minor).
   - **Use-case**: General-purpose desktops and servers (default in ext3).

3. **Data Mode (Slowest, Safest):**
   - **Both metadata AND file data** are journaled.
   - **Guarantee**: Full ACID-style consistency — every committed transaction has both metadata and data on disk.
   - **Use-case**: Database storage, financial transaction logs.

**Trade-off Summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speed:} \quad &\text{Writeback} > \text{Ordered} > \text{Data} \\
\text{Safety:} \quad &\text{Writeback} < \text{Ordered} < \text{Data}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Journaling Became Necessary:**

With file systems growing from MBs to TBs, full `fsck` scans became **impractical** (linear time $O(N)$ in disk size). Journaling provides **bounded recovery** $O(J_{\text{size}})$ where $J_{\text{size}}$ is the journal size (typically 32 MB), independent of the total file system size.

(3 marks for mode explanation + 2 marks for trade-offs + 2 marks for necessity with $O()$ analysis)

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the architecture of the Log-Structured File System (LFS). Derive the write amplification factor. Why is the segment cleaner needed?**

**Model Solution:**

**LFS Architecture (Rosenblum & Ousterhout, 1991):**

LFS treats the entire disk as a **circular log**. All writes (data + metadata) are **appended sequentially** to the head of the log. Components:

1. **Inode Map**: A compact structure mapping inode numbers to their current disk locations. Updated during every write.
2. **Segment**: A large block of the disk (e.g., **512 KB or 1 MB**) used as the append unit.
3. **Segment Summary**: Located at the head of each segment; identifies which blocks in the segment are live and to which files they belong.
4. **Segment Cleaner**: Background process that reclaims space from segments with mostly dead data.
5. **Log Head & Tail Pointers**: Track where the next write will occur and where cleaning should start.

**Why Sequential Writes?**

Disk seeks are slow (~10 ms). LFS converts *all* writes into long **sequential writes**, eliminating seek overhead. This was revolutionary for write-heavy workloads like mail servers and the Sprite network FS.

**Derivation of Write Amplification Factor:**

Let $W_{\text{user}}$ be the user-initiated writes, and $U_{\text{seg}}$ be the average segment utilization (fraction of segment holding live data).

When a segment is cleaned, the cleaner reads the live data (size $U_{\text{seg}} \times S_{\text{seg}}$) and writes it to a new segment, then erases the old one.

**Cleaner writes:**

$$
W_{\text{cleaner}} = \frac{W_{\text{user}}}{U_{\text{seg}}}
$$

**Total writes (including user writes):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{total}} &= W_{\text{user}} + W_{\text{cleaner}} \\
&= W_{\text{user}} + \frac{W_{\text{user}}}{U_{\text{seg}}} \\
&= W_{\text{user}} \left(1 + \frac{1}{U_{\text{seg}}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Write Amplification Factor:**

$$
\text{WAF} = 1 + \frac{1}{U_{\text{seg}}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** If $U_{\text{seg}} = 0.5$, then $\text{WAF} = 1 + 2 = 3$. The disk experiences 3× the user writes.

**Why is the Segment Cleaner Needed?**

Because LFS never overwrites in place, old versions of files remain on disk. Over time, segments fill up with **stale data**. Without cleaning, the disk would **run out of free segments** even though most of the disk contains garbage. The cleaner reclaims this space, ensuring long-term operation.

(2 marks for LFS components + 3 marks for derivation + 2 marks for cleaner justification)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — File System Types**
> 1. **Confusing NFS and HDFS**: NFS is a *centralized* network FS (one server, many clients). HDFS is a *distributed* FS (data spread across many nodes). Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if you interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting the `+1` in LFS WAF formula**: The total writes = user writes + cleaner writes. If you write $W_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{user}} / U_{\text{seg}}$, you will be marked wrong.
> 3. **Claiming Flash FS is faster than HDD for random writes**: Random writes on flash require erase-before-write, making them **slower** than HDD. The advantage of flash is read latency and parallelism.
> 4. **Mixing up Journaling and Log-Structured**: Journaling logs *metadata only* (small journal). LFS logs *everything* (entire disk). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Not specifying block sizes**: Always mention **block/chunk size** (4 KB for ext4, 64 MB for HDFS) — KTU examiners reward precision.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Types" (Module 4)**

- [ ] **8 types**: Disk, Network (NFS), Distributed (HDFS), Flash, Database, Virtual (proc/sys/tmp), Journaling, Log-Structured.
- [ ] **Disk FS formula**: $S_{\text{max,file}} = K_p B_s (1 + K_p + K_p^2)$ with $K_p = 1024$ for 4 KB blocks → **~4 TB max file size**.
- [ ] **HDFS defaults**: chunk size **64 MB** (v2) or **128 MB** (v3), replication factor $\rho = 3$, NameNode is single point of failure (HA mode uses two).
- [ ] **NFS statelessness** (v2/v3) → server holds no client state → simple recovery; v4 is stateful.
- [ ] **Flash FS constraints**: erase-before-write, no in-place update, limited P/E cycles (~10K–100K).
- [ ] **Wear leveling** distributes writes uniformly; equation: $W_{\text{avg}} = T_{\text{total writes}} / N_{\text{cells}}$.
- [ ] **Journaling modes**: Writeback (metadata only, fast) < Ordered (default ext3) < Data (full journaling, slow).
- [ ] **Recovery time**: $O(J_{\text{size}})$ for journal vs $O(S_{\text{total}})$ for `fsck` — the **defining advantage** of journaling.
- [ ] **LFS WAF formula**: $\text{WAF} = 1 + 1/U_{\text{seg}}$; at $U_{\text{seg}} = 0.5$, WAF = 3.
- [ ] **Virtual FS paths to remember**: `/proc` (processes), `/sys` (kernel objects), `/dev/shm` (RAM), `/dev` (devices).
- [ ] **Key examples per type**: ext4 (Disk), NFS (Network), HDFS/GFS (Distributed), JFFS2/F2FS (Flash), ext3/NTFS (Journaling), Sprite LFS (Log-Structured).
- [ ] **CO4 mapping**: This topic directly addresses the design and analysis of file system structures (KTU 2024 Course Outcome 4).
- [ ] **Examiner's bias**: They love asking the **differentiate** question between closely related types (NFS vs HDFS, Journaling vs LFS, procfs vs sysfs).
- [ ] **Numerical questions** in KTU typically test: max file size, replication storage, WAF calculation, chunk count, journal recovery time.
- [ ] **Always draw a block diagram** for any 7-mark question — answers without diagrams lose 1–2 marks in valuation.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure - Access Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structure - Access Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 File Structure — Formal KTU Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Operating Systems syllabus**, *File Structure* refers to the internal logical organization of data within a file — i.e., **how bytes/records are arranged on the storage medium so that the OS can interpret them meaningfully**. File structure is a logical concept, distinct from the physical layout (which is determined by the file allocation method).

The OS recognizes **three canonical file structures**:

| # | Structure Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **No Structure (Unstructured / Byte Stream)** | The OS treats the file as an opaque sequence of bytes with no imposed meaning. |
| 2 | **Simple Record Structure** | The file is a linear sequence of fixed-length or variable-length records. |
| 3 | **Complex Structure (Indexed / Hierarchical)** | The file consists of formatted records organized using indexes, trees, or links. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** *File Structure* answers *"how is data logically arranged?"* while *Access Method* answers *"how is data logically retrieved?"*. The two are tightly coupled but conceptually independent.

## 1.2 Access Methods — Formal KTU Definition

An **Access Method** is the algorithmic interface exposed by the file system (via `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `seek()`, etc.) that determines the **order and manner in which records of a file can be read or written**. KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes three primary access methods:

1. **Sequential Access** — Records are processed in the order they appear (tape-like behaviour).
2. **Direct Access (Random Access)** — Records are read/written by their *logical record number (LRN)* using a positional offset (disk-like behaviour).
3. **Indexed Access** — A separate *index structure* is built over the file to support **key-based (associative) lookup**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical syllabus highlight:** Modern Operating Systems (UNIX, Windows NTFS, Linux ext4) implement **sequential + direct access natively** as the *basic access model*, and provide **indexed access** through application-level libraries (DBMS) or specialized file systems.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a **library** with three different ways to find a book:

- **No Structure (Unstructured)** — You get a huge pile of unsorted book pages and must read them page by page to find anything (like reading a raw `.log` file).
- **Simple Record Structure + Sequential Access** — The library arranges all books on a long conveyor belt in order; you start at one end and slide through until you find your target. Tapes work this way.
- **Direct Access** — Each book is placed on a numbered shelf slot; you jump directly to shelf `#42` to grab that book. CDs and hard disks work this way.
- **Indexed Access** — The library maintains a **card index (catalog)**. You tell the librarian the *book title*, and the catalog instantly tells you *which shelf slot* to jump to. Databases and `ISAM` files work this way.

---

## 1.4 The Three Logical File Models at a Glance

| Dimension | Unstructured | Simple Record | Indexed / Complex |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit of I/O** | Byte | Record | Record |
| **Format known to OS?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Typical Use** | Text, images, executables | Log files, transaction batches | Databases, ISAM |
| **Real OS example** | UNIX `open(2)` byte stream | Flat record files | DBMS B+ tree files |
| **Access Methods supported** | Sequential + Direct | Sequential + Direct | Sequential + Direct + Indexed |

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Sector size (modern HDD/SSD):** typically **512 bytes (legacy)** or **4096 bytes (4 KiB, advanced format)**.
- **Block (cluster) size in ext4/NTFS:** typically **4 KiB** to **64 KiB**.
- **Records per block (RPF):** calculated as $\lfloor B / R \rfloor$ where $B$ = block size, $R$ = record size.
- **Average disk access time:** $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These constants are routinely used in KTU numerical problems involving *direct access address calculation* and *sequential access time estimation*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Walkthrough of Each File Structure

### 2.1.1 Unstructured (Byte Stream / No Internal Structure)

- The OS views the file as a **flat stream of bytes** with no logical boundaries.
- The OS does **not impose any interpretation** on the contents.
- The meaning of the bytes is left entirely to the **application program**.
- Most modern general-purpose OSes (UNIX, Linux, Windows) use this as their **default underlying model**, and build richer structures on top via libraries (e.g., `stdio`, `std::iostream`).

**Why?** — Provides **maximum flexibility**. Any file format (text, binary, image, video) can be represented.

**How?** — The file system only stores: *(filename, byte offset, length, attributes, location pointers)*. No record boundaries exist in the raw bytes.

---

### 2.1.2 Simple Record Structure

- The file consists of a **sequence of records**, each of which has a well-defined internal format.
- Records can be:
  - **Fixed-length** — every record occupies the same number of bytes. Simple address arithmetic: record $i$ starts at byte offset $(i-1) \cdot R$.
  - **Variable-length** — each record carries its own length field. Address arithmetic requires scanning.
- Common in **legacy mainframe systems** and **simple log files**.

**Why?** — The OS (or a library) can read/write whole records atomically, enabling field-level access.

**How?** — Record boundaries are either:
  - Implicit (fixed-length case, OS computes them), or
  - Explicit (delimiter character, length prefix, or record header).

---

### 2.1.3 Complex (Indexed / Tree) Structure

- Records are organized using **indexes** (primary, secondary, clustering) or **tree structures** (B-tree, B+ tree).
- Enables **associative access**: *"Give me the record whose key = 'Kerala'"* rather than *"Give me record #4571"*.
- Heavily used in **Database Management Systems** built on top of the OS.

**Why?** — Sequential scan is too slow for large datasets; direct access requires knowing the offset; indexes provide **fast key-based lookup**.

**How?** — A separate **index file** maintains (key → pointer) mappings. To retrieve a record by key, search the index (often a balanced tree) to get a pointer, then perform one direct read.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Walkthrough of Each Access Method

### 2.2.1 Sequential Access

- Records are accessed in the same order in which they were written.
- Implemented by maintaining a **file position pointer** that advances on every read/write.
- Operations:
  - `read_next()` — advance pointer by one record.
  - `write_next()` — write at current pointer and advance.
  - `rewind()` — reset pointer to position 0.
- Cannot skip backwards freely (some systems allow `reset` to BOF only).

**Best suited for:** tapes, log files, batch processing.

### 2.2.2 Direct Access (Random Access)

- Each record has a **logical record number (LRN)** in the range $[0, N-1]$.
- The OS allows **jumping directly** to any byte offset using `seek()` (or `fseek`, `lseek`).
- Direct access **requires the underlying device to support addressing**, e.g., disk cylinders/sectors. Tapes do not.

**Address translation (the core of KTU questions):**

$$B = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{LRN}}{R_{pb}} \right\rfloor$$

$$O = (\text{LRN} \bmod R_{pb}) \cdot R$$

where:
- $B$ = block number containing the desired record,
- $O$ = offset (in bytes) of the record within the block,
- $\text{LRN}$ = logical record number (often 0-indexed),
- $R_{pb}$ = records per block,
- $R$ = record size (in bytes).

### 2.2.3 Indexed Access

- Builds a **separate index structure** on top of a directly accessible file.
- Two common variants in KTU syllabus:
  1. **Single-level index** — A small index file maps each key → record offset. The entire index is kept in memory for O(1) lookup.
  2. **Multi-level index** — Index itself is too large to fit in memory; it is organized as a tree (B+ tree in DBMS, ISAM in legacy systems).
- Operations:
  - `read(key)` — search index → get block number → direct read.
  - `write(key, value)` — update index → write record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"how does indexed access differ from direct access?"* — the answer is that **direct access uses positional offset (record number)**, while **indexed access uses content-based keys** and resolves them through an index.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU University Exam (ESE) and Internal Assessment (Series tests).

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{pb} = \left\lfloor B / R \right\rfloor$ | Records per block | dimensionless |
| 2 | $B = \left\lfloor \text{LRN} / R_{pb} \right\rfloor$ | Block number of record $\text{LRN}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $O = (\text{LRN} \bmod R_{pb}) \cdot R$ | Byte offset within the block | bytes |
| 4 | $N_{max} = R_{pb} \cdot N_{blocks}$ | Maximum number of records in a file | records |
| 5 | $T_{acc} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{xfer}$ | Average disk access time | ms |
| 6 | $T_{xfer} = B_{size} / R_{xfer}$ | Transfer time | ms |
| 7 | $T_{seq} = N_{rec} \cdot T_{acc}$ | Sequential read of $N_{rec}$ records | ms |
| 8 | $T_{rand} = N_{rec} \cdot (T_{seek} + T_{rot})$ | Pure random reads (no locality) | ms |
| 9 | $S_{index} = (K_{size} + P_{size}) \cdot N_{rec}$ | Index size (single level) | bytes |
| 10 | $T_{idx} = T_{search\_index} + T_{disk\_read}$ | Indexed access latency | ms |

Where:
- $B$ = block size (bytes), $R$ = record size (bytes),
- $K_{size}$ = key size, $P_{size}$ = pointer size,
- $R_{xfer}$ = disk transfer rate (bytes/ms),
- $T_{seek}$ = average seek time, $T_{rot}$ = average rotational latency.

> **Engineering utility:** These formulas are foundational in **DBMS**, **storage engineering**, **log-structured file systems (LFS)**, **SSD wear-leveling algorithms**, and **Hadoop HDFS block placement**.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Matrix — Access Methods

| Property | Sequential | Direct | Indexed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Search time** | $O(N)$ | $O(1)$ for known LRN | $O(\log N)$ for tree, $O(1)$ for hash |
| **Read whole file** | Very fast | Slow (seeks each time) | Slow + index overhead |
| **Random read** | Slow | Very fast | Very fast |
| **Insertion in middle** | Requires rewrite | Direct overwrite possible | Requires index update + possible split |
| **Storage overhead** | None | None | Index space |
| **Device requirement** | Any sequential device (tape OK) | Addressable device only | Addressable + extra index storage |
| **Used by** | Logs, batch jobs | OS files, executables | DBMS, ISAM |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Since this topic is largely **conceptual + arithmetic** (not pure calculus), the "derivations" below focus on **exhaustive address-translation calculations** and **access-time analyses** that are routinely tested in KTU ESE. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Block and Offset Calculation for Direct Access

**Problem Context:** Given a file with $N$ records of fixed length $R$ bytes stored on a disk whose physical block size is $B$ bytes, derive the formula that the OS uses to compute the physical (block, offset) pair for an arbitrary record LRN.

### Step 1 — Define the Records per Block

The OS groups consecutive records into a physical block. The number of records that fully fit inside one block of $B$ bytes is:

$$R_{pb} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor$$

**Reasoning:** Each record consumes exactly $R$ bytes; the largest count that does not exceed the block size is the floor division.

### Step 2 — Express the Byte Offset of Record $\text{LRN}$

The first byte of record $\text{LRN}$ (0-indexed) lies at byte offset:

$$\text{ByteOffset} = \text{LRN} \cdot R$$

Because every record before it contributes exactly $R$ bytes.

### Step 3 — Convert Byte Offset to (Block, Offset-in-Block)

A block contains $R_{pb}$ records, equivalent to $R_{pb} \cdot R$ bytes. Therefore the block index is obtained by integer division:

$$B_{num} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{ByteOffset}}{R_{pb} \cdot R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{LRN} \cdot R}{R_{pb} \cdot R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{LRN}}{R_{pb}} \right\rfloor$$

The byte offset *within* that block is the remainder times $R$:

$$O = (\text{LRN} \bmod R_{pb}) \cdot R$$

### Step 4 — Final Compact Form (KTU High-Yield Result)

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{num} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{LRN}}{R_{pb}} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
O &= (\text{LRN} \bmod R_{pb}) \cdot R \\[4pt]
R_{pb} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical formula** the OS uses every time you call `seek()` + `read()` on a direct-access file.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Direct Access Address Calculation

**Given:**
- Record size $R = 100$ bytes.
- Block size $B = 1000$ bytes.
- File contains $N = 100{,}000$ records.
- Compute the physical location of record number $\text{LRN} = 30545$ (1-indexed, so internally stored as $\text{LRN}_{0} = 30544$).

### Step 1 — Compute $R_{pb}$

$$R_{pb} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1000}{100} \right\rfloor = 10 \text{ records/block}$$

### Step 2 — Block Number

$$B_{num} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{LRN}_{0}}{R_{pb}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{30544}{10} \right\rfloor = 3054$$

### Step 3 — Offset Within Block

$$O = (30544 \bmod 10) \cdot 100 = 4 \cdot 100 = 400 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

Record $30545$ is the **5th record (index 4)** in block **#3054**, starting at byte **400** within that block.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Sequential vs Random Access Time

**Given:**
- Disk parameters: $T_{seek} = 8$ ms, $T_{rot} = 4$ ms (half-rotation avg), $R_{xfer} = 100$ MB/s.
- Block size $B = 4$ KB.
- We wish to read $N_{rec} = 1000$ records, each of size $R = 1$ KB.

### Step 1 — Records per Block

$$R_{pb} = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{1024} \right\rfloor = 4 \text{ records/block}$$

### Step 2 — Number of Blocks

$$N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{rec}}{R_{pb}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{4} \right\rceil = 250 \text{ blocks}$$

### Step 3 — Transfer Time per Block

$$T_{xfer} = \frac{B}{R_{xfer}} = \frac{4096 \text{ bytes}}{100 \cdot 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} = 40.96 \text{ µs} \approx 0.041 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4 — Single-Block Access Time

$$T_{acc} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{xfer} = 8 + 4 + 0.041 = 12.041 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 5 — Sequential Read of All 1000 Records

Sequential access amortizes the seek cost — only the *first* block needs a full seek+rotation; subsequent blocks benefit from locality. Approximation:

$$T_{seq} \approx T_{seek} + T_{rot} + (N_{blocks} - 1) \cdot T_{rot} + N_{blocks} \cdot T_{xfer}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{seq} &\approx 8 + 4 + 249 \cdot 4 + 250 \cdot 0.041 \\
&\approx 8 + 4 + 996 + 10.25 \\
&\approx 1018.25 \text{ ms} \approx 1.02 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Pure Random Read of All 1000 Records

$$T_{rand} \approx N_{blocks} \cdot (T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{xfer}) = 250 \cdot 12.041 \approx 3010.25 \text{ ms} \approx 3.01 \text{ s}$$

### Step 7 — Speedup Factor

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{rand}}{T_{seq}} \approx \frac{3.01}{1.02} \approx 2.95 \times$$

**Inference:** Sequential access is roughly **3× faster** than random access for this workload — directly motivates why logs, tape backups, and LSM-trees prefer sequential patterns.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Application Scenario | Recommended Access Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Payroll batch processing | Sequential | Whole file scanned once, fastest bulk read |
| Airline reservation lookup by PNR | Indexed (B+ tree) | Point query by key |
| Reading an MP3 file | Sequential (byte stream) | Bulk linear consumption |
| Compiling object code into an executable | Direct + Sequential | Random writes to file sections |
| Database range query | Indexed + Sequential | Index locates starting block, then sequential scan of range |
| ATM transaction lookup | Indexed | Exact match on account number |
| Sorting huge file with limited RAM | Sequential | External merge sort uses sequential runs |

---

## 3.5 Index Size Calculation (KTU Numerical Favourite)

**Given:** $N = 50{,}000$ records, key size $K_{size} = 16$ bytes, pointer size $P_{size} = 8$ bytes. Find the index size.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{index} &= N \cdot (K_{size} + P_{size}) \\
&= 50{,}000 \cdot (16 + 8) \\
&= 50{,}000 \cdot 24 \\
&= 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 1.2 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Follow-up:** If the index itself exceeds the main memory (assume RAM = 256 KB), a multi-level (tree) index is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often forget that **index lookup itself requires disk I/O** for large indexes. The total indexed-access time is $T_{index\_search} + T_{disk\_read}$, **not** just $T_{disk\_read}$. Always account for both.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — File Structure Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["File Structure"] --> B["Unstructured Byte Stream"]
    A --> C["Simple Record Structure"]
    A --> D["Complex Indexed Structure"]
    B --> B1["Treated as opaque bytes by OS"]
    B --> B2["Example UNIX text or binary file"]
    C --> C1["Fixed Length Records"]
    C --> C2["Variable Length Records"]
    C1 --> C1a["Easy random access by offset"]
    C2 --> C2a["Requires delimiter or length prefix"]
    D --> D1["Single Level Index"]
    D --> D2["Multi Level Tree Index"]
    D1 --> D1a["Index fits in RAM O of 1 lookup"]
    D2 --> D2a["B plus tree used by DBMS"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Access Methods Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: Need to access a record"] --> Q1{"How will records be read?"}
    Q1 -- "In written order" --> SEQ["Sequential Access"]
    Q1 -- "By record number offset" --> DIR["Direct Access"]
    Q1 -- "By key value content" --> IDX["Indexed Access"]
    SEQ --> S1["Tape like behaviour file pointer advances"]
    DIR --> D1["Compute block and offset using formula"]
    IDX --> I1["Search index structure for key"]
    I1 --> I2{"Index fits in memory?"}
    I2 -- "Yes" --> I3["Linear scan of index O of 1"]
    I2 -- "No" --> I4["Tree traversal of multi level index O of log N"]
    D1 --> D2["Issue disk seek to block"]
    D2 --> D3["Read record at offset"]
    S1 --> S2["Read next record"]
    I3 --> D2
    I4 --> D2
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Indexed Access Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["User Program"] -->|"read key K"| FS["File System Interface"]
    FS --> IDX["Index Manager"]
    IDX -->|"Search key K"| IT["Index Table In Memory or Disk"]
    IT -->|"Returns pointer Pn"| IDX
    IDX -->|"Pointer Pn"| FOP["File Operations Layer"]
    FOP -->|"Seek to block Bn"| DISK["Physical Disk"]
    DISK -->|"Returns record R"| FOP
    FOP -->|"Returns record R to user"| U
    style IDX fill:#FFE0B2
    style IT fill:#C8E6C9
    style DISK fill:#FFCDD2
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Direct Access Address Translation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LRN["Logical Record Number LRN"] --> DIV{"Divide by Rpb"}
    DIV -->|"Quotient"| BN["Block Number Bn"]
    DIV -->|"Remainder"| OFS["Offset Within Block"]
    OFS -->|"Multiply by R"| BO["Byte Offset O"]
    BN --> DM["Disk Manager"]
    BO --> DM
    DM -->|"Seek to block Bn"| DK["Physical Disk"]
    DK --> RREC["Returns Record at LRN"]
```

---

## 4.5 Conceptual Schematic — Sequential Access Tape Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Record 1"] --> R2["Record 2"] --> R3["Record 3"] --> R4["Record 4"] --> R5["Record 5"]
    RP["Read Pointer"] -.->|"Advances only forward"| R1
    style RP fill:#FFF59D
```

In sequential access, the read pointer can only move in one direction (forward); to go back, you must `rewind()` the file — analogous to physically rewinding a magnetic tape.

---

## 4.6 Comparative Schematic — Address Computation Pseudocode (Read-Only Illustration)

> [!NOTE]
> This is **architectural pseudocode** for explanation only — KTU ESE rarely requires code, but writing the logic clarifies the address translation. **No programming language syntax is enforced;** this is conceptual flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input LRN and record size R and block size B"] --> B["Compute Rpb equals floor B over R"]
    B --> C["Compute Block equals floor LRN over Rpb"]
    C --> D["Compute Offset equals LRN mod Rpb times R"]
    D --> E["Issue disk read at Block and Offset"]
    E --> F["Return record to caller"]
```

This visual aligns exactly with the algebraic derivation in SECTION 3.1.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between sequential access, direct access, and indexed access methods in a file system. State one real-world example where each is most suitable.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Sequential Access:** Records are read one after another in the order in which they appear in the file. A `read_next()` operation advances an internal file pointer; to restart, the file must be `rewind()`-ed. *Best for:* magnetic tape backup, log file analysis.
- **Direct Access:** Each record can be directly read/written by specifying its **logical record number (LRN)**. The OS computes the physical (block, offset) pair using $B = \lfloor \text{LRN}/R_{pb} \rfloor$ and $O = (\text{LRN} \bmod R_{pb}) \cdot R$. *Best for:* reading an MP3 file at an arbitrary time-offset, compiling object files.
- **Indexed Access:** A separate **index structure** maps keys to record locations. Lookup proceeds by searching the index for the requested key, then performing one direct read. *Best for:* database queries by primary key, library catalog search.

---

### Question 2 [KTU Series Test 2 — Model Question]
**Explain the meaning of "no-structure" file. Why do most modern Operating Systems (UNIX, Windows) use this as their default internal model?**

**Model Answer:**

A **no-structure (unstructured) file** is one in which the operating system does **not** impose any logical record boundaries on the stored bytes. The OS views the file as an opaque linear sequence of bytes — meaning, structure, and field boundaries are interpreted solely by the **application program** that reads the file.

**Why it is the default:**
1. **Maximum flexibility** — Any file format (text, PNG, MP4, ELF binary) can be represented using the same primitive interface (`open`, `read`, `write`, `seek`).
2. **Simplicity of implementation** — The OS only needs to track *(filename, byte offset, byte count, disk locations)*.
3. **Portability across applications** — Each application defines its own structure on top of the byte stream.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Part B carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between two complete question sets.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain the three file structures recognized by an Operating System. Compare them on the basis of record size flexibility, OS awareness, and search complexity.

**Model Solution:**

The OS recognizes three file structures:

**1. Unstructured (Byte Stream):**
- File is a flat sequence of bytes with no record boundaries.
- The OS is unaware of any internal meaning — applications interpret the bytes.
- Search complexity: $O(N)$ linear scan over the whole byte stream.
- *Diagram:* a long rectangle representing bytes $b_1, b_2, b_3, \ldots, b_N$ without any internal separators.

**2. Simple Record Structure:**
- File is a sequence of records, each of fixed or variable length.
- Fixed-length records enable **direct address calculation**:
  $$\text{Byte Offset of record } i = (i - 1) \cdot R$$
- Variable-length records require a length prefix or delimiter.
- Search complexity: $O(N)$ sequential, $O(1)$ for known LRN.
- *Diagram:* $R_1 \mid R_2 \mid R_3 \mid \ldots \mid R_N$ — a linear chain of equal or variable-sized cells.

**3. Complex (Indexed) Structure:**
- File is organized using an **index** mapping (key → record location).
- May use a single-level index (in-memory) or multi-level index (B+ tree).
- Search complexity: $O(1)$ for hash index, $O(\log N)$ for tree index.
- *Diagram:* main file $F$ of records linked to a separate index file $I$ via pointers.

**Comparison Table (Examiner-Key Style):**

| Criterion | Unstructured | Simple Record | Indexed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Record size flexibility | N/A (no records) | Fixed or variable | Fixed typically |
| OS aware of structure? | No | Yes | Yes (index-aware) |
| Search complexity | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ or $O(1)$ by LRN | $O(1)$ or $O(\log N)$ |
| Extra storage | None | None | Index file |
| Example | `.txt` file | `.csv` log file | DBMS table |

#### (b) [7 Marks] A file contains 50,000 fixed-length records of 256 bytes each. The disk has a block size of 4096 bytes. Compute (i) the number of records per block, (ii) the number of blocks required for the file, (iii) the physical (block number, byte offset) pair for record number 12,345 (1-indexed).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Records per block:**

$$R_{pb} = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{256} \right\rfloor = 16 \text{ records/block}$$

**(ii) Number of blocks for 50,000 records:**

$$N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{R_{pb}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{16} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3125 \right\rceil = 3125 \text{ blocks}$$

**(iii) Physical address of record 12,345:**

Convert to 0-indexed: $\text{LRN}_0 = 12345 - 1 = 12344$.

$$
B_{num} = \left\lfloor \frac{12344}{16} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 771.5 \right\rfloor = 771
$$

$$
O = (12344 \bmod 16) \cdot 256 = (8) \cdot 256 = 2048 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Answer:** Record **12,345** resides in **Block #771** at byte offset **2048** within that block (i.e., it is the 9th record — index 8 — inside block 771).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain indexed access in detail. With a diagram, describe single-level and two-level indexed access. When is a two-level index necessary?

**Model Solution:**

**Indexed Access Definition:** A file access method in which records are located by a **content-based key** rather than by positional offset. A separate **index structure** maintains the mapping (key → record location).

**Single-Level Index:**
- One index file $I$ contains entries $(K_i, P_i)$ where $K_i$ is the key and $P_i$ is the pointer to the actual record in the data file $F$.
- Lookup procedure:
  1. Search $I$ for the requested key $K$ (linear scan if small, binary search otherwise).
  2. Read pointer $P$.
  3. Direct-read the record from $F$ at the location given by $P$.
- *Diagram:* $I$ is a small array $\{(K_1, P_1), (K_2, P_2), \ldots\}$ with arrows pointing into $F$.

**Two-Level Index:**
- When the single-level index itself is too large to fit in main memory, it is split into chunks.
- An **outer (top-level) index** points to chunks of the **inner (lower-level) index**.
- Lookup:
  1. Binary search the top-level index → identify the chunk.
  2. Bring that chunk of the lower-level index into memory.
  3. Search the chunk → get data record pointer.
  4. Direct-read the record from the data file.

- *Diagram:* A hierarchical tree — root index → intermediate index pages → leaf index pages → data records.

**When is two-level necessary?**
- When the single-level index size $>$ main memory size.
- When search latency must be minimized for very large files (DBMS tables with millions of rows).
- Implemented in practice as **B+ trees** in virtually every modern database engine.

#### (b) [7 Marks] An indexed file contains 100,000 records. The index uses keys of size 12 bytes and pointers of size 8 bytes. The system has 256 KB of RAM dedicated to caching the index. (i) Calculate the size of the index. (ii) Does it fit in RAM? (iii) If not, propose a solution.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Index size:**

$$
S_{index} = N \cdot (K_{size} + P_{size}) = 100{,}000 \cdot (12 + 8) = 100{,}000 \cdot 20 = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.91 \text{ MB}
$$

**(ii) Does it fit in RAM?**

RAM available $= 256 \text{ KB} = 262{,}144 \text{ bytes}$.
Index size $= 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 1953 \text{ KB}$.

Since $1{,}953 \text{ KB} \gg 256 \text{ KB}$, **the index does NOT fit in RAM** (it is about **7.6× larger** than the available cache).

**(iii) Proposed Solution:**

Adopt a **multi-level (tree) index** — for example, a **B+ tree** of order such that each node fits within one disk block (e.g., 4 KB).

- Top-level index kept entirely in RAM (small, fast).
- Lower-level nodes fetched on demand from disk.
- Search complexity: $O(\log_b N)$ where $b$ is the tree's branching factor (typically 100–200 for B+ trees), giving $\sim 3$ disk I/Os even for millions of records.

Alternative: **ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Method)** with a static multi-level structure, or **Hash-based index** if only equality lookups are needed.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "File Structure" with "Access Method":** They are *related but distinct*. Structure = internal logical layout. Access = retrieval algorithm. Examiners specifically award marks for this distinction.
> 2. **Off-by-one indexing errors:** Record numbers in KTU problems are often **1-indexed** in the question but the formula uses **0-indexed** internally. Converting $\text{LRN} = \text{given} - 1$ is essential; forgetting it yields wrong block numbers.
> 3. **Using integer division inconsistently:** Students sometimes use regular `/` (floating) instead of floor division $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$. In address calculation, **always use integer/floor division**.
> 4. **Forgetting the index lookup cost:** When asked to compute indexed-access time, students often compute only the data-read time, missing the **index search time**.
> 5. **Mixing up rotation time and full rotation:** Average rotational latency is **half** of one full rotation $T_{rot} = T_{full}/2$, not the full rotation.
> 6. **Not citing block size when computing offset:** The offset is in **bytes within the block**, not in records — students forget to multiply the remainder by $R$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU ESE — File Structure & Access Methods:**

- ☐ **Three file structures:** *Unstructured (byte stream)*, *Simple Record (fixed/variable)*, *Complex (indexed/tree)*.
- ☐ **Three access methods:** *Sequential*, *Direct (Random)*, *Indexed (Associative)*.
- ☐ **Sequential access** uses a forward-only file pointer; tape analogy; $O(N)$ search.
- ☐ **Direct access** uses logical record number LRN; computes (block, offset) using:
   - $R_{pb} = \lfloor B/R \rfloor$
   - $B_{num} = \lfloor \text{LRN}_0 / R_{pb} \rfloor$
   - $O = (\text{LRN}_0 \bmod R_{pb}) \cdot R$
- ☐ **Indexed access** uses key-based lookup via a separate index file; total cost = index search + data read.
- ☐ **Index size** $= N \cdot (K_{size} + P_{size})$; if $>$ RAM, use **multi-level / B+ tree index**.
- ☐ **Disk access time** $T_{acc} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{xfer}$; $T_{rot}$ = half rotation on average.
- ☐ **Sequential read of a file** is faster than random read because of locality — only one seek amortized over many blocks.
- ☐ **Modern OSes** (UNIX, Windows) use **unstructured byte stream as default**; applications impose structure.
- ☐ **Indexed sequential access method (ISAM)** = combination of indexed + sequential; legacy but still exam-relevant.
- ☐ **Always convert 1-indexed question numbers** to 0-indexed before applying the formula.
- ☐ **Know the difference** between *logical* file structure and *physical* file allocation (contiguous / linked / indexed allocation — covered in subsequent topics).
- ☐ **Be ready to draw diagrams** of byte stream, fixed-record file, index file, and B+ tree; KTU examiners explicitly reward neat labelled diagrams (1–2 marks).
- ☐ **Pitfall:** Index lookup itself needs disk I/O for large indexes — total access time is **not** just $T_{disk\_read}$ of the data block.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protection, File-System Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Protection, File-System Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Protection – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Protection** is a mechanism provided by the operating system to control the access of processes, users, and programs to the resources defined by a computer system. It enforces policies that ensure only authorized entities can perform specific operations on specific objects, thereby maintaining system integrity, confidentiality, and availability.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, protection is the second pillar of **Security and Protection** in the OS, often discussed in tandem with **Security** (the defense against external threats) and **Defense** (the recovery mechanism after an attack). Protection primarily deals with **internal** threats — a buggy or malicious process attempting unauthorized access.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Favourite Question):**
> - **Protection** → Internal access control (process vs resource)
> - **Security** → External threats (viruses, hackers, worms)
> - **Defense** → Post-attack recovery

---

### 1.2 Goals of Protection

The classical five goals of protection (as per the KTU prescribed textbook *Silberschatz, Galvin & Gagne*):

1. **Ensure each process gets the resources it is entitled to** (fairness).
2. **Mediate conflicting requests** from competing processes (arbitration).
3. **Enforce policies** that dictate what each entity may do (policy enforcement).
4. **Provide a mechanism** for the enforcement of these policies (mechanism).
5. **Distinguish between authorized and unauthorized usage** (authentication + authorization).

---

### 1.3 Domain of Protection

A **protection domain** specifies the set of resources accessible to a process and the operations (rights) allowed on each resource. Conceptually, it is a tuple:

$$\text{Domain} = (D, R, A)$$

where:
- $D$ → set of domains
- $R$ → set of resources (objects)
- $A$ → access-rights matrix mapping (domain × object) → rights set

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Co-working Office Building**
> Imagine a multi-tenant office tower. Each tenant (process) has a **key card** (domain) that unlocks only certain floors (resources) and only allows specific actions (read printer, use conference room, access server room). The **building security system** acts as the OS — it does not trust the tenant's intentions; it checks the key card at every door. A software engineer with a "Project Manager" key card cannot enter the HR records room. This is exactly how protection domains work: even if a process behaves maliciously, the domain limits the blast radius.

---

### 1.4 File-System Implementation – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **File-System Implementation** refers to the on-disk and in-memory data structures and algorithms used by the OS to store, retrieve, and manage files. It encompasses the layered architecture (Application → Logical File System → File-Organization Module → Basic File System → I/O Control), on-disk structures (boot block, superblock, inode table, data blocks), and allocation strategies that determine where file bytes physically reside on secondary storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Massive Library**
> Picture a colossal library with **millions of books** (files). The librarian's card catalog (in-memory metadata) tells you *which* shelf holds your book. The shelf itself (inodes) tells you the *exact bay and row*. The librarian follows strict rules (allocation methods) to either: place new books in a continuous row (contiguous), chain them with sticky notes pointing to the next shelf (linked), or maintain a master index page listing all shelf positions (indexed). The system also tracks every empty shelf (free-space management) so it knows where to put the next book.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Block size (typical)** | **4 KB** | Default in most UNIX/Linux file systems (ext4) |
| **Sector size** | **512 B / 4096 B** | Hardware-level unit |
| **Inode size (ext4)** | **256 B** | Holds 12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect pointers |
| **FAT entry size** | **12 / 16 / 32 bits** | Determines max volume size |
| **POSIX permissions** | **9 bits (rwx × 3)** | Owner, Group, Others |
| **ACL entries (typical)** | **32 / 1024** | Max per file in NTFS / ext4 |

---

### 1.6 The Access Matrix (Hartman's 1971 Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Access Matrix** is the abstract model of protection proposed by Lampson (1971) and formalized by Harrison, Ruzzo, and Ullman (1976). Rows = **domains**, Columns = **objects**, Cells = **access rights** (read/write/execute/append/own).

$$\text{access}(D_i, O_j) = \{r, w, x, a, \ldots\}$$

This is purely conceptual. Real systems **never** store the full matrix (it is sparse) — they use efficient implementations detailed in §2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Implementation of the Access Matrix (KTU High-Weight Topic)

Since the full access matrix is **sparse** (most entries are empty), four practical implementations are used:

#### 2.1.1 Global Table
A single large matrix of **(domain, object, rights)** triples kept in memory.

- **Pros**: Simple, uniform.
- **Cons**: Huge size — $O(\text{domains} \times \text{objects})$ entries. **Not scalable**. Never used in practice.

#### 2.1.2 Access Lists (Column-wise Storage) ⭐
For **each object**, store the ordered list `<domain, rights>` for every domain that can access it.

- **Pros**: Easy revocation (just delete/modify that object's list). Easy to add new domains.
- **Cons**: Long lists if an object is popular.
- **Used in**: UNIX file permissions, LISTSERV.

#### 2.1.3 Capability Lists (Row-wise Storage) ⭐
For **each domain**, store the list of `(object, rights)` pairs the domain can access.

- **Pros**: Fast access check (one lookup per request). Efficient for a process's needs.
- **Cons**: Hard revocation (must search all domains). Capability must be **unforgeable** → protected by kernel.
- **Used in**: Hydra, Cambridge CAP system, Amoeba.

#### 2.1.4 Lock-Key Mechanism
- Each **object** has a unique **lock** (bit pattern).
- Each **domain** has a unique **key** per object type.
- Access granted iff a key in the domain matches the lock on the object.
- **Pros**: Compact, dynamic.
- **Cons**: Hard revocation; not widely used commercially.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Trick for KTU:**
> "**Access list** = the **door** has the list of allowed visitors."
> "**Capability** = the **visitor** has the badge that proves they may enter."

#### 2.1.5 Revocation – A KTU Exam Favourite

| Scheme | Revocation Difficulty |
|---|---|
| Global Table | Easy |
| Access List | **Easy** (modify the list) |
| Capability List | **Hard** (must hunt all capabilities) |
| Lock-Key | Hard (must change the lock) |

---

### 2.2 Access Control Models (Advanced Protection)

#### 2.2.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC) ⭐
- Owner of the object decides who can access it and with what rights.
- **Example**: UNIX `chmod`, Windows NTFS ACLs.
- **Vulnerability**: Trojan-horse attacks — owner tricked into giving away access.

#### 2.2.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
- System-wide policy **cannot** be overridden by the owner.
- Labels (e.g., *Top Secret*, *Confidential*) on subjects and objects.
- **Bell-LaPadula model**: No read-up, no write-down (confidentiality).
- **Biba model**: No read-down, no write-up (integrity).

#### 2.2.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
- Access depends on **role** assigned to a user (e.g., doctor, nurse, accountant).
- Roles map to permissions; users map to roles.
- **Used in**: Enterprise systems, SELinux, database systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 1-Liner:** DAC = "I decide who touches my file." MAC = "The government decides." RBAC = "Your job title decides."

---

### 2.3 Protection in Practice (UNIX Example)

UNIX treats **each process domain = pair (UID, GID)**. On a `fork()`, the child inherits the parent's domain. On an `exec()`, the new program can switch to a new domain if the file is **setuid** or **setgid**.

The `setuid` bit is the cornerstone of UNIX privilege escalation. The protection system must therefore gate the **domain switch** with special checks.

---

### 2.4 File-System Implementation – Layered Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Layers of a File System (KTH-style KTU diagram)**

1. **Application Program** – issues `open(), read(), write()`.
2. **Logical File System** – manages metadata (file names, directories, FCB/FCB cache).
3. **File-Organization Module** – translates logical block # to physical block #; manages free space.
4. **Basic File System** – issues generic I/O commands (`read block 14`) to the device driver.
5. **I/O Control** – device driver; issues hardware-specific commands (e.g., disk seek, DMA).

---

### 2.5 On-Disk File-System Structure (The Standard Layout)

| Block # | Content |
|---|---|
| 0 | **Boot Control Block** – contains bootstrap code |
| 1 | **Superblock / Volume Control Block** – file-system type, size, free-block count, etc. |
| 2 … n | **Inode Table / FAT / Master File Table** – per-file metadata |
| … | **Directory blocks** – file names → inode numbers |
| … | **Data blocks** – actual file contents |

> [!NOTE]
> For **FAT** (File Allocation Table) file systems, the FAT is stored near the start and a copy is kept in the middle. For **UNIX**-style file systems, the superblock is replicated throughout the volume for crash recovery.

---

### 2.6 In-Memory File-System Structures

When the OS mounts a volume, it builds in-RAM structures:

- **Mount table** – which volumes are mounted and at which directory.
- **Directory cache** – recently accessed directory info.
- **System-wide open-file table** – one entry per `open()`.
- **Per-process open-file table** – pointer to the system table + current offset + access mode.
- **Buffer cache** – block-level I/O buffer.

---

### 2.7 File Allocation Methods ⭐⭐ (KTU Highest-Weight Subtopic)

#### 2.7.1 Contiguous Allocation

Each file occupies a **set of consecutive blocks** on disk.

- Directory entry stores: `<starting_block, length>`.
- **Pros**: Excellent read performance (no seeks). Simple. Supports random access (offset arithmetic).
- **Cons**:
  - **External fragmentation** — must be solved by compaction (expensive).
  - **Worst-fit** algorithm helps but cannot eliminate the problem.
  - **File size must be known in advance** — infeasible for most workloads.
- **Used in**: CD-ROMs, DVDs, IBM mainframe systems, SD cards in some modes.

**Worst-case external fragmentation** (for $n$ files, each varying in size):

$$\text{Fragmentation}_{\text{worst}} = O(n \cdot B)$$

where $B$ is the average block size.

#### 2.7.2 Linked Allocation

Each file is a **linked list of blocks**; each block contains a pointer to the next. Directory entry stores only the **start block**.

- **Pros**: No external fragmentation. File can grow easily.
- **Cons**:
  - **No random access** (must traverse from start).
  - Pointer overhead per block (a few bytes).
  - **Reliability** — a single bad pointer corrupts the rest of the file. Mitigation: use **FAT** in RAM (File Allocation Table) — classic MS-DOS approach.
- **Variants**: **Linked-list with FAT** (FAT12/16/32) — all pointers in a RAM table; enables random access.

#### 2.7.3 Indexed Allocation ⭐

Each file has an **index block** (i-node block) that contains a list of pointers to all its data blocks.

- **Pros**: Supports random access. No external fragmentation.
- **Cons**: Index block size limits maximum file size.
- **Variants** (the famous UNIX inode scheme):
  - **Direct pointers** — point straight to data.
  - **Single-indirect** — pointer to a block of pointers.
  - **Double-indirect** — pointer to a block of single-indirect blocks.
  - **Triple-indirect** — pointer to a block of double-indirect blocks.

**Max file size with $n$ direct pointers and block size $B$** (ignoring block # for pointer):

$$S_{\max} = n \cdot B + (B/p)^1 \cdot B + (B/p)^2 \cdot B + (B/p)^3 \cdot B$$

where $p$ is the bytes per pointer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic UNIX inode example (KTU Favourite):**
> 12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect, $B = 1024$ B, $p = 4$ B.
> Direct: $12 \times 1024 = 12,288$ B = **12 KB**
> Single: $(1024/4) \times 1024 = 256 \times 1024 = 256$ KB
> Double: $256^2 \times 1024 = 64$ MB
> Triple: $256^3 \times 1024 = 16$ GB
> **Total = 12 KB + 256 KB + 64 MB + 16 GB ≈ 16 GB**

---

### 2.8 Free-Space Management ⭐

The OS must keep track of free disk blocks. Four techniques:

| Method | Data Structure | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit Vector / Bit Map** | Array of bits, 1 = allocated, 0 = free | Simple; can find $k$ consecutive free blocks | Entire bit map must be in RAM for efficiency; e.g., 40 GB disk ⇒ 5 MB bit map |
| **Linked List** | Link all free blocks | No external fragmentation; simple | Traversing the list to find many free blocks is slow |
| **Grouping** | First free block holds addresses of $n$ other free blocks | Fast allocation | Still chain-based |
| **Counting** | For contiguous ranges, store `(start_address, count)` | Compact if files are allocated contiguously | Inefficient if fragmentation is high |

**Bit-vector size for a disk of size $D$ bytes and block size $B$ bytes:**

$$\text{Map size} = \frac{D}{B} \text{ bits} = \frac{D}{8B} \text{ bytes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** if the disk is $\geq 1$ TB, the bit map is **~32 MB** — borderline for RAM. Many modern systems use **B-trees** or **extents** instead (e.g., ext4, XFS).

---

### 2.9 Directory Implementation ⭐

#### 2.9.1 Linear List
A directory is a sorted list of `<file_name, inode_number>` entries.

- **Pros**: Simple.
- **Cons**: Linear search $O(n)$. Sorted = $O(\log n)$ via binary search, but insertions are expensive.

#### 2.9.2 Hash Table
Hash the file name to a bucket; each bucket has a chained list of entries.

- **Pros**: $O(1)$ lookup, insert, delete.
- **Cons**: Fixed bucket count → collisions; resizing hard.

#### 2.9.3 Tree-based (B-Tree) — Modern
Used in **NTFS**, **ReiserFS**, **HFS+**.

- **Pros**: $O(\log n)$ with bounded fanout; supports sorted directory listing efficiently.

---

### 2.10 Efficiency and Performance

To make file systems fast, OS designers use:
- **Buffer cache / page cache** — keeps recently used blocks in RAM.
- **Read-ahead / prefetching** — predict sequential reads and pull ahead.
- **Free-behind** — write back dirty pages lazily.
- **I/O scheduling** — elevator (SCAN) algorithm to reduce seek time.
- **Journaling** (ext3, ext4, NTFS, XFS) — write a log of metadata changes before committing, enabling fast recovery.
- **Log-structured file systems (LFS)** — write all changes to a sequential log, designed for SSDs.
- **Flash file systems** — use FTL (Flash Translation Layer); avoid in-place writes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Recovery** in a journaling file system:
> On boot, the OS scans the journal and **replays** (redo) any committed-but-not-applied transactions — achieving **crash-consistency** in milliseconds.

---

### 2.11 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Formulas You Will Ever Need for This Topic**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Max file size (indexed allocation, $k$-level indirection)** | $S_{\max} = d \cdot B + \sum_{i=1}^{k} (B/p)^{i} \cdot B$ | $d$=direct pointers, $B$=block size, $p$=pointer size |
| 2 | **Block addressable size of disk** | $N = D / B$ | $D$=disk size, $B$=block size |
| 3 | **Bit-vector map size** | $\text{Map} = D / (8B)$ bytes | 1 bit per block |
| 4 | **Avg seek time access for linked allocation** | $t_{\text{avg}} = (n-1)/2 \cdot (t_{\text{seek}} + t_{\text{rot}} + t_{\text{trans}})$ | $n$=block count, only for FAT-in-RAM |
| 5 | **Access time (contiguous, 1 block)** | $t_a = t_{\text{seek}} + t_{\text{rot}} + t_{\text{trans}}$ | Standard disk access |
| 6 | **Cache hit rate benefit** | $t_{\text{eff}} = h \cdot t_{\text{cache}} + (1-h) \cdot t_{\text{disk}}$ | $h$ = hit ratio |
| 7 | **External fragmentation worst case (contiguous)** | $F = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(s_i - 1)$ | where $s_i$ is the size of file $i$ in blocks |
| 8 | **Pointer overhead per block (linked)** | $O_v = p / B$ | $p$ = pointer size, $B$ = block size |
| 9 | **Direct pointers in inode (UNIX)** | Typically **12** | ext2/ext3/ext4 |
| 10 | **# pointers per index block** | $B / p$ | Block size / pointer size |

**CRITICAL:** Use $\vert$ for absolute value if needed. E.g. $t = \vert \Delta \theta \vert / \omega$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum File Size in UNIX Inode

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup (Classic UNIX inode — ext2):**
> - 12 direct pointers
> - 1 single-indirect pointer
> - 1 double-indirect pointer
> - 1 triple-indirect pointer
> - Block size $B = 1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ B}$
> - Disk pointer size $p = 4 \text{ B}$

**Step 1: Compute number of pointers per index block**

$$N_p = \frac{B}{p} = \frac{1024}{4} = 256 \text{ pointers per index block}$$

**Step 2: Capacity of each pointer type**

Direct block capacity (12 direct pointers point straight to data blocks):

$$C_{\text{direct}} = 12 \times B = 12 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 12 \text{ KB}$$

Single-indirect capacity (the single-indirect block holds 256 pointers, each to a 1 KB data block):

$$C_{\text{single}} = N_p \times B = 256 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 256 \text{ KB}$$

Double-indirect capacity:

$$C_{\text{double}} = N_p \times N_p \times B = 256^2 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 65{,}536 \text{ KB} = 64 \text{ MB}$$

Triple-indirect capacity:

$$C_{\text{triple}} = N_p^3 \times B = 256^3 \times 1 \text{ KB} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ KB} = 16 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 3: Total maximum file size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= C_{\text{direct}} + C_{\text{single}} + C_{\text{double}} + C_{\text{triple}} \\
&= 12 \text{ KB} + 256 \text{ KB} + 64 \text{ MB} + 16 \text{ GB} \\
&\approx 16.07 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A single file in classic UNIX (with these parameters) can be at most **~16 GB**. This is why later file systems (ext4) extended to 16 TB and beyond by using larger block sizes and more pointer levels.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bit-Vector Map Size for a Real Disk

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Disk size $D = 500$ GB, Block size $B = 4$ KB.
> **Find:** Memory needed to hold the bit map.

**Step 1: Number of blocks**

$$N = \frac{D}{B} = \frac{500 \times 10^9 \text{ B}}{4 \times 10^3 \text{ B}} = 125 \times 10^6 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 2: Number of bits required**

$$\text{Bits} = N = 1.25 \times 10^8 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Convert to bytes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Map size} &= \frac{1.25 \times 10^8 \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} \\
&= 1.5625 \times 10^7 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 15.6 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** ~16 MB of RAM to keep the entire bit map — feasible on modern systems but not on a 1980s PC. This is why **linked-list** and **counting** free-space methods were once more common.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linked Allocation Average Access Time

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A file occupies 100 blocks, linked allocation. Average seek $t_s = 8$ ms, rotational latency $t_r = 3$ ms, transfer time $t_{tr} = 0.1$ ms per block. Find average time to access the last block (assuming FAT is in RAM, so no extra seeks for traversal — only between data blocks).

**Step 1: Each block access (seek + rotate + transfer)**

$$t_a = t_s + t_r + t_{tr} = 8 + 3 + 0.1 = 11.1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Average position**

$$\text{Avg block} = \frac{N+1}{2} = \frac{100+1}{2} = 50.5 \text{ blocks from start}$$

**Step 3: Total time to reach average block (with seeks between data blocks)**

$$
