This is why "silent data corruption" is rare in HDDs. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | $T_s$ | Seek time (avg) | $T_s = T_s^{\min} + \dfrac{T_s^{\max} - T_s^{\min}}{3}$ | ms | One-third-of-the-way approximation | | $T_r$ | Rotational latency (avg) | $T_r = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{60}{R}$ where $R$ = RPM | ms | Average of full rotation | | $T_t$ | Transfer time | $T_t = \dfrac{B}{N \cdot \dfrac{R}{60}}$ | ms | $B$ = bytes to transfer, $N$ = bytes/track | | $T_{acc}$ | **Total Access Time** | $T_{acc} = T_s + T_r + T_t$ | ms | KTU favorite question | | $R$ | Platter rotation rate | — | RPM (revolutions per minute) | | | $C$ | Disk capacity | $C = \text{surfaces} \times \text{tracks/surface} \times \text{sectors/track} \times \text{sector size}$ | bytes | | | $T_{tot}$ | Time per full rotation | $T_{tot} = \dfrac{60}{R}$ | ms | | | $n_{max}$ | Max LBA sectors | $n_{max} = 2^{48} - 1$ | — | 48-bit LBA ⇒ up to **128 PiB** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **average** rotational latency is *half* a full rotation, not a full rotation. Average seek time is approximated as **1/3 of the full stroke** (not 1/2), because seeks are statistically biased toward middle tracks. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Database systems** (Oracle, PostgreSQL) measure performance in **IOPS** (I/O Operations Per Second) — directly derived from $1 / T_{acc}$. - **RAID controllers** stripe data across cylinders to minimize arm movement. - **SSD caching algorithms** (Intel SRT, bcache) exploit the **seek-time gap** to mask HDD latency. - **Disk scheduling algorithms** (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, FLOOK) are direct applications of cylinder geometry. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Disk Access Time Formula The total time to read or write a single logical block on a magnetic disk is composed of **three independent sequential delays**: $$ T_{acc} = T_s + T_r + T_t $$ We derive each term rigorously. ### Step 1 — Seek Time ($T_s$) The disk arm must be moved from its current cylinder to the target cylinder. - Let $T_s^{\min}$ = time to move arm by 1 cylinder (controller overhead, e.g., 0.5 ms). - Let $T_s^{\max}$ = time to move arm from innermost to outermost cylinder (e.g., 8 ms). - Assume linear acceleration: time is proportional to distance. The expected distance between a random source cylinder and a random destination cylinder (out of $N$ cylinders) is statistically: $$ E[\text{distance}] = \dfrac{N}{3} $$ Therefore the **average seek time** is: $$ T_s^{avg} = T_s^{\min} + \dfrac{T_s^{\max} - T_s^{\min}}{3} $$ > *Intuition:* "1/3 of the way" comes from the average absolute difference of two uniform random variables on $[0, N]$, which is $N/3$. ### Step 2 — Rotational Latency ($T_r$) After the head arrives on the correct track, the platter must rotate so that the target sector falls under the head. A full rotation takes: $$ T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{R} \text{ seconds} = \dfrac{60\,000}{R} \text{ ms} $$ Since the target sector can be anywhere in the rotation, the **average waiting time** is half a full rotation: $$ T_r = \dfrac{T_{rot}}{2} = \dfrac{30\,000}{R} \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 3 — Transfer Time ($T_t$) The read/write channel transfers bytes as the platter spins. The linear density of data is (approx) constant on a track — the entire track passes under the head in one rotation. Data rate of the track: $$ \text{Rate} = \dfrac{N_{track}}{T_{rot}} \quad [\text{bytes/sec}] $$ where $N_{track}$ = bytes per track. Transfer time for $B$ bytes: $$ T_t = \dfrac{B}{N_{track}} \cdot T_{rot} = \dfrac{B}{N_{track}} \cdot \dfrac{60}{R} $$ ### Step 4 — Composite $$ T_{acc} = T_s^{\min} + \dfrac{T_s^{\max} - T_s^{\min}}{3} \;+\; \dfrac{30\,000}{R} \;+\; \dfrac{60\,B}{R \cdot N_{track}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Variant) **Problem.** A disk has the following specifications: - Spindle speed: $R = 7200$ RPM - Average seek time: $T_s = 4$ ms - Bytes per track: $N_{track} = 256 \times 512 = 131\,072$ bytes - Request: read $B = 4096$ bytes (one 4-KiB block) **Compute $T_{acc}$.** ### Sub-step (i): Rotational latency $$ T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{7200} = 8.333 \text{ ms} $$ $$ T_r = \dfrac{T_{rot}}{2} = 4.167 \text{ ms} $$ ### Sub-step (ii): Transfer time $$ T_t = \dfrac{B}{N_{track}} \cdot T_{rot} = \dfrac{4096}{131072} \cdot 8.333 $$ $$ = 0.03125 \cdot 8.333 = 0.260 \text{ ms} $$ ### Sub-step (iii): Total access time $$ T_{acc} = T_s + T_r + T_t = 4 + 4.167 + 0.260 = \mathbf{8.427 \text{ ms}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering observation:** Transfer time is *negligible* compared to seek + rotational latency for a single block. **The mechanical delays dominate** — this is exactly why SSD performance is so disruptive (both $T_s$ and $T_r$ go to ~0). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CHS ↔ LBA Translation For a 2-platter disk (4 surfaces), 5000 tracks/surface, 1000 sectors/track, 512 bytes/sector. | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Surfaces (heads) $H$ | 4 | | Cylinders $C$ | 5000 | | Sectors per track $S$ | 1000 | | Sector size | 512 bytes | | Capacity | $4 \times 5000 \times 1000 \times 512 = \mathbf{10.24 \text{ GB}}$ | | LBA of $(Cyl, H, S) = (1234, 2, 5)$ | $L = 1234 \cdot (H \cdot S) + 2 \cdot S + 5$
$= 1234 \cdot 4000 + 2000 + 5$
$= \mathbf{4\,938\,005}$ | Inverse (recover CHS from LBA 4 938 005): - $tmp = L \div S = 4\,938\,005 \div 1000 = 4938$ remainder 5 - $H = 4938 \bmod 4 = 2$ - $C = 4938 \div 4 = 1234$ - $S = 5$ Thus $\text{LBA} \rightarrow (C, H, S) = (1234, 2, 5)$ ✓ --- ## 3.4 Performance Comparison: HDD vs SSD | Parameter | HDD | SSD (NAND Flash) | |---|---|---| | Seek time | 3–10 ms | **~0.05 ms** | | Rotational latency | 1–4 ms | **0** (no moving parts) | | Transfer rate | 100–250 MB/s | 500–3500 MB/s | | IOPS (random 4K) | 100–200 | 50 000–500 000 | | Cost / GB | ₹2–3 | ₹8–15 | | Endurance | Unlimited reads | Limited (P/E cycles) | | Data on power loss | Safe | **Unsafe** (needs supercap) | > [!TIP] > SSDs organize data in **pages** (4–16 KiB, read/write unit) and **blocks** (256–512 pages, *erase* unit). Writing to an already-written page requires an **erase-then-rewrite of the entire block** — this is the origin of *write amplification* and the *TRIM* command. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Disk Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Spindle Motor - rotates all platters in unison] B[Platter 1 - Top Surface - Head H1] C[Platter 1 - Bottom Surface - Head H2] D[Platter 2 - Top Surface - Head H3] E[Platter 2 - Bottom Surface - Head H4] F[Disk Arm Assembly - moves radially] G[Actuator - voice coil motor] H[Read Write Channel and Disk Controller] I[Host Interface - SATA - NVMe - SAS] A --- B A --- C A --- D A --- E B --- F C --- F D --- F E --- F F --- G G --- H H --- I classDef motor fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000 classDef platter fill:#dceefb,stroke:#36c,color:#000 classDef arm fill:#e2f7d8,stroke:#3a3,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#fff3c2,stroke:#c90,color:#000 class A motor class B,C,D,E platter class F,G arm class H,I ctrl ``` ## 4.2 Geometry Hierarchy — Tracks, Cylinders, Sectors ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PLATTER[Platter Surface - Top View] direction TB SP[Spindle - center] T0[Track 0 - outermost] T1[Track 1] T2[Track 2] T3[Track 3 - innermost] SP -.- T0 SP -.- T1 SP -.- T2 SP -.- T3 end subgraph SECTORS[Track 1 - Zoomed In] direction LR S1[Sector 0 - 512 B] S2[Sector 1 - 512 B] S3[Sector 2 - 512 B] S4[Sector 3 - 512 B] S5[Sector 4 - 512 B] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 end subgraph CYLINDER[3D Cylinder Concept - All Platters - Same Track Number] direction TB CT0[Track 3 on Surface 0] CT1[Track 3 on Surface 1] CT2[Track 3 on Surface 2] CT3[Track 3 on Surface 3] CT0 --- CT1 --- CT2 --- CT3 end PLATTER --> SECTORS PLATTER --> CYLINDER classDef geo fill:#e6e0f8,stroke:#636,color:#000 classDef sec fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000 classDef cyl fill:#dceefb,stroke:#36c,color:#000 class SP,T0,T1,T2,T3,PLATTER geo class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,SECTORS sec class CT0,CT1,CT2,CT3,CYLINDER cyl ``` ## 4.3 Logical Read Path — OS to Disk Controller ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant APP as User Application participant OS as Operating System participant FS as File System participant DRV as Disk Driver participant CTL as Disk Controller participant HDD as Platter and Head APP->>OS: read(filename, offset, len) OS->>FS: inode lookup - get block number FS-->>OS: block 4 938 005 OS->>DRV: request LBA 4 938 005 - 4 KiB DRV->>CTL: send LBA via SATA or NVMe CTL->>CTL: LBA to CHS translation
Cyl 1234 - Head 2 - Sector 5 CTL->>HDD: actuator move - seek HDD-->>CTL: head on track 1234 CTL->>HDD: wait for rotation HDD-->>CTL: target sector under head CTL->>HDD: read data via R/W channel HDD-->>CTL: 4 KiB plus ECC CTL-->>DRV: DMA to host memory DRV-->>OS: success - 4 KiB in buffer OS-->>APP: return data ``` ## 4.4 Sector Format — Inside a Single Sector ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Preamble - bit sync] AM[Address Mark - sector start] ID[ID Field - CHS info plus CRC] G2[Gap2] DT[Data Field - 512 B or 4 KiB] ECC[ECC - error correction] ISG[Inter-Sector Gap] P --> AM --> ID --> G2 --> DT --> ECC --> ISG ISG -.-> P classDef head fill:#fff3c2,stroke:#c90,color:#000 classDef data fill:#dceefb,stroke:#36c,color:#000 classDef meta fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000 class P,AM head class ID,G2,ECC,ISG meta class DT data ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **track**, **cylinder**, and **sector** in a magnetic disk. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Track** | A concentric ring on a single platter surface along which data is magnetically recorded. | | **Sector** | A fixed-size arc-shaped subdivision of a track — typically **512 bytes** — and the smallest addressable unit on the disk. | | **Cylinder** | The 3-D set of all tracks that have the **same radius** on **every surface** of the disk. Reading/writing within one cylinder requires *no* arm movement. | > A cylinder is therefore a vertical "column" through the stack of platters. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Explain the difference between **CHS** and **LBA** addressing. Why did the industry shift to LBA? (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** - **CHS** encodes each sector by its (Cylinder, Head, Sector) tuple. It is tied to physical geometry and is limited to $1024 \times 16 \times 63 \times 512 \text{ B} \approx 528 \text{ MB}$ under classic BIOS INT 13h. - **LBA** numbers every sector from $0$ to $N-1$ in a single linear address space. The disk's onboard controller performs the CHS translation internally. - The shift was driven by: (1) the 528 MB barrier, (2) **Zoned Bit Recording** making the physical sector count geometry-dependent and fluid, and (3) the need for large drives (>8.4 GB) and simpler OS code. - LBA is now extended to **48 bits** in SATA, supporting disks up to **128 PiB**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Sample]* > **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **physical structure of a magnetic hard disk**. Label the spindle, platters, surfaces, tracks, sectors, cylinders, actuator arm, and read/write heads. *(7 Marks)* > > **(b)** A disk rotates at **10 000 RPM**, has an **average seek time of 5 ms**, and stores **1 MB per track**. Compute the total access time to read a file of **64 KB** starting from a random location. *(7 Marks)* ### Model Solution — Part (a) A hard disk consists of: - **Spindle** – the central motor shaft on which 1 to 5 **platters** are mounted. All platters rotate at the same angular velocity. - **Platters** – rigid disks (3.5″ in modern drives) coated with a thin ferromagnetic film. Each platter has **two recording surfaces** (top and bottom). - **Read/Write heads** – one per surface. They "fly" nanometres above the surface on an air cushion, never touching it during operation. - **Actuator arm** – a comb-shaped assembly that holds all heads and pivots about a single axis (voice-coil motor). All heads move together, so they are always on **the same cylinder number**. - **Tracks** – concentric circles on each surface (typically > 500 000 per inch). - **Sectors** – arc segments of a track, holding **512 B** (legacy) or **4 KiB** (Advanced Format). - **Cylinders** – the set of all tracks vertically aligned across all surfaces. *(See SECTION 4.1 diagram for full schematic. Examiner's note: drawing a top-view *and* a 3-D side view of platters typically earns full 7 marks.)* ### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - $R = 10\,000$ RPM - $T_s = 5$ ms - $N_{track} = 1 \text{ MB} = 1\,048\,576$ bytes (using $2^{20}$) or $10^6$ bytes (using decimal). - $B = 64 \text{ KB} = 65\,536$ bytes **Step 1 — Rotational latency** $$ T_r = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{60}{10\,000} = \dfrac{30}{10\,000}\text{ s} = 3 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Transfer time** Track passes under head in one full rotation: $$ T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{10\,000} = 6 \text{ ms} $$ $$ T_t = \dfrac{B}{N_{track}} \cdot T_{rot} = \dfrac{65\,536}{1\,048\,576} \cdot 6 = 0.0625 \cdot 6 = 0.375 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Total access time** $$ T_{acc} = T_s + T_r + T_t = 5 + 3 + 0.375 = \mathbf{8.375 \text{ ms}} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Internal Choice for same module]* > **(a)** Explain the **logical block addressing (LBA) scheme**. How is an LBA translated to a physical CHS address on a disk with $H$ heads, $S$ sectors per track, and $C$ cylinders? *(7 Marks)* > > **(b)** Compare **HDD and SSD** with respect to (i) access time, (ii) IOPS, (iii) cost/GB, (iv) endurance, (v) data retention on power loss, and (vi) effect of fragmentation. *(7 Marks)* ### Model Solution — Part (a) **LBA (Logical Block Addressing):** - A linear addressing scheme in which every sector on the disk is assigned a unique integer from $0$ to $N-1$, where $N$ is the total number of sectors. - The OS, file system, and BIOS all use LBA; the disk controller hides the physical geometry. **Translation Formula** (CHS per sector stored in ascending sector order within a cylinder): $$ \text{LBA} = (Cyl \times H + Head) \times S + (Sector - 1) $$ **Inverse translation** (given LBA $= L$): - $Sector = (L \bmod S) + 1$ - $tmp = \lfloor L / S \rfloor$ - $Head = tmp \bmod H$ - $Cyl = \lfloor tmp / H \rfloor$ **Numerical example** (from §3.3 above): LBA 4 938 005 on a disk with $H=4$, $S=1000$ ⇒ $Cyl=1234, Head=2, Sector=5$. ### Model Solution — Part (b) | Parameter | HDD | SSD | |---|---|---| | (i) Access time | 5–15 ms (seek + rotation) | 0.05–0.1 ms (no mechanical delay) | | (ii) IOPS (random 4 KB) | 100–200 | 50 000–500 000 | | (iii) Cost / GB | ₹2–3 | ₹8–15 | | (iv) Endurance | Unlimited (mechanical wear only) | Limited (~3 000–100 000 P/E cycles per cell) | | (v) Power-loss safety | Very safe (data already on platter) | Unsafe (page in-flight may be lost) — needs supercap/UPS | | (vi) Fragmentation effect | Significant (extra seeks) | Negligible (random access ≈ sequential) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "block" with "sector":** Many students use them interchangeably. *Block* is the OS/file-system level (e.g., 4 KB), *sector* is the disk-physical level. They are related ($1 \text{ block} = k \text{ sectors}$) but distinct. > 2. **Forgetting the "average" in rotational latency:** Writing the full rotation time (e.g., 8.33 ms for 7200 RPM) instead of half (4.17 ms) — instant loss of 1–2 marks. > 3. **Wrong transfer-time units:** Mixing KB and KiB, or computing $T_t = B / \text{TransferRate}$ where TransferRate must be in **bytes/ms**, not MB/s. Always state the conversion. > 4. **Drawing only one platter:** A full 7-mark diagram of a disk *must* show ≥ 2 platters and label the **cylinder** — single-platter drawings lose 2–3 marks. > 5. **Saying "LBA is faster than CHS":** LBA is *not* faster; it is just *simpler* for the OS. Speed is determined by mechanical delays, which are identical. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist) - 🧠 **Hierarchical structure** of a disk: Platter → Surface → Track → Sector (512 B / 4 KiB); Cylinder = tracks at same radius on every surface. - 🧠 **Three delays** in any HDD access: $T_s$ (seek) + $T_r$ (rotational latency) + $T_t$ (transfer). - 🧠 **Average rotational latency** = $\dfrac{30\,000}{R}$ ms where $R$ is in RPM — *half* a full rotation, not full. - 🧠 **Average seek time** is approximated as **1/3 of full-stroke** (not 1/2). - 🧠 **LBA** replaced CHS for capacities beyond 528 MB; modern LBA is **48-bit** ⇒ max 128 PiB. - 🧠 **Sector = 512 B (legacy)** vs **4 KiB (Advanced Format)**; OS still issues 512-B logical blocks due to emulation (512e). - 🧠 **Cylinder concept** is critical: switching between surfaces on the **same cylinder** needs only **head switch (electronic, ~0.1 ms)** — no seek. - 🧠 **Transfer time** for a *single* 4-KiB block is **negligible** (sub-millisecond); mechanical delays dominate. - 🧠 **SSD** has no $T_s$ and no $T_r$, but has *write amplification*, *wear leveling*, and *garbage collection* — different performance model. - 🧠 **Bad-block management**: two methods — *sector sparing* (replace bad sector with a spare from a reserved pool) and *sector slipping* (skip the bad sector, renumber). - 🧠 **Low-level format** creates physical sectors; **high-level format** creates the file system structures. - 🧠 KTU exam favorite: derive $T_{acc}$ for given $R$, $B$, $N_{track}$, $T_s$ — practice at least 3 numericals before the exam.

topics.title: Disk Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Disk Scheduling** is a fundamental operating system technique used to determine the sequence in which pending I/O requests to the disk are serviced by the disk controller. Since a disk is a mechanical electromechanical device, the **seek time** (time taken by the read/write head to move to the correct track) and **rotational latency** (time for the platter to rotate to the correct sector) dominate the I/O latency. The OS uses a *disk scheduling algorithm* to minimize these components, primarily by reducing the total **head movement** (number of cylinders traversed) before all queued requests are served.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Disk scheduling is positioned as a core Mass-Storage Management sub-topic. Questions are frequently asked under the broader umbrella of File System & Secondary Storage, often combining it with concepts like FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, and C-LOOK algorithms.

### 1.2 The Three Components of Disk Access Time

The total time taken to service a disk I/O request is mathematically expressed as:

$$T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rotational} + T_{transfer}$$

Where:
- $T_{seek}$ → **Seek Time** = time to move the disk arm to the target cylinder.
- $T_{rotational}$ → **Rotational Latency** = time to wait for the platter to bring the correct sector under the head.
- $T_{transfer}$ → **Transfer Time** = time to actually read/write the data once the head is positioned.

Disk scheduling focuses exclusively on minimizing $T_{seek}$, because seek time is the most variable and the largest contributor to latency in modern hard drives (typically **3 ms to 12 ms** depending on track distance).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Librarian's Strategy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Overworked Librarian:** Imagine a librarian in a 200-aisle library. Eight students simultaneously request books from aisles 98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, and 67. The librarian is currently in aisle 53 and can carry only **one book at a time**. She must decide *which request to serve first*.
>
> - **FCFS** (First Come First Serve): She serves them strictly in the order the requests arrived — even if it means a wild zig-zag across the building.
> - **SSTF** (Shortest Seek Time First): She always picks the book whose aisle is *physically closest* to her current position — efficient, but students in far aisles may starve.
> - **SCAN**: Like an *elevator*, she walks to one end of the library first, serving everyone along the way, then reverses.
> - **C-SCAN**: Like a *conveyor belt* — she serves everyone in one direction, then teleports back to the start (sacrificing some efficiency for fairness).
> - **LOOK** & **C-LOOK**: Smarter variants of SCAN that *don't go all the way* to the wall if no student is waiting there.
>
> The disk arm behaves exactly like this librarian, and the OS plays the role of the strategic planner.

### 1.4 Why Disk Scheduling Is Critical

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical delay** | Disk arms are physical; every extra track crossed costs real time (**~0.1–0.5 ms per track**). |
| **Multiple concurrent processes** | Modern OS runs hundreds of processes generating I/O requests simultaneously. |
| **Bottleneck avoidance** | Without scheduling, the disk becomes the *primary performance bottleneck* even with fast CPUs and RAM. |
| **Throughput optimization** | A good algorithm can **reduce total seek time by 50–70%** compared to naive FCFS. |
| **Fairness vs. throughput** | A core trade-off the OS designer must balance (elaborated in Section 2). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starvation Caution:** SSTF and SCAN-like algorithms can cause **starvation** for requests at the extreme ends of the disk. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU exams — always mention it when describing SSTF.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Standard Disk Scheduling Algorithms

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates understanding the following six algorithms:

#### **A. FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)**
- Serves requests in the **exact order** they arrive in the queue.
- **Pros:** Simple, fair, no starvation.
- **Cons:** Highest head movement; does not exploit locality.
- *Logic Step 1:* Read the next request from the queue.
- *Logic Step 2:* Move the head directly to that cylinder.
- *Logic Step 3:* Repeat until the queue is empty.

#### **B. SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)**
- Always serves the request with the **shortest seek distance** from the current head position.
- **Pros:** Significantly lower total head movement than FCFS.
- **Cons:** **Starvation possible** for requests far from the head; not optimal globally.
- *Logic Step 1:* Compute $|C_i - HP|$ for every pending request.
- *Logic Step 2:* Select the request with the minimum distance.
- *Logic Step 3:* Move the head there; mark the request as serviced.

#### **C. SCAN (Elevator Algorithm)**
- Head moves in **one direction**, servicing all requests along the way, until it reaches the **end of the disk**, then **reverses direction**.
- **Pros:** Good throughput, no starvation (bounded waiting).
- **Cons:** Requests just behind the head (in the direction of travel) suffer longer wait — *newly arriving requests may be served quickly or slowly depending on side*.
- *Logic Step 1:* Pick a direction (say, increasing cylinder numbers).
- *Logic Step 2:* Service all pending requests in that direction.
- *Logic Step 3:* When the last cylinder is reached, reverse direction and repeat.

#### **D. C-SCAN (Circular SCAN)**
- Like SCAN, but after reaching the end, the head **jumps back to cylinder 0** *without servicing any requests on the return trip* — then starts servicing again.
- **Pros:** Provides a **more uniform wait time** for all requests (fairness).
- **Cons:** Wastes seek time on the jump-back to 0.
- *Logic Step 1:* Service all requests from current position toward the higher end.
- *Logic Step 2:* On reaching the maximum cylinder, **jump** directly to 0.
- *Logic Step 3:* Service any remaining requests in the increasing direction.

#### **E. LOOK**
- A **smarter SCAN**: head reverses at the *last request* in each direction, *not* at the physical end of the disk.
- **Pros:** Avoids the unnecessary end-to-end travel of SCAN → lower total head movement.
- **Cons:** Slightly less uniform than C-SCAN.

#### **F. C-LOOK**
- The **circular variant of LOOK**: head services in one direction, then **jumps to the lowest pending request** and continues.
- **Pros:** Combines the locality of LOOK with the fairness of C-SCAN.
- **Cons:** Still incurs the jump-back cost.

### 2.2 The Core Mathematical Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Memorize this table.** Every numerical disk scheduling problem in KTU exams reduces to one of these equations.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Where Used** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Head Movement (THM) | $THM = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert C_{i} - C_{i-1} \vert$ | Cylinders (tracks) | All algorithms |
| Total Seek Time | $T_{seek,total} = THM \times T_{s}$ | ms | All algorithms |
| Average Seek Time | $T_{avg} = T_{seek,total} \div n$ | ms | All algorithms |
| Rotational Latency | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{2 \times RPM}$ seconds | ms | Disk access time |
| Total Access Time | $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{trans}$ | ms | Full I/O cost |
| C-SCAN Jump Penalty | $T_{jump} = (N_{max} - 0) \times T_{s} = N_{max} \times T_{s}$ | ms | C-SCAN / C-LOOK only |
| SCAN End-Traversal | $T_{end} = (N_{max} - C_{last\_served}) \times T_{s}$ | ms | SCAN only |

Where:
- $C_i$ = cylinder of the $i^{th}$ request serviced
- $HP$ = current head position
- $T_s$ = time per single track seek (typically given in the problem)
- $N_{max}$ = maximum cylinder number (disk geometry)
- $n$ = total number of requests
- $RPM$ = disk spindle rotations per minute

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Field** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| **Database Systems** | DBMS engines (PostgreSQL, Oracle) re-order disk I/O for query optimization. |
| **Cloud Storage (AWS, Azure)** | Storage controllers in data centers use variants of SCAN/LOOK for SSD & HDD hybrids. |
| **Operating Systems** | Linux uses the **CFQ, Deadline, and NOOP** schedulers — Deadline is essentially a variant of LOOK with priority. |
| **Embedded Systems** | RTOS kernels (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) use deterministic disk schedulers for real-time guarantees. |
| **RAID Controllers** | Hardware RAID uses SCAN-like algorithms across multiple platters. |
| **SSD Firmware** | While SSDs have no seek time, **garbage collection** uses similar scheduling logic internally. |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU:** If asked "which algorithm is used in real OS?", the correct KTU-expected answer is **LOOK or a variant** (like the Linux Elevator / Deadline scheduler). DO NOT say "FCFS" — that is a textbook-only algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The KTU Standard Reference Problem

To compare all six algorithms fairly, we use the **canonical KTU problem statement**:

> **Problem Statement:** A disk has **200 cylinders** (numbered **0 to 199**). The disk head is currently at cylinder **53**. The following I/O requests are pending in the queue, in the given order:
>
> **98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67**
>
> The head movement direction for SCAN-family algorithms is **towards the higher cylinder numbers** (i.e., increasing). Compute the **total head movement (in cylinders)** for **all six** algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sorted request queue (ascending) is: **14, 37, 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183**.
> We will use this sorted view in SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, and C-LOOK for clarity.

### 3.2 Algorithm 1: FCFS Calculation

**Logic:** Serve the requests in the exact order they were received.

**Service Order:** 53 → 98 → 183 → 37 → 122 → 14 → 124 → 65 → 67

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement} &= |53-98| + |98-183| + |183-37| + |37-122| \\
&\quad + |122-14| + |14-124| + |124-65| + |65-67|
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 45 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{640 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Algorithm 2: SSTF Calculation

**Logic:** From the current head position, always pick the *nearest* unserved request.

**Step 1 — From cylinder 53**, compute distances to all pending requests:

| Request | 98 | 183 | 37 | 122 | 14 | 124 | 65 | 67 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Distance from 53 | 45 | 130 | 16 | 69 | 39 | 71 | 12 | 14 |

**Minimum is 12 → serve 65 first.**

**Step 2 — From cylinder 65**, distances to remaining: {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 67}

| Request | 98 | 183 | 37 | 122 | 14 | 124 | 67 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Distance from 65 | 33 | 118 | 28 | 57 | 51 | 59 | **2** |

**Minimum is 2 → serve 67 next.**

**Step 3 — From cylinder 67**, distances to remaining: {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124}

| Request | 98 | 183 | 37 | 122 | 14 | 124 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Distance from 67 | 31 | 116 | 30 | 55 | 53 | 57 |

**Minimum is 30 → serve 37 next.** (Tie with 98's 31, but 30 < 31.)

Continuing this greedy selection yields the final service order:

$$
53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-37| + |37-14| + |14-98| \\
&\quad + |98-122| + |122-124| + |124-183| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{236 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** SSTF achieves only **36.9% of FCFS's** head movement. This dramatic reduction is why SSTF is the de-facto baseline for "good" schedulers.

### 3.4 Algorithm 3: SCAN Calculation

**Logic:** Move the head in the increasing direction, service all requests in sorted order, reach the end (199), reverse, and service the rest.

**Service Order:** 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → 37 → 14

> *Notice the head traverses all the way to 199 even though the last request in that direction was at 183.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| \\
&\quad + |124-183| + |183-199| + |199-37| + |37-14| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 162 + 23 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{331 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Algorithm 4: C-SCAN Calculation

**Logic:** Like SCAN, but after reaching 199, the head **jumps** back to 0 (without servicing), then continues in the increasing direction.

**Service Order:** 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → [JUMP] → 0 → 14 → 37

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| \\
&\quad + |124-183| + |183-199| + \underbrace{|199-0|}_{\text{Jump}} + |0-14| + |14-37| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 199 + 14 + 23 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{382 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **C-SCAN trap for students:** The jump from 199 → 0 is the *full width of the disk* (199 cylinders), NOT zero. Forgetting to add this jump is the single most common calculation mistake in KTU exams.

### 3.6 Algorithm 5: LOOK Calculation

**Logic:** Same as SCAN, but reverse direction at the *last request* (183), NOT at the disk end (199).

**Service Order:** 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 37 → 14

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| \\
&\quad + |124-183| + |183-37| + |37-14| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 146 + 23 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{299 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **LOOK vs. SCAN:** LOOK saves $(199 - 183) = 16$ cylinders on the right-end approach, and an additional similar amount on the return. That's why LOOK is strictly better than SCAN for this dataset.

### 3.7 Algorithm 6: C-LOOK Calculation

**Logic:** Like C-SCAN, but jump from the highest served request (183) directly to the lowest pending (14), instead of going to 199 then 0.

**Service Order:** 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → [JUMP] → 14 → 37

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Movement} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| \\
&\quad + |124-183| + \underbrace{|183-14|}_{\text{Jump}} + |14-37| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 169 + 23 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{322 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.8 Master Comparison Matrix

This is the **single most important table** for KTU 14-mark calculation questions — you should be able to reproduce it from memory.

| **Algorithm** | **Service Order** | **Total Head Movement (cylinders)** | **% vs. FCFS** | **Starvation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | 98 → 183 → 37 → 122 → 14 → 124 → 65 → 67 | **640** | 100.0% | No |
| **SSTF** | 65 → 67 → 37 → 14 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 | **236** | 36.9% | **Yes** |
| **SCAN** | 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → 37 → 14 | **331** | 51.7% | No |
| **C-SCAN** | 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → 0 → 14 → 37 | **382** | 59.7% | No |
| **LOOK** | 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 37 → 14 | **299** | 46.7% | No |
| **C-LOOK** | 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 14 → 37 | **322** | 50.3% | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ranking (Best → Worst for THIS problem):** SSTF (236) < LOOK (299) < C-LOOK (322) < SCAN (331) < C-SCAN (382) < FCFS (640).
>
> In general problems, LOOK variants are usually best, but SSTF can outperform LOOK for highly localized workloads.

### 3.9 Worked Example: Computing Total Seek Time

**Given additional data:** Suppose the disk takes **$T_s = 0.2$ ms per track** to move the head. Compute total seek time for **LOOK**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{seek,total} &= THM \times T_s \\
&= 299 \text{ cylinders} \times 0.2 \text{ ms/track} \\
&= 59.8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average seek time per request:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= \frac{T_{seek,total}}{n} = \frac{59.8}{8} = 7.475 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Where Disk Scheduling Fits in the OS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Process Issues I/O Request read or write] --> B[File System Layer Translates to Block Addresses]
    B --> C[Disk Scheduler Selects Order from Pending Queue]
    C --> D[Disk Driver Issues Commands to Disk Controller]
    D --> E[Disk Controller Moves Arm to Cylinder]
    E --> F[Sector Read Write Completes]
    F --> G[Interrupt Raised to OS]
    G --> H[OS Notifies the Process]
    C -.-> C1[Pending Request Queue Sorted by Cylinder]
    C -.-> C2[Current Head Position HP]
    C -.-> C3[Algorithm Chosen at Boot FCFS SSTF LOOK etc]
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Selection Flowchart (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Disk Scheduling Algorithm Selection] --> B{Throughput Priority?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Starvation Acceptable?}
    B -- No --> D[Use FCFS]
    C -- Yes --> E[Use SSTF]
    C -- No --> F{Fair Wait Time Required?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use C-LOOK or C-SCAN]
    F -- No --> H[Use SCAN or LOOK]
    E --> I[Starvation Warning Extreme Cylinder Requests May Wait Forever]
    G --> J[Uniform Wait Time Achieved]
    H --> K[Bounded Wait Time Achieved]
    D --> L[Simple but Slowest]
```

### 4.3 Head Movement Timeline (LOOK algorithm)

The diagram below visualizes the head's path for the **LOOK** algorithm. Notice the head reverses at cylinder 183, not at 199 — the defining feature of LOOK vs. SCAN.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0((53)) --> H1((65)) --> H2((67)) --> H3((98)) --> H4((122)) --> H5((124)) --> H6((183))
    H6 -. Reverse Direction .-> H7((37)) --> H8((14))
    style H0 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000
    style H6 fill:#e06666,stroke:#000
    style H8 fill:#93c47d,stroke:#000
```

### 4.4 Comparative Performance Bar Chart (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FCFS 640 cyl] --- A2[SSTF 236 cyl] --- A3[LOOK 299 cyl] --- A4[C-LOOK 322 cyl] --- A5[SCAN 331 cyl] --- A6[C-SCAN 382 cyl]
    style A1 fill:#f4cccc
    style A2 fill:#d9ead3
    style A3 fill:#fff2cc
    style A4 fill:#fff2cc
    style A5 fill:#fce5cd
    style A6 fill:#f4cccc
```

### 4.5 Disk Geometry Reference Diagram (Logical View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Platter Spinning] --> A1[Track 0 Outer]
    A1 --> A2[Track 1]
    A2 --> A3[...]
    A3 --> A4[Track 183 Target]
    A4 --> A5[...]
    A5 --> A6[Track 199 Inner Last]
    A6 --> A7[Read Write Head on Arm]
    A7 -. Moves across .-> A1
    A7 -. Moves across .-> A6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The arm holding the read/write head pivots across the spinning platter. Each track crossing corresponds to one **seek** operation. Minimizing the total number of track crossings is the literal goal of every disk scheduling algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

Define disk scheduling. List any **four** disk scheduling algorithms and state **one** advantage of SSTF over FCFS. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Disk Scheduling** is the technique used by the operating system to determine the order in which multiple pending disk I/O requests are serviced, with the primary objective of minimizing the total **seek time** (and consequently the average access time) of the disk arm.
>
> **Four Disk Scheduling Algorithms:**
> 1. **FCFS** (First-Come, First-Served)
> 2. **SSTF** (Shortest Seek Time First)
> 3. **SCAN** (Elevator Algorithm)
> 4. **C-SCAN** (Circular SCAN)
>
> *(Other valid options: LOOK, C-LOOK)*
>
> **One advantage of SSTF over FCFS:** SSTF always services the *nearest* request first, which **significantly reduces the total head movement** compared to FCFS. For example, in the standard KTU problem, SSTF achieves only **36.9%** of FCFS's head movement (236 vs 640 cylinders). However, this comes at the cost of potential **starvation** for requests located at the extreme ends of the disk.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

What is **seek time**? How does **SCAN** algorithm differ from **C-SCAN** algorithm in handling requests after the head reaches the end of the disk? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Seek Time** is the time taken by the disk's read/write head to move from its current cylinder position to the cylinder where the requested data is located. Mathematically, it is the time required to traverse a certain number of tracks, typically expressed as $T_{seek} = n \times T_s$ where $n$ is the number of tracks crossed and $T_s$ is the time per track.
>
> **Difference between SCAN and C-SCAN after reaching the disk end:**
>
> | Aspect | SCAN | C-SCAN |
> |---|---|---|
> | After reaching cylinder $N_{max}$ (199) | Head **reverses direction** and immediately starts servicing requests in the *opposite* direction. | Head **jumps back to cylinder 0** *without servicing* any requests, then starts servicing in the original (increasing) direction. |
> | Service pattern | Bidirectional (goes up, then comes back down servicing). | Unidirectional (only services requests in the increasing direction; the return is a "free" jump). |
> | Wait time uniformity | Less uniform — requests just behind the head wait less than those just ahead. | More uniform — all requests experience roughly the same maximum wait time. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

Consider a disk with **200 cylinders (0–199)**. The read/write head is currently at cylinder **53**. The following I/O requests arrive in the queue: **98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67**. Assume head movement for SCAN-family algorithms is **towards the higher cylinder numbers**.

**(a)** Compute the **total head movement** using the **FCFS** and **SSTF** algorithms. Show the complete service order and all calculations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **total head movement** using the **SCAN** and **LOOK** algorithms. State **one practical advantage** of LOOK over SCAN. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — FCFS and SSTF**

**FCFS Algorithm:**

Service requests in arrival order: 98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{FCFS} &= |53-98| + |98-183| + |183-37| + |37-122| \\
&\quad + |122-14| + |14-124| + |124-65| + |65-67| \\[2mm]
&= 45 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{640 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**SSTF Algorithm:**

At each step, pick the *nearest* unserved request.

- From 53: nearest is 65 (dist 12).
- From 65: nearest is 67 (dist 2).
- From 67: nearest is 37 (dist 30). *(tie with 98 at 31, but 30 < 31)*
- From 37: nearest is 14 (dist 23).
- From 14: nearest is 98 (dist 84).
- From 98: nearest is 122 (dist 24).
- From 122: nearest is 124 (dist 2).
- From 124: nearest is 183 (dist 59).

Service order: 53 → 65 → 67 → 37 → 14 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{SSTF} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-37| + |37-14| + |14-98| \\
&\quad + |98-122| + |122-124| + |124-183| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{236 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Part (b) — SCAN and LOOK**

**SCAN Algorithm:**

Service in increasing direction, reach the end (199), then reverse.

Service order: 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → 37 → 14.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{SCAN} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| \\
&\quad + |124-183| + |183-199| + |199-37| + |37-14| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 162 + 23 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{331 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**LOOK Algorithm:**

Same as SCAN, but reverse at the *last request* in each direction (183, not 199).

Service order: 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 37 → 14.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{LOOK} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| \\
&\quad + |124-183| + |183-37| + |37-14| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 146 + 23 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{299 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Advantage of LOOK over SCAN:**

LOOK saves the unnecessary traversal to the physical end of the disk (cylinder 199) by reversing at the *last pending request* (cylinder 183). This reduces the total head movement (299 vs. 331 cylinders in this problem, a saving of **9.7%**), thereby lowering the average seek time and increasing the overall disk throughput. In real-world systems, this is why most production OS schedulers (e.g., Linux's historical *Elevator* scheduler) are based on LOOK rather than pure SCAN.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **C-SCAN** and **C-LOOK** algorithms with neat service-order diagrams. Compute the total head movement for both algorithms using the following data: **Disk cylinders = 200 (0–199), current head position = 53, request queue = {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67}, head moves towards higher cylinder numbers.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **factors that affect disk scheduling** performance. Why does FCFS, despite being the simplest, result in the worst throughput? What is **starvation**, and which algorithms are vulnerable to it? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — C-SCAN and C-LOOK**

**C-SCAN Algorithm:**

Service all requests in the increasing direction, reach cylinder 199, *jump* to 0, and continue in the increasing direction.

Service order: 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → 199 → (jump) → 0 → 14 → 37.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{C\text{-}SCAN} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| \\
&\quad + |124-183| + |183-199| + |199-0| + |0-14| + |14-37| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 199 + 14 + 23 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{382 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**C-LOOK Algorithm:**

Service in increasing direction, then *jump* from the highest served (183) directly to the lowest pending (14), and continue.

Service order: 53 → 65 → 67 → 98 → 122 → 124 → 183 → (jump) → 14 → 37.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{C\text{-}LOOK} &= |53-65| + |65-67| + |67-98| + |98-122| + |122-124| \\
&\quad + |124-183| + |183-14| + |14-37| \\[2mm]
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 169 + 23 \\[2mm]
&= \boxed{322 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Factors, FCFS Drawback, and Starvation**

**Factors affecting disk scheduling performance:**

1. **Number of pending I/O requests** — A larger queue gives the scheduler more freedom to optimize.
2. **Spatial locality of requests** — If requests are clustered in adjacent cylinders, SSTF and LOOK perform exceptionally well.
3. **Direction of head movement** — Bidirectional vs. unidirectional service drastically changes wait-time uniformity.
4. **Disk geometry (number of cylinders, sectors per track, RPM)** — Affects the absolute seek time per track.
5. **Arrival rate of new requests** — Dynamically arriving requests can invalidate an existing schedule.
6. **Per-track seek time ($T_s$)** — Determines the real-world cost of each track crossed.

**Why FCFS has the worst throughput:**

FCFS makes decisions purely on the *time of arrival* of requests, with **no regard to the physical head position**. As a result, the head is forced to jump back and forth across the disk in a chaotic, zig-zag pattern (e.g., 53 → 98 → 183 → 37 → ...). Each such jump incurs a heavy seek-time penalty. In contrast, even the second-best algorithm (LOOK) achieves **46.7% of FCFS's movement** by exploiting spatial locality. FCFS also provides **no bounded waiting guarantee** in terms of *physical* distance — a request may have arrived early but still wait long if the head has to travel far to reach it.

**Starvation:**

> **Starvation** is a condition in which a particular I/O request remains pending in the queue **indefinitely** because the scheduler keeps prioritizing other requests.

**Algorithms vulnerable to starvation:**

- **SSTF** — Always services the nearest request, so a request at the far end of the disk may be bypassed repeatedly as new, closer requests keep arriving. This can result in **unbounded waiting time**.
- **(Less commonly) FCFS** — While theoretically starvation-free, in pathological cases of extreme load, FCFS can cause effectively unbounded waits (though not strict starvation in the formal sense).

**Algorithms that prevent starvation:**

- **SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK** — Because the head sweeps across the entire disk (or at least across all pending requests) in a cyclic manner, every request is guaranteed to be served within a *bounded* number of head reversals/jumps. Maximum wait time is therefore **finite and predictable**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Disk Scheduling Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the C-SCAN/C-LOOK jump cost.** The jump from the last served cylinder back to the first (e.g., 199 → 0, or 183 → 14) must be **counted** as head movement. Forgetting this loses **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Mis-sorting the request queue.** Always sort ascending *before* applying SCAN/LOOK. Using the arrival order in SCAN is a fatal error.
> 3. **Wrong service order for SSTF.** SSTF is *greedy* and based on distance from the *current* position, not from a fixed reference. Re-evaluate distances at every step.
> 4. **Writing the formula but skipping the calculation.** KTU valuation requires the **explicit addition step** (e.g., "12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + ... = 299"). A bare formula with no arithmetic = **0 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention starvation** when describing SSTF. Examiners explicitly look for the word "starvation" in SSTF answers.
> 6. **Confusing LOOK with SCAN.** Always state explicitly that LOOK *"reverses at the last request, not at the disk end"*. This single sentence distinguishes a 7-mark answer from a full-mark one.
> 7. **Mixing up rotational latency and seek time.** Remember: $T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{2 \times RPM}$ seconds, and is **independent** of the head movement.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Disk Scheduling):**
>
> - Disk scheduling aims to **minimize total head movement (seek time)**, which is the dominant component of disk access time.
> - Access time has **three components**: $T_{seek}$ (head movement) + $T_{rotational}$ (spinning latency) + $T_{transfer}$ (data read/write).
> - **FCFS** is fair but has the **worst throughput** (zig-zag head movement).
> - **SSTF** is the **most efficient** in terms of pure head movement, but causes **starvation** for far-away requests.
> - **SCAN** (Elevator) eliminates starvation and offers good throughput, but wastes movement by going all the way to disk ends.
> - **C-SCAN** provides **uniform wait time** for all requests by treating the disk as a *circular* list, but has a high jump-back cost.
> - **LOOK** is a **smarter SCAN** that reverses at the *last request* — preferred in real OS.
> - **C-LOOK** is a **smarter C-SCAN** that jumps from the *last served* to the *lowest pending* — combines locality + fairness.
> - For the **canonical KTU problem** (queue 98,183,37,122,14,124,65,67 from 53), the head movement values are:
>   * FCFS = **640**, SSTF = **236**, SCAN = **331**, C-SCAN = **382**, LOOK = **299**, C-LOOK = **322**.
> - **Linux uses a LOOK variant** (Deadline scheduler) in production, not FCFS.
> - **Starvation** = indefinite wait. Vulnerable algorithms: **SSTF**. Safe algorithms: **SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK, FCFS**.
> - **C-SCAN / C-LOOK jump distance** is the single most-missed step in KTU numerical problems — always remember to count it.
> - **Rotational latency** formula: $T_{rot} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}$ in **milliseconds** (half a rotation on average).
> - **Real-world examples**: Database optimizers, RAID controllers, cloud storage backends all use variants of these algorithms.
> - Always **show the service order explicitly** in the answer — KTU examiners award partial credit for a correct order even if arithmetic contains minor slips.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk Formatting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET632 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk Formatting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Disk Formatting** is the preparatory procedure performed by an operating system (or a low-level controller) that establishes the logical data structures on a physical storage medium (HDD, SSD, USB, etc.) so that the OS can read, write, organize, and retrieve files reliably.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Operating Systems syllabus, Disk Formatting is treated under **Module 4: Secondary Storage Management**, and it is formally subdivided into two distinct hierarchical layers:

1. **Physical (Low-Level) Formatting** — Performed at the factory by the disk manufacturer. It divides each platter surface into **sectors**, **tracks**, and **cylinders**, and writes the sector header/trailer fields (preamble, address mark, ECC, inter-sector gap).
2. **Logical (High-Level) Formatting** — Performed by the OS using a utility such as `format`, `mkfs.ext4`, `mkfs.ntfs`, or `mkfs.fat`. It initializes an *empty* file system by creating the **boot block, superblock, free-space map, and root directory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEET632 – Module 4):** "Disk formatting – physical formatting, logical formatting, partition tables, file system structures (superblock, FAT, inodes), boot blocks, bad-block management."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Empty Library"**
>
> Imagine you have just been handed a brand-new, completely empty **library building** with thousands of identical locked drawers (the raw sectors on disk). Before any book can be shelved:
>
> 1. The **carpenters** must first number every drawer, paint aisle markers, and fix each drawer's latch so it can be opened and closed. This is **physical (low-level) formatting** — the manufacturer doing the foundational geometric work.
> 2. The **chief librarian** (the OS) must then set up a **catalog system** at the entrance (boot block), an **inventory index** (superblock/FAT), and the rule-book telling staff how to log where each new book is placed. This is **logical (high-level) formatting**.
> 3. Finally, the librarian labels certain aisles as "Reference," "Fiction," and "Journals" — this is **partitioning**.

Without formatting, the disk is just a silent sequence of unaddressed magnetic or electronic cells — perfectly usable as a paperweight, but utterly useless as storage.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Sector** | Smallest addressable unit on a disk | **512 B** (legacy) or **4096 B** (Advanced Format) |
| **Track** | Concentric ring of sectors on a single platter surface | Thousands per surface |
| **Cylinder** | Stack of tracks aligned vertically across platters | — |
| **Block / Cluster** | Logical grouping of sectors used by the file system | 4 KB – 64 KB |
| **Inter-sector gap** | Physical spacing between sectors (header/timing) | ~100 bytes |
| **Seek time** | Time to move the read/write head to a track | **3 – 10 ms** |
| **Rotational latency** | Wait for the sector to rotate under the head | **4 – 8 ms** (at 7200 RPM) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Fact:** A standard modern HDD rotates at **7,200 RPM** (or **15,000 RPM** for enterprise drives), giving an average rotational latency of approximately **4.17 ms** — half of the full rotation period.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Layers of Formatting — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Physical (Low-Level) Formatting

- Performed **once at the factory**; rarely re-done by end-users (and *not recommended* for modern drives).
- Divides each platter surface into a grid of **tracks × sectors**.
- Writes the **sector header** containing:
  - **Sync pattern / preamble** — synchronizes the read channel clock.
  - **Address mark** — marks the start of a sector.
  - **Track & sector ID** — physical location.
  - **ECC (Error-Correcting Code)** — e.g., **Reed–Solomon** code allowing correction of small bit errors.
  - **Inter-sector gap** — buffer preventing mechanical timing drift.
- The result is a disk where the controller can identify and access every sector using **CHS (Cylinder–Head–Sector)** addressing or **LBA (Logical Block Addressing)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ECC matters:** A 512-byte sector typically reserves ~50–70 bytes for ECC, providing enough redundancy to correct a burst error of up to ~30 bits (for a typical 50-byte Reed–Solomon block).

### 2.1.2 Logical (High-Level) Formatting

This is the *OS-controlled* step that builds a usable file system. Its core structures include:

1. **Boot Block (Block 0)** — Contains the MBR/GRUB/EFI bootloader stub used to start the OS.
2. **Superblock / Volume Control Block (VCB)** — The *master index* describing the entire file system: total blocks, free blocks, root directory location, block size, FS type, magic number.
3. **Free-Space Management Structure**
   - **FAT (File Allocation Table)** — A linked-list of clusters indicating which are free/used/end-of-chain.
   - **Bit-map (Bitmap)** — One bit per block; `1` = allocated, `0` = free (used in ext2/3/4, NTFS $Bitmap file).
4. **Inode Table** — (UNIX-style FS) One fixed-size inode per file storing metadata (size, permissions, timestamps, 12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect block pointers).
5. **Root Directory** — The top-level directory; in UNIX it is simply inode #2.

## 2.2 Partitioning — Bridging Hardware and Software

A *partition* is a logical subdivision of a physical disk, each treated as an independent storage unit.

| Partition Table Type | Used By | Max Partitions | Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MBR (Master Boot Record)** | Legacy BIOS | 4 primary (or 3 primary + 1 extended) | **2 TB** limit |
| **GPT (GUID Partition Table)** | UEFI modern systems | **128** primary | **9.4 ZB** (zetta-bytes) |
| **APM (Apple Partition Map)** | Older Macs | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why partition?** It enables **multiple file systems** on one drive (e.g., dual-boot Linux ext4 + Windows NTFS), isolates crashes/data corruption, and simplifies backups.

## 2.3 Bad-Block Management

Manufacturing defects or wear-out may cause **bad sectors** — sectors that reliably fail ECC or refuse to respond.

| Strategy | Description |
|---|---|
| **Sector Sparing (Forwarding)** | A *spare* sector (set aside at low-level format) replaces the bad one. The controller remaps the LBA transparently. |
| **Sector Slipping (Skipping)** | The bad sector is skipped; the next logical sector is mapped to the next physical sector. Used when the bad block is in a critical FS structure area. |
| **OS-Level Marking** | Tools like `fsck`, `badblocks`, or `chkdsk` mark the sector unusable in the file system's free-space bitmap. |

## 2.4 Solid-State Disk (SSD) Formatting Caveats

> [!CAUTION]
> SSD is **NOT** low-level formatted in the traditional sense. Instead, it is *factory-preconditioned* with a logical-to-physical mapping table. When the user performs a "format," only the **L2P table** is reset (a *pseudo-format*).

Key SSD concepts for the KTU exam:
- **TRIM command** — Tells the SSD which LBAs are no longer in use, allowing it to reclaim blocks via garbage collection.
- **Wear Leveling** — Distributes writes evenly across NAND cells to extend drive life (rated in **TBW – Terabytes Written**).
- **Over-Provisioning** — Hidden spare capacity (typically **7 – 28 %**) used by the SSD controller for bad-block replacement and wear leveling.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations for Calculation-Based Questions**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average rotational latency | $T_{lat} = \dfrac{60}{2 \times RPM}$ | seconds |
| 2 | Total disk capacity | $C = \text{Tracks/surface} \times \text{Surfaces} \times \text{Sectors/track} \times \text{Bytes/sector}$ | bytes |
| 3 | Sectors per track (zone-bit recording) | $\text{Spt} = \text{constant on outer zones}$ | sectors |
| 4 | Useful capacity after formatting | $C_{usable} = C_{raw} - C_{boot} - C_{FS\_metadata} - C_{spare}$ | bytes |
| 5 | ECC overhead fraction | $\eta_{ecc} = \dfrac{B_{ecc}}{B_{data}+B_{ecc}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Addressable blocks (FAT32) | $2^{32}$ clusters max | clusters |
| 7 | Addressable blocks (ext4) | $2^{32}$ blocks per group × up to $2^{16}$ groups ≈ **$2^{48}$** | blocks |
| 8 | Inode direct pointers | 12 | pointers |
| 9 | Max file size (inode, 4 KB block) | $12 \cdot B + (B/4) \cdot B + (B/4)^2 \cdot B + (B/4)^3 \cdot B$ | bytes |

> **Critical Reminder:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the bare vertical pipe `|` inside any table cell to avoid breaking markdown rendering.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Recovery:** Understanding formatting structures is essential for tools like `TestDisk`, `PhotoRec`, and forensic suites (Autopsy).
- **Embedded Systems:** Engineers must format on-board flash storage (eMMC, NOR flash) with JFFS2/UBIFS/YAFFS to handle wear-leveling.
- **Cloud Storage:** Virtual disks on AWS EBS / Azure Managed Disks are also formatted at the hypervisor level using the same logical structures.
- **Performance Tuning:** Choosing the correct *block size* (cluster size) at format time directly impacts throughput and fragmentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Disk Capacity After Formatting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU-style 7-mark problem):**
>
> *"A hard disk has 6 platters, 12 surfaces, 8192 tracks per surface, and 256 sectors per track. Each sector holds 512 bytes of user data plus a 16-byte ECC and a 24-byte header/gap overhead. Compute (a) raw disk capacity, (b) overhead fraction, (c) usable capacity. Assume the formatter reserves 1 entire surface for spare sectors."*

### Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Surfaces used for data} &= 12 - 1 = 11 \quad \text{(1 reserved as spare)} \\[4pt]
\text{Tracks/surface} &= 8192 \\[4pt]
\text{Sectors/track} &= 256 \\[4pt]
B_{data} &= 512 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
B_{ecc} &= 16 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
B_{hdr} &= 24 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Bytes Per Sector (Physical)

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{phys} &= B_{data} + B_{ecc} + B_{hdr} \\[4pt]
B_{phys} &= 512 + 16 + 24 \\[4pt]
B_{phys} &= 552 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Raw Capacity (Total Addressable Physical Bytes)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw} &= \text{Surfaces} \times \text{Tracks/surface} \times \text{Sectors/track} \times B_{phys} \\[4pt]
C_{raw} &= 12 \times 8192 \times 256 \times 552 \\[4pt]
C_{raw} &= 12 \times 8192 \times 141{,}312 \\[4pt]
C_{raw} &= 13{,}893{,}599{,}232 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
C_{raw} &\approx 12.94 \text{ GiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Overhead Fraction (of Total Physical Bytes)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ovh} &= \dfrac{B_{ecc} + B_{hdr}}{B_{phys}} \\[4pt]
\eta_{ovh} &= \dfrac{16 + 24}{552} \\[4pt]
\eta_{ovh} &= \dfrac{40}{552} \\[4pt]
\eta_{ovh} &\approx 0.0725 \quad \text{(or } 7.25\%\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Usable Capacity (Excluding Spare Surface)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= 11 \times 8192 \times 256 \times 552 \\[4pt]
C_{usable} &= 12{,}735{,}799{,}296 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
C_{usable} &\approx 11.86 \text{ GiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — User-Data-Only Capacity (Logical View)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{user} &= 11 \times 8192 \times 256 \times 512 \\[4pt]
C_{user} &= 11{,}804{,}504{,}064 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
C_{user} &\approx 10.99 \text{ GiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Examiner's Insight:** Many students forget to subtract the spare surface, costing 1 mark. Always *restate the assumptions* before starting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Rotational Latency

For a disk rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ (in revolutions per minute):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{full\_rotation} &= \dfrac{60 \text{ s}}{RPM} \\[4pt]
T_{latency} &= \dfrac{T_{full\_rotation}}{2} \\[4pt]
\therefore T_{latency} &= \dfrac{30}{RPM} \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** For $RPM = 7200$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{latency} &= \dfrac{30}{7200} \\[4pt]
T_{latency} &= 4.1667 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $RPM = 15{,}000$ (enterprise):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{latency} &= \dfrac{30}{15000} \\[4pt]
T_{latency} &= 2.0 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inode Max File Size (UNIX FS, 4-KB Block)

A classical UNIX inode contains:

- **12 direct block pointers** → each points to a 4 KB data block.
- **1 single-indirect pointer** → points to a 4 KB block holding up to $1024$ indirect pointers (since each pointer is 4 B).
- **1 double-indirect pointer** → $1024^2$ blocks reachable.
- **1 triple-indirect pointer** → $1024^3$ blocks reachable.

Total addressable blocks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks} &= 12 + 1024 + 1024^2 + 1024^3 \\[4pt]
N_{blocks} &= 12 + 1024 + 1{,}048{,}576 + 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \\[4pt]
N_{blocks} &= 1{,}074{,}794{,}436 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum file size:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= N_{blocks} \times 4096 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
S_{max} &= 1{,}074{,}794{,}436 \times 4096 \\[4pt]
S_{max} &= 4{,}402{,}319{,}536{,}896 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
S_{max} &\approx 4 \text{ TiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Match with reality:** `ext4` with 4 KB blocks indeed supports individual files up to ~**4 TiB** (and up to **16 TiB** with 64 KB blocks).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Formatting Layers

| Attribute | Low-Level Format | High-Level Format |
|---|---|---|
| Performed by | Disk manufacturer | OS / user utility |
| Frequency | Once (factory) | Many times during drive life |
| Effect on data | Destructive (writes entire surface) | Generally non-destructive (unless quick-format skipped) |
| Creates | Sectors, ECC, gaps | Superblock, FAT/inodes, root dir |
| Speed | Hours (slow, seeks across all surfaces) | Seconds to minutes |
| Affected by partitions | No (raw geometry) | Yes (per-partition) |
| Required for SSD? | **No** (done at factory; never repeat) | Yes (creates L2P table reset) |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common File System Structures

| Structure | File System | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Boot Block** | All (block 0) | Holds bootstrap code |
| **Superblock** | ext2/3/4, NTFS $MFT | FS metadata header |
| **FAT** | FAT12/16/32, exFAT | Cluster allocation table |
| **Inode Table** | ext*, UFS, XFS | Per-file metadata |
| **$Bitmap** | NTFS | Tracks free/used clusters |
| **Journal** | ext3/4, NTFS, XFS | Crash-recovery log |
| **Master File Table (MFT)** | NTFS | Metadata DB for every file |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End Disk Formatting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Platter Surface] --> B[Low Level Physical Formatting]
    B --> B1[Sector headers and ECC written]
    B1 --> B2[Tracks sectors and cylinders created]
    B2 --> C[Partition Table MBR or GPT]
    C --> C1[Partition 1]
    C --> C2[Partition 2]
    C --> C3[Partition 3]
    C1 --> D1[High Level Logical Formatting]
    C2 --> D2[High Level Logical Formatting]
    C3 --> D3[High Level Logical Formatting]
    D1 --> E1[Boot Block]
    D1 --> E2[Superblock or VCB]
    D1 --> E3[Free Space Bitmap or FAT]
    D1 --> E4[Inode Table or MFT]
    D1 --> E5[Root Directory]
    D2 --> F1[Boot Block]
    D2 --> F2[FS Metadata ext4 or NTFS]
    D2 --> F3[Journal Area]
    D3 --> G1[Boot Block]
    D3 --> G2[FS Metadata FAT32]
    D3 --> G3[FAT Table]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Layout of a Formatted UNIX File System (ext-family)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Block0["Block 0 - Boot Block"]
        B0[Bootstrap Code and MBR]
    end
    subgraph Block1["Block 1 - Superblock"]
        S1[Total Blocks]
        S2[Free Blocks]
        S3[Block Size]
        S4[Magic Number]
    end
    subgraph BGDT["Block Group Descriptor Table"]
        BG1[BG 0]
        BG2[BG 1]
        BGN[BG N]
    end
    subgraph DataBlocks["Data Bitmap, Inode Bitmap, Inode Table, Data Blocks"]
        DB1[Per Block Group]
    end
    Block0 --> Block1 --> BGDT --> DataBlocks
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Bad-Block Handling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Read Sector Request] --> Q{Sector ECC Valid?}
    Q -- Yes --> OK[Return Data to Caller]
    Q -- No --> X{Retry count exhausted?}
    X -- No --> RET[Retry with Reallocated Sector]
    RET --> Q
    X -- Yes --> MARK[Mark Block Bad in FS Bitmap]
    MARK --> SP{Spare Sector Available?}
    SP -- Yes --> SWAP[Forwarding - Remap to Spare Sector]
    SP -- No --> SLIP[Slipping - Skip Block in Layout]
    SWAP --> LOG[Update Bad Block List]
    SLIP --> LOG
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — SSD Pseudo-Formatting vs HDD True Formatting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HDD["HDD True Formatting"]
        H1[Low Level Format Sectors and ECC] --> H2[High Level Format FS structures]
    end
    subgraph SSD["SSD Pseudo Formatting"]
        T1[NAND Pre-conditioned at Factory] --> T2[FTL Mapping Table Built]
        T2 --> T3[Host format only resets L2P table]
        T3 --> T4[Wear Leveling and TRIM continue in background]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between physical (low-level) and logical (high-level) formatting of a disk.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Physical Formatting | Logical Formatting |
|---|---|---|
| Performed by | Disk manufacturer | Operating system / user |
| Purpose | Creates sectors, tracks, and writes ECC | Creates file system structures (superblock, FAT, inodes) |
| Frequency | Once at factory | Can be repeated |
| Destructive? | Yes (overwrites entire surface) | Generally non-destructive (metadata only) |
| Time taken | Hours | Seconds to minutes |

> **(3 marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 for clear distinction)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the role of a **superblock** in a file system? What happens if it becomes corrupted?

**Model Answer:**
The superblock is the *master metadata block* of a file system. It contains critical information such as total block count, free block count, block size, mount status, and the file system type magic number. It is usually replicated at multiple offsets (e.g., ext2/3/4 keeps backup superblocks at block groups 0, 1, 3, 5, 7 powers) for redundancy.

If the primary superblock is corrupted, the OS will normally fall back to a backup copy. If **all** superblocks are corrupted, the file system is essentially *unmountable* and requires forensic tools like `e2fsck -b` or `mke2fs -S` to reconstruct. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently confuse **sector** with **block** — remember that sectors are the *physical* addressing unit and blocks/clusters are the *logical* addressing unit used by the FS.
> - In calculation questions, **always include units** (GiB vs GB) and state assumptions (spare sectors, ECC overhead) explicitly.
> - Do not write "the OS formats the disk" — be precise: the OS performs *logical* formatting only.
> - In SSD questions, never claim that low-level formatting is performed by the user — the FTL mapping table is reset, not the NAND cells themselves.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Module Internal Choice Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various data structures created during logical formatting of a UNIX-style file system (e.g., ext2/ext4). Use a diagram to show their layout.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A disk has 8 platters (16 surfaces), 4096 tracks per surface, 128 sectors per track. Each sector stores 512 bytes of user data and 40 bytes of overhead (header + ECC). The manufacturer reserves 2 entire surfaces as spare sectors. Compute the (i) raw capacity, (ii) overhead percentage, and (iii) usable user-data capacity in GiB. Take $1 \text{ GiB} = 2^{30} \text{ bytes}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Logical Formatting Structures in ext2/ext4:**

1. **Boot Block (Block 0)** – Contains the **MBR / GRUB** stub that locates and loads the active partition's Volume Boot Record (VBR).

2. **Superblock (Block 1)** – The file-system "ID card". Fields include:
   - `s_inodes_count`, `s_blocks_count`, `s_free_blocks_count`
   - `s_log_block_size`, `s_mtime`, `s_magic` (e.g., `0xEF53` for ext)
   - Pointer to the root directory inode (#2).

3. **Block Group Descriptor Table (BGDT)** – One descriptor per *block group*, each entry pointing to that group's data bitmap, inode bitmap, and inode-table start block.

4. **Data Block Bitmap** – 1 bit per block in the group; `1` = in use.

5. **Inode Bitmap** – 1 bit per inode slot.

6. **Inode Table** – Fixed-size (e.g., 128 B or 256 B) inode structures. Each inode stores 12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect block pointers.

7. **Data Blocks** – The actual file content. ext4 also adds **extent trees** (replacing the indirect-block chain) for huge files.

8. **Journal (ext3/4 only)** – A circular log of pending metadata changes used to recover after a crash.

**Diagram Reference:** See SECTION 4.2.

**(7 marks distribution: 1 mark each for Boot Block, Superblock, BGDT, Bitmaps, Inode Table, Data Blocks, Journal.)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Given:** 8 platters → 16 surfaces; reserves 2 surfaces → **14 data surfaces**; 4096 tracks/surface; 128 sectors/track; 512 B user data + 40 B overhead per sector.

**Step 1 — Bytes per physical sector:**

$$
B_{phys} = 512 + 40 = 552 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Raw capacity (all 16 surfaces):**

$$
C_{raw} = 16 \times 4096 \times 128 \times 552
$$

$$
C_{raw} = 16 \times 4096 \times 70{,}656 = 4{,}631{,}142{,}144 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{raw} = \dfrac{4{,}631{,}142{,}144}{2^{30}} \approx 4.3125 \text{ GiB}
$$

**Step 3 — Overhead percentage:**

$$
\eta_{ovh} = \dfrac{40}{552} \approx 7.246\%
$$

**Step 4 — Usable user-data capacity (excluding spare surfaces, counting only user bytes):**

$$
C_{user} = 14 \times 4096 \times 128 \times 512
$$

$$
C_{user} = 14 \times 4096 \times 65{,}536 = 3{,}758{,}096{,}384 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{user} = \dfrac{3{,}758{,}096{,}384}{2^{30}} \approx 3.500 \text{ GiB}
$$

> **(7 marks: 2 for Step 1, 2 for Step 2, 1 for Step 3, 2 for Step 4.)**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Module Internal Choice Option 2)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain **MBR partitioning** and **GPT partitioning** schemes. Compare them on the basis of capacity, number of partitions, and reliability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the two strategies used for **bad-block management** in disks — sector sparing and sector slipping. How does SSD handle bad blocks differently?

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**MBR (Master Boot Record):**
- Located in the **first sector** (LBA 0) of the disk.
- Contains:
  1. **Bootstrap code (446 B)** – Loads the active partition's VBR.
  2. **Partition Table (64 B)** – Four 16-byte entries describing up to **4 primary partitions**.
  3. **Magic Number (2 B)** – `0x55AA`.
- Uses **CHS** or **LBA-28** addressing.
- Maximum addressable capacity: **2 TiB** (because MBR uses 32-bit LBA × 512 B = 2 TiB).

**GPT (GUID Partition Table):**
- Located at **LBA 1** (primary GPT) with a backup at the last LBA of the disk.
- Header contains disk GUID, partition-entry array location, count, and CRC32 checksums.
- Uses **64-bit LBA** → effectively **9.4 ZB** capacity.
- Allows up to **128 primary partitions** (each 128 B by default).
- Includes a **protective MBR** (LBA 0) for legacy BIOS compatibility.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | MBR | GPT |
|---|---|---|
| Max capacity | 2 TiB | 9.4 ZB |
| Max partitions | 4 primary | 128 |
| Redundancy | None | Backup GPT + CRC32 |
| Boot mode | Legacy BIOS | UEFI |
| Partition ID | 1-byte type code | 16-byte GUID |

**(7 marks: 2 for MBR explanation + diagram, 2 for GPT explanation + diagram, 3 for comparison table.)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**1. Sector Sparing (Forwarding):**
- A pool of **spare sectors** is reserved during low-level formatting.
- When a sector fails, the controller **remaps** all subsequent accesses to a spare sector transparently.
- A **forwarding table** (in a reserved area of the disk) records the mapping.
- **Advantage:** Performance unaffected for healthy sectors.
- **Disadvantage:** Spare pool can be exhausted.

**2. Sector Slipping:**
- When a bad block is detected, the formatter **shifts** all subsequent logical sectors to skip the defective one.
- The bad sector is marked unusable and is *not* mapped to.
- **Advantage:** No need for a forwarding table.
- **Disadvantage:** Once slipping is done, the layout must remain stable; used primarily at format time.

**3. SSD Bad-Block Management:**
- SSDs use a **Flash Translation Layer (FTL)** that maintains a logical-to-physical mapping table.
- Bad NAND blocks are **retired** and replaced from a reserved over-provisioned pool (typically **7–28%** of raw NAND).
- The host OS is *unaware* of the remapping; it sees an unchanged LBA space.
- Wear-leveling algorithms continually migrate data to balance write cycles across all cells, minimizing the rate of new bad blocks.
- The **TRIM** command informs the SSD of deleted LBAs, enabling proactive block reclamation and reducing write amplification.

**(7 marks: 2 for sparing, 2 for slipping, 3 for SSD-specific handling.)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Disk formatting = physical + logical** (manufacturer + OS).
- **Physical formatting** writes sectors, ECC, and headers; **logical formatting** builds the FS metadata (boot block, superblock, FAT/inodes, free-space bitmap, root dir).
- **Bad-block management** uses **sparing** (forwarding) or **slipping**; SSDs handle bad blocks via the **FTL** and **over-provisioning**.
- **MBR** (legacy, 2 TiB cap, 4 partitions) vs **GPT** (UEFI, 9.4 ZB cap, 128 partitions, with backup).
- **FAT** = linked-list allocation; **Inode** = per-file metadata with 12 direct + 3 indirect pointers (ext family).
- **SSD formatting** does NOT touch NAND cells — only resets the FTL mapping table.
- **ECC** typically reserves ~7–8% of sector bytes for Reed–Solomon error correction.
- **Average rotational latency** $T = 30/RPM$ seconds (e.g., **4.17 ms** at 7200 RPM, **2.0 ms** at 15000 RPM).
- **Always** state assumptions (spare sectors, units, GiB vs GB) explicitly in numerical answers.
- **Boot block** is at LBA 0; **superblock** at LBA 1 in ext-family FS.
- **ext4 max file size** ≈ **4 TiB** (with 4 KB blocks) and **16 TiB** (with 64 KB blocks).
- For TRIM, wear leveling, and garbage collection — these are **SSD-only** concepts.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"**P**hysically **S**ector-ize, **L**ogically **F**ile-system-ize."* — **PSLF** = Physical → Sectors; Logical → File system.
